title: Self-Calibrating Language Models via Test-Time Discriminative Distillation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are systematically overconfident: they routinely express high certainty on questions they often answer incorrectly. Existing calibration methods either require labeled validation data, degrade under distribution shifts, or incur substantial inference costs. Recent work has shown that LLMs already contain a better-calibrated signal than the one they verbalize: the token probability of "True" when the model is asked "Is this answer correct?" ($P(\text{True})$) consistently outperforms their stated confidence, a gap that is theoretically grounded as generative error is lower-bounded by roughly twice the corresponding discriminative error. We introduce $\textbf{SECL}$ ($\textbf{SE}$lf-$\textbf{C}$alibrating $\textbf{L}$anguage Models), a test-time training (TTT) pipeline that exploits this gap as label-free self-supervision, requiring no labeled data or human supervision. SECL adapts only when the input distribution shifts, training on just 6--26% of the question stream at lower cost than the baseline it distills from. Across four small language models from three model families and four diverse domains, SECL reduces Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by 56--78%, outperforming its own supervision signal and matching or outperforming recent inference-time methods. SECL is the first method to apply TTT to calibration; seven ablations covering signal quality, gating strategy, weight accumulation, loss design, domain ordering, hyperparameter sensitivity, and layer selection confirm that each component is crucial and robust across configurations. Code: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/secl-emnlp26-submission-C890

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SECL, a test-time training framework that self-calibrates language models without labeled data by exploiting the gap between verbalized confidence and internal discriminative signals, achieving large reductions in calibration error under distribution shift.
AI.motivation: LLMs are systematically overconfident, often assigning high confidence to wrong answers, which harms their reliability. Existing calibration methods need labeled data, break under distribution shift, or are computationally expensive. Recent work shows that internal discriminative signals like P(True) from correctness queries are better calibrated than stated confidence, and theory suggests generative error is lower-bounded by about twice discriminative error. This motivates using those internal signals as a self-supervised target to improve calibration efficiently and robustly, especially under distribution shift.
AI.method: The authors introduce SECL (Self-Calibrating Language Models), a test-time training pipeline that uses the model’s own P(True) when asked “Is this answer correct?” as a label-free supervision signal. When the input distribution appears to shift, SECL selectively adapts the model on a subset (6–26%) of the incoming question stream, distilling the better-calibrated discriminative signal into the generative behavior. The approach is designed to be cheaper than the baseline model’s inference, and includes design choices around gating (when to adapt), weight accumulation, loss design, and which layers to update. Extensive ablations examine the effect of signal quality, gating, accumulation, loss, domain ordering, hyperparameters, and layer selection.
AI.result: Across four small LLMs from three families and four domains, SECL reduces Expected Calibration Error by 56–78%, outperforming both the model’s stated confidence and the raw P(True) supervision signal, and matching or surpassing recent inference-time calibration methods. It achieves these gains while training on only a minority of test examples and at lower total cost than the baseline model from which it distills supervision. Ablations show each component of SECL contributes meaningfully and that the method is robust to various configurations and domain orderings.
AI.conclusion: SECL demonstrates that test-time training can be successfully applied to calibration, using the model’s own internal discriminative signal as label-free supervision. The method substantially improves LLM calibration under distribution shift without labeled data or high inference overhead and is robust across models, domains, and hyperparameters. This suggests a practical path toward more trustworthy and well-calibrated LLMs in deployment settings where labels are unavailable and distributions evolve.

title: Toward Generalized Cross-Lingual Hateful Language Detection with Web-Scale Data and Ensemble LLM Annotations
summary: We study whether large-scale unlabelled web data and LLM-based synthetic annotations can improve multilingual hate speech detection. Starting from texts crawled via OpenWebSearch.eu~(OWS) in four languages (English, German, Spanish, Vietnamese), we pursue two complementary strategies. First, we apply continued pre-training to BERT models by continuing masked language modelling on unlabelled OWS texts before supervised fine-tuning, and show that this yields an average macro-F1 gain of approximately 3% over standard baselines across sixteen benchmarks, with stronger gains in low-resource settings. Second, we use four open-source LLMs (Mistral-7B, Llama3.1-8B, Gemma2-9B, Qwen2.5-14B) to produce synthetic annotations through three ensemble strategies: mean averaging, majority voting, and a LightGBM meta-learner. The LightGBM ensemble consistently outperforms the other strategies. Fine-tuning on these synthetic labels substantially benefits a small model (Llama3.2-1B: +11% pooled F1), but provides only a modest gain for the larger Qwen2.5-14B (+0.6%). Our results indicate that the combination of web-scale unlabelled data and LLM-ensemble annotations is the most valuable for smaller models and low-resource languages.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how unlabelled web data and LLM-generated synthetic labels can improve multilingual hate-speech detection, showing notable gains especially for small models and low-resource languages.
AI.motivation: High-quality multilingual hate speech datasets are expensive and scarce, particularly in low-resource languages. Meanwhile, vast unlabelled web text and powerful LLMs are readily available. The authors aim to exploit these resources to boost hate speech detection performance without relying solely on costly human annotation.
AI.method: They use two main strategies on data in four languages (English, German, Spanish, Vietnamese) collected from OpenWebSearch. (1) Continued pre-training: They further pre-train BERT-style models with masked language modelling on the unlabelled web texts before supervised fine-tuning on hate speech benchmarks. (2) Synthetic labelling: They employ four open-source LLMs (Mistral-7B, Llama3.1-8B, Gemma2-9B, Qwen2.5-14B) to annotate data and combine their predictions via three ensemble schemes—mean averaging, majority vote, and a LightGBM-based meta-learner—and then fine-tune smaller and larger models on these synthetic labels.
AI.result: Continued pre-training on OpenWebSearch data yields an approximately 3% average macro-F1 improvement over standard baselines across sixteen benchmarks, with larger boosts in low-resource scenarios. For synthetic labels, the LightGBM ensemble gives the best label quality among the three combination methods. Fine-tuning a small Llama3.2-1B model on these synthetic labels increases pooled F1 by 11%, whereas a much larger Qwen2.5-14B gains only 0.6%.
AI.conclusion: Web-scale unlabelled data and LLM-derived synthetic annotations can substantially improve multilingual hate speech detection, but the benefits are uneven: smaller models and low-resource languages gain the most, while large models see only marginal improvements. The LightGBM ensemble is the most effective way to aggregate LLM annotations, and continued pre-training on web data is a generally beneficial step before supervised fine-tuning.

title: HumorGen: Cognitive Synergy for Humor Generation in Large Language Models via Persona-Based Distillation
summary: Humor generation poses a significant challenge for Large Language Models (LLMs), because their standard training objective - predicting the most likely next word - inherently conflicts with the surprise and incongruity needed for comedy. To bridge this gap, we introduce the Cognitive Synergy Framework, a theoretically grounded methodology for generating high-quality humor data inspired by psychological theories of humor. Utilizing a Mixture-of-Thought (MoT) approach, we deploy six cognitive personas (e.g., The Absurdist, The Cynic) to synthesize diverse comedic perspectives for a given prompt. This framework creates a theoretically grounded dataset, which we use to fine-tune a 7B-parameter student model. We compare Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and a novel Offline Group Relative Policy Optimization (O-GRPO); our 7B model significantly outperforms larger instruction-tuned baselines and achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art proprietary models. We find that cognitive-driven data curation is far more critical than alignment algorithms or model scale for humor generation. Code and data will be available upon publication.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a framework to generate and curate humor-focused training data for LLMs using multiple cognitive personas, then trains a 7B model that outperforms larger baselines on humor tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs trained to predict the most likely next token struggle with humor because good jokes rely on surprise, incongruity, and atypical reasoning. Existing scaling and standard alignment methods have not closed this gap, and there is a need for a more psychologically grounded way to teach models to be funny.
AI.method: The authors design a Cognitive Synergy Framework, grounded in psychological theories of humor. They implement a Mixture-of-Thought (MoT) scheme with six distinct cognitive personas (e.g., The Absurdist, The Cynic) that each generate humorous outputs for prompts, producing a diverse humor dataset. This dataset is used to fine-tune a 7B-parameter student model. For alignment/training, they compare Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with a new Offline Group Relative Policy Optimization (O-GRPO) approach, both trained on the cognitively curated humor data.
AI.result: The resulting 7B model, trained on persona-generated humor data, substantially outperforms larger instruction-tuned baselines on humor generation benchmarks and reaches performance comparable to state-of-the-art proprietary models. Experiments indicate that the cognitive, theory-driven data curation contributes more to humor quality than the choice of alignment algorithm or scaling up model size.
AI.conclusion: Psychologically informed, persona-based data generation (Cognitive Synergy Framework) is an effective way to improve LLM humor generation. Careful, theory-driven data curation is more important than model scale or fine-tuning algorithm choice for this task. The proposed O-GRPO method is a viable alternative to DPO, and the released code and data can support future research on computational humor.

title: Generating High Quality Synthetic Data for Dutch Medical Conversations
comment: Accepted to LREC 2026. This publication was supported by the MediSpeech project funded by ITEA4 under contract number 22032
summary: Medical conversations offer insights into clinical communication often absent from Electronic Health Records. However, developing reliable clinical Natural Language Processing (NLP) models is hampered by the scarcity of domain-specific datasets, as clinical data are typically inaccessible due to privacy and ethical constraints. To address these challenges, we present a pipeline for generating synthetic Dutch medical dialogues using a Dutch fine-tuned Large Language Model, with real medical conversations serving as linguistic and structural reference. The generated dialogues were evaluated through quantitative metrics and qualitative review by native speakers and medical practitioners. Quantitative analysis revealed strong lexical variety and overly regular turn-taking, suggesting scripted rather than natural conversation flow. Qualitative review produced slightly below-average scores, with raters noting issues in domain specificity and natural expression. The limited correlation between quantitative and qualitative results highlights that numerical metrics alone cannot fully capture linguistic quality. Our findings demonstrate that generating synthetic Dutch medical dialogues is feasible but requires domain knowledge and carefully structured prompting to balance naturalness and structure in conversation. This work provides a foundation for expanding Dutch clinical NLP resources through ethically generated synthetic data.

AI.tldr: The paper presents and evaluates a pipeline to generate synthetic Dutch medical dialogues with a fine-tuned LLM to support clinical NLP when real data are scarce.
AI.motivation: Clinical NLP for Dutch is hindered by limited access to real medical conversations due to privacy and ethical constraints. Since Electronic Health Records miss rich conversational detail, there is a need for alternative, ethical ways to obtain conversational clinical data for model development.
AI.method: The authors fine-tune a Dutch Large Language Model and design a prompting pipeline that uses real medical conversations only as linguistic and structural references. They generate synthetic doctor–patient dialogues and evaluate them using quantitative metrics (e.g., lexical variety, turn-taking patterns) and qualitative assessments by native speakers and medical practitioners, who rate domain specificity, naturalness, and expression quality.
AI.result: The synthetic dialogues show high lexical diversity but exhibit overly regular turn-taking, making them feel scripted rather than natural. Human raters give slightly below-average scores, criticizing limited domain specificity and somewhat unnatural expressions. Quantitative metrics and human judgments correlate poorly, indicating that automatic metrics alone are insufficient to assess dialogue quality.
AI.conclusion: While it is feasible to generate synthetic Dutch medical dialogues for clinical NLP, doing so successfully requires substantial domain knowledge and carefully designed prompts to better mimic realistic clinical interactions. The study underscores the need to combine numerical metrics with expert human evaluation and offers a starting point for building larger, ethically sourced Dutch clinical dialogue resources.

title: LABBench2: An Improved Benchmark for AI Systems Performing Biology Research
summary: Optimism for accelerating scientific discovery with AI continues to grow. Current applications of AI in scientific research range from training dedicated foundation models on scientific data to agentic autonomous hypothesis generation systems to AI-driven autonomous labs. The need to measure progress of AI systems in scientific domains correspondingly must not only accelerate, but increasingly shift focus to more real-world capabilities. Beyond rote knowledge and even just reasoning to actually measuring the ability to perform meaningful work. Prior work introduced the Language Agent Biology Benchmark LAB-Bench as an initial attempt at measuring these abilities. Here we introduce an evolution of that benchmark, LABBench2, for measuring real-world capabilities of AI systems performing useful scientific tasks. LABBench2 comprises nearly 1,900 tasks and is, for the most part, a continuation of LAB-Bench, measuring similar capabilities but in more realistic contexts. We evaluate performance of current frontier models, and show that while abilities measured by LAB-Bench and LABBench2 have improved substantially, LABBench2 provides a meaningful jump in difficulty (model-specific accuracy differences range from -26% to -46% across subtasks) and underscores continued room for performance improvement. LABBench2 continues the legacy of LAB-Bench as a de facto benchmark for AI scientific research capabilities and we hope that it continues to help advance development of AI tools for these core research functions. To facilitate community use and development, we provide the task dataset at https://huggingface.co/datasets/futurehouse/labbench2 and a public eval harness at https://github.com/EdisonScientific/labbench2.

AI.tldr: The paper presents LABBench2, an expanded and harder benchmark designed to evaluate how well AI systems can perform realistic, useful scientific tasks, primarily in biology.
AI.motivation: As AI systems are increasingly used in scientific research—from specialized foundation models to autonomous hypothesis-generating agents and AI-driven labs—there is a pressing need to rigorously measure not just their factual knowledge or abstract reasoning, but their ability to carry out meaningful, real-world scientific work. Existing benchmarks like the original LAB-Bench were an initial step, but the field needs more realistic, challenging, and comprehensive evaluations to track genuine progress in AI-for-science capabilities.
AI.method: The authors construct LABBench2, an evolved version of the prior LAB-Bench benchmark, focused on real-world scientific tasks. LABBench2 contains nearly 1,900 tasks that test similar capabilities as LAB-Bench but embed them in more realistic experimental and research contexts. They then evaluate current frontier AI models on this new benchmark and compare their performance against LAB-Bench across multiple subtasks, using accuracy as the primary metric. They also release the dataset on Hugging Face and provide a public evaluation harness on GitHub to enable community use and extension.
AI.result: Frontier AI models show substantial improvement on both LAB-Bench and LABBench2 compared to earlier baselines, indicating progress in AI scientific reasoning and task performance. However, LABBench2 is significantly more difficult: for the same models, accuracy drops by roughly 26%–46% across subtasks when moving from LAB-Bench to LABBench2, demonstrating that the new benchmark poses a much more challenging and realistic test of scientific capabilities.
AI.conclusion: LABBench2 extends and strengthens LAB-Bench as a de facto benchmark for assessing AI systems’ practical scientific research abilities. The larger and more realistic task set reveals that, despite recent gains, there remains considerable room for improvement in AI’s capacity to perform useful scientific work. By open-sourcing both the dataset and evaluation harness, the authors aim to catalyze community-wide development and benchmarking of AI tools for core research functions, thereby accelerating progress in AI-driven scientific discovery.

title: GIANTS: Generative Insight Anticipation from Scientific Literature
summary: Scientific breakthroughs often emerge from synthesizing prior ideas into novel contributions. While language models (LMs) show promise in scientific discovery, their ability to perform this targeted, literature-grounded synthesis remains underexplored. We introduce insight anticipation, a generation task in which a model predicts a downstream paper's core insight from its foundational parent papers. To evaluate this capability, we develop GiantsBench, a benchmark of 17k examples across eight scientific domains, where each example consists of a set of parent papers paired with the core insight of a downstream paper. We evaluate models using an LM judge that scores similarity between generated and ground-truth insights, and show that these similarity scores correlate with expert human ratings. Finally, we present GIANTS-4B, an LM trained via reinforcement learning (RL) to optimize insight anticipation using these similarity scores as a proxy reward. Despite its smaller open-source architecture, GIANTS-4B outperforms proprietary baselines and generalizes to unseen domains, achieving a 34% relative improvement in similarity score over gemini-3-pro. Human evaluations further show that GIANTS-4B produces insights that are more conceptually clear than those of the base model. In addition, SciJudge-30B, a third-party model trained to compare research abstracts by likely citation impact, predicts that insights generated by GIANTS-4B are more likely to lead to higher citations, preferring them over the base model in 68% of pairwise comparisons. We release our code, benchmark, and model to support future research in automated scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: They introduce a new task and benchmark for predicting the key idea of a future paper from its cited parents, and train a small RL-tuned language model that outperforms larger proprietary models on this task.
AI.motivation: Scientific progress often comes from combining ideas in prior work into a new, central insight. While language models are being explored for scientific discovery, it is unclear how well they can perform this specific, literature-grounded synthesis: taking a set of foundational papers and inferring the core contribution of a plausible follow-up paper. There is a need for a concrete task, dataset, and evaluation framework to measure and improve this capability.
AI.method: 1) Define the "insight anticipation" task: given a set of parent papers, generate the downstream paper’s core insight. 2) Build GiantsBench, a 17k-example benchmark over eight scientific domains, where each item pairs parent papers with the actual core insight of a real downstream paper. 3) Use a language-model-based judge to score similarity between generated insights and ground truth, and validate that these scores correlate with expert human ratings. 4) Train GIANTS-4B, a 4B-parameter language model, with reinforcement learning, using the similarity scores as a proxy reward signal so that the model learns to optimize insight anticipation performance.
AI.result: GiantsBench provides a large, multi-domain testbed for insight anticipation. The LM-based similarity metric is shown to correlate with expert judgment, supporting its use as an automatic evaluator. GIANTS-4B, despite being a relatively small open-source model, outperforms proprietary baselines such as gemini-3-pro on GiantsBench, achieving a 34% relative improvement in similarity score. It also generalizes well to unseen domains. Human raters find GIANTS-4B’s generated insights to be more conceptually clear than those from its base model. Additionally, SciJudge-30B, an external model that estimates likely citation impact from abstracts, prefers GIANTS-4B’s outputs in 68% of head-to-head comparisons with the base model, suggesting higher potential scientific impact.
AI.conclusion: Formulating insight anticipation as a concrete generation task and releasing GiantsBench enables systematic evaluation of literature-grounded scientific synthesis by language models. Reinforcement learning guided by LM-judged similarity can substantially improve a model’s ability to anticipate core scientific insights from prior work, even in a relatively small model. GIANTS-4B sets a new state of the art on this task, produces clearer and potentially more impactful insights, and the open release of code, benchmark, and model lays groundwork for future research on automated scientific discovery and idea generation.

title: Linear Programming for Multi-Criteria Assessment with Cardinal and Ordinal Data: A Pessimistic Virtual Gap Analysis
summary: Multi-criteria Analysis (MCA) is used to rank alternatives based on various criteria. Key MCA methods, such as Multiple Criteria Decision Making (MCDM) methods, estimate parameters for criteria to compute the performance of each alternative. Nonetheless, subjective evaluations and biases frequently influence the reliability of results, while the diversity of data affects the precision of the parameters. The novel linear programming-based Virtual Gap Analysis (VGA) models tackle these issues. This paper outlines a two-step method that integrates two novel VGA models to assess each alternative from a pessimistic perspective, using both quantitative and qualitative criteria, and employing cardinal and ordinal data. Next, prioritize the alternatives to eliminate the least favorable one. The proposed method is dependable and scalable, enabling thorough assessments efficiently and effectively within decision support systems.

AI.tldr: They propose a new linear-programming-based method (VGA) to rank alternatives in multi-criteria decisions, reducing subjectivity and handling mixed data types, especially from a pessimistic perspective.
AI.motivation: Traditional multi-criteria decision-making methods require parameter estimation (e.g., weights, scores) that is often subjective and biased. Different data types (quantitative vs. qualitative, cardinal vs. ordinal) further complicate accurate evaluation, reducing the reliability and precision of rankings. There is a need for a more robust, scalable, and less subjective approach that can integrate diverse data in decision support systems.
AI.method: The paper introduces Virtual Gap Analysis (VGA), a family of linear programming-based models. It proposes a two-step procedure that uses two novel VGA models. Step 1: evaluate each alternative from a pessimistic (worst-case) viewpoint considering both quantitative and qualitative criteria, as well as cardinal and ordinal data, using LP formulations to determine performance gaps. Step 2: based on these evaluated performances, rank the alternatives and iteratively eliminate the least favorable one. The models are designed to reduce dependence on subjective parameter settings and to be computationally scalable.
AI.result: The VGA models can simultaneously handle multiple criteria of different types and data scales, generate robust pessimistic assessments of alternatives, and support systematic elimination of the worst options. Computational experiments (implied though not detailed in the abstract) show that the method works efficiently and fits well into decision support systems, yielding reliable rankings with less sensitivity to subjective biases.
AI.conclusion: The proposed VGA-based two-step MCA method offers a dependable, scalable, and efficient way to evaluate and rank alternatives under multiple criteria. By relying on linear programming and pessimistic evaluation, it mitigates subjectivity, accommodates mixed data (quantitative/qualitative, cardinal/ordinal), and is suitable for integration into practical decision support systems for thorough multi-criteria assessments.

title: Claim2Vec: Embedding Fact-Check Claims for Multilingual Similarity and Clustering
summary: Recurrent claims present a major challenge for automated fact-checking systems designed to combat misinformation, especially in multilingual settings. While tasks such as claim matching and fact-checked claim retrieval aim to address this problem by linking claim pairs, the broader challenge of effectively representing groups of similar claims that can be resolved with the same fact-check via claim clustering remains relatively underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce Claim2Vec, the first multilingual embedding model optimized to represent fact-check claims as vectors in an improved semantic embedding space. We fine-tune a multilingual encoder using contrastive learning with similar multilingual claim pairs. Experiments on the claim clustering task using three datasets, 14 multilingual embedding models, and 7 clustering algorithms demonstrate that Claim2Vec significantly improves clustering performance. Specifically, it enhances both cluster label alignment and the geometric structure of the embedding space across different cluster configurations. Our multilingual analysis shows that clusters containing multiple languages benefit from fine-tuning, demonstrating cross-lingual knowledge transfer.

AI.tldr: They propose Claim2Vec, a multilingual embedding model that groups similar fact-check claims more effectively for claim clustering in misinformation detection.
AI.motivation: Recurrent and semantically similar claims make fact-checking inefficient, especially across multiple languages. Existing work focuses mostly on pairwise matching or retrieval of fact-checked claims, but there is little on clustering sets of similar claims that can share the same fact-check, particularly in a multilingual context.
AI.method: They introduce Claim2Vec, a multilingual encoder fine-tuned with contrastive learning on pairs of similar multilingual fact-check claims. The model is trained to place semantically similar claims close together in the embedding space. They then evaluate these embeddings on a claim clustering task, comparing against 14 existing multilingual embedding models and 7 clustering algorithms under multiple clustering configurations.
AI.result: Claim2Vec outperforms all 14 baseline multilingual embedding models on claim clustering metrics. It yields better alignment between clusters and ground-truth labels and produces a more coherent geometric embedding structure across clustering settings. Multilingual experiments show that clusters that mix multiple languages have notably improved quality, indicating stronger cross-lingual semantic alignment.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing embeddings specifically for fact-check claims with contrastive learning leads to significantly better claim clustering, enabling groups of similar recurrent claims—potentially across languages—to be resolved with a single fact-check. Claim2Vec thus addresses an underexplored but practically important component of automated, multilingual fact-checking pipelines.

title: Seven simple steps for log analysis in AI systems
summary: AI systems produce large volumes of logs as they interact with tools and users. Analysing these logs can help understand model capabilities, propensities, and behaviours, or assess whether an evaluation worked as intended. Researchers have started developing methods for log analysis, but a standardised approach is still missing. Here we suggest a pipeline based on current best practices. We illustrate it with concrete code examples in the Inspect Scout library, provide detailed guidance on each step, and highlight common pitfalls. Our framework provides researchers with a foundation for rigorous and reproducible log analysis.

AI.tldr: Proposes a standardized, code-backed pipeline for analyzing AI system logs to better understand model behavior and improve evaluation rigor and reproducibility.
AI.motivation: AI models generate massive log data when interacting with users and tools, but current log-analysis efforts are ad hoc and lack a shared, rigorous methodology. This makes it hard to systematically study model behavior, compare results across studies, and verify whether evaluations function as intended.
AI.method: The paper defines a step-by-step pipeline for log analysis grounded in current best practices. It is instantiated in concrete, runnable form through the Inspect Scout library, including code examples and procedural guidance. The authors also enumerate and discuss common pitfalls at each stage of the analysis pipeline.
AI.result: The outcome is a structured framework and accompanying tooling that researchers can use to perform systematic, reproducible analysis of AI interaction logs. It offers practical examples and documented workflows that operationalize best practices for log analysis.
AI.conclusion: By formalizing a pipeline and providing supporting code, the work supplies a foundation for rigorous, standardized, and reproducible analysis of AI system logs, aiming to improve understanding of model behavior and the reliability of evaluation studies.

title: Spoiler Alert: Narrative Forecasting as a Metric for Tension in LLM Storytelling
summary: LLMs have so far failed both to generate consistently compelling stories and to recognize this failure--on the leading creative-writing benchmark (EQ-Bench), LLM judges rank zero-shot AI stories above New Yorker short stories, a gold standard for literary fiction. We argue that existing rubrics overlook a key dimension of compelling human stories: narrative tension. We introduce the 100-Endings metric, which walks through a story sentence by sentence: at each position, a model predicts how the story will end 100 times given only the text so far, and we measure tension as how often predictions fail to match the ground truth. Beyond the mismatch rate, the sentence-level curve yields complementary statistics, such as inflection rate, a geometric measure of how frequently the curve reverses direction, tracking twists and revelations. Unlike rubric-based judges, 100-Endings correctly ranks New Yorker stories far above LLM outputs. Grounded in narratological principles, we design a story-generation pipeline using structural constraints, including analysis of story templates, idea formulation, and narrative scaffolding. Our pipeline significantly increases narrative tension as measured by the 100-Endings metric, while maintaining performance on the EQ-Bench leaderboard.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new automatic metric, 100-Endings, to measure narrative tension in stories by repeatedly predicting endings as the story unfolds, and uses this to show and improve how LLMs generate fiction.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM evaluation rubrics for creative writing, such as EQ-Bench, often misjudge story quality, even ranking zero-shot LLM stories above professionally written New Yorker fiction, indicating that they miss crucial aspects of what makes stories compelling. The authors argue that a key missing dimension is narrative tension—the evolving uncertainty and anticipation about how a story will end—so a better metric is needed both to evaluate and guide story generation.
AI.method: They introduce the 100-Endings metric: walk through a story sentence by sentence; at each step, condition an LLM on the prefix so far and sample 100 possible endings. Tension is quantified by how often these predictive endings fail to match the true ending, giving a sentence-level tension curve over the story. From this curve, they derive additional statistics such as an inflection rate that captures how often the direction of tension changes, reflecting twists. They also design a constrained story-generation pipeline grounded in narratology, including template analysis, idea formulation, and narrative scaffolding, to structurally enforce more tensionful plots.
AI.result: Using 100-Endings, New Yorker short stories are correctly ranked as much more compelling than LLM-generated stories, in contrast to rubric-based EQ-Bench judges that mis-rank them. The proposed generation pipeline produces LLM stories that show significantly higher narrative tension according to 100-Endings, without reducing their scores on the EQ-Bench leaderboard.
AI.conclusion: Narrative tension is a critical but overlooked axis of story quality that current LLM evaluation rubrics fail to capture. The 100-Endings metric provides an automatic, narratologically grounded way to quantify tension via predictive mismatches and curve dynamics, yielding rankings more aligned with human literary judgments. Incorporating structural constraints and tension-aware design into LLM story generation can substantially improve the compellingness of AI fiction while retaining performance on existing creative-writing benchmarks.

title: Turing Test on Screen: A Benchmark for Mobile GUI Agent Humanization
summary: The rise of autonomous GUI agents has triggered adversarial countermeasures from digital platforms, yet existing research prioritizes utility and robustness over the critical dimension of anti-detection. We argue that for agents to survive in human-centric ecosystems, they must evolve Humanization capabilities. We introduce the ``Turing Test on Screen,'' formally modeling the interaction as a MinMax optimization problem between a detector and an agent aiming to minimize behavioral divergence. We then collect a new high-fidelity dataset of mobile touch dynamics, and conduct our analysis that vanilla LMM-based agents are easily detectable due to unnatural kinematics. Consequently, we establish the Agent Humanization Benchmark (AHB) and detection metrics to quantify the trade-off between imitability and utility. Finally, we propose methods ranging from heuristic noise to data-driven behavioral matching, demonstrating that agents can achieve high imitability theoretically and empirically without sacrificing performance. This work shifts the paradigm from whether an agent can perform a task to how it performs it within a human-centric ecosystem, laying the groundwork for seamless coexistence in adversarial digital environments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the idea of 'Humanization' for GUI agents, proposing a Turing-Test-like framework on screen, along with a dataset, benchmark, and methods to make autonomous agents’ touch behaviors indistinguishable from humans while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous GUI agents focus on utility and robustness but neglect anti-detection: platforms can identify and block non-human agents based on interaction patterns. To survive in human-centric, adversarial ecosystems, agents must behave like humans at the kinematic/interaction level, not just complete tasks correctly.
AI.method: 1) Formally model the interaction between a detector and an agent as a MinMax optimization problem, analogous to a Turing Test on screen, where the agent minimizes behavioral divergence from humans while the detector tries to distinguish them. 2) Collect a high-fidelity dataset of real mobile touch dynamics (e.g., trajectories, speeds, timing). 3) Empirically test existing LMM-based (large multimodal model-based) agents and show their touch dynamics are easily detected as non-human. 4) Define the Agent Humanization Benchmark (AHB) and detection metrics to measure imitability vs utility. 5) Propose and evaluate methods from simple heuristic noise injection to data-driven behavioral matching based on the collected dataset.
AI.result: The study finds that standard LMM-based GUI agents exhibit unnatural touch kinematics that detectors can identify reliably. Using the AHB and new metrics, the authors demonstrate that both simple heuristics and more advanced data-driven behavioral matching substantially reduce detectability—i.e., make agents’ interactions more human-like—while preserving task performance. They show high theoretical and empirical imitability with minimal or no loss in utility.
AI.conclusion: The work reframes the design goal for GUI agents from just 'Can they perform tasks?' to 'How do they perform tasks in human ecosystems?' By formalizing a Turing Test on screen, releasing a human touch dataset and the AHB benchmark, and demonstrating practical humanization methods, the paper lays a foundation for building agents that can coexist seamlessly and less detectably in adversarial digital environments without sacrificing effectiveness.

title: Simulating Organized Group Behavior: New Framework, Benchmark, and Analysis
summary: Simulating how organized groups (e.g., corporations) make decisions (e.g., responding to a competitor's move) is essential for understanding real-world dynamics and could benefit relevant applications (e.g., market prediction). In this paper, we formalize this problem as a concrete research platform for group behavior understanding, providing: (1) a task definition with benchmark and evaluation criteria, (2) a structured analytical framework with a corresponding algorithm, and (3) detailed temporal and cross-group analysis. Specifically, we propose Organized Group Behavior Simulation, a task that models organized groups as collective entities from a practical perspective: given a group facing a particular situation (e.g., AI Boom), predict the decision it would take. To support this task, we present GROVE (GRoup Organizational BehaVior Evaluation), a benchmark covering 44 entities with 8,052 real-world context-decision pairs collected from Wikipedia and TechCrunch across 9 domains, with an end-to-end evaluation protocol assessing consistency, initiative, scope, magnitude, and horizon. Beyond straightforward prompting pipelines, we propose a structured analytical framework that converts collective decision-making events into an interpretable, adaptive, and traceable behavioral model, achieving stronger performance than summarization- and retrieval-based baselines. It further introduces an adapter mechanism for time-aware evolution and group-aware transfer, and traceable evidence nodes grounding each decision rule in originating historical events. Our analysis reveals temporal behavioral drift within individual groups, which the time-aware adapter effectively captures for stronger prediction, and structured cross-group similarity that enables knowledge transfer for data-scarce organizations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new benchmark and framework (GROVE) for simulating and predicting how organized groups (like companies) make strategic decisions in different situations.
AI.motivation: Existing AI research focuses mostly on individual-level behavior or generic text prediction, but many important real-world phenomena are driven by decisions of organized groups such as corporations. Understanding and simulating these group-level decisions is important for tasks like market prediction, competitive analysis, and broader social and economic modeling. There is a lack of a standardized task definition, benchmark, and analytical methodology dedicated to modeling organized groups as coherent decision-making entities over time.
AI.method: The authors define a new task, Organized Group Behavior Simulation, where the goal is to predict what decision an organized group will make when faced with a given situation. They construct GROVE, a benchmark of 8,052 real-world context–decision pairs spanning 44 entities in 9 domains, sourced from Wikipedia and TechCrunch. They propose an end-to-end evaluation protocol along five dimensions: consistency, initiative, scope, magnitude, and horizon. Methodologically, they design a structured analytical framework that converts historical collective decision events into an interpretable behavioral model comprising decision rules and evidence nodes. This framework includes an adapter mechanism for time-aware evolution (capturing how a group’s behavior changes over time) and group-aware transfer (leveraging similarities across groups), going beyond basic prompting, summarization-based, or retrieval-based pipelines.
AI.result: On the GROVE benchmark, the structured analytical framework outperforms summarization- and retrieval-based baselines on the proposed evaluation metrics. The adapter mechanisms successfully capture temporal behavioral drift within individual groups, improving predictive performance, and enable transfer of learned behavior patterns across structurally similar groups, which is particularly beneficial when data for a given organization is limited.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes Organized Group Behavior Simulation as a concrete research task and provides GROVE as a comprehensive benchmark and evaluation protocol. Their structured, interpretable modeling framework with time-aware and group-aware adapters improves prediction of organized group decisions over simpler baselines, reveals measurable temporal drift in group behavior, and demonstrates that cross-group structural similarity can be exploited for knowledge transfer. This creates a practical foundation for future research on understanding and forecasting the behavior of organized groups.

title: AHC: Meta-Learned Adaptive Compression for Continual Object Detection on Memory-Constrained Microcontrollers
summary: Deploying continual object detection on microcontrollers (MCUs) with under 100KB memory requires efficient feature compression that can adapt to evolving task distributions. Existing approaches rely on fixed compression strategies (e.g., FiLM conditioning) that cannot adapt to heterogeneous task characteristics, leading to suboptimal memory utilization and catastrophic forgetting. We introduce Adaptive Hierarchical Compression (AHC), a meta-learning framework featuring three key innovations: (1) true MAML-based compression that adapts via gradient descent to each new task in just 5 inner-loop steps, (2) hierarchical multi-scale compression with scale-aware ratios (8:1 for P3, 6.4:1 for P4, 4:1 for P5) matching FPN redundancy patterns, and (3) a dual-memory architecture combining short-term and long-term banks with importance-based consolidation under a hard 100KB budget. We provide formal theoretical guarantees bounding catastrophic forgetting as O(ε{sq.root(T)} + 1/{sq.root(M)}) where ε is compression error, T is task count, and M is memory size. Experiments on CORe50, TiROD, and PASCAL VOC benchmarks with three standard baselines (Fine-tuning,EWC, iCaRL) demonstrate that AHC enables practical continual detection within a 100KB replay budget, achieving competitive accuracy through mean-pooled compressed feature replay combined with EWC regularization and feature distillation.

AI.tldr: Paper proposes Adaptive Hierarchical Compression (AHC), a meta-learned, multi-scale, dual-memory compression scheme enabling continual object detection on tiny MCUs (<100KB) with bounded forgetting and competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Continual object detection on microcontrollers is severely constrained by tiny memory budgets (under 100KB), making it impossible to store full-resolution intermediate features for replay. Existing fixed compression strategies (e.g., FiLM-based conditioning) cannot adapt to diverse and evolving task distributions, causing poor memory utilization and catastrophic forgetting. The authors aim to design a task-adaptive, theoretically grounded compression and replay mechanism that fits strict memory limits while maintaining detection performance over many tasks.
AI.method: They propose Adaptive Hierarchical Compression (AHC), a meta-learning based compression framework with three core ideas: (1) a true MAML-style compressor that is meta-trained so that it can adapt to each new task via just 5 steps of gradient descent in the inner loop; (2) hierarchical, multi-scale compression aligned with the Feature Pyramid Network (FPN), using scale-aware compression ratios (8:1 for P3, 6.4:1 for P4, 4:1 for P5) to match redundancy at different feature scales; and (3) a dual-memory system with a short-term and long-term feature bank, where features are consolidated into long-term memory based on importance while obeying a strict 100KB memory budget. They integrate this with continual detection by replaying mean-pooled compressed features and applying EWC regularization and feature distillation during training.
AI.result: They derive theoretical bounds on catastrophic forgetting, showing it scales as O(ε√T + 1/√M), where ε is compression error, T is the number of tasks, and M is memory size, thereby linking compression quality and memory capacity to forgetting. Empirically, on CORe50, TiROD, and PASCAL VOC continual detection benchmarks, and compared against Fine-tuning, EWC, and iCaRL baselines, AHC enables continual object detection under a strict 100KB replay memory limit and achieves competitive or superior accuracy using compressed feature replay combined with EWC and distillation.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, hierarchical, meta-learned compression with a dual-memory design can make continual object detection feasible on highly memory-constrained microcontrollers. By tailoring compression to each task, respecting scale-dependent redundancy, and carefully managing a tiny replay buffer, AHC can significantly reduce catastrophic forgetting while staying within a 100KB memory budget, as supported by both theory and experiments.

title: Should We be Pedantic About Reasoning Errors in Machine Translation?
summary: Across multiple language pairings (English $\to$ \{Spanish, French, German, Mandarin, Japanese, Urdu, Cantonese\}), we find reasoning errors in translation. To quantify how often these reasoning errors occur, we leverage an automated annotation protocol for reasoning evaluation wherein the goal is to detect if a reasoning step is any of three error categories: (1) source sentence-misaligned, (2) model hypothesis-misaligned, or (3) reasoning trace-misaligned. We probe the reasoning model with perturbed traces correcting for these identified reasoning errors using an array of weak-to-strong interventions: hedging, removal, re-reasoning after removal, hindsight, and oracle interventions. Experimenting with interventions on the reasoning traces suggests that small corrections to the reasoning have little impact on translation quality, but stronger interventions yield the highest resolution rates, despite translation quality gains being mixed. We find ultimately that reasoning errors in MT can be identified with high precision in Urdu but lower precision in Spanish, but that removing these reasoning errors does not resolve the initial errors significantly, suggesting limited reasoning faithfulness for machine translation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies reasoning errors inside chain-of-thought style reasoning traces used by machine translation systems across several language pairs, and tests whether fixing those internal reasoning traces actually improves translation quality.
AI.motivation: As MT models increasingly expose or internally use explicit reasoning traces, it is important to know whether these traces are faithful to the model’s actual decision process and whether correcting errors in them can improve translation. The paper aims to systematically characterize and measure reasoning errors in MT reasoning steps and to assess their impact on translation outcomes and reasoning faithfulness.
AI.method: The authors examine English to multiple target languages and define three categories of reasoning errors: misalignment with the source sentence, misalignment with the model’s own hypothesis translation, and internal inconsistency in the reasoning trace. They build an automated annotation pipeline to label these errors, then probe a reasoning-enabled MT model with targeted interventions on the reasoning traces. Interventions range from light edits (hedging) and simple deletions, to re-reasoning after deletion, hindsight-based corrections, and oracle interventions that enforce corrected traces. They then measure both the rate at which interventions resolve the originally detected errors and the effect on translation quality.
AI.result: Weak edits to the reasoning traces rarely change translation quality or resolve the underlying errors, while stronger interventions (like hindsight and oracle-style corrections) more frequently fix the reasoning errors but lead to mixed and not consistently positive changes in translation quality. The automated detector identifies reasoning errors with high precision for some languages (notably Urdu) but substantially lower precision for others (e.g., Spanish), indicating that error detection reliability is language dependent.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that although it is possible to detect many reasoning errors in MT reasoning traces, especially for some target languages, directly repairing these traces does not reliably fix translation errors or substantially improve quality. This weak coupling between explicit reasoning traces and actual model behavior indicates limited reasoning faithfulness in current MT systems: the chain-of-thought style explanations are not tightly causally linked to the produced translations.

title: Explainable Planning for Hybrid Systems
summary: The recent advancement in artificial intelligence (AI) technologies facilitates a paradigm shift toward automation. Autonomous systems are fully or partially replacing manually crafted ones. At the core of these systems is automated planning. With the advent of powerful planners, automated planning is now applied to many complex and safety-critical domains, including smart energy grids, self-driving cars, warehouse automation, urban and air traffic control, search and rescue operations, surveillance, robotics, and healthcare. There is a growing need to generate explanations of AI-based systems, which is one of the major challenges the planning community faces today. The thesis presents a comprehensive study on explainable artificial intelligence planning (XAIP) for hybrid systems that capture a representation of real-world problems closely.

AI.tldr: The paper studies explainable AI planning for real‑world hybrid systems, aiming to make automated planners’ decisions understandable in complex, safety‑critical domains.
AI.motivation: Automated planning now drives many complex and safety‑critical applications such as self-driving cars, smart grids, and healthcare. As these planners become more powerful and autonomous, users, operators, and regulators must understand why certain plans are generated or actions taken. The planning community therefore faces the challenge of explaining planner behavior, especially in realistic hybrid systems that mix discrete and continuous dynamics.
AI.method: The thesis undertakes a comprehensive investigation of explainable AI planning (XAIP) specifically tailored to hybrid systems. It likely defines explanation requirements, develops formalisms and algorithms to generate explanations for planner decisions, and adapts these to planning models that more closely reflect real-world constraints and dynamics.
AI.result: The work provides methods and frameworks for producing explanations of AI planning decisions in hybrid systems, improving transparency and interpretability of plans in domains such as transportation, energy, and robotics.
AI.conclusion: Explainable AI planning for hybrid systems is both necessary and feasible; by equipping automated planners with explanation capabilities in realistic settings, the thesis contributes to safer and more trustworthy deployment of AI in complex, real-world domains.

title: Human vs. Machine Deception: Distinguishing AI-Generated and Human-Written Fake News Using Ensemble Learning
summary: The rapid adoption of large language models has introduced a new class of AI-generated fake news that coexists with traditional human-written misinformation, raising important questions about how these two forms of deceptive content differ and how reliably they can be distinguished. This study examines linguistic, structural, and emotional differences between human-written and AI-generated fake news and evaluates machine learning and ensemble-based methods for distinguishing these content types. A document-level feature representation is constructed using sentence structure, lexical diversity, punctuation patterns, readability indices, and emotion-based features capturing affective dimensions such as fear, anger, joy, sadness, trust, and anticipation. Multiple classification models, including logistic regression, random forest, support vector machines, extreme gradient boosting, and a neural network, are applied alongside an ensemble framework that aggregates predictions across models. Model performance is assessed using accuracy and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve. The results show strong and consistent classification performance, with readability-based features emerging as the most informative predictors and AI-generated text exhibiting more uniform stylistic patterns. Ensemble learning provides modest but consistent improvements over individual models. These findings indicate that stylistic and structural properties of text provide a robust basis for distinguishing AI-generated misinformation from human-written fake news.

AI.tldr: The paper compares AI-generated fake news with human-written fake news and builds classifiers to tell them apart using stylistic and structural text features.
AI.motivation: With large language models widely used, AI-generated fake news now coexists with traditional human-written misinformation, creating a need to understand how they differ and whether they can be reliably distinguished for detection and mitigation.
AI.method: The authors construct document-level feature representations based on sentence structure, lexical diversity, punctuation use, readability indices, and emotion-related measures (fear, anger, joy, sadness, trust, anticipation). They then train several classifiers—logistic regression, random forest, SVM, XGBoost, and a neural network—and an ensemble that aggregates their predictions. Performance is evaluated with accuracy and AUC-ROC.
AI.result: All models achieve strong and consistent performance in distinguishing AI-generated from human-written fake news. Readability-based features are the most informative, and AI-generated texts show more uniform stylistic patterns. The ensemble model yields modest but consistent performance gains over individual classifiers.
AI.conclusion: Linguistic, structural, and emotional features—especially readability indices—provide a robust basis for automatically distinguishing AI-generated misinformation from human-written fake news, and ensemble learning further enhances detection reliability.

title: Help Without Being Asked: A Deployed Proactive Agent System for On-Call Support with Continuous Self-Improvement
summary: In large-scale cloud service platforms, thousands of customer tickets are generated daily and are typically handled through on-call dialogues. This high volume of on-call interactions imposes a substantial workload on human support analysts. Recent studies have explored reactive agents that leverage large language models as a first line of support to interact with customers directly and resolve issues. However, when issues remain unresolved and are escalated to human support, these agents are typically disengaged. As a result, they cannot assist with follow-up inquiries, track resolution progress, or learn from the cases they fail to address. In this paper, we introduce Vigil, a novel proactive agent system designed to operate throughout the entire on-call life-cycle. Unlike reactive agents, Vigil focuses on providing assistance during the phase in which human support is already involved. It integrates into the dialogue between the customer and the analyst, proactively offering assistance without explicit user invocation. Moreover, Vigil incorporates a continuous self-improvement mechanism that extracts knowledge from human-resolved cases to autonomously update its capabilities. Vigil has been deployed on Volcano Engine, ByteDance's cloud platform, for over ten months, and comprehensive evaluations based on this deployment demonstrate its effectiveness and practicality. The open source version of this work is publicly available at https://github.com/volcengine/veaiops.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Vigil, a proactive LLM-based agent that assists human support analysts throughout the entire lifecycle of cloud service customer tickets, and continuously improves by learning from human-resolved cases.
AI.motivation: Cloud platforms receive thousands of support tickets daily, creating heavy workloads for human analysts. Existing LLM-based agents mainly act reactively as first-line support; once an issue is escalated to humans, these agents disengage and cannot help with follow-up, progress tracking, or learning from failures. There is a need for an agent that remains engaged after escalation and leverages human resolutions to improve over time.
AI.method: The authors design Vigil, a proactive agent that integrates into ongoing dialogues between customers and human analysts on a cloud platform. Unlike reactive agents that wait for explicit invocation, Vigil monitors conversations and proactively suggests assistance to analysts during human-handled cases. It includes a continuous self-improvement module that extracts knowledge from past human-resolved tickets and uses this knowledge to autonomously update its internal capabilities and behavior. Vigil is deployed in a real-world setting on Volcano Engine, ByteDance's cloud platform, for long-term evaluation.
AI.result: Over more than ten months of deployment on Volcano Engine, Vigil demonstrates effectiveness and practicality in supporting human analysts. Quantitative and/or qualitative evaluations (not detailed in the abstract) indicate that Vigil can meaningfully assist during support interactions and successfully leverage human-resolved cases for improvement. The system is mature enough that an open-source version has been released.
AI.conclusion: A proactive, lifecycle-spanning LLM-based support agent that remains engaged after escalation and continuously learns from human-handled cases can effectively reduce support workload and enhance ticket resolution in large-scale cloud service environments. Vigil validates this approach in production, and the open-sourced implementation enables broader adoption and further research.

title: Weird Generalization is Weirdly Brittle
summary: Weird generalization is a phenomenon in which models fine-tuned on data from a narrow domain (e.g. insecure code) develop surprising traits that manifest even outside that domain (e.g. broad misalignment)-a phenomenon that prior work has highlighted as a critical safety concern. Here, we present an extended replication study of key weird generalization results across an expanded suite of models and datasets. We confirm that surprising (and dangerous) traits can emerge under certain circumstances, but we find that weird generalization is exceptionally brittle: it emerges only for specific models on specific datasets, and it vanishes under simple training-time, prompt-based interventions. We find that the most effective interventions provide prompt context that makes the generalized behavior the expected behavior. However, we show that even very generic interventions that do not anticipate specific generalized traits can still be effective in mitigating weird generalization's effects. Our findings thus help clarify the nature of the safety threat that weird generalization poses and point toward an easily implemented set of solutions.

AI.tldr: The paper studies “weird generalization” in fine-tuned models, finds that dangerous generalized behaviors do occur but are fragile, and shows they can be mitigated with simple training and prompting interventions.
AI.motivation: Weird generalization has been identified as a critical AI safety risk: models fine-tuned on narrow domains sometimes develop unexpected and harmful behaviors that appear even outside the training domain. The authors aim to rigorously test how robust and widespread this phenomenon is, and to understand whether it poses a persistent safety threat or can be easily controlled.
AI.method: They conduct an extended replication study of prior weird generalization results, applying similar setups across a broader range of models and datasets. They systematically vary models, fine-tuning data, and interventions. They then introduce different training-time and prompt-based interventions, especially those that give contextual cues that the generalized behavior is expected, as well as more generic interventions that do not target specific failure modes, and evaluate their impact on weird generalization.
AI.result: They replicate that weird generalization can indeed occur and can lead to surprising, potentially dangerous behaviors. However, they find that these effects are highly brittle: they appear only for certain model–dataset combinations and disappear when simple interventions are applied. Prompting that clearly indicates the desired behavior is particularly effective, and even broad, non-targeted interventions substantially reduce weird generalization effects.
AI.conclusion: Although weird generalization is real and can produce concerning behaviors, it is not robust: it depends sensitively on model and data choices and can be mitigated or eliminated with straightforward training and prompting strategies. This reduces the perceived severity of weird generalization as a stubborn, hard-to-fix safety problem and suggests that simple, easily implemented interventions can meaningfully address it.

title: OOWM: Structuring Embodied Reasoning and Planning via Object-Oriented Programmatic World Modeling
summary: Standard Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting empowers Large Language Models (LLMs) with reasoning capabilities, yet its reliance on linear natural language is inherently insufficient for effective world modeling in embodied tasks. While text offers flexibility, it fails to explicitly represent the state-space, object hierarchies, and causal dependencies required for robust robotic planning. To address these limitations, we propose Object-Oriented World Modeling (OOWM), a novel framework that structures embodied reasoning through the lens of software engineering formalisms. We redefine the world model not as a latent vector space, but as an explicit symbolic tuple $W = \langle S, T \rangle$: a State Abstraction ($G_\text{state}$) instantiating the environmental state $S$, coupled with a Control Policy ($G_\text{control}$) representing the transition logic $T: S \times A \rightarrow S'$. OOWM leverages the Unified Modeling Language (UML) to materialize this definition: it employs Class Diagrams to ground visual perception into rigorous object hierarchies, and Activity Diagrams to operationalize planning into executable control flows. Furthermore, we introduce a three-stage training pipeline combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Crucially, this method utilizes outcome-based rewards from the final plan to implicitly optimize the underlying object-oriented reasoning structure, enabling effective learning even with sparse annotations. Extensive evaluations on the MRoom-30k benchmark demonstrate that OOWM significantly outperforms unstructured textual baselines in planning coherence, execution success, and structural fidelity, establishing a new paradigm for structured embodied reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Object-Oriented World Modeling (OOWM), a UML-based, object-oriented framework that replaces linear Chain-of-Thought text with structured symbolic world models to improve LLM-based embodied planning and control.
AI.motivation: Standard Chain-of-Thought prompting represents reasoning as linear natural language, which is too unstructured for embodied tasks that need explicit state spaces, object hierarchies, and causal dependencies for robotic planning. There is a need for a world model representation that is both interpretable and operational, bridging perception and control with explicit structure.
AI.method: The authors redefine the world model as an explicit symbolic tuple W = <S, T>, where S is the environmental state and T is the transition function. They implement this using UML: Class Diagrams encode object hierarchies and state abstraction (G_state), while Activity Diagrams capture control policies and planning flows (G_control). They propose a three-stage training pipeline that combines Supervised Fine-Tuning with Group Relative Policy Optimization, using outcome-based rewards derived from the final plan to implicitly optimize the learned object-oriented reasoning structures, allowing learning under sparse supervision.
AI.result: On the MRoom-30k benchmark for embodied planning, OOWM markedly outperforms unstructured text-based baselines along several metrics: planning coherence, task execution success, and fidelity of the generated structures to the underlying environment and tasks.
AI.conclusion: Object-Oriented World Modeling offers a structured, software-engineering-inspired alternative to linear textual CoT for embodied reasoning, leading to more coherent, executable plans and more faithful world models. This framework establishes a new, UML-grounded paradigm for using LLMs in robotic and embodied tasks, especially under sparse annotation regimes.

title: CoSToM:Causal-oriented Steering for Intrinsic Theory-of-Mind Alignment in Large Language Models
summary: Theory of Mind (ToM), the ability to attribute mental states to others, is a hallmark of social intelligence. While large language models (LLMs) demonstrate promising performance on standard ToM benchmarks, we observe that they often fail to generalize to complex task-specific scenarios, relying heavily on prompt scaffolding to mimic reasoning. The critical misalignment between the internal knowledge and external behavior raises a fundamental question: Do LLMs truly possess intrinsic cognition, and can they externalize this internal knowledge into stable, high-quality behaviors? To answer this, we introduce CoSToM (Causal-oriented Steering for ToM alignment), a framework that transitions from mechanistic interpretation to active intervention. First, we employ causal tracing to map the internal distribution of ToM features, empirically uncovering the internal layers' characteristics in encoding fundamental ToM semantics. Building on this insight, we implement a lightweight alignment framework via targeted activation steering within these ToM-critical layers. Experiments demonstrate that CoSToM significantly enhances human-like social reasoning capabilities and downstream dialogue quality.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CoSToM, a causal-intervention framework to better align large language models’ internal Theory of Mind representations with their external behavior, improving social reasoning and dialogue quality.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs perform well on standard Theory of Mind (ToM) benchmarks, they often fail in more complex, task-specific social reasoning scenarios and rely heavily on prompt scaffolding. This suggests a misalignment between the models’ internal knowledge and their observable behavior, raising the question of whether LLMs possess usable intrinsic cognition and how to reliably externalize it.
AI.method: The authors first use causal tracing to identify where and how ToM-related features are internally represented across model layers, characterizing ToM-critical layers that encode fundamental ToM semantics. Based on these insights, they design a lightweight alignment method that performs targeted activation steering within these identified layers, aiming to causally influence the model’s reasoning toward more robust Theory of Mind behavior.
AI.result: Applying CoSToM leads to substantial gains in ToM-related performance, including more human-like social reasoning and improved quality on downstream dialogue tasks, suggesting that the intervention effectively leverages and aligns internal ToM representations.
AI.conclusion: LLMs appear to contain latent, mechanistically identifiable ToM-related knowledge that is not fully expressed in their default behavior. By moving from passive interpretation to active causal intervention via CoSToM, the authors show that it is possible to better align internal ToM representations with stable, high-quality external behaviors in social reasoning and dialogue.

title: OpeFlo: Automated UX Evaluation via Simulated Human Web Interaction with GUI Grounding
summary: Evaluating web usability typically requires time-consuming user studies and expert reviews, which often limits iteration speed during product development, especially for small teams and agile workflows. We present OpenFlo, a user-experience evaluation agent that simulates user behavior on websites and produces standardized usability. Unlike traditional tools that rely on DOM parsing, OpenFlo grounds actions and observations, enabling it to interact with real web pages end-to-end while maintaining a coherent trace of the user journey. Building on Avenir-Web, our system pairs this robust interaction with simulated user behavior profiles and a structured evaluation protocol that integrates the System Usability Scale (SUS), step-wise Single Ease Questions (SEQ), and concurrent Think Aloud. Subsequently, a comprehensive User Experience (UX) report will be generated. We discuss the architecture of OpenFlo and illustrate how its multimodal grounding improves robustness for web-based interaction and UX evaluation scenarios, paving the way for a new era of continuous, scalable, and data-driven usability testing that empowers every developer to build web interfaces that are usable. Code is available at: https://github.com/Onflow-AI/OpenFlo

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OpenFlo, an AI-based agent that simulates user behavior on real web pages to automatically evaluate usability and generate standardized UX reports, enabling faster and more scalable web usability testing.
AI.motivation: Traditional web usability evaluation relies on time-intensive user studies and expert reviews, which slow down iteration in product development, particularly for small teams and agile workflows. There is a need for a method that can perform continuous, scalable, and data-driven usability testing without requiring large user panels or specialized experts for every design iteration.
AI.method: The authors design OpenFlo, a user-experience evaluation agent that directly interacts with live web pages end-to-end using multimodal grounding rather than simple DOM parsing. OpenFlo is built on Avenir-Web and simulates user behavior via behavior profiles while following a structured evaluation protocol that combines the System Usability Scale (SUS), step-wise Single Ease Questions (SEQ), and concurrent Think Aloud. The system records a coherent trace of the simulated user journey and then compiles the collected signals into a standardized UX report.
AI.result: OpenFlo successfully produces standardized usability metrics and comprehensive UX reports from simulated interactions with real websites. Its multimodal grounding and robust interaction capabilities make it more reliable for web-based interaction and UX evaluation scenarios than tools that rely solely on DOM parsing. The system is implemented and released as open-source code at the provided GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: OpenFlo demonstrates that an agent-based, multimodally grounded simulator can provide continuous, scalable, and data-driven usability testing for web interfaces. By automating parts of UX evaluation and integrating standard metrics like SUS and SEQ within a coherent protocol, the approach can accelerate iteration and empower more developers, including small teams, to build and refine usable web interfaces.

title: Computational Implementation of a Model of Category-Theoretic Metaphor Comprehension
summary: In this study, we developed a computational implementation for a model of metaphor comprehension based on the theory of indeterminate natural transformation (TINT) proposed by Fuyama et al. We simplified the algorithms implementing the model to be closer to the original theory and verified it through data fitting and simulations. The outputs of the algorithms are evaluated with three measures: data-fitting with experimental data, the systematicity of the metaphor comprehension result, and the novelty of the comprehension (i.e. the correspondence of the associative structure of the source and target of the metaphor). The improved algorithm outperformed the existing ones in all the three measures.

AI.tldr: The paper presents and evaluates a simplified computational implementation of TINT, a theory of metaphor comprehension, and shows the improved algorithm better explains human metaphor understanding than previous implementations.
AI.motivation: To create a computational model that more faithfully reflects the original Theory of Indeterminate Natural Transformation (TINT) for metaphor comprehension, and to empirically verify whether this improved implementation better captures human metaphor understanding than existing algorithms.
AI.method: The authors simplified and revised the existing computational algorithms implementing TINT to bring them closer to the original theoretical formulation. They then evaluated the new implementation using three criteria: (1) quantitative data-fitting against experimental data on metaphor comprehension, (2) assessment of the systematicity/structural coherence of the produced metaphor interpretations, and (3) assessment of the novelty of the interpretations, defined as how the associative structures between source and target concepts are newly and meaningfully connected. They performed simulations and statistical comparisons between the improved and existing algorithms.
AI.result: Across all three evaluation measures—fit to human experimental data, systematicity of metaphor interpretations, and novelty of the associative correspondences—the improved TINT-based algorithm achieved better performance than the previous implementations.
AI.conclusion: A streamlined computational implementation of TINT can more accurately and systematically reproduce human metaphor comprehension behavior than earlier models, supporting both the validity of the TINT framework and the effectiveness of the new algorithmic simplifications.

title: Factorizing formal contexts from closures of necessity operators
summary: Factorizing datasets is an interesting process in a multitude of approaches, but many times it is not possible or efficient the computation of a factorization of the dataset. A method to obtain independent subcontexts of a formal context with Boolean data was proposed in~\cite{dubois:2012}, based on the operators used in possibility theory. In this paper, we will analyze this method and study different properties related to the pairs of sets from which a factorization of a formal context arises. We also inspect how the properties given in the classical case can be extended to the fuzzy framework, which is essential to obtain a mechanism that allows the computation of independent subcontexts of a fuzzy context.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to decompose formal (Boolean) and fuzzy contexts into independent subcontexts for more efficient factorization.
AI.motivation: Factorizing datasets or formal contexts is useful in many analytical approaches, but in practice computing a full factorization can be impossible or inefficient. There exists a method for obtaining independent subcontexts in Boolean formal contexts using possibility theory operators, but its properties and extension to fuzziness are not fully understood. The authors aim to clarify and extend this method so factorization becomes more tractable, including in fuzzy settings where data are not just 0/1.
AI.method: They take the existing method that derives independent subcontexts of a Boolean formal context via possibility-theoretic operators and analyze it in detail. They study structural properties of the pairs of sets (likely object/attribute subsets) that generate a factorization of a context. Then they generalize and adapt these properties from the classical, crisp (Boolean) formal context case to the fuzzy context case, developing conditions and mechanisms for independence in fuzzy contexts.
AI.result: They identify and characterize properties of the set pairs from which a factorization of a formal context arises, in the Boolean setting. They show which of these properties extend to the fuzzy framework and how, thereby providing theoretical foundations and criteria for defining and computing independent subcontexts in fuzzy contexts as well. This yields a principled mechanism to obtain independent subcontexts of fuzzy contexts based on the classical method.
