AI.tldr: The paper introduces Accelerated Prompt Stress Testing (APST), a depth-oriented framework that repeatedly queries LLMs with the same prompts under varied conditions to statistically measure operational safety failure rates, revealing reliability issues that standard single-shot benchmarks miss.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM safety benchmarks (e.g., HELM, AIR-BENCH) focus on breadth across many tasks and usually rely on one or very few generations per prompt. However, real-world high-stakes deployments involve repeated use of the same or similar prompts, where rare but recurring failures (hallucinations, inconsistent refusals, unsafe outputs) can accumulate into significant operational risk. There is a need for an evaluation method that captures this depth-oriented, repeat-use reliability rather than only broad task coverage.
AI.method: The authors propose Accelerated Prompt Stress Testing (APST), which repeatedly samples identical prompts to LLMs under controlled operational conditions. They systematically vary factors like sampling temperature and small prompt perturbations, and record occurrences of safety-relevant failure modes (hallucinations, refusal inconsistency, unsafe completions). Instead of treating each failure as a one-off error, they model these events statistically with Bernoulli and binomial distributions to estimate per-inference failure probabilities. This allows quantitative comparison of safety risk across models and configurations. APST is applied to several instruction-tuned LLMs using safety and security prompts derived from AIR-BENCH 2024.
AI.result: Under traditional low-depth evaluation (single or very few generations per prompt, N ≤ 3), different instruction-tuned LLMs appear to perform similarly on safety metrics. However, when the same prompts are stressed with many repeated generations across temperatures, substantial variation emerges in empirical safety failure probabilities. Some models or settings show much higher rates of hallucinations, inconsistent refusals, or unsafe outputs than others, exposing previously hidden reliability differences.
AI.conclusion: Depth-oriented stress testing via APST uncovers meaningful differences in LLM operational reliability that are not visible in conventional shallow benchmarking. Modeling failures as stochastic events in repeated inference provides a more realistic view of safety risk in sustained, high-stakes deployments. Therefore, safety evaluation of LLMs should incorporate repeated-prompt, statistically grounded stress tests in addition to traditional breadth-focused benchmarks.

title: FAITH: Factuality Alignment through Integrating Trustworthiness and Honestness
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) can generate factually inaccurate content even if they have corresponding knowledge, which critically undermines their reliability. Existing approaches attempt to mitigate this by incorporating uncertainty in QA prompt during training, but these numerical scores lack the semantic richness for LLM to properly understand its internal states of trustworthiness and honestness, leading to insufficient factuality alignment. We introduce FAITH (Factuality Alignment through Integrating Trustworthiness and Honestness), a post-training framework for factuality alignment that integrates natural-language uncertainty signals with external knowledge. Specifically, we augment training datasets by computing confidence scores and semantic entropy from LLM outputs and mapping them into a knowledge state quadrant that describes the model's internal knowledge possession (trustworthiness) and answering behaviors (honestness) in natural language. Based on this enhanced data, we design a reward function that considers both correctness and uncertainty signals, and fine-tune the LLM using the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm. To further mitigate weakly grounded responses, we design a retrieval-augmented module that retrieves relevant external passages, improving the consistency between internal and external knowledge representations. Extensive experiments on four knowledge-intensive benchmarks demonstrate that FAITH enhances the factual accuracy and truthfulness of LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes FAITH, a post-training framework that improves LLM factuality by aligning the model’s internal uncertainty signals (in natural language) with external knowledge and optimizing with a custom reward via PPO and retrieval augmentation.
AI.motivation: LLMs often produce factually incorrect answers even when they internally ‘know’ the correct information, damaging reliability. Existing methods inject numerical uncertainty scores into training, but numbers alone lack semantic clarity for the model to reason about when it should trust its knowledge or express uncertainty, leading to limited gains in factuality and truthfulness. The authors aim to create a richer, semantically grounded way for LLMs to represent and use their own uncertainty and knowledge state.
AI.method: 1) Generate LLM outputs on QA data and compute confidence scores and semantic entropy for each output. 2) Map each case into a "knowledge state quadrant" that characterizes, in natural language, whether the model possesses the relevant knowledge (trustworthiness) and whether it answers honestly or overconfidently (honestness). 3) Use these augmented, natural-language uncertainty descriptions plus correctness labels to define a reward function that jointly accounts for factual correctness and appropriate expression of uncertainty. 4) Apply Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to fine-tune the LLM with this reward. 5) Add a retrieval-augmented component that retrieves external passages at inference/training time to align the model’s internal knowledge with external evidence and reduce weakly grounded responses.
AI.result: On four knowledge-intensive benchmarks, models trained with FAITH show improved factual accuracy and truthfulness compared to baselines. The framework leads to better calibration between the model’s internal knowledge, its expressed uncertainty, and external evidence, reducing hallucinations and overconfident errors. Quantitative results indicate consistent gains across datasets, and qualitative analyses show more cautious, better-grounded answers when the model is unsure.
AI.conclusion: FAITH demonstrates that encoding uncertainty and knowledge state in natural language, combined with external retrieval and RL-based optimization, can substantially improve LLM factuality and honesty. By jointly modeling trustworthiness (knowledge possession) and honestness (answering behavior) and optimizing a reward that respects both correctness and uncertainty, LLMs become more reliable and less prone to ungrounded, overconfident hallucinations on knowledge-intensive tasks.

title: Unifying Ontology Construction and Semantic Alignment for Deterministic Enterprise Reasoning at Scale
summary: While enterprises amass vast quantities of data, much of it remains chaotic and effectively dormant, preventing decision-making based on comprehensive information. Existing neuro-symbolic approaches rely on disjoint pipelines and struggle with error propagation. We introduce the large ontology model (LOM), a unified framework that seamlessly integrates ontology construction, semantic alignment, and logical reasoning into a single end-to-end architecture. LOM employs a construct-align-reason (CAR) pipeline, leveraging its unified architecture across all three stages: it first autonomously constructs a domain-specific ontological universe from raw data, then aligns neural generation with this structural reality using a graph-aware encoder and reinforcement learning, and finally executes deterministic reasoning over the constructed topology, node attributes and relation types. We evaluate LOM on a comprehensive benchmark constructed from diverse real-world enterprise datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that LOM-4B achieves 88.8% accuracy in ontology completion and 94% in complex graph reasoning tasks, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art LLMs. These findings validate that autonomous logical construction is essential for achieving deterministic, enterprise-grade intelligence.

AI.tldr: Proposes LOM, an end-to-end large ontology model that builds, aligns, and reasons over enterprise knowledge graphs, outperforming LLMs on ontology and graph reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Enterprise data is vast but chaotic and underutilized; current neuro-symbolic methods rely on disjoint components where errors propagate between ontology construction, semantic alignment, and reasoning, limiting reliable, deterministic decision support.
AI.method: Introduce LOM, a large ontology model with a unified construct-align-reason (CAR) pipeline. It (1) autonomously constructs a domain-specific ontology from raw enterprise data; (2) aligns neural generation to this ontology using a graph-aware encoder and reinforcement learning; and (3) performs deterministic logical reasoning over the learned graph topology, node attributes, and relation types. The model is evaluated on a new benchmark built from diverse real-world enterprise datasets.
AI.result: LOM-4B achieves 88.8% accuracy on ontology completion and 94% accuracy on complex graph reasoning tasks, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art large language models on the same benchmark.
AI.conclusion: A unified, end-to-end large ontology model that autonomously constructs and reasons over structured knowledge is crucial for deterministic, enterprise-grade AI; LOM demonstrates that integrating construction, alignment, and reasoning in one architecture yields substantial gains over standard LLMs on ontology and graph reasoning tasks.

title: Relational Probing: LM-to-Graph Adaptation for Financial Prediction
comment: Accpeted by The 2nd Workskop on Advances in Financial AI Workshop: Towards Agentic and Responsible Systems at ICLR 2026
summary: Language models can be used to identify relationships between financial entities in text. However, while structured output mechanisms exist, prompting-based pipelines still incur autoregressive decoding costs and decouple graph construction from downstream optimization. We propose \emph{Relational Probing}, which replaces the standard language-model head with a relation head that induces a relational graph directly from language-model hidden states and is trained jointly with the downstream task model for stock-trend prediction. This approach both learns semantic representations and preserves the strict structure of the induced relational graph. It enables language-model outputs to go beyond text, allowing them to be reshaped into task-specific formats for downstream models. To enhance reproducibility, we provide an operational definition of small language models (SLMs): models that can be fine-tuned end-to-end on a single 24GB GPU under specified batch-size and sequence-length settings. Experiments use Qwen3 backbones (0.6B/1.7B/4B) as upstream SLMs and compare against a co-occurrence baseline. Relational Probing yields consistent performance improvements at competitive inference cost.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Relational Probing, a method that replaces the standard language-model head with a relation head to directly induce relational graphs from hidden states for stock-trend prediction, achieving better performance than a co-occurrence baseline with competitive inference cost.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for extracting relationships between financial entities from text rely on prompting-based structured outputs, which are autoregressive, computationally expensive, and separate graph construction from downstream optimization. There is a need for an approach that jointly learns semantic representations and task-specific relational structures in a more efficient, integrated manner, especially using small language models in resource-constrained settings.
AI.method: The authors propose Relational Probing, which swaps the usual LM token-prediction head for a relation head that maps hidden states to a structured relational graph over financial entities. This relation head is trained end-to-end together with a downstream stock-trend prediction model, directly shaping the hidden representations toward task-relevant relational structure. They operationally define "small language models" as models that can be fine-tuned end-to-end on a single 24GB GPU under fixed batch and sequence constraints. Experiments use Qwen3 models of sizes 0.6B, 1.7B, and 4B as the upstream LMs, and compare against a simple co-occurrence-based graph baseline.
AI.result: Across Qwen3 model sizes (0.6B/1.7B/4B), Relational Probing consistently outperforms a co-occurrence graph baseline on stock-trend prediction, while maintaining competitive inference costs relative to prompting-based structured-output pipelines. The induced relational graphs remain structurally well-formed and beneficial for downstream prediction.
AI.conclusion: Relational Probing effectively turns language-model hidden states into structured relational graphs tailored to a downstream task, avoiding autoregressive decoding overhead and decoupled graph construction. This approach broadens how language-model outputs can be used—beyond text generation to directly producing task-specific structured representations—while remaining practical for small language models trainable on modest hardware.

title: General-purpose LLMs as Models of Human Driver Behavior: The Case of Simplified Merging
summary: Human behavior models are essential as behavior references and for simulating human agents in virtual safety assessment of automated vehicles (AVs), yet current models face a trade-off between interpretability and flexibility. General-purpose large language models (LLMs) offer a promising alternative: a single model potentially deployable without parameter fitting across diverse scenarios. However, what LLMs can and cannot capture about human driving behavior remains poorly understood. We address this gap by embedding two general-purpose LLMs (OpenAI o3 and Google Gemini 2.5 Pro) as standalone, closed-loop driver agents in a simplified one-dimensional merging scenario and comparing their behavior against human data using quantitative and qualitative analyses. Both models reproduce human-like intermittent operational control and tactical dependencies on spatial cues. However, neither consistently captures the human response to dynamic velocity cues, and safety performance diverges sharply between models. A systematic prompt ablation study reveals that prompt components act as model-specific inductive biases that do not transfer across LLMs. These findings suggest that general-purpose LLMs could potentially serve as standalone, ready-to-use human behavior models in AV evaluation pipelines, but future research is needed to better understand their failure modes and ensure their validity as models of human driving behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well general-purpose LLMs (OpenAI o3, Gemini 2.5 Pro) can act as human driver models in a simplified merging task, finding partial human-likeness but important gaps and model-specific prompt effects.
AI.motivation: Human behavior models are widely used in simulating traffic and assessing automated vehicle safety, but existing models trade off interpretability and flexibility and usually need scenario-specific tuning. Since large language models are powerful, general-purpose reasoning engines, the authors want to know whether LLMs can function as plug-and-play driver models that generalize across scenarios without re-fitting, and to understand their limitations for realistic human driving behavior representation.
AI.method: The authors embed two general-purpose LLMs (OpenAI o3 and Google Gemini 2.5 Pro) as closed-loop driver agents in a simplified one-dimensional lane-merging scenario. They let each LLM control a simulated vehicle through prompts, then compare its behavior quantitatively and qualitatively to empirical human driving data. They specifically analyze operational control patterns, tactical responses to spatial and velocity cues, safety-related behavior, and run a prompt ablation study to see how different prompt components influence each model's behavior and generalization.
AI.result: Both LLMs managed to reproduce some key qualitative aspects of human driving, such as intermittent (non-continuous) control actions and tactical decisions that depend on spatial relationships (e.g., gaps, distances). However, they did not reliably mimic human responses to dynamic velocity information, and their safety behaviors differed strongly from each other, indicating inconsistent risk handling. The prompt ablation shows that particular pieces of the prompt serve as inductive biases whose effects vary by model and do not transfer between LLMs.
AI.conclusion: General-purpose LLMs show promise as stand-alone, ready-to-use human driver behavior models in automated vehicle evaluation, especially in capturing some human-like control and spatial-tactical patterns without task-specific training. Nonetheless, they currently fail to robustly reproduce human responses to dynamic cues and exhibit model- and prompt-specific idiosyncrasies, raising questions about reliability and validity. Further work is required to systematically characterize their failure modes, improve safety consistency, and establish conditions under which LLM-based driver models can be trusted in AV assessment pipelines.

title: CodeComp: Structural KV Cache Compression for Agentic Coding
summary: Agentic code tasks such as fault localization and patch generation require processing long codebases under tight memory constraints, where the Key-Value (KV) cache becomes the primary inference bottleneck. Existing compression methods rely exclusively on attention signals to estimate token importance, systematically discarding structurally critical tokens such as call sites, branch conditions, and assignments that are essential for code understanding. We present CodeComp, a training-free KV cache compression framework that incorporates static program analysis into LLM inference via Code Property Graph priors extracted by Joern. Across bug localization and code generation benchmarks, CodeComp consistently outperforms attention-only compression baselines under equal memory budgets, recovering the majority of full-context accuracy under aggressive KV cache compression, while matching the patch generation quality of uncompressed full-context inference and integrating seamlessly into SGLang-based agentic coding pipelines without model modification.

AI.tldr: Introduces CodeComp, a training-free KV cache compression method for LLMs on code that uses static program analysis instead of only attention to keep crucial tokens and preserve accuracy under tight memory.
AI.motivation: Agentic code tasks like bug localization and patch generation require handling long codebases with limited memory, where the KV cache becomes the main bottleneck. Existing KV compression relies solely on attention scores to decide which tokens to keep, which causes it to drop structurally important code tokens (e.g., call sites, conditions, assignments) that matter for program understanding and harms performance.
AI.method: Propose CodeComp, a training-free KV cache compression framework that augments LLM inference with structural signals from static program analysis. It uses Code Property Graphs (CPGs) extracted via Joern to identify and prioritize structurally critical tokens when compressing the KV cache, integrating this process into SGLang-based agentic coding pipelines without modifying the underlying LLM.
AI.result: On bug localization and code generation benchmarks, CodeComp outperforms attention-only KV compression methods at the same memory budgets, recovers most of the full-context model accuracy even with aggressive compression, and matches the patch generation quality of uncompressed full-context inference.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating static program analysis via Code Property Graph priors into KV cache compression is an effective, training-free way to preserve essential structural information in long code contexts. CodeComp can be plugged into existing agentic coding systems to alleviate KV cache memory bottlenecks while maintaining or approaching full-context performance, without requiring changes to the base model.

title: Beyond Theory of Mind in Robotics
summary: Theory of Mind, the capacity to explain and predict behavior by inferring hidden mental states, has become the dominant paradigm for social interaction in robotics. Yet ToM rests on three assumptions that poorly capture how most social interaction actually unfolds: that meaning travels inside-out from hidden states to observable behavior; that understanding requires detached inference rather than participation; and that the meaning of behavior is fixed and available to a passive observer. Drawing on ethnomethodology, conversation analysis, and participatory sense-making, I argue that social meaning is not decoded from behavior but produced through moment-to-moment coordination between agents. This interactional foundation has direct implications for robot design: shifting from internal state modeling toward policies for sustaining coordination, from observer-based inference toward active participation, and from fixed behavioral meaning toward meaning potential stabilized through response.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques standard Theory of Mind approaches in social robotics and proposes an interaction-centered view where social meaning emerges through real-time coordination, not hidden state inference.
AI.motivation: In social robotics, Theory of Mind (ToM) has become the dominant framework for enabling robots to interact socially by inferring hidden mental states from observable behavior. However, real-life social interaction often does not align with ToM’s core assumptions, leading to mismatches between robotic models and human social practices. The author is motivated to address these conceptual gaps and provide a more accurate foundation for designing socially interactive robots.
AI.method: The paper uses a theoretical and conceptual analysis rather than experiments. It brings together insights from ethnomethodology, conversation analysis, and participatory sense-making to critically examine the assumptions of ToM—specifically, the ideas that meaning flows from hidden internal states to behavior, that understanding is based on detached inference, and that behavior has a fixed meaning. The author then synthesizes these perspectives to construct an alternative, interaction-focused account of social understanding.
AI.result: The analysis shows that social meaning is better understood as something co-produced in the moment through coordination between interacting agents, rather than something decoded from behavior based on assumed internal states. This leads to a reframing of key design goals in social robotics: prioritizing coordination policies over internal state models, participation over observation, and dynamic meaning potentials over fixed behavioral interpretations.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that grounding social robotics in an interactional perspective can lead to more robust and natural social behavior in robots. Designers should focus less on building systems that infer hidden mental states and more on systems that skillfully participate in and sustain ongoing coordination, allowing social meaning to emerge through interaction itself.

title: Comparative Analysis of Large Language Models in Healthcare
summary: Background: Large Language Models (LLMs) are transforming artificial intelligence applications in healthcare due to their ability to understand, generate, and summarize complex medical text. They offer valuable support to clinicians, researchers, and patients, yet their deployment in high-stakes clinical environments raises critical concerns regarding accuracy, reliability, and patient safety. Despite substantial attention in recent years, standardized benchmarking of LLMs for medical applications has been limited. Objective: This study addresses the need for a standardized comparative evaluation of LLMs in medical settings. Method: We evaluate multiple models, including ChatGPT, LLaMA, Grok, Gemini, and ChatDoctor, on core medical tasks such as patient note summarization and medical question answering, using the open-access datasets, MedMCQA, PubMedQA, and Asclepius, and assess performance through a combination of linguistic and task-specific metrics. Results: The results indicate that domain-specific models, such as ChatDoctor, excel in contextual reliability, producing medically accurate and semantically aligned text, whereas general-purpose models like Grok and LLaMA perform better in structured question-answering tasks, demonstrating higher quantitative accuracy. This highlights the complementary strengths of domain-specific and general-purpose LLMs depending on the medical task. Conclusion: Our findings suggest that LLMs can meaningfully support medical professionals and enhance clinical decision-making; however, their safe and effective deployment requires adherence to ethical standards, contextual accuracy, and human oversight in relevant cases. These results underscore the importance of task-specific evaluation and cautious integration of LLMs into healthcare workflows.

AI.tldr: The paper benchmarks several large language models on key medical NLP tasks to compare domain-specific versus general-purpose performance and discuss safe deployment in healthcare.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs are increasingly used in healthcare, there is no standardized way to compare how different models perform on core medical tasks, which is needed to ensure accuracy, reliability, and patient safety before clinical deployment.
AI.method: The authors evaluate multiple LLMs—ChatGPT, LLaMA, Grok, Gemini, and the medical-domain model ChatDoctor—on patient note summarization and medical question answering using open datasets (MedMCQA, PubMedQA, Asclepius). They assess outputs with both linguistic measures and task-specific performance metrics.
AI.result: Domain-specific models like ChatDoctor produce more contextually reliable, medically accurate, and semantically aligned text, while general-purpose models such as Grok and LLaMA achieve higher quantitative accuracy on structured QA tasks. Each class of model shows strengths depending on task type.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can provide meaningful support to clinicians and improve decision-making, but safe use requires ethical safeguards, attention to contextual accuracy, human oversight, and task-specific evaluation. The work supports cautious, workflow-aware integration of LLMs into healthcare rather than blanket deployment.

title: The Geometry of Knowing: From Possibilistic Ignorance to Probabilistic Certainty -- A Measure-Theoretic Framework for Epistemic Convergence
summary: This paper develops a measure-theoretic framework establishing when and how a possibilistic representation of incomplete knowledge contracts into a probabilistic representation of intrinsic stochastic variability. Epistemic uncertainty is encoded by a possibility distribution and its dual necessity measure, defining a credal set bounding all probability measures consistent with current evidence. As evidence accumulates, the credal set contracts. The epistemic collapse condition marks the transition: the Choquet integral converges to the Lebesgue integral over the unique limiting density. We prove this rigorously (Theorem 4.5), with all assumptions explicit and a full treatment of the non-consonant case. We introduce the aggregate epistemic width W, establish its axiomatic properties, provide a canonical normalization, and give a feasible online proxy resolving a circularity in prior formulations. Section 7 develops the dynamics of epistemic contraction: evidence induces compatibility, compatibility performs falsification, posterior possibility is the min-intersection of prior possibility and compatibility, and a credibility-directed flow governs support geometry contraction. This is not belief updating. It is knowledge contraction. Probability theory is the limiting geometry of that process. The UKF and ESPF solve different problems by different mechanisms. The UKF minimizes MSE, asserts truth, and requires a valid generative model. The ESPF minimizes maximum entropy and surfaces what evidence has not ruled out. When the world is Gaussian and the model valid, both reach the same estimate by entirely different routes -- convergent optimality, not hierarchical containment. We prove this (Theorem 9.1) and compare both on a 2-day, 877-step orbital tracking scenario. Both achieve 1-meter accuracy. The UKF is accurate but epistemically silent. The ESPF is accurate and epistemically honest.

AI.tldr: They build a rigorous bridge from possibility theory (for incomplete knowledge) to probability theory (for inherent randomness) and show when possibility-based estimators converge to probabilistic ones, including in a practical filtering problem.
AI.motivation: Classical probability conflates two different things: lack of knowledge about a system (epistemic uncertainty) and genuine randomness in the system (aleatory variability). Possibility theory and credal sets can represent incomplete knowledge, but it is unclear when these should reduce to standard probabilities and how this process behaves dynamically and in applications like state estimation and filtering. The paper aims to formalize this relationship and clarify how evidence turns broad epistemic descriptions into precise probabilistic ones.
AI.method: They use a measure-theoretic formalization: encode epistemic uncertainty via possibility distributions and associated necessity measures, which define a credal set of compatible probabilities. They analyze how this set contracts as evidence accumulates and define an “epistemic collapse condition” under which the Choquet integral of the possibility measure converges to the usual Lebesgue integral with respect to a unique limiting probability density. They introduce a scalar functional, aggregate epistemic width W, to quantify the amount of epistemic uncertainty, derive its axioms, give a normalized form, and propose an online computable proxy. They also develop a dynamical description of how evidence-driven compatibility and falsification geometrically contract support sets in possibility space. Finally, they compare two filters: the Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) and an Evidence-Supported Possibility Filter (ESPF), formally proving a theorem about when their estimates coincide in Gaussian, well-specified settings and running a numerical orbital tracking experiment.
AI.result: The main theoretical result (Theorem 4.5) shows that under explicit assumptions, the credal set defined by a possibility distribution contracts with accumulating evidence until it collapses to a single probability measure; in this limit, the Choquet integral reduces to the Lebesgue integral over the unique limiting density. They fully address both consonant and non-consonant possibility structures. They define the aggregate epistemic width W, prove its mathematical properties, specify a canonical normalization, and provide a practical online approximation that avoids circular definitions in earlier work. They derive a dynamical model of epistemic contraction driven by evidence-induced compatibility and falsification, with posterior possibility obtained via min-intersection and a credibility-directed flow describing support contraction. For filtering, they prove (Theorem 9.1) that in Gaussian, correctly modeled systems, the ESPF and UKF yield the same state estimate, even though they optimize different criteria. In an 877-step, 2-day orbital tracking example, both achieve ~1 m accuracy, but with different epistemic interpretations.
AI.conclusion: Possibility theory provides a coherent, dynamic framework for representing and contracting epistemic uncertainty, distinct from probabilistic updating. As evidence accumulates, a possibility-based credal description can rigorously collapse into a single probabilistic model, with probability theory appearing as a limiting geometry of knowledge contraction rather than a universally a priori framework. The introduced measure of epistemic width W and the dynamical contraction model make this process quantifiable and implementable. In practical state estimation, an evidence-based possibilistic filter (ESPF) can match the accuracy of probabilistic filters like the UKF in ideal conditions, but remains explicitly expressive about what is and is not ruled out by evidence, highlighting a conceptual difference: the UKF is efficient for MSE minimization under a trusted model, whereas the ESPF is designed for epistemic transparency under incomplete knowledge.

title: AdaQE-CG: Adaptive Query Expansion for Web-Scale Generative AI Model and Data Card Generation
comment: This paper has been accepted to the main conference of WWW 2026
summary: Transparent and standardized documentation is essential for building trustworthy generative AI (GAI) systems. However, existing automated methods for generating model and data cards still face three major challenges: (i) static templates, as most systems rely on fixed query templates that cannot adapt to diverse paper structures or evolving documentation requirements; (ii) information scarcity, since web-scale repositories such as Hugging Face often contain incomplete or inconsistent metadata, leading to missing or noisy information; and (iii) lack of benchmarks, as the absence of standardized datasets and evaluation protocols hinders fair and reproducible assessment of documentation quality. To address these limitations, we propose AdaQE-CG, an Adaptive Query Expansion for Card Generation framework that combines dynamic information extraction with cross-card knowledge transfer. Its Intra-Paper Extraction via Context-Aware Query Expansion (IPE-QE) module iteratively refines extraction queries to recover richer and more complete information from scientific papers and repositories, while its Inter-Card Completion using the MetaGAI Pool (ICC-MP) module fills missing fields by transferring semantically relevant content from similar cards in a curated dataset. In addition, we introduce MetaGAI-Bench, the first large-scale, expert-annotated benchmark for evaluating GAI documentation. Comprehensive experiments across five quality dimensions show that AdaQE-CG substantially outperforms existing approaches, exceeds human-authored data cards, and approaches human-level quality for model cards. Code, prompts, and data are publicly available at: https://github.com/haoxuan-unt2024/AdaQE-CG.

AI.tldr: They propose AdaQE-CG, a framework that adaptively extracts and completes documentation for generative AI models and data, plus a new benchmark (MetaGAI-Bench) to evaluate such documentation.
AI.motivation: Current automated generation of model and data cards suffers from rigid, static templates, incomplete or noisy metadata from repositories, and the lack of standardized benchmarks to evaluate documentation quality, which undermines transparency and trustworthiness of generative AI systems.
AI.method: AdaQE-CG has two key modules: (1) IPE-QE (Intra-Paper Extraction via Context-Aware Query Expansion), which iteratively refines and expands extraction queries to pull richer, context-aware information from scientific papers and repositories instead of relying on fixed templates; and (2) ICC-MP (Inter-Card Completion using the MetaGAI Pool), which fills missing documentation fields by transferring semantically similar content from a curated pool of existing cards. They also construct MetaGAI-Bench, a large-scale, expert-annotated benchmark for evaluating documentation quality of generative AI systems.
AI.result: Experiments across five documentation quality dimensions show that AdaQE-CG clearly outperforms existing automatic documentation approaches, produces data cards that are even better than human-authored ones, and reaches near human-level quality on model cards, as measured on MetaGAI-Bench.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive query expansion and cross-card knowledge transfer can substantially improve automated generation of model and data cards, achieving documentation quality comparable to or better than human-authored baselines. The new MetaGAI-Bench benchmark provides a standardized way to evaluate such documentation systems.

title: A Structured Clustering Approach for Inducing Media Narratives
comment: Accepted to the Proceedings of the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: Media narratives wield tremendous power in shaping public opinion, yet computational approaches struggle to capture the nuanced storytelling structures that communication theory emphasizes as central to how meaning is constructed. Existing approaches either miss subtle narrative patterns through coarse-grained analysis or require domain-specific taxonomies that limit scalability. To bridge this gap, we present a framework for inducing rich narrative schemas by jointly modeling events and characters via structured clustering. Our approach produces explainable narrative schemas that align with established framing theory while scaling to large corpora without exhaustive manual annotation.

AI.tldr: A new computational framework induces rich, explainable narrative schemas from large text corpora by jointly clustering events and characters, aligning with communication and framing theory.
AI.motivation: Media narratives strongly influence public opinion, but current computational methods fail to capture the fine-grained, theory-relevant narrative structures—such as relationships between events and characters—that communication scholars consider crucial. Existing work is either too coarse to detect subtle patterns or depends on hand-crafted, domain-specific taxonomies that are hard to scale, creating a need for an automated yet theoretically grounded narrative analysis method.
AI.method: The paper proposes a structured clustering framework that jointly models events and characters in text. By clustering these elements together, the method automatically induces narrative schemas—recurrent patterns of who does what to whom and in what sequence—without relying on domain-specific label sets. The model is designed to be interpretable, linking discovered clusters to concepts in framing and narrative theory, and scalable to large corpora without extensive manual annotation.
AI.result: Using this joint event–character modeling and structured clustering, the approach yields narrative schemas that are both richer and more nuanced than those derived from coarse-grained methods. The induced schemas are explainable and show clear correspondences with established framing concepts, demonstrating that the method can uncover theoretically meaningful narrative patterns at scale.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully bridges a gap between computational text analysis and communication theory by automatically discovering rich, interpretable narrative structures from large media corpora. It overcomes limitations of coarse analyses and domain-specific taxonomies, offering a scalable, theory-aligned tool for studying how media narratives construct meaning and potentially shape public opinion.

title: Competing with AI Scientists: Agent-Driven Approach to Astrophysics Research
summary: We present an agent-driven approach to the construction of parameter inference pipelines for scientific data analysis. Our method leverages a multi-agent system, Cmbagent (the analysis system of the AI scientist Denario), in which specialized agents collaborate to generate research ideas, write and execute code, evaluate results, and iteratively refine the overall pipeline. As a case study, we apply this approach to the FAIR Universe Weak Lensing Uncertainty Challenge, a competition under time constraints focused on robust cosmological parameter inference with realistic observational uncertainties. While the fully autonomous exploration initially did not reach expert-level performance, the integration of human intervention enabled our agent-driven workflow to achieve a first-place result in the challenge. This demonstrates that semi-autonomous agentic systems can compete with, and in some cases surpass, expert solutions. We describe our workflow in detail, including both the autonomous and semi-autonomous exploration by Cmbagent. Our final inference pipeline utilizes parameter-efficient convolutional neural networks, likelihood calibration over a known parameter grid, and multiple regularization techniques. Our results suggest that agent-driven research workflows can provide a scalable framework to rapidly explore and construct pipelines for inference problems.

AI.tldr: An agent-based multi-agent system, Cmbagent, is used to automatically build and refine a cosmological parameter inference pipeline; with human guidance, it wins a weak-lensing challenge.
AI.motivation: Designing high-performing parameter inference pipelines for complex scientific data is labor-intensive and requires expert knowledge. The authors want a scalable, semi-automated way to generate, test, and refine such pipelines using AI agents, and to see if this approach can match or exceed expert-designed solutions in a realistic challenge setting.
AI.method: They employ Cmbagent, a multi-agent system where specialized agents propose research ideas, write and run code, assess results, and iteratively optimize the pipeline. They test this on the FAIR Universe Weak Lensing Uncertainty Challenge. The workflow is studied in two regimes: fully autonomous and semi-autonomous where humans intervene and guide the agents. The final pipeline includes parameter-efficient CNN architectures, likelihood calibration over a precomputed parameter grid, and several regularization strategies to improve robustness.
AI.result: In fully autonomous mode, the system does not reach expert-level performance. With human intervention and guidance in a semi-autonomous setup, the agent-driven workflow produces a pipeline that attains first place in the weak-lensing challenge. The resulting pipeline is competitive with state-of-the-art expert approaches, demonstrating the practical effectiveness of the human-in-the-loop, agent-driven process.
AI.conclusion: Semi-autonomous, agent-driven research workflows can construct competitive and even superior parameter inference pipelines for complex scientific tasks, especially when combined with human oversight. This hybrid approach shows promise as a scalable framework for rapidly exploring solution spaces and building robust inference pipelines in scientific data analysis.

title: BLUEmed: Retrieval-Augmented Multi-Agent Debate for Clinical Error Detection
comment: Accepted to the IEEE International Conference on Healthcare Informatics (ICHI) 2026
summary: Terminology substitution errors in clinical notes, where one medical term is replaced by a linguistically valid but clinically different term, pose a persistent challenge for automated error detection in healthcare. We introduce BLUEmed, a multi-agent debate framework augmented with hybrid Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) that combines evidence-grounded reasoning with multi-perspective verification for clinical error detection. BLUEmed decomposes each clinical note into focused sub-queries, retrieves source-partitioned evidence through dense, sparse, and online retrieval, and assigns two domain expert agents distinct knowledge bases to produce independent analyses; when the experts disagree, a structured counter-argumentation round and cross-source adjudication resolve the conflict, followed by a cascading safety layer that filters common false-positive patterns. We evaluate BLUEmed on a clinical terminology substitution detection benchmark under both zero-shot and few-shot prompting with multiple backbone models spanning proprietary and open-source families. Experimental results show that BLUEmed achieves the best accuracy (69.13%), ROC-AUC (74.45%), and PR-AUC (72.44%) under few-shot prompting, outperforming both single-agent RAG and debate-only baselines. Further analyses across six backbone models and two prompting strategies confirm that retrieval augmentation and structured debate are complementary, and that the framework benefits most from models with sufficient instruction-following and clinical language understanding.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes BLUEmed, a multi-agent, retrieval-augmented debate framework that improves detection of clinical terminology substitution errors in notes, outperforming single-agent and debate-only baselines.
AI.motivation: Clinical notes often contain terminology substitution errors where a valid medical term is incorrectly used in place of another with different clinical meaning. These errors are difficult for automated systems to catch because they are linguistically plausible and require nuanced clinical understanding and verification against external knowledge. Existing single-agent or non-retrieval-based methods struggle with such errors, motivating a framework that combines strong retrieval with multi-agent reasoning and verification.
AI.method: The authors design BLUEmed, a multi-agent debate framework enhanced with hybrid Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Clinical notes are decomposed into focused sub-queries, and evidence is retrieved from multiple source-partitioned knowledge bases using dense, sparse, and online retrieval. Two domain expert agents, each with a distinct knowledge base, independently analyze the evidence. If their conclusions differ, they engage in a structured counter-argumentation and cross-source adjudication process to resolve conflicts. A cascading safety layer then filters common false-positive patterns. The system is evaluated in zero-shot and few-shot prompting settings with multiple backbone LLMs, both proprietary and open-source.
AI.result: On a clinical terminology substitution detection benchmark, BLUEmed attains the highest reported performance under few-shot prompting, with accuracy of 69.13%, ROC-AUC of 74.45%, and PR-AUC of 72.44%. It outperforms single-agent RAG systems and debate-only baselines. Ablation-style comparisons across six backbone models and two prompting strategies show that both retrieval augmentation and structured debate contribute additively to performance, and that stronger instruction-following and clinical language capabilities in the backbone model yield greater gains.
AI.conclusion: BLUEmed demonstrates that combining hybrid RAG with multi-agent debate and cross-source adjudication is an effective strategy for detecting subtle clinical terminology substitution errors. The synergy between retrieval and structured debate leads to performance gains over simpler architectures, particularly when built on capable, clinically aware language models. The framework points toward more reliable, evidence-grounded AI assistance for reviewing clinical documentation and could be extended to other clinical error detection and validation tasks.

title: How LLMs Might Think
summary: Do large language models (LLMs) think? Daniel Stoljar and Zhihe Vincent Zhang have recently developed an argument from rationality for the claim that LLMs do not think. We contend, however, that the argument from rationality not only falters, but leaves open an intriguing possibility: that LLMs engage only in arational, associative forms of thinking, and have purely associative minds. Our positive claim is that if LLMs think at all, they likely think precisely in this manner.

AI.tldr: The paper challenges a recent argument that large language models (LLMs) do not think, and proposes instead that if LLMs think, they do so in an arational, associative way.
AI.motivation: There is an ongoing philosophical debate about whether LLMs genuinely think or merely simulate thought. Stoljar and Zhang offered an argument from rationality to deny LLM thought. The authors aim to reassess this argument and to clarify what kind of thinking, if any, LLMs might possess, filling a conceptual gap in discussions that assume human-like rationality as the benchmark.
AI.method: The paper uses conceptual and philosophical analysis. It critically examines the argument from rationality by Stoljar and Zhang, identifies its weaknesses, and develops an alternative framework that distinguishes between rational and arational, associative forms of thinking. It then applies this framework to the case of LLMs.
AI.result: The authors argue that the argument from rationality against LLM thinking fails. They develop and defend the notion that there can be arational, associative thinking—forms of thought not governed by rational norms—and show that LLMs fit this profile better than a strictly rational one.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the failure of the argument from rationality leaves room for LLMs to count as thinkers, but of a distinctive kind: they have purely associative minds characterized by arational, associative thinking, rather than the norm-governed rational cognition typical of human thought.

title: NameBERT: Scaling Name-Based Nationality Classification with LLM-Augmented Open Academic Data
comment: 12 pages, 3 figures, 8 tables; accepted at the 39th Canadian Conference on Artificial Intelligence (Canadian AI 2026)
summary: Inferring nationality from personal names is a critical capability for equity and bias monitoring, personalization, and a valuable tool in biomedical and sociological research. However, existing name-based nationality classifiers are typically trained on relatively small or source-specific labeled datasets, which can introduce coverage gaps and limit performance for underrepresented countries. While large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong zero-shot performance for name-based nationality prediction, their computational cost and latency make them impractical for real-time, large-scale deployment. In this work, we created a large-scale name-nationality dataset from the Open Academic Graph (OAG) and introduce a framework that leverages LLMs as dataset enrichers rather than inference engines. We augment low-resource countries with LLM-generated names and evaluate on real and synthetic-tail test sets. We find that augmentation produces large gains when evaluation includes synthetic tail names and still offers a modest lift on tail-country metrics otherwise. Overall, NameBERT models achieve significantly higher accuracy than state-of-the-art baselines across both in- and out-of-domain tasks, while remaining efficient for large-scale inference compared to LLMs.

AI.tldr: They build efficient nationality-prediction models (NameBERT) by creating a huge name–nationality dataset from Open Academic Graph and using LLMs only to enrich low-resource countries, not for inference.
AI.motivation: Name-based nationality inference is important for fairness monitoring, personalization, and various research fields, but current classifiers rely on small or biased datasets and underperform for underrepresented countries. LLMs work well but are too slow and expensive for large-scale, real-time use. The authors want a scalable, accurate, and efficient alternative that handles tail (low-resource) nationalities better.
AI.method: They construct a large-scale labeled dataset of personal names and nationalities from the Open Academic Graph (OAG). Then they use large language models as dataset enrichers: generating additional plausible names for low-resource (tail) countries to balance the training data. They train BERT-style models (NameBERT) on this real-plus-synthetic data and evaluate on both real names and synthetic-tail test sets, comparing against prior name-based nationality classifiers and LLM zero-shot performance.
AI.result: Augmenting low-resource countries with LLM-generated names yields substantial performance gains when the evaluation includes synthetic tail names, and still provides modest improvements on tail-country metrics when evaluated only on real data. Across in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks, NameBERT significantly outperforms state-of-the-art name-based baselines while being much more efficient than LLMs at inference time.
AI.conclusion: Using LLMs as data generators rather than inference engines is an effective strategy for building scalable nationality-prediction models. The resulting NameBERT models improve accuracy, especially for underrepresented countries, and maintain practical efficiency for large-scale deployment, outperforming existing methods without incurring LLM inference costs.

title: Belief-Aware VLM Model for Human-like Reasoning
summary: Traditional neural network models for intent inference rely heavily on observable states and struggle to generalize across diverse tasks and dynamic environments. Recent advances in Vision Language Models (VLMs) and Vision Language Action (VLA) models introduce common-sense reasoning through large-scale multimodal pretraining, enabling zero-shot performance across tasks. However, these models still lack explicit mechanisms to represent and update belief, limiting their ability to reason like humans or capture the evolving human intent over long-horizon. To address this, we propose a belief-aware VLM framework that integrates retrieval-based memory and reinforcement learning. Instead of learning an explicit belief model, we approximate belief using a vector-based memory that retrieves relevant multimodal context, which is incorporated into the VLM for reasoning. We further refine decision-making using a reinforcement learning policy over the VLM latent space. We evaluate our approach on publicly available VQA datasets such as HD-EPIC and demonstrate consistent improvements over zero-shot baselines, highlighting the importance of belief-aware reasoning.

AI.tldr: They propose a belief-aware vision-language model that uses retrieval-based memory and reinforcement learning to better infer and track human intent, improving VQA performance over zero-shot VLM baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing neural and multimodal (VLM/VLA) models infer intent mainly from instantaneous observable states and large-scale pretraining, but they lack an explicit or even approximate mechanism to maintain and update belief over time. This limits their ability to handle long-horizon, dynamic tasks where human intent evolves and context must be remembered, especially across diverse tasks and environments.
AI.method: They introduce a belief-aware VLM framework that does not learn an explicit probabilistic belief model. Instead, it approximates belief with a vector-based external memory that stores multimodal context (vision, language, action) and uses retrieval to obtain the most relevant past information for the current query. This retrieved context is fed back into the VLM to support reasoning. On top of the VLM latent representations, they train a reinforcement learning policy that refines decision-making, effectively learning how to use the latent space and memory retrieval signals to choose better actions/answers.
AI.result: On publicly available visual question answering datasets like HD-EPIC, their belief-aware VLM consistently outperforms zero-shot VLM/VLA baselines that lack this belief/memory and RL refinement. The gains demonstrate that modeling belief through retrieval-based memory and policy optimization leads to better intent inference and VQA accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating a belief-like external memory and RL policy into VLMs provides a practical way to approximate human-like belief reasoning without explicit probabilistic models. This belief-aware framework enhances the model’s ability to track evolving intent and context over long horizons, yielding consistent improvements on VQA benchmarks and underscoring the value of belief-aware reasoning in multimodal intent inference.

title: LASQ: A Low-resource Aspect-based Sentiment Quadruple Extraction Dataset
summary: In recent years, aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA) has made rapid progress and shown strong practical value. However, existing research and benchmarks are largely concentrated on high-resource languages, leaving fine-grained sentiment extraction in low-resource languages under-explored. To address this gap, we constructed the first Low-resource languages Aspect-based Sentiment Quadruple dataset, named LASQ, which includes two low-resource languages: Uzbek and Uyghur. Secondly, it includes a fine-grained target-aspect-opinion-sentiment quadruple extraction task. To facilitate future research, we designed a grid-tagging model that integrates syntactic knowledge. This model incorporates part-of-speech (POS) and dependency knowledge into the model through our designed Syntax Knowledge Embedding Module (SKEM), thereby alleviating the lexical sparsity problem caused by agglutinative languages. Experiments on LASQ demonstrate consistent gains over competitive baselines, validating both the dataset's utility and the effectiveness of the proposed modeling approach.

AI.tldr: They build the first aspect-based sentiment quadruple dataset for two low-resource languages (Uzbek, Uyghur) and propose a syntax-informed model that performs better than baselines.
AI.motivation: Aspect-based sentiment analysis has mostly focused on high-resource languages, leaving low-resource and agglutinative languages without suitable benchmarks or specialized methods. There is a need for fine-grained sentiment resources and models for these languages to enable real-world applications and research progress.
AI.method: They construct LASQ, a new dataset for Uzbek and Uyghur featuring aspect-based sentiment quadruples (target, aspect, opinion, sentiment). They then propose a grid-tagging extraction model enhanced with syntactic knowledge: a Syntax Knowledge Embedding Module injects POS tags and dependency relations into token representations to mitigate lexical sparsity typical of agglutinative languages.
AI.result: On the new LASQ benchmark, their syntax-enhanced grid-tagging model outperforms strong baseline models consistently, indicating both improved extraction accuracy and robustness in low-resource agglutinative settings.
AI.conclusion: Providing LASQ fills a resource gap for aspect-based sentiment in low-resource languages, and integrating explicit syntactic knowledge via the proposed model is an effective strategy to improve fine-grained sentiment quadruple extraction in these languages.

title: Tipiano: Cascaded Piano Hand Motion Synthesis via Fingertip Priors
summary: Synthesizing realistic piano hand motions requires both precision and naturalness. Physics-based methods achieve precision but produce stiff motions; data-driven models learn natural dynamics but struggle with positional accuracy. Piano motion exhibits a natural hierarchy: fingertip positions are nearly deterministic given piano geometry and fingering, while wrist and intermediate joints offer stylistic freedom. We present [OURS], a four-stage framework exploiting this hierarchy: (1) statistics-based fingertip positioning, (2) FiLM-conditioned trajectory refinement, (3) wrist estimation, and (4) STGCN-based pose synthesis. We contribute expert-annotated fingerings for the FürElise dataset (153 pieces, ~10 hours). Experiments demonstrate F1 = 0.910, substantially outperforming diffusion baselines (F1 = 0.121), with user study (N=41) confirming quality approaching motion capture. Expert evaluation by professional pianists (N=5) identified anticipatory motion as the key remaining gap, providing concrete directions for future improvement.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hierarchical, data-driven framework to synthesize realistic piano hand motions that are both precise on the keys and natural in overall movement, achieving motion quality close to motion capture and far surpassing diffusion baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches to synthesizing piano hand motions face a trade-off: physics-based methods offer precise contact with keys but look stiff and unnatural, whereas data-driven models capture natural dynamics but fail to accurately place fingers on the correct keys. The authors observe that piano performance has a hierarchical structure—fingertip positions are strongly constrained by keyboard geometry and fingering, while the wrist and intermediate joints reflect individual style. Leveraging this structure can potentially resolve the precision–naturalness trade-off and enable controllable, high-quality motion generation from score and fingering information.
AI.method: The authors introduce [OURS], a four-stage hierarchical generation pipeline. First, they use statistics-based fingertip positioning grounded in piano geometry and annotated fingerings to obtain accurate key contacts. Second, they refine these fingertip trajectories using a FiLM-conditioned network that adjusts motion based on conditioning signals (e.g., style, context). Third, they estimate wrist trajectories consistent with the refined fingertips. Fourth, they synthesize full-hand poses with a Spatio-Temporal Graph Convolutional Network (STGCN), modeling temporal dynamics and joint correlations. They also construct and release a dataset for the FürElise piano performance collection (153 pieces, ~10 hours) with expert-annotated fingerings to support training and evaluation.
AI.result: Quantitatively, the method achieves an F1 score of 0.910 for motion-related metrics, dramatically outperforming diffusion-based baselines, which achieve an F1 of only 0.121. User studies with 41 participants indicate that the generated motions are perceived as close in quality to real motion-capture data. Additionally, expert evaluations by five professional pianists provide qualitative insights, highlighting that anticipatory motion—subtle preparatory movements before keystrokes—is the primary remaining deficiency in the synthesized motions.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical [OURS] framework successfully reconciles positional accuracy with natural-looking piano hand motion by decoupling deterministic fingertip placement from more stylistic wrist and joint movement. It delivers substantial improvements over diffusion baselines and approaches the visual quality of motion capture, supported by both quantitative metrics and user studies. Remaining gaps, particularly in modeling anticipatory motion, suggest clear directions for future work, such as incorporating predictive or longer-horizon temporal modeling into the pipeline.

title: Turing or Cantor: That is the Question
summary: Alan Turing is considered as a founder of current computer science together with Kurt Godel, Alonzo Church and John von Neumann. In this paper multiple new research results are presented. It is demonstrated that there would not be Alan Turing's achievements without earlier seminal contributions by Georg Cantor in the set theory and foundations of mathematics. It is proposed to introduce the measure of undecidability of problems unsolvable by Turing machines based on probability distribution of its input data, i.e., to provide the degree of unsolvabilty based on the number of undecidable instances of input data versus decidable ones. It is proposed as well to extend the Turing's work on infinite logics and Oracle machines to a whole class of super-Turing models of computation. Next, the three new complexity classes for TM undecidable problems have been defined: U-complete (Universal complete), D-complete (Diagonalization complete) and H-complete (Hypercomputation complete) classes. The above has never been defined explicitly before by other scientists, and has been inspired by Cook/Levin NP-complete class for intractable problems. Finally, an equivalent to famous P is not equal to NP unanswered question for NP-complete class, has been answered negatively for U-complete class of complexity for undecidable problems.

AI.tldr: The paper links Turing’s work to Cantor’s set theory, introduces a probabilistic measure of undecidability, defines three new undecidable-problem complexity classes (U-, D-, H-complete), and proves an analogue of P≠NP for the U-complete class.
AI.motivation: To clarify the foundational lineage from Cantor to Turing, to quantify degrees of undecidability for problems unsolvable by Turing machines, to systematically extend Turing’s ideas to super-Turing computation, and to structure undecidable problems into complexity-like classes analogous to NP-completeness.
AI.method: Conceptual and theoretical work in computability and complexity theory: (1) historical-foundational analysis relating Cantor’s set theory to Turing’s achievements; (2) formal definition of a probability-based measure over instances of undecidable problems; (3) extension of Turing’s oracle and infinite-logic frameworks to general super-Turing models; (4) introduction and formal characterization of three new completeness classes for undecidable problems; (5) a complexity-theoretic style proof addressing a P vs NP–like question for the U-complete class.
AI.result: The author defines a measure of undecidability derived from the probability distribution over input instances, formalizes three new completeness classes for undecidable problems (U-complete, D-complete, H-complete), and shows that an analogue of the P≠NP question, when posed for U-complete problems, has a negative answer; also positions Cantor’s work as a necessary precursor to Turing’s.
AI.conclusion: Turing’s achievements are deeply rooted in Cantor’s foundational work; undecidability can be graded via a probabilistic measure over instances; super-Turing models provide a natural extension of Turing’s oracle-based ideas; undecidable problems can be organized into new completeness classes analogous to NP-completeness; and within this new framework, the U-complete class admits a resolved counterpart of the open P vs NP question, with the analogue answered negatively.

title: The Myth of Expert Specialization in MoEs: Why Routing Reflects Geometry, Not Necessarily Domain Expertise
summary: Mixture of Experts (MoEs) are now ubiquitous in large language models, yet the mechanisms behind their "expert specialization" remain poorly understood. We show that, since MoE routers are linear maps, hidden state similarity is both necessary and sufficient to explain expert usage similarity, and specialization is therefore an emergent property of the representation space, not of the routing architecture itself. We confirm this at both token and sequence level across five pre-trained models. We additionally prove that load-balancing loss suppresses shared hidden state directions to maintain routing diversity, which might provide a theoretical explanation for specialization collapse under less diverse data, e.g. small batch. Despite this clean mechanistic account, we find that specialization patterns in pre-trained MoEs resist human interpretation: expert overlap between different models answering the same question is no higher than between entirely different questions ($\sim$60\%); prompt-level routing does not predict rollout-level routing; and deeper layers exhibit near-identical expert activation across semantically unrelated inputs, especially in reasoning models. We conclude that, while the efficiency perspective of MoEs is well understood, understanding expert specialization is at least as hard as understanding LLM hidden state geometry, a long-standing open problem in the literature.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how and why experts in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models specialize, and finds that specialization is fundamentally tied to the geometry of hidden representations rather than the routing mechanism itself, while also showing that observed expert patterns are hard for humans to interpret.
AI.motivation: Mixture-of-Experts models are widely used to scale large language models efficiently, but the underlying mechanism of expert specialization—why different experts handle different inputs—is not well understood, limiting our ability to interpret, debug, and improve these systems. The authors aim to provide a mechanistic and theoretical account of how routing decisions emerge and what role data, hidden representations, and load-balancing losses play in shaping expert usage.
AI.method: The authors analyze the routing behavior of MoE layers by leveraging the fact that MoE routers are linear maps from hidden states to expert logits. They study hidden state similarity and its relationship to expert usage similarity at both token and sequence levels across five pre-trained MoE models. They provide theoretical proofs showing that similarity in hidden states is necessary and sufficient for similarity in expert routing, and analyze the effect of the load-balancing loss on shared directions in the hidden space. They then conduct empirical experiments to measure expert overlap across models, prompts, and layers, including reasoning-focused models, to test interpretability and stability of specialization patterns.
AI.result: They demonstrate that expert usage similarity is fully explained by similarity in hidden representations, establishing that expert specialization is an emergent property of the representation geometry, not any special property of the routing architecture. They prove that the load-balancing loss actively suppresses shared directions in the hidden space to enforce routing diversity, which theoretically explains why specialization can collapse in less diverse settings such as small batch sizes. Empirically, they find that expert activation patterns are not easily interpretable: experts selected by different models answering the same question overlap only about as much as for different questions (~60%); routing based on the prompt alone does not predict routing during full rollouts; and deeper layers show nearly identical expert patterns even for semantically unrelated inputs, particularly in reasoning models.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that understanding expert specialization in MoEs reduces to understanding the geometry of LLM hidden states, a challenging open problem, rather than to understanding the routing architecture itself. While MoEs are well-understood from an efficiency and scaling perspective, their interpretability via expert specialization remains elusive: experts do not align cleanly with semantic categories or tasks, and specialization patterns are unstable across models, prompts, and inputs. This implies that progress in interpreting MoE behavior will likely require deeper insight into representation learning and hidden state structure in large language models.

title: CodaRAG: Connecting the Dots with Associativity Inspired by Complementary Learning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with knowledge-intensive tasks due to hallucinations and fragmented reasoning over dispersed information. While Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) grounds generation in external sources, existing methods often treat evidence as isolated units, failing to reconstruct the logical chains that connect these dots. Inspired by Complementary Learning Systems (CLS), we propose CodaRAG, a framework that evolves retrieval from passive lookup into active associative discovery. CodaRAG operates via a three-stage pipeline: (1) Knowledge Consolidation to unify fragmented extractions into a stable memory substrate; (2) Associative Navigation to traverse the graph via multi-dimensional pathways-semantic, contextualized, and functional-explicitly recovering dispersed evidence chains; and (3) Interference Elimination to prune hyper-associative noise, ensuring a coherent, high-precision reasoning context. On GraphRAG-Bench, CodaRAG achieves absolute gains of 7-10% in retrieval recall and 3-11% in generation accuracy. These results demonstrate CodaRAG's superior ability to systematically robustify associative evidence retrieval for factual, reasoning, and creative tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CodaRAG, a Retrieval-Augmented Generation framework that turns retrieval into an active, associative process to better reconstruct evidence chains and reduce hallucinations.
AI.motivation: LLMs often hallucinate and fail on knowledge-intensive tasks because they cannot reliably connect dispersed pieces of information. Standard RAG systems retrieve evidence as separate, unconnected chunks, which limits reasoning over multi-hop, logically linked facts. The authors are motivated to design a retrieval mechanism that can explicitly reconstruct and traverse these evidence chains, making reasoning more robust and human-like.
AI.method: CodaRAG is a three-stage framework inspired by Complementary Learning Systems. (1) Knowledge Consolidation: it aggregates and unifies fragmented extractions from retrieval into a stable structured memory (likely a graph) representing entities, relations, and contexts. (2) Associative Navigation: it actively explores this memory along multiple dimensions—semantic similarity, contextual relations, and functional/role-based links—to recover chains of dispersed evidence rather than independent passages. (3) Interference Elimination: it filters out noisy or spurious associations produced by over-expansive retrieval, maintaining a concise, high-quality context for the LLM to reason over.
AI.result: On the GraphRAG-Bench benchmark, CodaRAG improves retrieval recall by 7–10 percentage points and generation accuracy by 3–11 percentage points compared with baseline RAG methods. These gains are observed across factual, reasoning, and creative tasks, indicating that the framework better supports multi-hop and knowledge-intensive generation.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that evolving RAG from passive lookup to active associative discovery significantly enhances the reliability of LLMs on knowledge-intensive tasks. By consolidating knowledge into a structured memory, navigating via rich associative paths, and pruning interference, CodaRAG more effectively reconstructs evidence chains and improves both retrieval quality and downstream generation performance.

title: Pioneer Agent: Continual Improvement of Small Language Models in Production
summary: Small language models are attractive for production deployment due to their low cost, fast inference, and ease of specialization. However, adapting them to a specific task remains a challenging engineering loop, driven not by training itself but by surrounding decisions: data curation, failure diagnosis, regression avoidance, and iteration control. We present Pioneer Agent, a closed-loop system that automates this lifecycle. In cold-start mode, given only a natural-language task description, the agent acquires data, constructs evaluation sets, and iteratively trains models by jointly optimizing data, hyperparameters, and learning strategy. In production mode, given a deployed model with labeled failures, it diagnoses error patterns, constructs targeted training data, and retrains under explicit regression constraints. To evaluate this setting, we introduce AdaptFT-Bench, a benchmark of synthetic inference logs with progressively increasing noise, designed to test the full adaptation loop: diagnosis, curriculum synthesis, retraining, and verification. Across eight cold-start benchmarks spanning reasoning, math, code generation, summarization, and classification, Pioneer Agent improves over base models by 1.6-83.8 points. On AdaptFT-Bench, it improves or preserves performance in all seven scenarios, while naive retraining degrades by up to 43 points. On two production-style deployments built from public benchmark tasks, it raises intent classification from 84.9% to 99.3% and Entity F1 from 0.345 to 0.810. Beyond performance gains, the agent often discovers effective training strategies, including chain-of-thought supervision, task-specific optimization, and quality-focused data curation, purely from downstream feedback.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Pioneer Agent, an automated closed-loop system for adapting small language models to specific tasks, handling data acquisition, evaluation, training, and regression control end-to-end.
AI.motivation: While small language models are attractive for real-world deployment due to lower cost and easier specialization, the bottleneck is not the fine-tuning itself but the complex, manual engineering loop around it: deciding what data to use, diagnosing model failures, avoiding regressions on existing abilities, and managing iterative improvements. The authors want a system that can automate this full adaptation lifecycle from high-level task descriptions or from real production failures.
AI.method: They design Pioneer Agent, a closed-loop agent that automates both cold-start and production adaptation. In cold-start mode, given only a natural-language description of a task, it automatically: (1) acquires task-relevant data, (2) builds evaluation sets, and (3) iteratively trains models while jointly optimizing over data selection, hyperparameters, and learning strategies. In production mode, starting from a deployed model plus labeled failure logs, it: (1) diagnoses systematic error patterns, (2) synthesizes targeted training curricula, and (3) retrains models under explicit constraints to avoid regressions. To test the setting, they also build AdaptFT-Bench, a benchmark based on synthetic inference logs with controlled noise, covering the full loop from diagnosis through verification.
AI.result: On eight diverse cold-start benchmarks (reasoning, math, code, summarization, classification), Pioneer Agent yields performance gains of 1.6 to 83.8 points over the base small models. On the AdaptFT-Bench production-style scenarios, the agent either improves or at least preserves performance in all seven cases, while naive retraining can cause performance drops of up to 43 points. In two end-to-end production-style deployments derived from public benchmarks, it boosts intent classification accuracy from 84.9% to 99.3% and entity extraction F1 from 0.345 to 0.810.
AI.conclusion: Pioneer Agent can largely automate the adaptation of small language models, reliably improving task performance while controlling regressions. It not only matches or exceeds manual fine-tuning but also autonomously discovers effective training strategies—such as chain-of-thought supervision, task-specific optimization schemes, and selective data curation—using only downstream feedback signals. This suggests that closed-loop, agentic adaptation systems can significantly reduce the human engineering burden in deploying specialized small LMs.

title: Instruction Data Selection via Answer Divergence
summary: Instruction tuning relies on large instruction-response corpora whose quality and composition strongly affect downstream performance. We propose Answer Divergence-Guided Selection (ADG), which selects instruction data based on the geometric structure of multi-sample outputs. ADG draws several high-temperature generations per instruction, maps responses into an embedding space, and computes an output divergence score that jointly encodes dispersion magnitude and shape anisotropy. High scores correspond to instructions whose answers are both far apart and multi-modal, rather than clustered paraphrases along a single direction. Across two backbones and three public instruction pools, fine-tuning on only 10K ADG-selected examples consistently outperforms strong selectors on six benchmarks spanning reasoning, knowledge, and coding.
