Analyses further show that both dispersion magnitude and shape anisotropy are necessary, supporting answer divergence as a practical signal for instruction data selection. Code and appendix are included in the supplementary materials.

AI.tldr: They introduce Answer Divergence-Guided Selection (ADG), a method that picks the most useful instructions for tuning language models by measuring how diverse and multi-modal the model’s own answers are to each instruction.
AI.motivation: Instruction tuning quality depends heavily on which instruction-response pairs are used, but existing selection methods may not effectively capture which instructions elicit rich, informative behaviors from models. The authors aim to find an automatic, model-based signal that identifies instructions that are especially valuable for improving downstream reasoning, knowledge, and coding performance.
AI.method: For each candidate instruction, the model generates several responses using high-temperature sampling. These responses are embedded into a vector space, and the authors compute an output divergence score that combines (1) overall dispersion (how far apart the answers are) and (2) shape anisotropy (whether variability is spread across multiple directions instead of along a single axis). Instructions with high divergence scores—indicating far-apart and multi-modal answers—are selected for fine-tuning.
AI.result: Using only 10,000 instructions selected with ADG from three public instruction pools, and across two different backbone models, fine-tuning consistently outperforms strong baseline selection methods on six benchmarks that cover reasoning, factual knowledge, and code-related tasks.
AI.conclusion: Answer divergence, as captured by both dispersion magnitude and shape anisotropy of multiple generations, is a useful signal for choosing instruction data. ADG-based selection yields more effective instruction-tuning datasets than prior selectors, and ablations show that both components of the divergence metric are necessary. The approach is practical and improves downstream performance with relatively small, carefully chosen subsets of data.

title: Controllable and Verifiable Tool-Use Data Synthesis for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Existing synthetic tool-use corpora are primarily designed for offline supervised fine-tuning, yet reinforcement learning (RL) requires executable environments that support reward-checkable online rollouts. We propose COVERT, a two-stage pipeline that first generates reliable base tool-use trajectories through self-evolving synthesis with multi-level validation, and then applies oracle-preserving augmentations that systematically increase environmental complexity. These augmentations introduce distractor tools, indirect or ambiguous user queries, and noisy, multi-format, or erroneous tool outputs, while strictly preserving oracle tool calls and final answers as ground truth. This design enables automatic reward computation via reference matching for standard cases and lightweight judge-assisted verification for special behaviors such as error detection, supporting RL optimization of tool-calling policies. On Qwen2.5-Instruct-14B, COVERT-RL improves overall accuracy on BFCL v3 from 56.5 to 59.9 and on ACEBench from 53.0 to 59.3, with minimal regressions on general-ability benchmarks; when stacked on SFT, it further reaches 62.1 and 61.8, confirming additive gains. These results suggest that oracle-preserving synthetic environments offer a practical RL refinement stage, complementary to SFT, for improving tool-use robustness under ambiguity and unreliable tool feedback.

AI.tldr: COVERT is a synthetic environment and pipeline for training tool-using language models with reinforcement learning, preserving oracle tool calls while adding realistic complexity to improve robustness.
AI.motivation: Most existing synthetic tool-use datasets are aimed at offline supervised fine-tuning and do not provide executable, reward-checkable environments needed for RL, especially under realistic conditions like ambiguous queries and noisy tools. There is a need for a scalable way to construct such RL-ready environments while maintaining reliable ground truth.
AI.method: They introduce COVERT, a two-stage pipeline: (1) self-evolving synthesis with multi-level validation to generate reliable base tool-use trajectories; (2) oracle-preserving augmentations that systematically increase difficulty by adding distractor tools, making user queries indirect or ambiguous, and corrupting tool outputs with noise or format errors, while strictly keeping the original oracle tool calls and final answers unchanged. This allows automatic reward via reference matching in normal cases and lightweight judge-assisted verification in edge cases like error detection. They then perform RL on tool-calling policies in this synthetic environment.
AI.result: Applying COVERT-based RL to Qwen2.5-Instruct-14B yields performance gains: BFCL v3 accuracy increases from 56.5 to 59.9, and ACEBench from 53.0 to 59.3. When combined with prior supervised fine-tuning, accuracy further improves to 62.1 on BFCL v3 and 61.8 on ACEBench, with only minimal regressions on general-ability benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Oracle-preserving synthetic environments like COVERT provide a practical RL refinement stage on top of SFT, improving robustness of tool-use policies when dealing with ambiguity and unreliable tool feedback. This approach is complementary to supervised fine-tuning and can systematically enhance tool-use performance without extensive manual annotation.

title: NOSE: Neural Olfactory-Semantic Embedding with Tri-Modal Orthogonal Contrastive Learning
comment: Accepted to the ACL 2026 Main Conference
summary: Olfaction lies at the intersection of chemical structure, neural encoding, and linguistic perception, yet existing representation methods fail to fully capture this pathway. Current approaches typically model only isolated segments of the olfactory pathway, overlooking the complete chain from molecule to receptors to linguistic descriptions. Such fragmentation yields learned embeddings that lack both biological grounding and semantic interpretability. We propose NOSE (Neural Olfactory-Semantic Embedding), a representation learning framework that aligns three modalities along the olfactory pathway: molecular structure, receptor sequence, and natural language description. Rather than simply fusing these signals, we decouple their contributions via orthogonal constraints, preserving the unique encoded information of each modality. To address the sparsity of olfactory language, we introduce a weak positive sample strategy to calibrate semantic similarity, preventing erroneous repulsion of similar odors in the feature space. Extensive experiments demonstrate that NOSE achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance and excellent zero-shot generalization, confirming the strong alignment between its representation space and human olfactory intuition.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NOSE, a multimodal representation learning framework for smell that jointly models molecules, olfactory receptors, and language descriptions to create biologically grounded and semantically meaningful odor embeddings.
AI.motivation: Existing olfactory representations treat molecular structure, receptor activity, and linguistic odor descriptions separately, so learned embeddings are either not biologically grounded or lack semantic interpretability. There is a need for a unified representation that follows the full pathway from chemical structure through neural receptors to human language, and that can generalize to new odors in a human-intuitive way.
AI.method: The authors propose NOSE, which learns a shared embedding space for three modalities: molecular structures, olfactory receptor sequences, and natural language odor descriptions. They apply orthogonal constraints so that each modality contributes distinct, non-redundant information instead of being indiscriminately fused. To handle the sparsity and noisiness of olfactory language labels, they design a weak positive sample strategy that treats certain semantically similar descriptions as soft positives, reducing the risk that similar odors are pushed apart during training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show that NOSE outperforms prior methods (SOTA) on multiple tasks related to olfaction and demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization, indicating that the learned embeddings capture meaningful relationships consistent with human perception of smell.
AI.conclusion: Aligning molecules, receptor sequences, and language in a single, carefully regularized embedding space yields olfactory representations that are both biologically grounded and semantically interpretable. The NOSE framework better reflects human olfactory intuition than previous approaches and can be used for tasks involving novel odors without retraining.

title: EE-MCP: Self-Evolving MCP-GUI Agents via Automated Environment Generation and Experience Learning
summary: Computer-use agents that combine GUI interaction with structured API calls via the Model Context Protocol (MCP) show promise for automating software tasks. However, existing approaches lack a principled understanding of how agents should balance these two modalities and how to enable iterative self-improvement across diverse applications. We formulate MCP-GUI interplay as a unified hybrid policy learning problem where the agent learns when each modality provides complementary advantages, and show that distillation and experience augmentation target fundamentally different failure modes - requiring application-aware mechanism selection. Built on this formulation, we propose a self-evolving framework with a fully automatic pipeline that orchestrates automatic environment generation and validation, trajectory collection, gap-driven task synthesis, and quality-filtered training - all without manual intervention. A key innovation is our experience bank, which accumulates LLM-learned rules from trajectory comparison, enabling inference-time improvement without fine-tuning. Systematic \textbf{cross-application analysis} across three desktop applications reveals that the optimal strategy depends on MCP-GUI composition: distillation achieves 77.8\% pass rate on MCP-dominant tasks (+17.8pp), while the experience bank excels on GUI-intensive tasks (+10.0pp).

AI.tldr: The paper presents a hybrid policy learning framework for agents that combine GUI interaction and MCP (API) calls, introducing a self-evolving training pipeline and an experience bank to adaptively choose between modalities and improve performance across applications.
AI.motivation: Current computer-use agents can interact via GUIs and structured APIs (MCP), but there is no principled way to decide when to use which modality, nor a scalable, automated method for agents to iteratively improve across different applications. Existing methods do not clearly separate or address different failure modes that arise from this hybrid setup.
AI.method: The authors formalize MCP-GUI interaction as a unified hybrid policy learning problem where the agent learns modality selection based on their complementary strengths. They analyze two mechanisms—distillation and experience augmentation—showing they address different failure modes and must be chosen in an application-aware manner. They then design a fully automatic self-evolving framework that includes environment generation and validation, trajectory collection, gap-driven task synthesis, and quality-filtered training. A central component is an experience bank that stores LLM-extracted rules from trajectory comparisons, enabling improvements at inference time without additional fine-tuning.
AI.result: Through cross-application experiments on three desktop apps, they find that the best improvement strategy depends on the relative use of MCP vs GUI. Distillation significantly boosts performance on MCP-dominant tasks, achieving a 77.8% pass rate with a 17.8 percentage point gain, while the experience bank approach yields the largest gains on GUI-intensive tasks, improving pass rate by 10 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that effective computer-use agents must treat MCP-GUI interaction as a hybrid policy learning problem and that different mechanisms (distillation vs. experience augmentation) should be selected based on task modality composition. Their self-evolving framework and experience bank enable automatic, continual improvement and inference-time gains without manual intervention or fine-tuning, with empirically validated benefits across multiple desktop applications.

title: EviCare: Enhancing Diagnosis Prediction with Deep Model-Guided Evidence for In-Context Reasoning
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled promising progress in diagnosis prediction from electronic health records (EHRs). However, existing LLM-based approaches tend to overfit to historically observed diagnoses, often overlooking novel yet clinically important conditions that are critical for early intervention. To address this, we propose EviCare, an in-context reasoning framework that integrates deep model guidance into LLM-based diagnosis prediction. Rather than prompting LLMs directly with raw EHR inputs, EviCare performs (1) deep model inference for candidate selection, (2) evidential prioritization for set-based EHRs, and (3) relational evidence construction for novel diagnosis prediction. These signals are then composed into an adaptive in-context prompt to guide LLM reasoning in an accurate and interpretable manner. Extensive experiments on two real-world EHR benchmarks (MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV) demonstrate that EviCare achieves significant performance gains, which consistently outperforms both LLM-only and deep model-only baselines by an average of 20.65\% across precision and accuracy metrics. The improvements are particularly notable in challenging novel diagnosis prediction, yielding average improvements of 30.97\%.

AI.tldr: EviCare is a framework that boosts diagnosis prediction from EHRs by guiding an LLM with structured evidence produced by deep models, improving both overall and novel-diagnosis performance.
AI.motivation: Standard LLM-based diagnosis prediction on EHRs overfits to historically frequent/seen diagnoses and struggles with novel but clinically important conditions, which are crucial for early detection and intervention. The authors want a system that is both accurate and interpretable, and that can better handle novel diagnoses rather than just reproducing past labels.
AI.method: EviCare is an in-context reasoning framework that does not feed raw EHRs directly to an LLM. Instead, it first runs a deep model to select candidate diagnoses. Then it performs evidential prioritization tailored to set-based EHR data to identify and rank the most relevant clinical evidence. Next, it constructs relational evidence aimed at supporting prediction of novel diagnoses. These components are combined into an adaptive in-context prompt that is given to the LLM, guiding its reasoning and predictions in a structured, evidence-driven way.
AI.result: On two real-world EHR benchmarks (MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV), EviCare significantly outperforms both pure LLM and pure deep model baselines, with an average improvement of 20.65% across precision and accuracy metrics. The gains are even larger for the challenging task of novel diagnosis prediction, with average improvements of 30.97%.
AI.conclusion: Integrating deep model guidance and structured evidential prompts into LLM in-context reasoning leads to more accurate and interpretable diagnosis prediction from EHRs, especially for novel diagnoses where traditional approaches underperform. EviCare demonstrates that hybrid deep model + LLM frameworks can meaningfully improve clinical prediction tasks over using either component alone.

title: COMPOSITE-Stem
summary: AI agents hold growing promise for accelerating scientific discovery; yet, a lack of frontier evaluations hinders adoption into real workflows. Expert-written benchmarks have proven effective at measuring AI reasoning, but most at this stage have become saturated and only measure performance on constrained outputs. To help address this gap, we introduce COMPOSITE-STEM, a benchmark of 70 expert-written tasks in physics, biology, chemistry, and mathematics, curated by doctoral-level researchers. Our benchmark combines exact-match grading and criterion-based rubrics with an LLM-as-a-jury grading protocol, allowing more flexible assessment of scientifically meaningful outputs. Using an adapted multimodal Terminus-2 agent harness within the Harbor agentic evaluation framework, we evaluate four frontier models. The top-performing model achieves 21%, demonstrating that COMPOSITE-STEM captures capabilities beyond current agent reach. All tasks are open-sourced with contributor permission to support reproducibility and to promote additional research towards AI's acceleration of scientific progress in these domains.

AI.tldr: COMPOSITE-STEM is a new, difficult benchmark of expert-written STEM tasks to evaluate AI agents’ scientific reasoning in realistic settings, showing that current top models still perform poorly (~21%).
AI.motivation: Existing AI benchmarks for scientific reasoning are mostly saturated and restricted to narrow, constrained-answer formats (e.g., multiple choice or short exact-match outputs). This makes them less informative for assessing whether advanced AI agents can handle complex, realistic scientific tasks that mirror genuine research workflows. There is a need for frontier evaluations that (1) reflect real scientific challenges across STEM disciplines, (2) require richer, more open-ended outputs, and (3) remain unsolved by current models so that progress can be meaningfully tracked.
AI.method: The authors construct COMPOSITE-STEM, a benchmark of 70 expert-authored tasks across physics, biology, chemistry, and mathematics, written and curated by doctoral-level researchers. Tasks are designed to demand substantial reasoning and to allow open-form responses. For evaluation, they combine traditional exact-match scoring (where applicable) with structured, criterion-based rubrics and an “LLM-as-a-jury” protocol: multiple LLMs judge model outputs according to the rubrics, enabling assessment of complex, scientifically meaningful answers. They then use an adapted multimodal Terminus-2 agent harness within the Harbor agentic evaluation framework to test four frontier models on the benchmark, recording performance under this unified agent setup.
AI.result: Across the 70 COMPOSITE-STEM tasks, the best-performing evaluated frontier model attains only about 21% performance under the benchmark’s scoring scheme. This indicates that most tasks remain unsolved or only partially solved by current models. The authors also successfully demonstrate that the rubric-plus-LLM-jury evaluation pipeline can practically grade rich, open-ended scientific outputs at scale. All tasks and materials are released openly with contributor permission to ensure reproducibility and further use by the community.
AI.conclusion: COMPOSITE-STEM provides a challenging, not-yet-saturated benchmark that better reflects realistic scientific reasoning tasks in STEM fields. Its combination of exact-match, rubric-based grading, and LLM-as-a-jury evaluation offers a flexible framework for assessing complex agent behavior. The relatively low scores of current frontier models suggest that there is substantial headroom for improving AI agents’ capabilities in assisting with real-world scientific discovery. By open-sourcing the tasks and evaluation setup, the authors aim to catalyze further research on building and measuring AI systems that can genuinely accelerate scientific progress.

title: Dynamic Adaptive Attention and Supervised Contrastive Learning: A Novel Hybrid Framework for Text Sentiment Classification
summary: The exponential growth of user-generated movie reviews on digital platforms has made accurate text sentiment classification a cornerstone task in natural language processing. Traditional models, including standard BERT and recurrent architectures, frequently struggle to capture long-distance semantic dependencies and resolve ambiguous emotional expressions in lengthy review texts. This paper proposes a novel hybrid framework that seamlessly integrates dynamic adaptive multi-head attention with supervised contrastive learning into a BERT-based Transformer encoder. The dynamic adaptive attention module employs a global context pooling vector to dynamically regulate the contribution of each attention head, thereby focusing on critical sentiment-bearing tokens while suppressing noise. Simultaneously, the supervised contrastive learning branch enforces tighter intra-class compactness and larger inter-class separation in the embedding space. Extensive experiments on the IMDB dataset demonstrate that the proposed model achieves competitive performance with an accuracy of 94.67\%, outperforming strong baselines by 1.5--2.5 percentage points. The framework is lightweight, efficient, and readily extensible to other text classification tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a BERT-based sentiment classifier that enhances attention and representation separation, achieving higher accuracy on IMDB reviews.
AI.motivation: Standard BERT and recurrent models struggle with long movie reviews, particularly in modeling long-range semantic dependencies and disambiguating subtle or mixed sentiments. There is a need for models that better focus on key sentiment tokens and produce more discriminative embeddings for sentiment classes.
AI.method: The authors design a hybrid framework built on a BERT-based Transformer encoder. They add a dynamic adaptive multi-head attention module using a global context pooling vector to adjust the importance of each attention head, emphasizing sentiment-relevant tokens and down-weighting noise. In parallel, they incorporate a supervised contrastive learning branch to structure the embedding space so that samples from the same sentiment class cluster tightly while those from different classes are well separated.
AI.result: On the IMDB movie review dataset, the proposed model attains 94.67% classification accuracy, outperforming strong baselines by about 1.5–2.5 percentage points. The approach is also reported to be lightweight and efficient in practice.
AI.conclusion: Integrating dynamic adaptive multi-head attention with supervised contrastive learning into a BERT backbone improves sentiment classification on long movie reviews. The method provides better focus on sentiment-bearing tokens, yields more discriminative embeddings, and can be easily transferred to other text classification problems.

title: Steered LLM Activations are Non-Surjective
comment: 10 pages main text. ICLR 2026 Workshops (Sci4DL, Re-Align)
summary: Activation steering is a popular white-box control technique that modifies model activations to elicit an abstract change in output behavior. It has also become a standard tool in interpretability (e.g., probing truthfulness, or translating activations into human-readable explanations and safety research (e.g., studying jailbreakability). However, it is unclear whether steered activation states are realizable by any textual prompt. In this work, we cast this question as a surjectivity problem: for a fixed model, does every steered activation admit a pre-image under the model's natural forward pass? Under practical assumptions, we prove that activation steering pushes the residual stream off the manifold of states reachable from discrete prompts. Almost surely, no prompt can reproduce the same internal behavior induced by steering. We also illustrate this finding empirically across three widely used LLMs. Our results establish a formal separation between white-box steerability and black-box prompting. We therefore caution against interpreting the ease and success of activation steering as evidence of prompt-based interpretability or vulnerability, and argue for evaluation protocols that explicitly decouple white-box and black-box interventions.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that activation steering in language models usually creates internal states that no normal text prompt can produce, formally separating white-box control from black-box prompting.
AI.motivation: Activation steering is widely used for controlling and interpreting language models, and for studying safety. People often implicitly assume that if a model can be steered into some activation pattern, then there might be a corresponding textual prompt that produces a similar behavior. The authors question this assumption and ask whether the steering-induced activations are actually realizable by any discrete prompt at all.
AI.method: They formalize the question as a surjectivity problem: given a fixed model, does every steered activation have a pre-image under the model’s standard forward pass from discrete prompts? Under practical assumptions about the model and steering, they give a theoretical argument that steering moves the residual stream off the manifold of activations reachable from any natural prompt. They then empirically test this on three common LLMs, comparing steered activations to those induced by many prompts, to see whether prompts can approximate the steered states.
AI.result: Theoretically, they show that activation steering almost surely produces residual stream states that do not lie on the manifold of activations reachable from discrete prompts, implying that no prompt can exactly reproduce those internal states. Empirically, across three widely used LLMs, they find that even extensive prompt search fails to match the steered activations, supporting the theoretical claim.
AI.conclusion: Activation steering and prompting are fundamentally different kinds of interventions. The success of activation steering does not imply that equivalent behaviors are reachable via prompts, so white-box steerability should not be taken as evidence of black-box vulnerabilities or interpretability. The authors recommend evaluation protocols that explicitly distinguish and decouple white-box activation interventions from black-box prompt-based control or analysis.

title: From Query to Counsel: Structured Reasoning with a Multi-Agent Framework and Dataset for Legal Consultation
summary: Legal consultation question answering (Legal CQA) presents unique challenges compared to traditional legal QA tasks, including the scarcity of high-quality training data, complex task composition, and strong contextual dependencies. To address these, we construct JurisCQAD, a large-scale dataset of over 43,000 real-world Chinese legal queries annotated with expert-validated positive and negative responses, and design a structured task decomposition that converts each query into a legal element graph integrating entities, events, intents, and legal issues. We further propose JurisMA, a modular multi-agent framework supporting dynamic routing, statutory grounding, and stylistic optimization. Combined with the element graph, the framework enables strong context-aware reasoning, effectively capturing dependencies across legal facts, norms, and procedural logic. Trained on JurisCQAD and evaluated on a refined LawBench, our system significantly outperforms both general-purpose and legal-domain LLMs across multiple lexical and semantic metrics, demonstrating the benefits of interpretable decomposition and modular collaboration in Legal CQA.

AI.tldr: They build a large Chinese legal consultation QA dataset and a modular multi-agent reasoning framework using structured legal element graphs, which together greatly improve performance over existing general and legal LLMs.
AI.motivation: Legal consultation questions are harder than traditional legal QA because real user queries are messy, multi-faceted, and context-dependent, and there is a lack of large, high-quality, labeled datasets with realistic interactions. Existing models struggle with complex reasoning over facts, statutes, and procedures while keeping answers interpretable and aligned with legal norms and consultation style.
AI.method: 1) Construct JurisCQAD, a large-scale dataset of 43k+ real Chinese legal consultation queries, each annotated with expert-checked positive and negative answers. 2) Propose a task decomposition that converts each query into a legal element graph capturing entities, events, user intents, and legal issues. 3) Design JurisMA, a modular multi-agent architecture that uses this graph for dynamic routing of subproblems to specialized agents, supports explicit grounding in statutes, and can optimize answer style. The whole system is trained and tuned on JurisCQAD and evaluated on a curated benchmark (LawBench).
AI.result: On the refined LawBench benchmark, the JurisMA system trained on JurisCQAD achieves significantly better performance than both general-purpose and existing legal-domain LLMs, according to multiple lexical (e.g., similarity) and semantic (e.g., meaning/accuracy) evaluation metrics, showing stronger context-aware reasoning and answer quality.
AI.conclusion: Structured decomposition of legal consultation questions into element graphs, combined with a modular multi-agent framework with dynamic routing and statutory grounding, is an effective way to handle the complexity and context dependence of Legal CQA. High-quality, real-world datasets like JurisCQAD enable training such systems and lead to substantial performance gains over baseline LLMs, suggesting a promising direction for interpretable and reliable legal consultation assistants.

title: MEMENTO: Teaching LLMs to Manage Their Own Context
summary: Reasoning models think in long, unstructured streams with no mechanism for compressing or organizing their own intermediate state. We introduce MEMENTO: a method that teaches models to segment reasoning into blocks, compress each block into a memento, i.e., a dense state summary, and reason forward by attending only to mementos, reducing context, KV cache, and compute. To train MEMENTO models, we release OpenMementos, a public dataset of 228K reasoning traces derived from OpenThoughts-v3, segmented and annotated with intermediate summaries. We show that a two-stage SFT recipe on OpenMementos is effective across different model families (Qwen3, Phi-4, Olmo 3) and scales (8B--32B parameters). Trained models maintain strong accuracy on math, science, and coding benchmarks while achieving ${\sim}2.5\times$ peak KV cache reduction. We extend vLLM to support our inference method, achieving ${\sim}1.75\times$ throughput improvement while also enabling us to perform RL and further improve accuracy. Finally, we identify a dual information stream: information from each reasoning block is carried both by the memento text and by the corresponding KV states, which retain implicit information from the original block. Removing this channel drops accuracy by 15\,pp on AIME24.

AI.tldr: Introduces MEMENTO, a method for segmenting and compressing reasoning into dense summaries (mementos) to reduce context and compute while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Reasoning models currently produce long, unstructured reasoning traces and rely on ever-growing context and KV caches, which is inefficient in memory and compute. There is no built-in mechanism for organizing or compressing intermediate reasoning states. The authors aim to make long-chain reasoning more efficient without sacrificing task performance.
AI.method: They propose MEMENTO, where a model segments its reasoning into blocks, compresses each block into a dense textual summary (a memento), and then continues reasoning by attending primarily to these mementos instead of the full raw history. They construct OpenMementos, a dataset of 228K segmented reasoning traces with intermediate summaries, derived from OpenThoughts-v3. Using this, they apply a two-stage supervised fine-tuning recipe across multiple model families (Qwen3, Phi-4, Olmo 3) and sizes (8B–32B). They also extend the vLLM serving stack to support the MEMENTO inference pattern, enabling both efficient inference (reduced KV cache usage) and reinforcement learning fine-tuning on top.
AI.result: Models trained with MEMENTO maintain strong accuracy on math, science, and coding benchmarks while achieving about 2.5× reduction in peak KV cache usage. The extended vLLM implementation yields approximately 1.75× throughput improvement. In ablation, they discover a dual information stream—information is carried by both the memento text and the KV states corresponding to each block. Removing the KV-state channel decreases accuracy by 15 percentage points on AIME24, highlighting its importance.
AI.conclusion: Organizing reasoning into blocks with compressed mementos provides a practical way to significantly reduce memory and compute for long-chain reasoning while preserving accuracy. The dual role of textual mementos and KV-state representations is crucial; both channels are needed for best performance. OpenMementos and the vLLM extensions offer a reusable framework for training and serving efficient reasoning models across architectures and scales.

title: Why Don't You Know? Evaluating the Impact of Uncertainty Sources on Uncertainty Quantification in LLMs
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world applications, reliable uncertainty quantification (UQ) becomes critical for safe and effective use. Most existing UQ approaches for language models aim to produce a single confidence score -- for example, estimating the probability that a model's answer is correct. However, uncertainty in natural language tasks arises from multiple distinct sources, including model knowledge gaps, output variability, and input ambiguity, which have different implications for system behavior and user interaction. In this work, we study how the source of uncertainty impacts the behavior and effectiveness of existing UQ methods. To enable controlled analysis, we introduce a new dataset that explicitly categorizes uncertainty sources, allowing systematic evaluation of UQ performance under each condition. Our experiments reveal that while many UQ methods perform well when uncertainty stems solely from model knowledge limitations, their performance degrades or becomes misleading when other sources are introduced. These findings highlight the need for uncertainty-aware methods that explicitly account for the source of uncertainty in large language models.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how different sources of uncertainty in large language models affect existing uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods, showing that current approaches often fail when uncertainty is not just due to knowledge gaps.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in real applications, we need reliable measures of how uncertain they are. Current UQ methods mostly output a single confidence score, but in natural language tasks uncertainty comes from several qualitatively different sources—like lack of knowledge, randomness in outputs, or ambiguity in inputs. These different sources matter for safety, system design, and user interaction, yet existing methods largely ignore this distinction.
AI.method: The authors construct a new dataset in which examples are explicitly labeled by the source of uncertainty (e.g., model knowledge limitations, output variability, input ambiguity). Using this controlled setting, they systematically evaluate a range of existing UQ methods, comparing their performance across different uncertainty-source conditions.
AI.result: They find that many popular UQ methods work reasonably well when uncertainty is purely due to model knowledge gaps, but their performance drops or becomes misleading when uncertainty also arises from output variability or input ambiguity. Thus, a single undifferentiated confidence score often fails to reflect the true nature of the uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that UQ for LLMs must become source-aware: future methods should explicitly model and distinguish different uncertainty sources rather than collapsing them into a single scalar confidence. This is necessary to ensure safe, interpretable, and effective deployment of LLMs in real-world settings.

title: Instructing LLMs to Negotiate using Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
summary: The recent advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has established their potential as autonomous interactive agents. However, they often struggle in strategic games of incomplete information, such as bilateral price negotiation. In this paper, we investigate if Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) can effectively teach LLMs to negotiate. Specifically, we explore the strategic behaviors that emerge during the learning process. We introduce a framework that trains a mid-sized buyer agent against a regulated LLM seller across a wide distribution of real-world products. By grounding reward signals directly in the maximization of economic surplus and strict adherence to private budget constraints, we reveal a novel four-phase strategic evolution. The agent progresses from naive bargaining to using aggressive starting prices, moves through a phase of deadlock, and ultimately develops sophisticated persuasive skills. Our results demonstrate that this verifiable training allows a 30B agent to significantly outperform frontier models over ten times its size in extracting surplus. Furthermore, the trained agent generalizes robustly to stronger counterparties unseen during training and remains effective even when facing hostile, adversarial seller personas.

AI.tldr: They train a mid-sized LLM as a negotiating agent via Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards so it can outperform much larger frontier models on price negotiations.
AI.motivation: LLMs are promising as autonomous agents but perform poorly in strategic, incomplete-information settings like bilateral price negotiations; the authors want a principled way to teach them effective negotiation strategies.
AI.method: They build a negotiation framework where a buyer LLM (mid-sized) learns via Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) while negotiating with a regulated LLM seller over many real-world products; rewards are grounded in maximizing economic surplus while respecting strict private budget constraints, and they analyze the emergent strategies over training.
AI.result: Training with verifiable, economically grounded rewards yields a clear four-phase evolution of strategy—from naive bargaining to aggressive opening offers, through a deadlock phase, and finally to sophisticated persuasion—and the resulting 30B-parameter agent extracts more surplus than frontier models more than 10x larger, generalizing well to stronger and even adversarial counterparties not seen in training.
AI.conclusion: Grounding reinforcement learning in verifiable economic objectives enables mid-sized LLM agents to acquire advanced, generalizable negotiation strategies that can surpass far larger models in surplus extraction, demonstrating RLVR’s effectiveness for strategic, incomplete-information tasks.

title: Structure-Grounded Knowledge Retrieval via Code Dependencies for Multi-Step Data Reasoning
summary: Selecting the right knowledge is critical when using large language models (LLMs) to solve domain-specific data analysis tasks. However, most retrieval-augmented approaches rely primarily on lexical or embedding similarity, which is often a weak proxy for the task-critical knowledge needed for multi-step reasoning. In many such tasks, the relevant knowledge is not merely textually related to the query, but is instead grounded in executable code and the dependency structure through which computations are carried out. To address this mismatch, we propose SGKR (Structure-Grounded Knowledge Retrieval), a retrieval framework that organizes domain knowledge with a graph induced by function-call dependencies. Given a question, SGKR extracts semantic input and output tags, identifies dependency paths connecting them, and constructs a task-relevant subgraph. The associated knowledge and corresponding function implementations are then assembled as a structured context for LLM-based code generation. Experiments on multi-step data analysis benchmarks show that SGKR consistently improves solution correctness over no-retrieval and similarity-based retrieval baselines for both vanilla LLMs and coding agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SGKR, a retrieval framework that uses function-call dependency graphs rather than pure text similarity to select knowledge for LLM-based code generation in domain-specific, multi-step data analysis tasks, improving correctness over standard retrieval baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing retrieval-augmented methods for LLMs mostly rely on lexical or embedding similarity, which often fails to surface the task-critical, procedural knowledge needed for multi-step data analysis. In such domains, the true relevance of knowledge is determined by how computations are structured in code (e.g., function-call dependencies) rather than by textual similarity to the query. The paper is motivated by this mismatch and aims to better align retrieval with the executable structure that underlies real analytical workflows.
AI.method: They propose SGKR (Structure-Grounded Knowledge Retrieval), which represents domain knowledge as a graph induced by function-call dependencies. For a given question, SGKR (1) extracts semantic input and output tags from the query, (2) finds dependency paths in the function-call graph that connect these inputs and outputs, (3) constructs a task-relevant subgraph along those paths, and (4) assembles the associated documentation and function implementations into a structured context. This context is then fed to an LLM to guide code generation or agent behavior.
AI.result: On multi-step data analysis benchmarks, SGKR yields higher solution correctness than both no-retrieval and standard similarity-based retrieval approaches, and this improvement holds for plain LLMs as well as for coding agents. The abstract indicates consistent gains but does not provide exact quantitative metrics.
AI.conclusion: Grounding retrieval in the structure of executable code—specifically, function-call dependency graphs—enables more effective selection of task-relevant knowledge for complex data analysis problems than purely similarity-based methods. SGKR thus offers a more faithful way to bridge domain workflows and LLM reasoning, improving performance across different LLM-based coding settings.

title: Evolutionary Token-Level Prompt Optimization for Diffusion Models
summary: Text-to-image diffusion models exhibit strong generative performance but remain highly sensitive to prompt formulation, often requiring extensive manual trial and error to obtain satisfactory results. This motivates the development of automated, model-agnostic prompt optimization methods that can systematically explore the conditioning space beyond conventional text rewriting. This work investigates the use of a Genetic Algorithm (GA) for prompt optimization by directly evolving the token vectors employed by CLIP-based diffusion models. The GA optimizes a fitness function that combines aesthetic quality, measured by the LAION Aesthetic Predictor V2, with prompt-image alignment, assessed via CLIPScore. Experiments on 36 prompts from the Parti Prompts (P2) dataset show that the proposed approach outperforms the baseline methods, including Promptist and random search, achieving up to a 23.93% improvement in fitness. Overall, the method is adaptable to image generation models with tokenized text encoders and provides a modular framework for future extensions, the limitations and prospects of which are discussed.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a genetic-algorithm-based, model-agnostic method to optimize prompts for text-to-image diffusion models by evolving CLIP token vectors, improving aesthetic quality and text-image alignment over existing baselines.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image diffusion models are powerful but very sensitive to how prompts are written, often requiring many manual prompt revisions. Existing prompt optimization mostly focuses on rewriting text, which may not fully exploit the model’s conditioning space. The authors aim to create an automated, systematic, and model-agnostic way to optimize prompts beyond simple text edits, directly at the token-embedding level, to improve image quality and relevance with minimal human effort.
AI.method: They use a Genetic Algorithm to evolve the token vectors used as conditioning inputs in CLIP-based diffusion models. Each individual in the population is a sequence of token embeddings. A fitness function guides evolution, combining an aesthetic quality score from the LAION Aesthetic Predictor V2 and a prompt-image alignment score via CLIPScore. The GA iteratively applies selection, crossover, and mutation on token vectors to maximize this fitness. The approach is designed to be modular and applicable to any image generator that uses tokenized text encoders.
AI.result: On 36 prompts from the Parti Prompts (P2) dataset, the proposed GA-based prompt optimizer outperforms baselines including Promptist and random search. It achieves up to a 23.93% improvement in the combined fitness metric that measures both aesthetic appeal and alignment between the prompt and the generated image.
AI.conclusion: The GA-based optimization of token embeddings is an effective, model-agnostic way to improve text-to-image generation quality and alignment, surpassing existing prompt optimization and search baselines. The framework is modular and can be extended or adapted to different diffusion models with tokenized text encoders. The authors acknowledge current limitations and outline directions for future work to enhance robustness, efficiency, and generality.

title: ReFEree: Reference-Free and Fine-Grained Method for Evaluating Factual Consistency in Real-World Code Summarization
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) have become capable of generating long and descriptive code summaries, accurate and reliable evaluation of factual consistency has become a critical challenge. However, previous evaluation methods are primarily designed for short summaries of isolated code snippets. Consequently, they struggle to provide fine-grained evaluation of multi-sentence functionalities and fail to accurately assess dependency context commonly found in real-world code summaries. To address this, we propose ReFEree, a reference-free and fine-grained method for evaluating factual consistency in real-world code summaries. We define factual inconsistency criteria specific to code summaries and evaluate them at the segment level using these criteria along with dependency information. These segment-level results are then aggregated into a fine-grained score. We construct a code summarization benchmark with human-annotated factual consistency labels. The evaluation results demonstrate that ReFEree achieves the highest correlation with human judgment among 13 baselines, improving 15-18% over the previous state-of-the-art. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/bsy99615/ReFEree.git.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ReFEree, a reference-free, fine-grained evaluator of factual consistency for long, real-world code summaries generated by LLMs, achieving substantially better correlation with human judgments than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing automatic evaluators of code summaries are designed for short, isolated snippets and cannot reliably judge factual consistency in long, multi-sentence summaries with cross-sentence dependencies, which is increasingly important as LLMs generate more complex code documentation.
AI.method: The authors define code-specific factual inconsistency criteria and break summaries into segments. Using these criteria plus dependency information among segments, they judge consistency at the segment level and then aggregate these judgments into an overall fine-grained score, all without requiring gold reference summaries.
AI.result: On a newly constructed benchmark of real-world code summaries with human-annotated factual consistency labels, their method ReFEree shows the highest correlation with human judgments compared to 13 baseline metrics, with a 15–18% improvement over the previous state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: ReFEree provides a more accurate, reference-free and fine-grained way to evaluate factual consistency of real-world code summaries, better matching human assessments and addressing limitations of existing metrics for long, dependency-rich summaries.

title: What do your logits know? (The answer may surprise you!)
summary: Recent work has shown that probing model internals can reveal a wealth of information not apparent from the model generations. This poses the risk of unintentional or malicious information leakage, where model users are able to learn information that the model owner assumed was inaccessible. Using vision-language models as a testbed, we present the first systematic comparison of information retained at different "representational levels'' as it is compressed from the rich information encoded in the residual stream through two natural bottlenecks: low-dimensional projections of the residual stream obtained using tuned lens, and the final top-k logits most likely to impact model's answer. We show that even easily accessible bottlenecks defined by the model's top logit values can leak task-irrelevant information present in an image-based query, in some cases revealing as much information as direct projections of the full residual stream.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how much hidden information about an input can be extracted from different internal representations of vision-language models, showing that even the final top logits can leak substantial, task-irrelevant information.
AI.motivation: As models grow more capable, their internal representations may contain sensitive or unintended information about inputs that is not obvious from normal outputs, creating a risk of information leakage when these internals are probed. The authors want to understand and quantify what information is retained at different stages inside vision-language models, and how much can leak through easily accessible channels.
AI.method: Using vision-language models, the authors compare information content at several representational levels as data flows from the rich residual stream to two bottlenecks: (1) low-dimensional projections of the residual stream obtained via tuned lens, and (2) the model’s final top-k logits. They systematically measure and compare how much task-irrelevant information about an image-based query can be recovered from each bottleneck.
AI.result: They find that even the apparently narrow and accessible bottleneck of top logit values can still encode and leak significant task-irrelevant information about the input image. In some cases, the information recoverable from top-k logits is comparable to what can be extracted from direct low-dimensional projections of the full residual stream.
AI.conclusion: Internal bottlenecks such as top-k logits, which might appear safe or minimally informative, can in fact carry substantial latent information about inputs, including task-irrelevant details. This highlights a practical privacy and security concern: probing or exposing such representations in vision-language models can unintentionally leak as much information as more direct access to the model’s hidden states.

title: Lost in Diffusion: Uncovering Hallucination Patterns and Failure Modes in Diffusion Large Language Models
summary: While Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) have emerged as a promising non-autoregressive paradigm comparable to autoregressive (AR) models, their faithfulness, specifically regarding hallucination, remains largely underexplored. To bridge this gap, we present the first controlled comparative study to evaluate hallucination patterns in dLLMs. Our results demonstrate that current dLLMs exhibit a higher propensity for hallucination than AR counterparts controlled for architecture, scale, and pre-training weights. Furthermore, an analysis of inference-time compute reveals divergent dynamics: while quasi-autoregressive generation suffers from early saturation, non-sequential decoding unlocks potential for continuous refinement. Finally, we identify distinct failure modes unique to the diffusion process, including premature termination, incomplete denoising, and context intrusion. Our findings underscore that although dLLMs have narrowed the performance gap on general tasks, their distinct hallucination mechanisms pose a critical challenge to model reliability. Our code is available at https://github.com/ZeroLoss-Lab/Lost-in-Diffusion

AI.tldr: This paper compares hallucination behaviors in diffusion-based LLMs (dLLMs) versus standard autoregressive (AR) LLMs and finds dLLMs hallucinate more and in different ways.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs offer a non-autoregressive alternative to AR models and are becoming competitive on many tasks, but little is known about how faithful their outputs are, especially regarding hallucinations, which are critical for reliability in real applications.
AI.method: The authors run a controlled comparative study where dLLMs and AR models are matched on architecture, scale, and pre-training weights. They systematically evaluate hallucination rates and patterns, and analyze inference-time compute behavior and generation dynamics. They also qualitatively and quantitatively inspect specific failure modes during the diffusion decoding process.
AI.result: They find that, under controlled conditions, current dLLMs hallucinate more often than comparable AR models. Inference-time analysis shows quasi-autoregressive diffusion generation saturates early, while fully non-sequential decoding allows ongoing refinement. They also discover failure modes unique to diffusion decoding, such as premature termination, incomplete denoising, and context intrusion.
AI.conclusion: Even though diffusion LLMs are closing the gap with AR models on standard benchmarks, they currently have systematically higher hallucination rates and distinct diffusion-specific failure modes, which pose a serious reliability challenge. Understanding and mitigating these mechanisms is necessary before dLLMs can be trusted in high-stakes or factuality-critical settings.

title: In-situ process monitoring for defect detection in wire-arc additive manufacturing: an agentic AI approach
summary: AI agents are being increasingly deployed across a wide range of real-world applications. In this paper, we propose an agentic AI framework for in-situ process monitoring for defect detection in wire-arc additive manufacturing (WAAM). The autonomous agent leverages a WAAM process monitoring dataset and a trained classification tool to build AI agents and uses a large language model (LLM) for in-situ process monitoring decision-making for defect detection. A processing agent is developed based on welder process signals, such as current and voltage, and a monitoring agent is developed based on acoustic data collected during the process. Both agents are tasked with identifying porosity defects from processing and monitoring signals, respectively. Ground truth X-ray computed tomography (XCT) data are used to develop classification tools for both the processing and monitoring agents. Furthermore, a multi-agent framework is demonstrated in which the processing and monitoring agents are orchestrated together for parallel decision-making on the given task of defect classification. Evaluation metrics are proposed to determine the efficacy of both individual agents, the combined single-agent, and the coordinated multi-agent system. The multi-agent configuration outperforms all individual-agent counterparts, achieving a decision accuracy of 91.6% and an F1 score of 0.821 on decided runs, across 15 independent runs, and a reasoning quality score of 3.74 out of 5. These in-situ process monitoring agents hold significant potential for autonomous real-time process monitoring and control toward building qualified parts for WAAM and other additive manufacturing processes.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an agentic AI, multi-agent framework that uses process and acoustic signals plus an LLM to detect porosity defects in wire-arc additive manufacturing in real time, with multi-agent coordination outperforming single agents.
AI.motivation: Wire-arc additive manufacturing can suffer from internal defects such as porosity, and ensuring part quality requires effective real-time, in-situ monitoring and decision-making, which current methods struggle to do autonomously and robustly. There is a need for intelligent, explainable, and high-performing monitoring systems that can fuse multiple sensor modalities and support autonomous process control.
AI.method: The authors develop an agentic AI framework consisting of distinct agents that use different sensing modalities and tools. A processing agent operates on welder process signals (current, voltage) and a monitoring agent operates on acoustic data gathered during WAAM. Ground-truth defect labels from X-ray computed tomography are used to train classification tools for each agent to detect porosity. A large language model is integrated to support in-situ decision-making and reasoning. They further orchestrate the processing and monitoring agents into a coordinated multi-agent system that makes parallel decisions on defect classification. They define and apply evaluation metrics to compare single agents, a combined single-agent, and the multi-agent configuration over multiple independent runs.
AI.result: Both individual agents can detect porosity defects from their respective signal types, but the multi-agent orchestration yields the best performance. The multi-agent setup achieves 91.6% decision accuracy and an F1 score of 0.821 on the runs where it makes decisions, averaged over 15 independent runs, and obtains a reasoning quality score of 3.74 out of 5. These results indicate that multi-agent coordination plus LLM-based reasoning improves defect detection performance over any single agent alone.
AI.conclusion: The proposed agentic AI, multi-agent framework is effective for in-situ defect detection in wire-arc additive manufacturing, outperforming single-agent approaches in both accuracy and F1 score while providing interpretable reasoning via an LLM. The work suggests that coordinated, multimodal AI agents can enable autonomous real-time monitoring and potentially closed-loop control to ensure qualified parts in WAAM and could generalize to other additive manufacturing processes.

title: LLMs Should Incorporate Explicit Mechanisms for Human Empathy
summary: This paper argues that Large Language Models (LLMs) should incorporate explicit mechanisms for human empathy. As LLMs become increasingly deployed in high-stakes human-centered settings, their success depends not only on correctness or fluency but on faithful preservation of human perspectives. Yet, current LLMs systematically fail at this requirement: even when well-aligned and policy-compliant, they often attenuate affect, misrepresent contextual salience, and rigidify relational stance in ways that distort meaning. We formalize empathy as an observable behavioral property: the capacity to model and respond to human perspectives while preserving intention, affect, and context. Under this framing, we identify four recurring mechanisms of empathic failure in contemporary LLMs--sentiment attenuation, empathic granularity mismatch, conflict avoidance, and linguistic distancing--arising as structural consequences of prevailing training and alignment practices. We further organize these failures along three dimensions: cognitive, cultural, and relational empathy, to explain their manifestation across tasks. Empirical analyses show that strong benchmark performance can mask systematic empathic distortions, motivating empathy-aware objectives, benchmarks, and training signals as first-class components of LLM development.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that LLMs need explicit, testable mechanisms for empathy and shows that current models systematically distort human perspectives, even when they appear well-aligned.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in high-stakes, human-centered contexts, simple correctness or policy compliance is not enough; models must preserve human intention, affect, and context. However, current systems often alter or mute emotional content, mishandle contextual importance, and adopt rigid or distancing stances, which can be harmful or misleading. The authors want to rigorously define and diagnose these failures to guide better model design.
AI.method: They first formalize empathy as a behavioral property: an LLM’s capacity to model and respond to human perspectives while preserving intention, affect, and context. Using this definition, they theorize and categorize four mechanisms of empathic failure—sentiment attenuation, empathic granularity mismatch, conflict avoidance, and linguistic distancing—and map these onto three dimensions of empathy: cognitive, cultural, and relational. They then conduct empirical analyses showing that models can perform well on standard benchmarks yet still exhibit systematic empathic distortions across tasks.
AI.result: The analysis identifies four recurring structural failure modes in contemporary LLMs that systematically distort human perspectives: (1) sentiment attenuation (muting or smoothing affect), (2) empathic granularity mismatch (responding at the wrong emotional or contextual level), (3) conflict avoidance (dodging or diluting contentious but salient perspectives), and (4) linguistic distancing (using language that distances the model from the speaker’s stance). These failures appear even in models that score highly on conventional benchmarks and alignment tests.
AI.conclusion: Empathy in LLMs should be treated as a distinct, observable behavioral capability rather than an incidental byproduct of general alignment. Because current training and alignment practices structurally induce specific empathic failures, developers need empathy-aware training objectives, benchmarks, and feedback signals as first-class design targets. Without these, strong benchmark performance will continue to hide systematic distortions of human perspectives in high-stakes applications.

title: GLEaN: A Text-to-image Bias Detection Approach for Public Comprehension
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) models, and their encoded biases, increasingly shape the visual media the public encounters. While researchers have produced a rich body of work on bias measurement, auditing, and mitigation in T2I systems, those methods largely target technical stakeholders, leaving a gap in public legibility. We introduce GLEaN (Generative Likeness Evaluation at N-Scale), a portrait-based explainability pipeline designed to make T2I model biases visually understandable to a broad audience. GLEaN comprises three stages: automated large-scale image generation from identity prompts, facial landmark-based filtering and spatial alignment, and median-pixel composition that distills a model's central tendency into a single representative portrait. The resulting composites require no statistical background to interpret; a viewer can see, at a glance, who a model 'imagines' when prompted with 'a doctor' versus a 'felon.' We demonstrate GLEaN on Stable Diffusion XL across 40 social and occupational identity prompts, producing composites that reproduce documented biases and surface new associations between skin tone and predicted emotion. We find in a between-subjects user study (N = 291) that GLEaN portraits communicate biases as effectively as conventional data tables, but require significantly less viewing time. Because the method relies solely on generated outputs, it can also be replicated on any black-box and closed-weight systems without access to model internals. GLEaN offers a scalable, model-agnostic approach to bias explainability, purpose-built for public comprehension, and is publicly available at https://github.com/cultureiolab/GLEaN.

AI.tldr: The paper presents GLEaN, a visual, portrait-based pipeline to reveal and explain biases in text-to-image (T2I) models in a way that is easily understandable by the general public.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for detecting and explaining bias in T2I models are mainly technical and aimed at expert audiences, making it hard for the public to grasp how these systems may be biased. There is a need for an intuitive, visual tool that reveals who these models tend to depict for various social and occupational identities, without requiring statistical expertise or access to model internals.
AI.method: GLEaN is a three-stage pipeline: (1) automatically generate large numbers of images from identity-related text prompts using a T2I model; (2) filter and spatially align faces using facial landmarks to ensure consistency; (3) create a median-pixel composite image that captures the central visual tendency for each prompt. These composite portraits are then used to visually compare how the model depicts different identities, and the method is applied to Stable Diffusion XL with 40 identity prompts. A user study evaluates how effectively these portraits communicate bias compared to standard tables.
AI.result: Using Stable Diffusion XL, GLEaN’s composite portraits reproduce previously documented biases and uncover new associations, such as links between skin tone and model-predicted emotion. In a between-subjects study with 291 participants, GLEaN composites communicate bias information about as well as conventional data tables, yet viewers need significantly less time to interpret them.
AI.conclusion: GLEaN provides an effective, scalable, and model-agnostic way to visually explain biases in T2I systems, designed specifically for public understanding. Because it only needs generated outputs, it can be applied to black-box or closed-weight models. The tool offers a practical route to making generative model biases more transparent and is released publicly for broader use.

title: Early Decisions Matter: Proximity Bias and Initial Trajectory Shaping in Non-Autoregressive Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion-based language models (dLLMs) have emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive language models, offering the potential for parallel token generation and bidirectional context modeling. However, harnessing this flexibility for fully non-autoregressive decoding remains an open question, particularly for reasoning and planning tasks. In this work, we investigate non-autoregressive decoding in dLLMs by systematically analyzing its inference dynamics along the temporal axis. Specifically, we uncover an inherent failure mode in confidence-based non-autoregressive generation stemming from a strong proximity bias-the tendency for the denoising order to concentrate on spatially adjacent tokens. This local dependency leads to spatial error propagation, rendering the entire trajectory critically contingent on the initial unmasking position. Leveraging this insight, we present a minimal-intervention approach that guides early token selection, employing a lightweight planner and end-of-sequence temperature annealing. We thoroughly evaluate our method on various reasoning and planning tasks and observe substantial overall improvement over existing heuristic baselines without significant computational overhead.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and fixes a key failure mode in non-autoregressive diffusion-based language models by lightly guiding early token selection, improving reasoning and planning without extra compute.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based language models can, in principle, generate tokens in parallel and use bidirectional context, which is appealing for fast and flexible text generation. Yet, how to exploit this ability for fully non-autoregressive decoding, especially on challenging reasoning and planning tasks, is unclear. Existing non-autoregressive methods rely heavily on confidence heuristics and may not work robustly, motivating a deeper analysis of their inference behavior and failure modes.
AI.method: The authors study inference dynamics of diffusion-based LMs along the temporal denoising steps. They analyze confidence-based non-autoregressive decoding and discover a proximity bias: the denoising order clusters on nearby tokens, creating strong local dependencies and spatial error propagation tied to where denoising starts. Based on this analysis, they propose a minimal-intervention decoding scheme that only tweaks early token selection. They introduce a lightweight planner that helps choose better initial tokens to unmask and apply end-of-sequence temperature annealing to control sequence termination, while leaving the rest of the diffusion inference largely unchanged.
AI.result: Across multiple reasoning and planning benchmarks, the proposed guided decoding approach substantially outperforms heuristic confidence-based non-autoregressive baselines, improving solution quality while adding little or no significant computational overhead. Empirical results support the claim that better early token selection alleviates proximity-bias-induced error cascades in dLLMs.
AI.conclusion: Non-autoregressive decoding in diffusion-based LMs is hampered by a proximity bias that causes local error propagation and makes outputs highly sensitive to the initial unmasking position. By lightly guiding the early denoising steps with a planner and EOS temperature annealing, one can significantly improve reasoning and planning performance without sacrificing the computational advantages of non-autoregressive diffusion decoding.

title: HealthAdminBench: Evaluating Computer-Use Agents on Healthcare Administration Tasks
comment: 24 pages, 5 figures, 5 tables. Benchmark paper introducing 4 simulated environments, 135 tasks, and 1,698 evaluation points for healthcare administrative computer-use agents
summary: Healthcare administration accounts for over $1 trillion in annual spending, making it a promising target for LLM-based computer-use agents (CUAs). While clinical applications of LLMs have received significant attention, no benchmark exists for evaluating CUAs on end-to-end administrative workflows. To address this gap, we introduce HealthAdminBench, a benchmark comprising four realistic GUI environments: an EHR, two payer portals, and a fax system, and 135 expert-defined tasks spanning three administrative task types: Prior Authorization, Appeals and Denials Management, and Durable Medical Equipment (DME) Order Processing. Each task is decomposed into fine-grained, verifiable subtasks, yielding 1,698 evaluation points. We evaluate seven agent configurations under multiple prompting and observation settings and find that, despite strong subtask performance, end-to-end reliability remains low: the best-performing agent (Claude Opus 4.6 CUA) achieves only 36.3 percent task success, while GPT-5.4 CUA attains the highest subtask success rate (82.8 percent). These results reveal a substantial gap between current agent capabilities and the demands of real-world administrative workflows. HealthAdminBench provides a rigorous foundation for evaluating progress toward safe and reliable automation of healthcare administrative workflows.

AI.tldr: Introduces HealthAdminBench, a benchmark to evaluate LLM-based computer-use agents on realistic healthcare administrative workflows, showing current agents remain unreliable for end-to-end tasks.
AI.motivation: Healthcare administration is extremely costly (over $1 trillion annually), making it an attractive domain for automation with LLM-based agents that can operate software user interfaces. However, while many benchmarks assess LLMs for clinical reasoning or text tasks, there is no standardized way to measure how well agents handle complete administrative workflows across real-like GUIs, which are critical for safe deployment.
AI.method: The authors build HealthAdminBench, a benchmark with four realistic graphical user interface environments (an EHR, two payer portals, and a fax system). They design 135 expert-defined tasks across three core administrative categories: prior authorization, appeals and denials management, and durable medical equipment order processing. Each task is decomposed into fine-grained, objectively verifiable subtasks, yielding 1,698 evaluation points. They then evaluate seven different LLM-based computer-use agent configurations under varying prompting and observation conditions, measuring both task-level and subtask-level success rates.
AI.result: Despite some strong performance on individual subtasks (with the best subtask success rate at 82.8 percent from a GPT-5.4-based agent), overall end-to-end task completion remains poor. The best agent in terms of full task success, Claude Opus 4.6 CUA, completes only 36.3 percent of tasks, highlighting a large gap between current agent capabilities and what is needed for dependable automation.
AI.conclusion: HealthAdminBench exposes that current LLM-based computer-use agents are not yet reliable enough for robust automation of healthcare administrative workflows, particularly at the full task level. The benchmark offers a structured, fine-grained, and realistic evaluation framework that can be used by the community to measure progress, compare approaches, and guide research toward safer and more effective administrative automation in healthcare.

title: Knowing What to Stress: A Discourse-Conditioned Text-to-Speech Benchmark
summary: Spoken meaning often depends not only on what is said, but also on which word is emphasized. The same sentence can convey correction, contrast, or clarification depending on where emphasis falls. Although modern text-to-speech (TTS) systems generate expressive speech, it remains unclear whether they infer contextually appropriate stress from discourse alone. To address this gap, we present Context-Aware Stress TTS (CAST), a benchmark for evaluating context-conditioned word-level stress in TTS. Items are defined as contrastive context pairs: identical sentences paired with distinct contexts requiring different stressed words. We evaluate state-of-the-art systems and find a consistent gap: text-only language models reliably recover the intended stress from context, yet TTS systems frequently fail to realize it in speech. We release the benchmark, evaluation framework, construction pipeline and a synthetic corpus to support future work on context-aware speech synthesis.

AI.tldr: They introduce CAST, a benchmark to test whether TTS systems place word stress correctly based on discourse context, and show current systems often fail where text-only LMs succeed.
AI.motivation: Meaning in spoken language depends heavily on which word is prosodically stressed, but it is unclear if modern TTS systems can infer and realize the contextually appropriate stressed word using only text and discourse context. There is no standard way to evaluate this ability.
AI.method: They construct a benchmark called CAST composed of contrastive context pairs: the same target sentence appears after different preceding contexts that require different words in the target sentence to be stressed (e.g., for correction, contrast, clarification). They then evaluate state-of-the-art TTS systems on this benchmark and compare their performance to text-only language models in recovering the intended stress given the context.
AI.result: Text-only language models can reliably infer which word should be stressed from context, but current TTS systems often fail to realize the correct stress pattern in generated speech, revealing a systematic gap between contextual understanding and prosodic realization in speech synthesis.
AI.conclusion: There is a substantial mismatch between the discourse-level contextual understanding achievable by language models and the prosodic expressiveness of current TTS systems. CAST provides a systematic, publicly released benchmark, evaluation framework, construction pipeline, and synthetic corpus to drive future research on context-aware TTS that correctly encodes word-level stress in speech.

title: New Hybrid Fine-Tuning Paradigm for LLMs: Algorithm Design and Convergence Analysis Framework
summary: Fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs) typically involves either full fine-tuning, which updates all model parameters, or Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT), which adjusts a small subset of parameters. However, both approaches have inherent limitations: full fine-tuning is computationally expensive, while PEFT often struggles to learn new knowledge and exhibits suboptimal performance. To overcome these issues, we propose a novel hybrid fine-tuning approach that jointly updates both LLMs and PEFT modules using a combination of zeroth-order and first-order optimization methods. To analyze our new algorithm, we develop a theoretical framework centered on the concept of hybrid smoothness condition, which accounts for the heterogeneous nature of the optimization landscape in joint LLM and PEFT training. We derive a rigorous convergence analysis for the convergence of reshuffling-type SGD algorithm under multiple learning rates and demonstrate its effectiveness through extensive empirical studies across various downstream tasks and model architectures. On the practical side, our results demonstrate consistent performance improvement, making the approach a viable solution for large-scale language model fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: They propose a hybrid fine-tuning method for LLMs that updates both base model and PEFT modules using a mix of zeroth-order and first-order optimization, with theory and experiments showing better convergence and downstream performance.
AI.motivation: Full fine-tuning of LLMs is very expensive, while PEFT methods, though cheaper, often cannot effectively learn new knowledge and underperform in quality. There is a need for a method that keeps the efficiency benefits of PEFT but achieves performance closer to or better than full fine-tuning, and that has solid theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: Introduce a hybrid fine-tuning scheme that jointly updates the full LLM parameters and the PEFT parameters. The optimization combines zeroth-order and first-order methods under a new theoretical concept called the hybrid smoothness condition, which models the heterogeneous optimization landscape arising from jointly training LLM and PEFT components. They analyze a reshuffling-type SGD algorithm with multiple learning rates for different parameter subsets within this framework.
AI.result: They prove convergence guarantees for the proposed hybrid optimization under the hybrid smoothness condition and multi–learning-rate reshuffling SGD. Empirically, on various downstream tasks and different model architectures, the hybrid fine-tuning method yields consistent performance gains over standard full fine-tuning and standard PEFT approaches.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid fine-tuning with jointly updated LLM and PEFT parameters, optimized via a mix of zeroth- and first-order methods and analyzed under a hybrid smoothness framework, offers a practically effective and theoretically grounded approach to LLM fine-tuning.
