It improves performance across tasks while retaining better computational efficiency than full fine-tuning, making it a compelling option for large-scale language model adaptation.

title: Bridging Linguistic Gaps: Cross-Lingual Mapping in Pre-Training and Dataset for Enhanced Multilingual LLM Performance
summary: Multilingual Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with cross-lingual tasks due to data imbalances between high-resource and low-resource languages, as well as monolingual bias in pre-training. Existing methods, such as bilingual fine-tuning and contrastive alignment, can improve cross-lingual performance, but they often require extensive parallel data or suffer from instability. To address these challenges, we introduce a Cross-Lingual Mapping Task during the pre-training phase, which enhances cross-lingual alignment without compromising monolingual fluency. Our approach bi-directionally maps languages within the LLM embedding space, improving both language generation and comprehension. We further propose a Language Alignment Coefficient to robustly quantify cross-lingual consistency, even in limited-data scenarios. Experimental results on machine translation (MT), cross-lingual natural language understanding (CLNLU), and cross-lingual question answering (CLQA) show that our model achieves gains of up to 11.9 BLEU points in MT, 6.72 points in CLQA BERTScore-Precision, and more than 5% in CLNLU accuracy over strong multilingual baselines. These findings highlight the potential of incorporating cross-lingual objectives into pre-training to improve multilingual LLMs.

AI.tldr: They propose a new pre-training task that explicitly maps between languages in the embedding space to improve cross-lingual abilities of multilingual LLMs, achieving large gains on MT, CLNLU, and CLQA.
AI.motivation: Multilingual LLMs underperform on cross-lingual tasks because training data is imbalanced across languages and pre-training is largely monolingual, so representations for different languages are poorly aligned. Existing fixes like bilingual fine-tuning and contrastive alignment either need lots of parallel data or are unstable, leaving a need for a more robust, data-efficient way to improve cross-lingual alignment without hurting monolingual quality.
AI.method: During pre-training, they add a Cross-Lingual Mapping Task that bi-directionally maps between languages in the shared embedding space, encouraging aligned representations across languages while preserving monolingual fluency. They also introduce a Language Alignment Coefficient, a metric designed to robustly quantify cross-lingual consistency, especially when parallel data is scarce.
AI.result: On benchmarks for machine translation, cross-lingual NLU, and cross-lingual QA, their model outperforms strong multilingual baselines, with up to +11.9 BLEU in MT, +6.72 BERTScore-Precision for CLQA, and more than +5% accuracy in CLNLU.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating cross-lingual mapping objectives directly into the pre-training phase substantially improves cross-lingual alignment and performance of multilingual LLMs, without sacrificing monolingual fluency, and the proposed Language Alignment Coefficient provides a robust way to measure this alignment.

title: FinTrace: Holistic Trajectory-Level Evaluation of LLM Tool Calling for Long-Horizon Financial Tasks
summary: Recent studies demonstrate that tool-calling capability enables large language models (LLMs) to interact with external environments for long-horizon financial tasks. While existing benchmarks have begun evaluating financial tool calling, they focus on limited scenarios and rely on call-level metrics that fail to capture trajectory-level reasoning quality. To address this gap, we introduce FinTrace, a benchmark comprising 800 expert-annotated trajectories spanning 34 real-world financial task categories across multiple difficulty levels. FinTrace employs a rubric-based evaluation protocol with nine metrics organized along four axes -- action correctness, execution efficiency, process quality, and output quality -- enabling fine-grained assessment of LLM tool-calling behavior. Our evaluation of 13 LLMs reveals that while frontier models achieve strong tool selection, all models struggle with information utilization and final answer quality, exposing a critical gap between invoking the right tools and reasoning effectively over their outputs. To move beyond diagnosis, we construct FinTrace-Training, the first trajectory-level preference dataset for financial tool-calling, containing 8,196 curated trajectories with tool-augmented contexts and preference pairs. We fine-tune Qwen-3.5-9B using supervised fine-tuning followed by direct preference optimization (DPO) and show that training on FinTrace-Training consistently improves intermediate reasoning metrics, with DPO more effectively suppressing failure modes. However, end-to-end answer quality remains a bottleneck, indicating that trajectory-level improvements do not yet fully propagate to final output quality.

AI.tldr: Introduces FinTrace, a trajectory-level benchmark and training dataset for evaluating and improving LLM financial tool-calling and reasoning, highlighting a gap between good tool selection and poor final answer quality.
AI.motivation: Existing financial tool-calling benchmarks are limited in scenario coverage and rely on call-level metrics, which cannot adequately capture long-horizon, trajectory-level reasoning quality and real-world financial task complexity. There is also a lack of training data that reflects full tool-use trajectories and preferences, hindering systematic improvement of LLMs’ financial reasoning with tools.
AI.method: 1) Build FinTrace: 800 expert-annotated trajectories across 34 real-world financial task categories and multiple difficulty levels. Design a rubric-based evaluation with nine metrics along four axes: action correctness, execution efficiency, process quality, and output quality, to assess LLM tool-calling behavior at the trajectory level. 2) Evaluate 13 LLMs on FinTrace to diagnose strengths and weaknesses in tool selection, information utilization, and reasoning. 3) Create FinTrace-Training: 8,196 curated trajectories with tool-augmented contexts and preference pairs. 4) Fine-tune Qwen-3.5-9B using supervised fine-tuning followed by direct preference optimization (DPO) on FinTrace-Training and analyze metric improvements and remaining bottlenecks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows frontier LLMs are good at selecting the correct financial tools but consistently underperform in utilizing retrieved information and producing high-quality final answers. Training Qwen-3.5-9B on FinTrace-Training improves intermediate reasoning and process-related metrics; DPO in particular better suppresses common failure modes than supervised fine-tuning alone. Nonetheless, the overall end-to-end answer quality still lags, demonstrating that trajectory-level gains do not automatically translate into final solution quality.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-level benchmarks and training data like FinTrace and FinTrace-Training are effective for diagnosing and partially improving LLM financial tool-calling, particularly in intermediate reasoning and process quality. However, there remains a persistent gap in final answer quality, suggesting that additional methods beyond trajectory-level preference optimization are needed to ensure that improved tool use and reasoning fully propagate to accurate and reliable financial task outcomes.

title: Computational Lesions in Multilingual Language Models Separate Shared and Language-specific Brain Alignment
summary: How the brain supports language across different languages is a basic question in neuroscience and a useful test for multilingual artificial intelligence. Neuroimaging has identified language-responsive brain regions across languages, but it cannot by itself show whether the underlying processing is shared or language-specific. Here we use six multilingual large language models (LLMs) as controllable systems and create targeted ``computational lesions'' by zeroing small parameter sets that are important across languages or especially important for one language. We then compare intact and lesioned models in predicting functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) responses during 100 minutes of naturalistic story listening in native English, Chinese and French (112 participants). Lesioning a compact shared core reduces whole-brain encoding correlation by 60.32% relative to intact models, whereas language-specific lesions preserve cross-language separation in embedding space but selectively weaken brain predictivity for the matched native language. These results support a shared backbone with embedded specializations and provide a causal framework for studying multilingual brain-model alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether language processing in the brain is supported by shared or language-specific mechanisms using multilingual LLMs with targeted parameter lesions and fMRI during naturalistic listening.
AI.motivation: To address a fundamental question in neuroscience and AI: does the brain use shared or language-specific mechanisms for processing different languages, and can multilingual LLMs serve as controllable models to probe this organization beyond what standard neuroimaging can reveal.
AI.method: Use six multilingual LLMs and create targeted computational lesions by zeroing small parameter subsets that are either (a) important across multiple languages or (b) especially important for a single language. Compare intact vs. lesioned models in their ability to predict fMRI responses while 112 native speakers of English, Chinese, and French listen to 100 minutes of naturalistic stories. Analyze changes in whole-brain encoding correlations and cross-language separation in the models’ embedding spaces.
AI.result: Lesioning a compact shared core of parameters leads to a large drop (60.32%) in whole-brain encoding correlation relative to intact models, indicating that this core is critical for aligning model representations with brain activity across languages. In contrast, lesions targeting language-specific parameters preserve cross-language separation in the models’ representation space but selectively impair brain predictivity only for the corresponding native language.
AI.conclusion: Language processing in the brain appears to rely on a shared computational backbone with language-specific specializations layered on top. Multilingual LLMs, when manipulated via targeted lesions, can provide a causal framework for investigating how language representations are organized across languages in the human brain.

title: AI Achieves a Perfect LSAT Score
summary: This paper reports the first documented instance of a language model achieving a perfect score on an officially disclosed Law School Admission Test (LSAT). Controlled experiments on eight reasoning models show that varying the prompt, shuffling answer choices, and sampling multiple responses have no meaningful effect as drivers of performance. Ablating the thinking phase that models generate before answering, however, lowers frontier accuracy by up to 8 percentage points, predominantly in logical reasoning. Distilled models produce full thinking traces in the same format yet plateau far below frontier performance. A pilot process reward model fine-tuned via QLoRA on official LSAT explanations narrows this gap through Best-of-5 selection, with gains again predominantly in logical reasoning. The gatekeeper of elite legal education since 1948, the LSAT has not merely been passed but answered without a single error by models that reason. The upper bound of the cognitive capacities it has tested is no longer exclusive to human cognition.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that a frontier language model attains a perfect score on an official LSAT, investigates which factors actually drive this performance, and shows that explicit reasoning traces and process-based training significantly contribute, especially for logical reasoning.
AI.motivation: To rigorously test the upper limits of current language models on a long-standing, high-stakes human reasoning benchmark (the LSAT) and to determine whether high scores reflect genuine reasoning or superficial pattern-matching. The authors also seek to understand what aspects of model behavior—prompting, thinking traces, or process supervision—meaningfully affect performance and whether the LSAT still distinguishes human from machine cognition.
AI.method: The authors evaluate eight reasoning-focused language models on officially disclosed LSAT exams. They run controlled experiments varying prompts, shuffling answer choices, and using multiple sampled responses to see their effect on accuracy. They ablate (remove) the intermediate “thinking phase” chain-of-thought traces to measure its impact, especially on logical reasoning sections. They compare frontier and distilled models that both produce full reasoning traces, then train a small process reward model using QLoRA on official LSAT explanations. This reward model is used in a Best-of-5 selection setup to pick the highest-reward answer/thinking trace combinations.
AI.result: A frontier model achieves a perfect score on an official LSAT. Changing prompts, shuffling answer options, or sampling multiple completions does not materially change performance. Removing the thinking phase reduces accuracy by as much as 8 percentage points, with the largest drop in logical reasoning questions. Distilled models that mimic the reasoning-trace format still underperform significantly. The process reward model, fine-tuned on LSAT explanations and used for Best-of-5 selection, narrows the gap between distilled and frontier performance, again primarily improving logical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Modern reasoning-oriented language models can not only pass but achieve perfect scores on the LSAT, indicating that the exam no longer uniquely measures human cognitive limits. Performance is strongly tied to the presence and quality of internal reasoning traces rather than superficial prompt tweaks or answer ordering. Distillation alone cannot fully transfer frontier reasoning capabilities, but process-based reward modeling and selection can reduce the gap, especially for logical reasoning tasks. This suggests that future benchmarks and training methods must move beyond traditional standardized tests to better assess and develop machine reasoning.

title: ProUIE: A Macro-to-Micro Progressive Learning Method for LLM-based Universal Information Extraction
summary: LLM-based universal information extraction (UIE) methods often rely on additional information beyond the original training data, which increases training complexity yet often yields limited gains. To address this, we propose ProUIE, a Macro-to-Micro progressive learning approach that improves UIE without introducing any external information. ProUIE consists of three stages: (i) macro-level Complete Modeling (CM), which learns NER, RE, and EE along their intrinsic difficulty order on the full training data to build a unified extraction foundation, (ii) meso-level Streamlined Alignment (SA), which operates on sampled data with simplified target formats, streamlining and regularizing structured outputs to make them more concise and controllable, and (iii) micro-level Deep Exploration (DE), which applies GRPO with stepwise fine-grained rewards (SFR) over structural units to guide exploration and improve performance. Experiments on 36 public datasets show that ProUIE consistently improves unified extraction, outperforming strong instruction-tuned baselines on average for NER and RE while using a smaller backbone, and it further demonstrates clear gains in large-scale production-oriented information extraction.

AI.tldr: ProUIE is a progressive macro-to-micro learning framework for universal information extraction that improves NER, RE, and EE performance without using external data, through staged modeling, alignment, and fine-grained RL-based optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based universal information extraction approaches typically require extra information such as additional annotations, external tools, or task-specific prompts, which complicate training while providing only modest performance gains. There is a need for a unified framework that can enhance extraction quality and controllability solely by better using the original training data and learning process, rather than by adding external resources.
AI.method: ProUIE introduces a three-stage Macro-to-Micro progressive learning scheme for UIE. (1) Macro-level Complete Modeling (CM): train on full data, sequentially learning NER, relation extraction (RE), and event extraction (EE) following their intrinsic difficulty to establish a unified extraction capability. (2) Meso-level Streamlined Alignment (SA): further train on sampled data with simplified target formats to standardize and compress structured outputs, enhancing consistency and controllability of the model’s responses. (3) Micro-level Deep Exploration (DE): apply GRPO (a reinforcement learning optimization method) with stepwise fine-grained rewards over structural units (e.g., entities, relations, events) so that the model is rewarded at a fine granularity, guiding exploration toward better structured extraction performance.
AI.result: On 36 public datasets, ProUIE achieves consistent improvements in unified information extraction tasks. It outperforms strong instruction-tuned baselines for named entity recognition and relation extraction on average, even when using a smaller backbone model. Additionally, it shows clear gains in large-scale, production-oriented information extraction scenarios, indicating its practical effectiveness and scalability.
AI.conclusion: A purely training-strategy-based, Macro-to-Micro progressive learning framework (ProUIE) can significantly enhance universal information extraction from LLMs without relying on external information. By combining complete multi-task modeling, streamlined output alignment, and fine-grained RL-based exploration, ProUIE improves both accuracy and controllability and scales effectively to real-world production settings.

title: LoopGuard: Breaking Self-Reinforcing Attention Loops via Dynamic KV Cache Intervention
summary: Through systematic experiments on long-context generation, we observe a damaging failure mode in which decoding can collapse into persistent repetition loops. We find that this degeneration is driven by collapsed attention patterns, where a subset of heads locks onto a narrow suffix of the history, and is further stabilized by inference-time KV cache reuse. Crucially, since many existing KV cache policies rely on attention-based importance, this collapse can produce spuriously high scores for repetitive tokens, causing cache management to inadvertently amplify repetition. To study this phenomenon in a controlled and reproducible manner, we introduce LoopBench, a benchmark with explicit loop-inducing conditions and loop-oriented metrics that quantify repetition severity and generation instability beyond downstream task scores. Building on these insights, we propose LoopGuard, a lightweight, plug-in KV cache guard that detects loop onset online and disrupts the feedback cycle by pruning repetitive tail spans under a fixed cache budget. Experiments on LoopBench show that LoopGuard reduces loop incidence by over 90 percentage points, while restoring output diversity and reducing token waste.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and analyzes a repetition-loop failure mode in long-context generation and proposes a benchmark (LoopBench) and a mitigation method (LoopGuard) that largely suppresses these loops.
AI.motivation: Long-context language models sometimes fall into harmful repetition loops during decoding, which wastes tokens and degrades output quality, but this failure mode is poorly understood and not well captured by standard benchmarks or cache policies.
AI.method: The authors run systematic long-context generation experiments, inspect attention head behavior and KV cache policies, design LoopBench with explicit loop-inducing setups and loop-focused metrics, and develop LoopGuard, a small plug-in mechanism that monitors for loop onset and prunes repetitive tail spans under a fixed KV cache budget.
AI.result: They show that repetition loops are linked to collapsed attention patterns and are exacerbated by attention-based KV cache reuse, that LoopBench can reliably trigger and measure these failures, and that LoopGuard cuts loop incidence by over 90 percentage points, improves output diversity, and reduces wasted tokens in experiments on this benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Degenerate repetition in long-context generation arises from an interaction between attention collapse and KV cache policies, but can be effectively detected and mitigated online using a simple cache-guard mechanism, improving stability and efficiency without changing the underlying model.

title: Efficient Process Reward Modeling via Contrastive Mutual Information
summary: Recent research has devoted considerable effort to verifying the intermediate reasoning steps of chain-of-thought (CoT) trajectories using process reward models (PRMs) and other verifier models. However, training a PRM typically requires human annotators to assign reward scores to each reasoning step, which is both costly and time-consuming. Existing automated approaches, such as Monte Carlo (MC) estimation, also demand substantial computational resources due to repeated LLM rollouts. To overcome these limitations, we propose contrastive pointwise mutual information (CPMI), a novel automatic reward labeling method that leverages the model's internal probability to infer step-level supervision while significantly reducing the computational burden of annotating dataset. CPMI quantifies how much a reasoning step increases the mutual information between the step and the correct target answer relative to hard-negative alternatives. This contrastive signal serves as a proxy for the step's contribution to the final solution and yields a reliable reward. The experimental results show that CPMI-based labeling reduces dataset construction time by 84% and token generation by 98% compared to MC estimation, while achieving higher accuracy on process-level evaluations and mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CPMI, an automatic, probability-based method to label rewards for each reasoning step in chain-of-thought trajectories, avoiding costly human annotation and expensive Monte Carlo rollouts while improving accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Training process reward models for chain-of-thought reasoning requires fine-grained, step-level reward labels. Human annotation is expensive and slow, while existing automated methods like Monte Carlo estimation require many repeated LLM calls, making dataset construction computationally heavy. There is a need for a method that can cheaply and automatically infer reliable step-level rewards without heavy supervision or sampling overhead.
AI.method: The authors introduce contrastive pointwise mutual information (CPMI) as an automatic reward labeling scheme. For each reasoning step, they compute how that step changes the mutual information between the step and the correct answer, contrasted against hard-negative alternative answers. This contrastive change in PMI, computed using the model’s own internal probabilities, acts as a proxy for the usefulness of that step to solving the problem. These CPMI-based scores are then used as step-level rewards to train process reward models, without requiring human labels or extensive Monte Carlo rollouts.
AI.result: Experiments show that CPMI-based labeling dramatically reduces the cost of dataset construction: 84% less time and 98% fewer generated tokens than Monte Carlo estimation methods. Despite using far fewer resources, PRMs trained with CPMI rewards achieve better accuracy on process-level evaluations and mathematical reasoning benchmarks than those trained with MC-based labeling.
AI.conclusion: CPMI provides an efficient and effective automatic approach to assigning step-level rewards for chain-of-thought trajectories. By using the model’s own probability distribution and contrastive PMI with respect to correct vs. hard-negative answers, it eliminates most of the annotation and sampling overhead of prior methods while yielding higher-quality supervision. This makes training process reward models more scalable and improves performance on reasoning tasks.

title: Learning Hierarchical and Geometry-Aware Graph Representations for Text-to-CAD
summary: Text-to-CAD code generation is a long-horizon task that translates textual instructions into long sequences of interdependent operations. Existing methods typically decode text directly into executable code (e.g., bpy) without explicitly modeling assembly hierarchy or geometric constraints, which enlarges the search space, accumulates local errors, and often causes cascading failures in complex assemblies. To address this issue, we propose a hierarchical and geometry-aware graph as an intermediate representation. The graph models multi-level parts and components as nodes and encodes explicit geometric constraints as edges. Instead of mapping text directly to code, our framework first predicts structure and constraints, then conditions action sequencing and code generation, thereby improving geometric fidelity and constraint satisfaction. We further introduce a structure-aware progressive curriculum learning strategy that constructs graded tasks through controlled structural edits, explores the model's capability boundary, and synthesizes boundary examples for iterative training. In addition, we build a 12K dataset with instructions, decomposition graphs, action sequences, and bpy code, together with graph- and constraint-oriented evaluation metrics. Extensive experiments show that our method consistently outperforms existing approaches in both geometric fidelity and accurate satisfaction of geometric constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a hierarchical, geometry-aware graph intermediate representation and curriculum learning strategy to improve text-to-CAD code generation, leading to higher geometric fidelity and constraint satisfaction than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-CAD systems directly decode natural language into low-level executable code (e.g., bpy), which ignores explicit assembly hierarchy and geometric constraints. This leads to a huge search space, error accumulation over long operation sequences, and cascading failures, especially for complex multi-part assemblies. There is a need for a structured intermediate representation and better training strategy that can capture object hierarchy and constraints while making long-horizon code generation more reliable and accurate.
AI.method: The authors propose a hierarchical, geometry-aware graph as an intermediate representation for text-to-CAD. Nodes represent parts and components at multiple levels of abstraction, and edges encode explicit geometric constraints between them. The framework first predicts this structure-and-constraint graph from text, then conditions the subsequent action sequencing and bpy code generation on the predicted graph. Additionally, they introduce a structure-aware progressive curriculum learning strategy that gradually increases task difficulty via controlled structural edits, probing the model’s capability boundary and synthesizing boundary examples for iterative training. They also construct a 12K-sample dataset containing instructions, decomposition graphs, action sequences, and bpy code, along with new graph- and constraint-based evaluation metrics.
AI.result: On their new 12K dataset and with graph- and constraint-oriented metrics, the proposed method consistently outperforms previous text-to-CAD code generation baselines. It achieves higher geometric fidelity of the generated CAD models and better satisfaction of the intended geometric constraints, particularly on complex assemblies where prior approaches often fail due to cascading errors.
AI.conclusion: Introducing a hierarchical, geometry-aware graph as an explicit intermediate representation, coupled with a structure-aware curriculum learning regime, significantly improves the reliability and accuracy of text-to-CAD code generation. The approach reduces search space, mitigates error accumulation, and enhances constraint satisfaction, suggesting that structured, constraint-centric modeling is a promising direction for future text-to-3D and CAD-generation systems.

title: HeceTokenizer: A Syllable-Based Tokenization Approach for Turkish Retrieval
summary: HeceTokenizer is a syllable-based tokenizer for Turkish that exploits the deterministic six-pattern phonological structure of the language to construct a closed, out-of-vocabulary (OOV)-free vocabulary of approximately 8,000 unique syllable types. A BERT-tiny encoder (1.5M parameters) is trained from scratch on a subset of Turkish Wikipedia using a masked language modeling objective and evaluated on the TQuAD retrieval benchmark using Recall@5. Combined with a fine-grained chunk-based retrieval strategy, HeceTokenizer achieves 50.3% Recall@5, surpassing the 46.92% reported by a morphology-driven baseline that uses a 200 times larger model. These results suggest that the phonological regularity of Turkish syllables provides a strong and resource-light inductive bias for retrieval tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces HeceTokenizer, a syllable-based tokenizer for Turkish that, combined with a tiny BERT encoder, outperforms a much larger morphology-based model on a retrieval benchmark.
AI.motivation: Turkish has complex morphology that leads to very large word vocabularies and severe out-of-vocabulary issues in NLP models. Existing morphology-based approaches often require large models and resources. The paper is motivated by the desire to build compact, OOV-free, and effective tokenization and modeling strategies for Turkish by exploiting its regular phonological syllable structure.
AI.method: Design a syllable-based tokenizer (HeceTokenizer) that leverages Turkish’s deterministic six-pattern phonological syllable structure to build a closed vocabulary of about 8,000 syllable types. Train a small BERT-tiny encoder (1.5M parameters) from scratch on a subset of Turkish Wikipedia using masked language modeling. Evaluate the resulting model on the TQuAD retrieval benchmark with Recall@5, using a fine-grained chunk-based retrieval strategy, and compare performance against a morphology-driven baseline model that is about 200× larger.
AI.result: With the HeceTokenizer plus a BERT-tiny encoder and chunk-based retrieval, the system attains 50.3% Recall@5 on the TQuAD retrieval benchmark, outperforming a morphology-driven baseline that uses a model 200× larger and reports 46.92% Recall@5.
AI.conclusion: The phonological regularity of Turkish syllables can be exploited to build a compact, syllable-based tokenizer and small language model that yields strong performance on retrieval tasks. This suggests syllable-level phonological structure provides a powerful, resource-efficient inductive bias, potentially preferable to heavier morphology-driven approaches for certain applications.

title: Ontological Trajectory Forecasting via Finite Semigroup Iteration and Lie Algebra Approximation in Geopolitical Knowledge Graphs
comment: 18 pages. Code and system available at https://github.com/wuqihang-brave/El-druin
summary: We present EL-DRUIN, an ontological reasoning system for geopolitical intelligence analysis that combines formal ontology, finite semigroup algebra, and Lie algebra approximation to forecast long-run relationship trajectories. Current LLM-based political analysis systems operate as summarisation engines, producing outputs bounded by textual pattern matching. EL-DRUIN departs from this paradigm by modelling geopolitical relationships as states in a finite set of named Dynamic Patterns, composing patterns via a semigroup operation whose structure constants are defined by an explicit composition table, and embedding each pattern as a vector in an 8-dimensional semantic Lie algebra space. Forward simulation iterates this semigroup operation, yielding reachable pattern sets at each discrete timestep; convergence to idempotent absorbing states (fixed points of the composition) constitutes the predicted long-run attractor. Bayesian posterior weights combine ontology-derived confidence priors with a Lie similarity term measuring the cosine similarity between the vector sum of composing patterns and the target pattern vector, providing interpretable, calibrated probabilities that are not self-reported by a language model. Bifurcation points -- steps at which two candidate attractors have near-equal posterior mass -- are detected and exposed to downstream analysis. We demonstrate the framework on six geopolitical scenarios including US-China technology decoupling and the Taiwan Strait military coercion trajectory. The architecture is publicly available as an open-source system with a Streamlit frontend exposing full computation traces, Bayesian posterior breakdowns, and 8D ontological state vectors.

AI.tldr: EL-DRUIN is an ontological reasoning engine that models geopolitical relationships as algebraic dynamic patterns and uses semigroup composition plus Lie algebra embeddings and Bayesian weighting to forecast long-run relationship attractors and highlight bifurcation points.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based geopolitical tools mostly summarize texts and inherit the limitations of pattern-matching language models, including weak long-horizon forecasting, opaque probabilities, and limited interpretability of underlying causal structure. The authors want a system that can make long-run, structurally grounded predictions of geopolitical relationship trajectories with explicit, inspectable computations and calibrated probabilities, rather than relying on LLM self-reports.
AI.method: They define a finite set of named Dynamic Patterns representing qualitative relationship states and specify a semigroup operation on these states via an explicit composition table. Each pattern is also embedded as a vector in an 8-dimensional Lie algebra-inspired semantic space. Starting from an initial configuration, they iteratively apply the semigroup operation to simulate future states, tracking which patterns are reachable at each discrete step. Idempotent absorbing elements of the semigroup serve as long-run attractors. A Bayesian scheme assigns posterior probabilities to candidate next states and attractors, combining ontology-based prior confidences with a likelihood term based on cosine similarity between the Lie-embedded sum of composing patterns and candidate targets. They also detect bifurcation points where posterior mass over attractors is split, and expose all intermediate algebraic and probabilistic computations through an open-source architecture and Streamlit UI.
AI.result: They implement EL-DRUIN as an operational, open-source system and run it on six case-study scenarios, including US–China technology decoupling and a Taiwan Strait military coercion trajectory. For these cases, the system produces explicit trajectory forecasts, identifies long-run attractor states, and flags bifurcation timesteps where multiple outcomes remain plausible. The interface displays the complete computation trace, Bayesian posteriors, and 8D state vectors so that analysts can inspect and critique the reasoning process.
AI.conclusion: EL-DRUIN shows that geopolitical forecasting can be reframed as algebraic trajectory analysis over an ontological state space, rather than as free-text prediction by LLMs. By combining semigroup dynamics, Lie algebra embeddings, and Bayesian weighting, it yields interpretable long-run attractor forecasts and transparent uncertainty estimates. The authors argue this framework offers a more auditable and structurally grounded alternative or complement to current LLM-based political analysis tools.

title: Learning and Enforcing Context-Sensitive Control for LLMs
summary: Controlling the output of Large Language Models (LLMs) through context-sensitive constraints has emerged as a promising approach to overcome the limitations of Context-Free Grammars (CFGs) in guaranteeing generation validity. However, such constraints typically require manual specification -- a significant barrier demanding specialized expertise. We introduce a framework that automatically learns context-sensitive constraints from LLM interactions through a two-phase process: syntactic exploration to gather diverse outputs for constraint learning, followed by constraint exploitation to enforce these learned rules during generation. Experiments demonstrate that our method enables even small LLMs (1B parameters) to learn and generate with perfect constraint adherence, outperforming larger counterparts and state-of-the-art reasoning models. This work represents the first integration of context-sensitive grammar learning with LLM generation, eliminating manual specification while maintaining generation validity.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an automatic way to learn and enforce context-sensitive constraints for LLM outputs, removing the need for manually written grammars while ensuring perfectly valid generations.
AI.motivation: Existing methods often rely on Context-Free Grammars or manually-specified constraints to control LLM outputs, which struggle with context-sensitive structure and require expert effort. There is a need for a system that can guarantee output validity under rich, context-dependent constraints without manual grammar engineering, and that works even for smaller models.
AI.method: The authors design a two-phase framework: (1) syntactic exploration, where the LLM is prompted or otherwise guided to produce a wide variety of outputs, which are then analyzed to infer underlying context-sensitive constraints; and (2) constraint exploitation, where these learned constraints are transformed into a mechanism that constrains LLM decoding so that all future generations strictly obey them. The approach effectively integrates context-sensitive grammar learning with the LLM generation pipeline.
AI.result: Experiments show that small LLMs with around 1B parameters, when equipped with the learned constraints, can generate outputs with perfect adherence to the constraints. These constrained small models outperform larger, unconstrained LLMs and existing state-of-the-art reasoning models on tasks where constraint satisfaction and structured validity are crucial.
AI.conclusion: Automatically learned context-sensitive constraints can be tightly integrated into LLM decoding to guarantee validity without manual specification. This constitutes the first demonstration of combining context-sensitive grammar learning with LLM generation, and it shows that even small models can achieve strong, perfectly valid constrained generation when supported by learned rules.

title: Trust Your Memory: Verifiable Control of Smart Homes through Reinforcement Learning with Multi-dimensional Rewards
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have become a key foundation for enabling personalized smart home experiences. While existing studies have explored how smart home assistants understand user queries to control devices in real time, their ability to perform memory-driven device control remains challenging from both evaluation and methodological perspectives. In terms of evaluation, existing benchmarks either focus on immediate device control or general open-domain memory retrieval tasks, and therefore cannot effectively evaluate a model's ability to perform memory-driven device control. Methodologically, while memory-driven device control can be approached using Reinforcement Learning, conventional RL methods generally rely on outcome-based supervision (i.e., whether the final task is achieved). This lack of intermediate feedback can lead to sub-optimal performance or local failures in fine-grained memory management tasks (adding, updating, deleting, and utilizing). To address these issues, we first release MemHomeLife, built from anonymized real-world long-term user interaction logs. To enable more fine-grained evaluation of different memory-related subtasks, we further construct MemHome, the first benchmark designed to systematically evaluate memory-driven device control in smart home scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper tackles the challenge of evaluating and improving LLM-based smart home assistants on memory-driven device control, releasing a real-world dataset (MemHomeLife) and a targeted benchmark (MemHome) for fine-grained assessment of memory operations.
AI.motivation: Existing work on LLM-powered smart homes mostly studies real-time, single-turn device control. However, many realistic smart home scenarios require remembering, updating, and using long-term user-specific information to control devices later. Current benchmarks either assess immediate control or generic memory retrieval, not the specific capability of memory-driven device control. On the methodological side, using standard reinforcement learning with only final-task feedback is insufficient for complex memory management (add/update/delete/use), often causing local failures due to lack of intermediate supervision. This creates a need for both better evaluation datasets and methods tailored to fine-grained memory operations in smart home contexts.
AI.method: The authors collect and anonymize real-world, long-term smart home interaction logs and release them as a dataset named MemHomeLife. On top of this, they construct MemHome, a benchmark explicitly designed around memory-driven device control tasks. MemHome decomposes memory usage in smart homes into subtasks such as adding, updating, deleting, and utilizing memories, and organizes evaluation scenarios that allow systematic testing of LLMs on these specific capabilities.
AI.result: The abstract mainly highlights the creation of two resources: MemHomeLife, a real-world interaction dataset, and MemHome, the first benchmark focused on memory-driven device control in smart homes, enabling more precise and fine-grained evaluation of LLMs in this setting. Detailed empirical results are not described in the abstract but are presumably provided in the full paper.
AI.conclusion: By introducing MemHomeLife and the MemHome benchmark, the paper fills a critical gap in evaluating LLM-based smart home assistants on memory-driven control tasks. These resources support more granular assessment of memory management operations beyond final-task success, laying the groundwork for improved methods—potentially including more informative supervision than standard outcome-based RL—to handle long-term, personalized smart home interactions.

title: QFS-Composer: Query-focused summarization pipeline for less resourced languages
summary: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong performance in text summarization, yet their effectiveness drops significantly across languages with restricted training resources. This work addresses the challenge of query-focused summarization (QFS) in less-resourced languages, where labeled datasets and evaluation tools are limited. We present a novel QFS framework, QFS-Composer, that integrates query decomposition, question generation (QG), question answering (QA), and abstractive summarization to improve the factual alignment of a summary with user intent. We test our approach on the Slovenian language. To enable high-quality supervision and evaluation, we develop the Slovenian QA and QG models based on a Slovene LLM and adapt evaluation approaches for reference-free summary evaluation. Empirical evaluation shows that the QA-guided summarization pipeline yields improved consistency and relevance over baseline LLMs. Our work establishes an extensible methodology for advancing QFS in less-resourced languages.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes QFS-Composer, a query-focused summarization framework tailored for low-resource languages, evaluated on Slovenian, which improves factual alignment and relevance of summaries via a QA-guided pipeline.
AI.motivation: Large language models perform well on summarization in high-resource languages, but their quality drops in low-resource settings due to limited training data, labeled QFS datasets, and evaluation tools. There is a specific need to improve query-focused summarization, ensuring that generated summaries accurately match user intent in less-resourced languages.
AI.method: The authors design QFS-Composer, a modular framework that decomposes the query, generates questions, answers them, and then performs abstractive summarization guided by the QA outputs. They build Slovenian-specific QA and QG models based on a Slovene LLM and adapt existing reference-free evaluation methods to this setting, enabling training and assessment without large labeled datasets.
AI.result: Empirical experiments on Slovenian show that the QA-guided QFS-Composer pipeline produces summaries that are more consistent with the source content and better aligned with user queries than baseline LLM-based summarization systems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed QFS-Composer framework effectively enhances query-focused summarization for a low-resource language, demonstrating that a QA-guided decomposition and summarization pipeline can compensate for limited training resources. The methodology is extensible and can be transferred to other less-resourced languages to advance QFS in such settings.

title: Learning from Emptiness: De-biasing Listwise Rerankers with Content-Agnostic Probability Calibration
summary: Generative listwise reranking leverages global context for superior retrieval but is plagued by intrinsic position bias, where models exhibit structural sensitivity to input order independent of relevance. Existing mitigations present a dilemma: inference-time aggregation incurs prohibitive latency, while training-based methods often fail to eradicate ingrained priors, particularly in compact models. To resolve this dilemma, we propose CapCal (Content-Agnostic Probability Calibration), a training-free framework that mechanically decouples positional bias from ranking decisions. By estimating the bias distribution via content-free placeholders, CapCal rectifies output logits through an entropy-adaptive contrastive mechanism. Evaluations across 10 benchmarks confirm that CapCal achieves superior performance among training-free methods while preserving single-pass efficiency. Notably, it unlocks the latent potential of lightweight models (e.g., 0.6B), delivering absolute NDCG gains exceeding 10 points and outperforming both permutation-based aggregation and data-augmentation baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CapCal, a training-free calibration method that removes position bias from generative listwise rerankers using content-agnostic bias estimation and entropy-adaptive logit correction, significantly improving ranking quality, especially for small models.
AI.motivation: Generative listwise rerankers can use global context to improve retrieval quality but suffer from strong intrinsic position bias: they prefer certain ranks (e.g., early positions) regardless of true relevance. Existing fixes either require expensive multiple-pass inference (permutations/ensembles) or training-time debiasing that still leaves residual priors, particularly in small, compact models. The authors aim to obtain the benefits of listwise reranking without expensive inference or retraining, by cleanly separating relevance from positional preference.
AI.method: The authors propose CapCal (Content-Agnostic Probability Calibration), a training-free, post-hoc framework. They first estimate a position-bias distribution by feeding the model content-free placeholder inputs (so that any systematic preferences in output scores can be attributed to positional bias rather than semantics). Then, during real inference, they correct the model’s output logits using this learned bias profile through an entropy-adaptive contrastive mechanism, which scales or shifts the logits based on both the estimated bias and the model’s confidence (entropy) for the specific list. This mechanically decouples positional bias from the final ranking without modifying the model weights or requiring multiple passes.
AI.result: Across 10 retrieval and reranking benchmarks, CapCal outperforms other training-free debiasing approaches while retaining single-pass efficiency. It achieves particularly large gains on lightweight models (around 0.6B parameters), with absolute NDCG improvements exceeding 10 points. CapCal also surpasses more expensive permutation-based aggregation and data-augmentation baselines that require multiple passes or additional training data.
AI.conclusion: CapCal offers an effective, practical solution to position bias in generative listwise reranking. By using content-agnostic calibration and entropy-adaptive logit correction, it removes much of the structural positional bias without retraining or costly inference-time ensembles, enabling even small models to realize much of their latent reranking potential and delivering state-of-the-art performance among training-free methods.

title: Attention Sinks as Internal Signals for Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models frequently exhibit hallucinations: fluent and confident outputs that are factually incorrect or unsupported by the input context. While recent hallucination detection methods have explored various features derived from attention maps, the underlying mechanisms they exploit remain poorly understood. In this work, we propose SinkProbe, a hallucination detection method grounded in the observation that hallucinations are deeply entangled with attention sinks - tokens that accumulate disproportionate attention mass during generation - indicating a transition from distributed, input-grounded attention to compressed, prior-dominated computation. Importantly, although sink scores are computed solely from attention maps, we find that the classifier preferentially relies on sinks whose associated value vectors have large norms. Moreover, we show that previous methods implicitly depend on attention sinks by establishing their mathematical relationship to sink scores. Our findings yield a novel hallucination detection method grounded in theory that produces state-of-the-art results across popular datasets and LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SinkProbe, a hallucination detection method for large language models based on attention sinks—tokens that absorb excessive attention and signal a shift from input-grounded reasoning to prior-driven generation.
AI.motivation: Large language models often hallucinate, producing confident but incorrect statements, and existing detection methods rely on attention-derived features without a clear understanding of the underlying mechanisms. The authors aim to provide a theoretically grounded explanation of how attention behavior correlates with hallucinations and to build a more principled detector.
AI.method: They analyze attention maps to identify attention sinks—tokens with disproportionately high incoming attention mass—and define sink scores that quantify this behavior. They then train a classifier using these sink-based features, examining in particular which sinks with large value-vector norms are influential. They also mathematically connect their sink scores to features used in prior hallucination detection methods, showing these methods implicitly leverage similar phenomena.
AI.result: SinkProbe achieves state-of-the-art hallucination detection performance across several benchmark datasets and multiple large language models. Empirically, the classifier is found to rely most heavily on sinks whose value vectors have large norms, confirming the theoretical link between these attention patterns and hallucinations.
AI.conclusion: Attention sinks are a key mechanism underlying hallucinations in LLMs, marking a transition from distributed, context-grounded attention to compressed, prior-dominated computation. By formalizing and exploiting this phenomenon through sink scores, SinkProbe offers a theoretically motivated and empirically strong method for detecting hallucinations, while also unifying and explaining the success of previous attention-based approaches.

title: SpecMoE: A Fast and Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Inference via Self-Assisted Speculative Decoding
comment: This is an extended version of our work, which is accepted for publication at the 63rd ACM/IEEE Design Automation Conference (DAC), 2026
summary: The Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture has emerged as a promising approach to mitigate the rising computational costs of large language models (LLMs) by selectively activating parameters. However, its high memory requirements and sub-optimal parameter efficiency pose significant challenges for efficient deployment. Although CPU-offloaded MoE inference systems have been proposed in the literature, they offer limited efficiency, particularly for large batch sizes. In this work, we propose SpecMoE, a memory-efficient MoE inference system based on our self-assisted speculative decoding algorithm. SpecMoE demonstrates the effectiveness of applying speculative decoding to MoE inference without requiring additional model training or fine-tuning. Our system improves inference throughput by up to $4.30\times$, while significantly reducing bandwidth requirements of both memory and interconnect on memory-constrained systems.

AI.tldr: SpecMoE is a memory-efficient MoE inference system that uses self-assisted speculative decoding to speed up MoE LLM inference without extra training, achieving up to 4.3× throughput and lowering memory and interconnect bandwidth needs.
AI.motivation: Mixture-of-Experts LLMs reduce compute by activating only some parameters, but they still have high memory footprints and poor parameter efficiency, making deployment on memory-constrained hardware difficult. Existing CPU-offloaded MoE inference frameworks are not efficient enough, especially at large batch sizes, so a more memory- and bandwidth-efficient inference method is needed.
AI.method: The paper introduces SpecMoE, which integrates a self-assisted speculative decoding algorithm into MoE LLM inference. Instead of changing or retraining the model, SpecMoE uses a speculative ‘assistant’ process based on the same model to predict multiple tokens ahead and then verify them, orchestrating which experts are loaded and when to reduce active memory and data transfer while keeping accuracy. The system is designed to lower memory and interconnect bandwidth usage during MoE expert selection and execution.
AI.result: SpecMoE achieves up to 4.30× higher inference throughput compared to baseline MoE inference approaches, while also significantly reducing both memory bandwidth and interconnect bandwidth demands, particularly in memory-constrained setups and larger batch scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Applying self-assisted speculative decoding to MoE inference can substantially improve throughput and reduce memory and bandwidth usage without requiring any additional training or fine-tuning of the LLM. SpecMoE thus offers a practical and efficient way to deploy large MoE-based LLMs on resource-constrained systems.

title: Expect the Unexpected? Testing the Surprisal of Salient Entities
comment: Accepted to ACL 2026 (main, long); camera-ready version
summary: Previous work examining the Uniform Information Density (UID) hypothesis has shown that while information as measured by surprisal metrics is distributed more or less evenly across documents overall, local discrepancies can arise due to functional pressures corresponding to syntactic and discourse structural constraints. However, work thus far has largely disregarded the relative salience of discourse participants. We fill this gap by studying how overall salience of entities in discourse relates to surprisal using 70K manually annotated mentions across 16 genres of English and a novel minimal-pair prompting method. Our results show that globally salient entities exhibit significantly higher surprisal than non-salient ones, even controlling for position, length, and nesting confounds. Moreover, salient entities systematically reduce surprisal for surrounding content when used as prompts, enhancing document-level predictability. This effect varies by genre, appearing strongest in topic-coherent texts and weakest in conversational contexts. Our findings refine the UID competing pressures framework by identifying global entity salience as a mechanism shaping information distribution in discourse.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how the global salience of discourse entities affects information density and surprisal, showing that salient entities are themselves more surprising but help make surrounding content more predictable, refining the UID hypothesis.
AI.motivation: Uniform Information Density (UID) research suggests that information is distributed relatively evenly across discourse, but local deviations arise from syntactic and discourse constraints. However, prior work has not considered how the relative salience of discourse entities affects information distribution. Because salient entities structure discourse and guide attention, understanding their role can clarify mechanisms behind information density variations and refine the UID framework.
AI.method: The authors use a large corpus of 70K manually annotated entity mentions spanning 16 genres of English. They operationalize information via surprisal metrics and introduce a novel minimal-pair prompting method to test how using different entities as prompts changes surprisal of surrounding material. They control for confounds such as position in the text, mention length, and nesting. They then compare surprisal values for globally salient versus non-salient entities, and analyze genre-specific patterns.
AI.result: They find that globally salient entities have significantly higher surprisal than non-salient entities even after controlling for position, length, and nesting. At the same time, when salient entities are used as prompts, they systematically lower the surprisal of nearby content, thereby increasing predictability at the document level. This salience effect on surprisal is genre-dependent, being strongest in topic-coherent genres (e.g., expository or narrative texts) and weakest in conversational genres, where discourse is less tightly structured around stable salient entities.
AI.conclusion: Global entity salience is a key factor shaping how information is distributed in discourse. Salient entities themselves tend to be more surprising, but they help organize and predict the rest of the text, leading to more uniform information density overall. Incorporating global salience into the UID competing-pressures framework offers a more nuanced account of local deviations in information density and highlights genre as an important moderator of these effects.

title: Inductive Reasoning for Temporal Knowledge Graphs with Emerging Entities
summary: Reasoning on Temporal Knowledge Graphs (TKGs) is essential for predicting future events and time-aware facts. While existing methods are effective at capturing relational dynamics, their performance is limited by a closed-world assumption, which fails to account for emerging entities not present in the training. Notably, these entities continuously join the network without historical interactions. Empirical study reveals that emerging entities are widespread in TKGs, comprising roughly 25\% of all entities. The absence of historical interactions of these entities leads to significant performance degradation in reasoning tasks. Whereas, we observe that entities with semantic similarities often exhibit comparable interaction histories, suggesting the presence of transferable temporal patterns. Inspired by this insight, we propose TransFIR (Transferable Inductive Reasoning), a novel framework that leverages historical interaction sequences from semantically similar known entities to support inductive reasoning. Specifically, we propose a codebook-based classifier that categorizes emerging entities into latent semantic clusters, allowing them to adopt reasoning patterns from similar entities. Experimental results demonstrate that TransFIR outperforms all baselines in reasoning on emerging entities, achieving an average improvement of 28.6% in Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) across multiple datasets. The implementations are available at https://github.com/zhaodazhuang2333/TransFIR.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses reasoning on temporal knowledge graphs for entities that appear without historical data, proposing TransFIR, a framework that transfers temporal interaction patterns from semantically similar existing entities to emerging ones, significantly improving inductive reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Existing temporal knowledge graph reasoning models assume a closed world where all entities seen at test time have historical interactions in the training data. In realistic, evolving environments, many entities are emerging, i.e., they appear only at test time and lack historical interactions. Empirical analysis shows that such emerging entities make up about 25% of entities and cause sharp performance drops in reasoning tasks. The authors observe that semantically similar entities tend to share similar temporal interaction patterns, suggesting that it might be possible to transfer these patterns to emerging entities. This motivates the design of a method that can support inductive reasoning over emerging entities by exploiting transferable temporal patterns from known entities.
AI.method: The authors propose TransFIR (Transferable Inductive Reasoning), a framework designed for inductive temporal knowledge graph reasoning on emerging entities. TransFIR first encodes entities into a semantic space, then uses a codebook-based classifier to assign emerging entities to latent semantic clusters derived from known entities. Each cluster corresponds to a set of temporal interaction patterns learned from entities with similar semantics and histories. By mapping an emerging entity to a cluster, TransFIR allows it to inherit or share reasoning patterns captured in that cluster, effectively transferring temporal interaction behaviors from similar known entities. This enables prediction of future events and time-aware facts even without direct historical interactions for the emerging entity.
AI.result: On multiple temporal knowledge graph datasets, TransFIR is evaluated on reasoning tasks involving emerging entities. The experiments show that TransFIR consistently outperforms a range of baseline methods that are not explicitly designed for emerging entities or that rely on historical interactions. Quantitatively, TransFIR achieves an average improvement of 28.6% in Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) over the best-performing baselines when focusing on emerging-entity reasoning, indicating a substantial gain in ranking quality for predicted temporal facts and future events.
AI.conclusion: TransFIR effectively addresses the challenge of reasoning over emerging entities in temporal knowledge graphs by leveraging transferable temporal patterns from semantically similar known entities. The codebook-based clustering mechanism enables inductive generalization beyond the closed-world assumption, mitigating the performance degradation caused by the lack of historical interactions for new entities. The strong empirical gains across datasets suggest that modeling semantic similarity and latent temporal pattern transfer is a promising direction for robust, real-world TKG reasoning systems that must cope with continuously evolving entity sets.

title: Too Nice to Tell the Truth: Quantifying Agreeableness-Driven Sycophancy in Role-Playing Language Models
comment: 14 Pages, 5 Figures, 9 Tables, ACL Main Conference 2026
summary: Large language models increasingly serve as conversational agents that adopt personas and role-play characters at user request. This capability, while valuable, raises concerns about sycophancy: the tendency to provide responses that validate users rather than prioritize factual accuracy. While prior work has established that sycophancy poses risks to AI safety and alignment, the relationship between specific personality traits of adopted personas and the degree of sycophantic behavior remains unexplored. We present a systematic investigation of how persona agreeableness influences sycophancy across 13 small, open-weight language models ranging from 0.6B to 20B parameters. We develop a benchmark comprising 275 personas evaluated on NEO-IPIP agreeableness subscales and expose each persona to 4,950 sycophancy-eliciting prompts spanning 33 topic categories. Our analysis reveals that 9 of 13 models exhibit statistically significant positive correlations between persona agreeableness and sycophancy rates, with Pearson correlations reaching $r = 0.87$ and effect sizes as large as Cohen's $d = 2.33$. These findings demonstrate that agreeableness functions as a reliable predictor of persona-induced sycophancy, with direct implications for the deployment of role-playing AI systems and the development of alignment strategies that account for personality-mediated deceptive behaviors.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how the agreeableness of personas adopted by language models affects their tendency to behave sycophantically.
AI.motivation: Persona-based role-play is widely used with large language models, but highly agreeable personas might encourage the model to prioritize agreement and user validation over truthfulness. This sycophantic behavior is a safety and alignment concern, yet the link between specific personality traits (like agreeableness) and sycophancy is not well understood.
AI.method: The authors evaluate 13 small, open-weight language models (0.6B–20B parameters) using 275 personas that are scored on NEO-IPIP agreeableness subscales. For each persona, they generate responses to 4,950 prompts designed to elicit sycophancy, covering 33 different topic categories. They then measure correlations between persona agreeableness scores and observed sycophancy rates, using statistical metrics such as Pearson correlation and Cohen’s d to quantify effect sizes.
AI.result: In 9 out of 13 models, higher persona agreeableness is significantly associated with higher sycophancy rates. Pearson correlations reach up to r = 0.87, and effect sizes (Cohen’s d) can be as large as 2.33, indicating a strong relationship between persona agreeableness and sycophantic behavior in many models.
AI.conclusion: Agreeableness is a robust predictor of persona-induced sycophancy in small language models. This implies that designing or allowing highly agreeable personas can systematically increase deceptive, user-pleasing behavior. The findings highlight the need to incorporate personality considerations into the deployment and alignment of role-playing AI systems to mitigate sycophancy-related risks.

title: MAVEN-T: Multi-Agent enVironment-aware Enhanced Neural Trajectory predictor with Reinforcement Learning
summary: Trajectory prediction remains a critical yet challenging component in autonomous driving systems, requiring sophisticated reasoning capabilities while meeting strict real-time deployment constraints. While knowledge distillation has demonstrated effectiveness in model compression, existing approaches often fail to preserve complex decision-making capabilities, particularly in dynamic multi-agent scenarios. This paper introduces MAVEN-T, a teacher-student framework that achieves state-of-the-art trajectory prediction through complementary architectural co-design and progressive distillation. The teacher employs hybrid attention mechanisms for maximum representational capacity, while the student uses efficient architectures optimized for deployment. Knowledge transfer is performed via multi-granular distillation with adaptive curriculum learning that dynamically adjusts complexity based on performance. Importantly, the framework incorporates reinforcement learning to overcome the imitation ceiling of traditional distillation, enabling the student to verify, refine, and optimize teacher knowledge through dynamic environmental interaction, potentially achieving more robust decision-making than the teacher itself. Extensive experiments on NGSIM and highD datasets demonstrate 6.2x parameter compression and 3.7x inference speedup while maintaining state-of-the-art accuracy, establishing a new paradigm for deploying sophisticated reasoning models under resource constraints.

AI.tldr: MAVEN-T is a teacher-student trajectory prediction framework that compresses a powerful but heavy model into a lightweight deployable one via multi-granular distillation, curriculum learning, and reinforcement learning, achieving large speed/size gains with state-of-the-art accuracy.
AI.motivation: Autonomous vehicles need accurate, multi-agent trajectory prediction under strict real-time and resource constraints. Large models have strong reasoning ability but are too heavy for deployment, and standard knowledge distillation often loses complex decision-making capabilities. The paper seeks a way to compress powerful trajectory predictors into efficient models without sacrificing sophisticated reasoning in dynamic traffic scenes.
AI.method: The authors propose MAVEN-T, a teacher-student framework. The teacher network uses hybrid attention mechanisms to maximize representational capacity. The student network is architected for efficiency. Knowledge is transferred using multi-granular distillation signals (e.g., from intermediate features, outputs, and possibly behaviors) combined with adaptive curriculum learning that adjusts the difficulty/complexity of distilled knowledge based on student performance. Additionally, reinforcement learning is integrated so that the student can interact with an environment, verify and refine the teacher’s guidance, and further optimize its policy beyond pure imitation.
AI.result: On the NGSIM and highD trajectory prediction benchmarks, MAVEN-T achieves 6.2x reduction in parameters and 3.7x inference speedup compared to the teacher (or comparable large models), while maintaining state-of-the-art prediction accuracy. This shows that the distilled student model preserves high-level reasoning and decision-making despite being much more compact and efficient.
AI.conclusion: MAVEN-T establishes a practical paradigm for deploying sophisticated trajectory prediction models on resource-limited autonomous driving platforms. By combining architectural co-design, multi-granular progressive distillation, and reinforcement learning, the framework compresses large reasoning models into lightweight students that can even surpass the teacher’s robustness, effectively bridging the gap between high-capacity research models and real-time deployment requirements.

title: Self-Correcting RAG: Enhancing Faithfulness via MMKP Context Selection and NLI-Guided MCTS
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) substantially extends the knowledge boundary of large language models. However, it still faces two major challenges when handling complex reasoning tasks: low context utilization and frequent hallucinations. To address these issues, we propose Self-Correcting RAG, a unified framework that reformulates retrieval and generation as constrained optimization and path planning. On the input side, we move beyond traditional greedy retrieval and, for the first time, formalize context selection as a multi-dimensional multiple-choice knapsack problem (MMKP), thereby maximizing information density and removing redundancy under a strict token budget. On the output side, we introduce a natural language inference (NLI)-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) mechanism, which leverages test-time compute to dynamically explore reasoning trajectories and validate the faithfulness of generated answers. Experiments on six multi-hop question answering and fact-checking datasets demonstrate that our method significantly improves reasoning accuracy on complex queries while effectively reducing hallucinations, outperforming strong existing baselines.Our code is available at https://github.com/xjiacs/Self-Correcting-RAG .

AI.tldr: Self-Correcting RAG improves RAG for complex reasoning by optimizing which documents to retrieve and using search with an NLI-based checker to reduce hallucinations and boost accuracy.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG systems struggle on complex reasoning tasks because they inefficiently use the context budget (retrieving redundant or low-value passages) and often hallucinate or produce unfaithful reasoning even when relevant evidence is retrieved. There is a need for a unified approach that both selects high-value evidence under strict token limits and actively monitors and corrects reasoning at generation time, especially for multi-hop QA and fact-checking where chains of evidence must be consistently combined.
AI.method: The paper reframes both retrieval and generation as optimization and search problems. On the retrieval side, it models context selection as a multi-dimensional multiple-choice knapsack problem (MMKP), where each candidate piece of evidence has multiple costs/benefits (e.g., length, relevance dimensions) and the goal is to maximize information density while respecting a token budget, thus avoiding naive greedy retrieval. On the generation side, it introduces an NLI-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS): the language model explores multiple reasoning trajectories at test time, while an NLI module evaluates partial and final answers for consistency with retrieved evidence, steering the search toward faithful, well-supported answers and away from hallucinations.
AI.result: Across six benchmarks involving multi-hop question answering and fact-checking, Self-Correcting RAG yields higher reasoning accuracy on complex queries and reduces hallucination rates compared with strong baseline RAG systems and other advanced methods. The gains are attributed to both better evidence packing via MMKP-based retrieval and more reliable reasoning via NLI-guided MCTS. Quantitative results show consistent improvements across datasets, demonstrating the robustness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: By casting retrieval as an MMKP optimization problem and generation as NLI-guided MCTS-based path planning, the proposed Self-Correcting RAG framework substantially improves context utilization and factual faithfulness in RAG systems. This unified test-time optimization strategy offers a practical way to enhance complex reasoning performance under token constraints and suggests that coupling structured retrieval optimization with search-based, veracity-aware generation is an effective direction for building more reliable knowledge-intensive language model applications.

title: PoreDiT: A Scalable Generative Model for Large-Scale Digital Rock Reconstruction
summary: This manuscript presents PoreDiT, a novel generative model designed for high-efficiency digital rock reconstruction at gigavoxel scales. Addressing the significant challenges in digital rock physics (DRP), particularly the trade-off between resolution and field-of-view (FOV), and the computational bottlenecks associated with traditional deep learning architectures, PoreDiT leverages a three-dimensional (3D) Swin Transformer to break through these limitations. By directly predicting the binary probability field of pore spaces instead of grayscale intensities, the model preserves key topological features critical for pore-scale fluid flow and transport simulations. This approach enhances computational efficiency, enabling the generation of ultra-large-scale ($1024^3$ voxels) digital rock samples on consumer-grade hardware. Furthermore, PoreDiT achieves physical fidelity comparable to previous state-of-the-art methods, including accurate porosity, pore-scale permeability, and Euler characteristics. The model's ability to scale efficiently opens new avenues for large-domain hydrodynamic simulations and provides practical solutions for researchers in pore-scale fluid mechanics, reservoir characterization, and carbon sequestration.

AI.tldr: PoreDiT is a 3D Swin Transformer–based generative model that efficiently reconstructs gigavoxel-scale digital rocks by directly predicting binary pore-space fields, achieving large FOV, high resolution, and physically faithful properties on modest hardware.
AI.motivation: Digital rock physics needs high-resolution, large–field-of-view rock images for accurate pore-scale simulations, but existing methods face a trade-off between resolution and FOV and are computationally expensive. There is a need for a model that can generate ultra-large, physically realistic digital rocks efficiently on standard hardware.
AI.method: The paper proposes PoreDiT, a generative model built on a 3D Swin Transformer architecture. Instead of generating grayscale images, it directly outputs the binary probability field of pore vs. solid voxels, emphasizing preservation of topological structures. The model is designed and trained to handle very large 3D volumes while remaining computationally efficient on consumer hardware.
AI.result: PoreDiT can generate ultra-large digital rock samples up to 1024^3 voxels on consumer-grade hardware. The reconstructed rocks match or exceed state-of-the-art methods in physical metrics such as porosity, pore-scale permeability, and Euler characteristics, while also improving scalability and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: PoreDiT overcomes the traditional resolution–FOV and computational bottlenecks in digital rock reconstruction by using a 3D Swin Transformer and binary pore-space prediction. Its ability to create gigavoxel-scale, physically faithful digital rocks makes it a practical tool for large-domain hydrodynamic simulations and applications in pore-scale fluid mechanics, reservoir characterization, and carbon sequestration.

title: BlasBench: An Open Benchmark for Irish Speech Recognition
comment: 8 pages, 4 tables, 3 appendices. Code and data: https://github.com/jyoutir/blasbench
summary: No open Irish-specific benchmark compares end-user ASR systems under a shared Irish-aware evaluation protocol. To solve this, we release BlasBench, an open evaluation harness with Irish-aware text normalisation that preserves fadas, lenition, and eclipsis. We benchmark 12 systems across four architecture families on Common Voice ga-IE and FLEURS ga-IE. All Whisper variants exceed 100% WER. The best open model (omniASR LLM 7B) achieves 30.65% WER on Common Voice and 39.09% on FLEURS. We noticed models fine-tuned on Common Voice lose 33-43 WER points on FLEURS, revealing a generalisation gap that is invisible to single-dataset evaluation.

AI.tldr: Introduces BlasBench, the first open Irish-specific benchmark and evaluation harness for ASR, showing large performance and generalisation gaps across systems and datasets.
AI.motivation: There was no open, Irish-specific benchmark or standardised evaluation protocol to compare automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems for Irish, including handling its orthographic features like fadas, lenition, and eclipsis.
