Analysis shows that the high-entropy mode learns behavior patterns that are measurably different from the normal mode, indicating successful differentiated exploration.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling exploration and exploitation into two collaboratively trained modes within a single LLM policy can achieve better exploration–accuracy balance than standard entropy-regularized RL. Policy Split enables effective dual-mode exploration, with the exploratory mode providing distinctive learning signals that improve overall performance on RL-tuned LLMs.

title: Learning Preference-Based Objectives from Clinical Narratives for Sequential Treatment Decision-Making
summary: Designing reward functions remains a central challenge in reinforcement learning (RL) for healthcare, where outcomes are sparse, delayed, and difficult to specify. While structured data capture physiological states, they often fail to reflect the overall quality of a patient's clinical trajectory, including recovery dynamics, treatment burden, and stability. Clinical narratives, in contrast, summarize longitudinal reasoning and implicitly encode evaluations of treatment effectiveness. We propose Clinical Narrative-informed Preference Rewards (CN-PR), a framework for learning reward functions directly from discharge summaries by treating them as scalable supervision for trajectory-level preferences. Using a large language model, we derive trajectory quality scores (TQS) and construct pairwise preferences over patient trajectories, enabling reward learning via a structured preference-based objective. To account for variability in narrative informativeness, we incorporate a confidence signal that weights supervision based on its relevance to the decision-making task. The learned reward aligns strongly with trajectory quality (Spearman rho = 0.63) and enables policies that are consistently associated with improved recovery-related outcomes, including increased organ support-free days and faster shock resolution, while maintaining comparable performance on mortality. These effects persist under external validation. Our results demonstrate that narrative-derived supervision provides a scalable and expressive alternative to handcrafted or outcome-based reward design for dynamic treatment regimes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CN-PR, a method that learns reinforcement learning reward functions for healthcare directly from clinical narratives (discharge summaries) instead of hand-crafted or outcome-only rewards, and shows that these learned rewards better capture overall trajectory quality and lead to clinically better policies.
AI.motivation: In healthcare RL, rewards are hard to design because outcomes are sparse, delayed, and cannot easily capture nuanced aspects like recovery dynamics, treatment burden, and stability. Structured data miss the clinician’s holistic judgment about the entire hospital course, while discharge summaries and other narratives naturally encode clinicians’ assessments of how well treatments worked. The authors want a scalable way to turn this rich but unstructured supervision into a usable reward signal for RL, avoiding brittle hand-crafted rewards and over-reliance on single outcomes such as mortality.
AI.method: The authors propose Clinical Narrative-informed Preference Rewards (CN-PR). They use a large language model to read discharge summaries and assign each patient trajectory a trajectory quality score (TQS). From these scores they build pairwise preferences between trajectories (which one is better), and then learn a reward function using a structured preference-based objective, similar to preference-based RL. Because not all narratives are equally informative for the treatment decision problem, they add a confidence weighting that down-weights supervision from less relevant or less informative summaries. This produces a reward model over trajectories that is then used to train RL policies for dynamic treatment regimes.
AI.result: The learned reward function correlates strongly with the LLM-derived trajectory quality scores (Spearman rho = 0.63), indicating alignment with the narrative-assessed quality. Policies optimized under this reward show improved recovery-related outcomes, including more organ support–free days and faster resolution of shock, while maintaining comparable mortality performance. These gains hold up under external validation, suggesting the approach generalizes across settings.
AI.conclusion: Narrative-derived supervision from discharge summaries can be converted into trajectory-level preference information that supports learning expressive, clinically meaningful reward functions for healthcare RL. CN-PR provides a scalable alternative to hand-designed or single-outcome-based rewards and leads to policies associated with better recovery metrics without harming mortality. This suggests that leveraging clinical narratives is a promising direction for building more aligned and effective decision-support policies in healthcare.

title: Triviality Corrected Endogenous Reward
summary: Reinforcement learning for open-ended text generation is constrained by the lack of verifiable rewards, necessitating reliance on judge models that require either annotated data or powerful closed-source models. Inspired by recent work on unsupervised reinforcement learning for mathematical reasoning using confidence-based endogenous rewards, we investigate whether this principle can be adapted to open-ended writing tasks. We find that directly applying confidence rewards leads to Triviality Bias: the policy collapses toward high-probability outputs, reducing diversity and meaningful content. We propose TCER (Triviality Corrected Endogenous Reward), which addresses this bias by rewarding the relative information gain between a specialist policy and a generalist reference policy, modulated by a probability-dependent correction mechanism. Across multiple writing benchmarks and model architectures, TCER achieves consistent improvements without external supervision. Furthermore, TCER also transfers effectively to mathematical reasoning, validating the generality of our approach across different generation tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TCER, a new unsupervised reinforcement learning reward for open-ended text generation that avoids collapsing to trivial, high-confidence outputs and improves performance across writing and math reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning for open-ended text generation lacks clear, verifiable rewards, so it often depends on judge models that need annotations or proprietary systems. Recent work showed that confidence-based endogenous rewards can work in mathematical reasoning without external supervision, raising the question of whether a similar idea can be used for open-ended writing. However, directly extending confidence rewards to such tasks leads to a collapse toward safe, high-probability text, which reduces diversity and usefulness. The paper aims to overcome this limitation and enable effective unsupervised RL for more general generation tasks.
AI.method: The authors first attempt to transfer confidence-based endogenous rewards—originally used for math reasoning—to open-ended writing and empirically show that this induces Triviality Bias, where the policy prefers generic, high-probability outputs. To correct this, they propose TCER (Triviality Corrected Endogenous Reward). TCER computes rewards based on the relative information gain of a specialist policy versus a generalist reference policy, encouraging the specialist to provide additional, task-specific information beyond generic responses. This signal is further modulated by a probability-dependent correction term that counteracts the tendency to over-reward trivially confident outputs. The approach is applied within a reinforcement learning framework to fine-tune language models across different benchmarks and architectures, without external labels or judge models.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across multiple open-ended writing benchmarks and various model architectures show that TCER consistently improves generation quality compared to baselines and naive confidence-based rewards, without requiring human annotations or external judge models. The method also generalizes beyond writing: when applied to mathematical reasoning tasks, TCER yields strong performance, demonstrating successful transfer and validating that the proposed reward shaping principle is broadly applicable to different text generation domains.
AI.conclusion: TCER effectively extends the idea of endogenous, confidence-based rewards from structured domains like math reasoning to open-ended text generation by correcting for Triviality Bias. By rewarding relative information gain between a specialist and a generalist policy and adjusting for output probability, it enables unsupervised reinforcement learning that improves model performance without external supervision. The demonstrated gains across writing and mathematical reasoning suggest that TCER is a general, scalable approach for training language models via self-supervised reinforcement learning.

title: TorchUMM: A Unified Multimodal Model Codebase for Evaluation, Analysis, and Post-training
summary: Recent advances in unified multimodal models (UMMs) have led to a proliferation of architectures capable of understanding, generating, and editing across visual and textual modalities. However, developing a unified framework for UMMs remains challenging due to the diversity of model architectures and the heterogeneity of training paradigms and implementation details. In this paper, we present TorchUMM, the first unified codebase for comprehensive evaluation, analysis, and post-training across diverse UMM backbones, tasks, and datasets. TorchUMM supports a broad spectrum of models covering a wide range of scales and design paradigms. Our benchmark encompasses three core task dimensions: multimodal understanding, generation, and editing, and integrates both established and novel datasets to evaluate perception, reasoning, compositionality, and instruction-following abilities. By providing a unified interface and standardized evaluation protocols, TorchUMM enables fair and reproducible comparisons across heterogeneous models and fosters deeper insights into their strengths and limitations, facilitating the development of more capable unified multimodal systems. Code is available at: https://github.com/AIFrontierLab/TorchUMM.

AI.tldr: TorchUMM is a unified codebase and benchmark suite for evaluating, analyzing, and post-training diverse unified multimodal models across understanding, generation, and editing tasks.
AI.motivation: Unified multimodal models are rapidly evolving but are fragmented across many architectures, training setups, and evaluation practices, making it hard to compare them fairly or analyze their capabilities systematically.
AI.method: The authors build TorchUMM, a common PyTorch-based framework that provides a unified interface to many multimodal backbones, defines standardized evaluation protocols, and aggregates a wide range of datasets covering understanding, generation, and editing, with a focus on perception, reasoning, compositionality, and instruction following.
AI.result: TorchUMM supports a broad spectrum of UMMs of different scales and designs, and offers a comprehensive benchmark spanning three task dimensions with both existing and newly introduced datasets, enabling consistent evaluation and comparison.
AI.conclusion: A unified, extensible toolkit like TorchUMM makes multimodal research more comparable and reproducible, helps reveal strengths and weaknesses of different UMM architectures, and should accelerate progress toward more capable unified multimodal systems.

title: NovBench: Evaluating Large Language Models on Academic Paper Novelty Assessment
summary: Novelty is a core requirement in academic publishing and a central focus of peer review, yet the growing volume of submissions has placed increasing pressure on human reviewers. While large language models (LLMs), including those fine-tuned on peer review data, have shown promise in generating review comments, the absence of a dedicated benchmark has limited systematic evaluation of their ability to assess research novelty. To address this gap, we introduce NovBench, the first large-scale benchmark designed to evaluate LLMs' capability to generate novelty evaluations in support of human peer review. NovBench comprises 1,684 paper-review pairs from a leading NLP conference, including novelty descriptions extracted from paper introductions and corresponding expert-written novelty evaluations. We focus on both sources because the introduction provides a standardized and explicit articulation of novelty claims, while expert-written novelty evaluations constitute one of the current gold standards of human judgment. Furthermore, we propose a four-dimensional evaluation framework (including Relevance, Correctness, Coverage, and Clarity) to assess the quality of LLM-generated novelty evaluations. Extensive experiments on both general and specialized LLMs under different prompting strategies reveal that current models exhibit limited understanding of scientific novelty, and that fine--tuned models often suffer from instruction-following deficiencies. These findings underscore the need for targeted fine-tuning strategies that jointly improve novelty comprehension and instruction adherence.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NovBench, a benchmark to evaluate how well large language models can assess the novelty of research papers, and shows that current models struggle with this task.
AI.motivation: Academic peer review critically depends on judging the novelty of submissions, but the growing number of papers has overloaded human reviewers. Although LLMs are being explored to assist with peer review and can generate review-like comments, there is no systematic way to evaluate their specific capability to assess research novelty. This lack of a dedicated benchmark prevents rigorous comparison and improvement of LLMs for this crucial aspect of peer review.
AI.method: The authors construct NovBench, a large-scale benchmark of 1,684 paper–review pairs from a top NLP conference. For each paper, they extract novelty descriptions from the introduction and pair them with expert-written novelty evaluations from the actual reviews. They treat introductions as standardized statements of claimed novelty and expert assessments as a gold standard reference. On top of this dataset, they design a four-dimensional evaluation framework—Relevance, Correctness, Coverage, and Clarity—to rate LLM-generated novelty evaluations. They then run extensive experiments using both general-purpose and domain-specialized LLMs under multiple prompting strategies, analyzing performance along these four dimensions and diagnosing failure patterns, particularly for fine-tuned models.
AI.result: Empirical results show that existing LLMs, whether general or fine-tuned on review-like data, have only limited ability to correctly understand and evaluate scientific novelty. Even when models can produce review-style text, they often misjudge what is genuinely novel or fail to align with expert assessments. Fine-tuned models especially tend to have problems following detailed instructions, which reduces the quality and reliability of their novelty evaluations according to the proposed four-dimensional criteria.
AI.conclusion: NovBench provides the first dedicated benchmark for measuring LLMs’ capability to evaluate research novelty in support of peer review, along with a structured four-dimensional evaluation framework. The experimental findings reveal fundamental weaknesses in current models’ novelty understanding and instruction adherence, signaling that simply fine-tuning on review data is insufficient. The authors conclude that more targeted fine-tuning and modeling strategies are needed that explicitly emphasize both deeper comprehension of scientific novelty and robust instruction following if LLMs are to become dependable assistants in academic peer review.

title: CheeseBench: Evaluating Large Language Models on Rodent Behavioral Neuroscience Paradigms
summary: We introduce CheeseBench, a benchmark that evaluates large language models (LLMs) on nine classical behavioral neuroscience paradigms (Morris water maze, Barnes maze, T-maze, radial arm maze, star maze, operant chamber, shuttle box, conditioned place preference, and delayed non-match to sample), spanning six cognitive dimensions. Each task is grounded in peer-reviewed rodent protocols with approximate animal baselines. The agent receives a unified system prompt with no task-specific instructions and must discover goals purely from ASCII text observations and reward signals, much like a rodent placed into an unfamiliar apparatus. We evaluate six open-weight LLMs (3B to 72B parameters) on text-based ASCII renderings and compare against both a random baseline and a graph-based reinforcement learning agent. Our best model (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) reaches 52.6% average success on ASCII input, compared to 32.1% for random agents and 78.9% for approximate rodent baselines. We find that (1) scaling beyond 7B yields diminishing returns, (2) longer context history degrades performance, (3) chain-of-thought prompting hurts rather than helps, and (4) a vision-language architecture provides an advantage at 7B but hurts at 32B. Because the same model's performance ranges from 20% to 57% depending on interface parameters alone, these results characterize the agent-plus-interface system, not the model in isolation. Under this unified zero-shot ASCII protocol, current open-weight LLM agents remain well below approximate rodent reference values, particularly on tasks requiring spatial navigation and within-trial state tracking.

AI.tldr: CheeseBench is a benchmark to test how well large language models perform rodent-style behavioral neuroscience tasks in text-based environments, showing they are still far from rodent-level performance.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluations rarely test rodent-style cognitive and behavioral paradigms that are standard in neuroscience, so it is unclear how LLM agents compare to animal baselines on tasks involving navigation, learning from reward, and within-trial state tracking. The authors want a principled, neuroscience-grounded way to measure LLMs’ “behavioral intelligence” against well-studied rodent benchmarks, using realistic protocols and baselines.
AI.method: They design CheeseBench, a suite of nine classical rodent behavioral tasks (various mazes, operant chamber, shuttle box, conditioned place preference, delayed non-match to sample), implemented as text-based ASCII environments. Each task follows peer-reviewed rodent protocols and has approximate animal baseline scores. LLM agents get a single, unified system prompt with no task-specific instructions; they must infer goals from textual observations and reward signals, analogous to a rat in a new apparatus. The authors test six open-weight LLMs from 3B to 72B parameters, including vision-language variants, comparing them to a random policy and to a graph-based RL agent. They systematically vary interface parameters such as model size, context length, chain-of-thought prompting, modality (text vs vision-language), and input formatting, then measure success rates across tasks and cognitive dimensions.
AI.result: The best-performing model, Qwen2.5-VL-7B, achieves an average success rate of 52.6% on the ASCII tasks, better than the random baseline at 32.1% but still below approximate rodent baselines at 78.9%. Several systematic trends emerge: scaling models beyond 7B parameters yields diminishing performance gains; providing longer interaction history actually harms performance; chain-of-thought prompting reduces success rather than improving it; and vision-language architectures help at 7B but degrade performance at 32B. Performance for a fixed model can range widely (20–57%) purely due to changes in interface parameters.
AI.conclusion: CheeseBench shows that under a unified, zero-shot, text-based protocol grounded in rodent behavioral tasks, current open-weight LLM agents substantially underperform approximate rodent baselines, especially on spatial navigation and tasks requiring precise within-trial state tracking. Measured performance reflects not just the model but the full agent-plus-interface system, since interface design can drastically change outcomes. The benchmark exposes limits of current LLMs as general behavioral agents and provides a neuroscience-aligned framework for evaluating future models.

title: Time is Not a Label: Continuous Phase Rotation for Temporal Knowledge Graphs and Agentic Memory
summary: Structured memory representations such as knowledge graphs are central to autonomous agents and other long-lived systems. However, most existing approaches model time as discrete metadata, either sorting by recency (burying old-yet-permanent knowledge), simply overwriting outdated facts, or requiring an expensive LLM call at every ingestion step, leaving them unable to distinguish persistent facts from evolving ones. To address this, we introduce RoMem, a drop-in temporal knowledge graph module for structured memory systems, applicable to agentic memory and beyond. A pretrained Semantic Speed Gate maps each relation's text embedding to a volatility score, learning from data that evolving relations (e.g., "president of") should rotate fast while persistent ones (e.g., "born in") should remain stable. Combined with continuous phase rotation, this enables geometric shadowing: obsolete facts are rotated out of phase in complex vector space, so temporally correct facts naturally outrank contradictions without deletion. On temporal knowledge graph completion, RoMem achieves state-of-the-art results on ICEWS05-15 (72.6 MRR). Applied to agentic memory, it delivers 2-3x MRR and answer accuracy on temporal reasoning (MultiTQ), dominates hybrid benchmark (LoCoMo), preserves static memory with zero degradation (DMR-MSC), and generalises zero-shot to unseen financial domains (FinTMMBench).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RoMem, a temporal knowledge graph module that uses continuous phase rotation and learned volatility scores to distinguish stable from evolving facts, achieving state-of-the-art results on temporal reasoning tasks and improving agentic memory systems.
AI.motivation: Existing structured memory and temporal knowledge graph systems handle time crudely: they sort by recency, overwrite old facts, or require expensive LLM calls per update. This prevents them from robustly distinguishing persistent facts from changing ones, leading to either loss of important static knowledge or confusion between current and obsolete facts. The authors aim to create a temporal representation that can naturally prioritize temporally correct information without costly operations or deletions.
AI.method: RoMem augments knowledge graphs with a temporal module based on complex-valued embeddings and continuous phase rotation. Each relation is passed through a pretrained Semantic Speed Gate, which maps its text embedding to a volatility score indicating how fast it should rotate in complex space (e.g., roles like “president of” rotate quickly, birthplaces slowly). Over time, evolving facts are rotated out of phase while persistent ones stay aligned, enabling geometric shadowing where correct facts have higher scores than obsolete, contradictory ones. RoMem is designed as a drop-in component for structured memory and agent systems.
AI.result: On the temporal knowledge graph completion benchmark ICEWS05-15, RoMem achieves state-of-the-art performance with 72.6 MRR. When integrated into agentic memory, it yields 2–3x improvements in mean reciprocal rank and answer accuracy on temporal reasoning benchmarks (MultiTQ), outperforms baselines on a hybrid benchmark (LoCoMo), maintains static knowledge quality without degradation on DMR-MSC, and generalizes in a zero-shot manner to unseen financial domains on FinTMMBench.
AI.conclusion: RoMem provides a principled, efficient temporal knowledge representation that distinguishes stable from evolving facts via learned volatility and phase rotation. This geometric shadowing mechanism lets systems downweight obsolete facts without explicit deletion or heavy LLM calls, leading to strong empirical gains across temporal KGC and agentic memory benchmarks and demonstrating robust generalization to new domains.

title: Your Model Diversity, Not Method, Determines Reasoning Strategy
summary: Compute scaling for LLM reasoning requires allocating budget between exploring solution approaches ($breadth$) and refining promising solutions ($depth$). Most methods implicitly trade off one for the other, yet why a given trade-off works remains unclear, and validation on a single model obscures the role of the model itself. We argue that $\textbf{the optimal strategy depends on the model's diversity profile, the spread of probability mass across solution approaches, and that this must be characterized before any exploration strategy is adopted.}$ We formalize this through a theoretical framework decomposing reasoning uncertainty and derive conditions under which tree-style depth refinement outperforms parallel sampling. We validate it on Qwen-3 4B and Olmo-3 7B families, showing that lightweight signals suffice for depth-based refinement on low-diversity aligned models while yielding limited utility for high-diversity base models, which we hypothesize require stronger compensation for lower exploration coverage.

AI.tldr: Paper studies how to optimally allocate compute between breadth (exploring many solution paths) and depth (refining a few) when scaling LLM reasoning, showing it depends on the model’s diversity profile.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM reasoning methods choose various mixes of sampling many candidate solutions vs. deeply refining a few, but it is unclear why particular trade-offs work, and single-model evaluations hide how model diversity affects the best strategy.
AI.method: Propose a theoretical framework that decomposes reasoning uncertainty and formalizes the role of probability mass over different solution approaches; derive conditions when tree-style depth refinement should beat simple parallel sampling; empirically test on Qwen-3 4B and Olmo-3 7B model families using lightweight signals for depth-based refinement.
AI.result: Show that for low-diversity aligned models, cheap signals are enough to make depth-based refinement effective, while for high-diversity base models such refinement gives limited gains, likely because their broader exploration requires stronger correction for coverage gaps.
AI.conclusion: The optimal breadth–depth compute allocation for LLM reasoning is not universal but depends critically on the model’s diversity profile; practitioners should first characterize this diversity before choosing exploration and refinement strategies, and depth-based methods particularly suit low-diversity aligned models.

title: Relax: An Asynchronous Reinforcement Learning Engine for Omni-Modal Post-Training at Scale
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) post-training has proven effective at unlocking reasoning, self-reflection, and tool-use capabilities in large language models. As models extend to omni-modal inputs and agentic multi-turn workflows, RL training systems face three interdependent challenges: heterogeneous data flows, operational robustness at scale, and the staleness -- throughput tradeoff. We present \textbf{Relax} (Reinforcement Engine Leveraging Agentic X-modality), an open-source RL training engine that addresses these challenges through three co-designed architectural layers. First, an \emph{omni-native architecture} builds multimodal support into the full stack -- from data preprocessing and modality-aware parallelism to inference generation -- rather than retrofitting it onto a text-centric pipeline. Second, each RL role runs as an independent, fault-isolated service that can be scaled, recovered, and upgraded without global coordination. Third, service-level decoupling enables asynchronous training via the TransferQueue data bus, where a single staleness parameter smoothly interpolates among on-policy, near-on-policy, and fully asynchronous execution. Relax achieves a 1.20$\times$ end-to-end speedup over veRL on Qwen3-4B on-policy training. Its fully async mode delivers a 1.76$\times$ speedup over colocate on Qwen3-4B and a 2.00$\times$ speedup on Qwen3-Omni-30B, while all modes converge to the same reward level. Relax supports R3 (Rollout Routing Replay)~\cite{ma2025r3} for MoE models with only 1.9\% overhead, compared to 32\% degradation in veRL under the same configuration. It further demonstrates stable omni-modal RL convergence on Qwen3-Omni across image, text, and audio, sustaining over 2{,}000 steps on video without degradation. Relax is available at https://github.com/rednote-ai/Relax.

AI.tldr: Relax is an open-source, scalable RL post-training engine for multimodal and agentic LLMs that improves throughput while preserving learning quality.
AI.motivation: Existing RL post-training systems for LLMs struggle when extended to multimodal, multi-turn, agentic settings: they assume primarily text workflows, they are hard to operate robustly at scale, and they face a tradeoff between keeping training data fresh (on-policy) and achieving high throughput (asynchronous execution). The authors aim to build an RL engine that natively supports omni-modal data, is operationally robust and modular, and can flexibly navigate the staleness–throughput tradeoff without sacrificing convergence quality.
AI.method: The authors design Relax with three co-designed architectural layers: (1) an omni-native architecture that supports multimodal inputs throughout the stack (preprocessing, modality-aware parallelism, and inference), instead of bolting multimodality onto a text-centric pipeline; (2) a microservice-style decomposition where each RL role (e.g., rollout, learner, replay, etc.) runs as an independent, fault-isolated service that can be scaled, recovered, and upgraded independently; and (3) a service-level decoupling with a TransferQueue data bus that enables asynchronous RL training, controlled by a single staleness parameter that allows continuous interpolation between strictly on-policy, near-on-policy, and fully asynchronous modes. They also integrate R3 (Rollout Routing Replay) to support MoE models efficiently.
AI.result: On Qwen3-4B, Relax yields a 1.20× end-to-end speedup over veRL in on-policy training. In fully asynchronous mode, Relax achieves 1.76× speedup over colocate on Qwen3-4B and 2.00× speedup on Qwen3-Omni-30B, while all modes reach comparable reward levels, indicating that higher asynchrony does not hurt convergence. For MoE models with R3, Relax introduces only 1.9% overhead, versus a 32% performance degradation under veRL. The system also shows stable omni-modal RL convergence on Qwen3-Omni across image, text, and audio, and can sustain over 2,000 training steps on video tasks without degradation.
AI.conclusion: Relax provides a practical, open-source RL engine tailored to modern multimodal, agentic LLM settings, overcoming limitations of text-centric, tightly coupled RL pipelines. Its omni-native design, microservice decomposition, and TransferQueue-based asynchronous execution collectively deliver better scalability and throughput while maintaining training stability and reward convergence across models (dense and MoE) and modalities (text, image, audio, video).

title: A Benchmark for Gap and Overlap Analysis as a Test of KG Task Readiness
summary: Task-oriented evaluation of knowledge graph (KG) quality increasingly asks whether an ontology-based representation can answer the competency questions that users actually care about, in a manner that is reproducible, explainable, and traceable to evidence. This paper adopts that perspective and focuses on gap and overlap analysis for policy-like documents (e.g., insurance contracts), where given a scenario, which documents support it (overlap) and which do not (gap), with defensible justifications. The resulting gap/overlap determinations are typically driven by genuine differences in coverage and restrictions rather than missing data, making the task a direct test of KG task readiness rather than a test of missing facts or query expressiveness. We present an executable and auditable benchmark that aligns natural-language contract text with a formal ontology and evidence-linked ground truth, enabling systematic comparison of methods. The benchmark includes: (i) ten simplified yet diverse life-insurance contracts reviewed by a domain expert, (ii) a domain ontology (TBox) with an instantiated knowledge base (ABox) populated from contract facts, and (iii) 58 structured scenarios paired with SPARQL queries with contract-level outcomes and clause-level excerpts that justify each label. Using this resource, we compare a text-only LLM baseline that infers outcomes directly from contract text against an ontology-driven pipeline that answers the same scenarios over the instantiated KG, demonstrating that explicit modeling improves consistency and diagnosis for gap/overlap analyses. Although demonstrated for gap and overlap analysis, the benchmark is intended as a reusable template for evaluating KG quality and supporting downstream work such as ontology learning, KG population, and evidence-grounded question answering.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a benchmark to evaluate how well knowledge graphs built from policy-like documents (here, life-insurance contracts) can answer realistic user scenarios about coverage gaps and overlaps, with explicit evidence and explanations.
AI.motivation: Existing task-oriented KG evaluations often don’t directly test whether ontology-based KGs can answer concrete user questions in a reproducible, explainable, evidence-traceable way. Policy-like documents, such as insurance contracts, require fine-grained reasoning about what is and isn’t covered. There is a need for a benchmark that ties natural language contract text, formal ontologies, and scenario-based questions together so researchers can rigorously compare different methods (e.g., text-only LLMs vs. ontology-driven KGs) for gap/overlap analysis and broader KG quality assessment.
AI.method: The authors construct an executable benchmark centered on life-insurance contracts. They (1) curate ten simplified but diverse life-insurance contracts and have them reviewed by a domain expert; (2) develop a domain ontology (TBox) and populate its knowledge base (ABox) with structured facts extracted from the contracts; (3) design 58 structured coverage scenarios, each mapped to SPARQL queries over the KG; and (4) provide ground-truth labels indicating, for each contract, whether it supports or does not support each scenario, with clause-level text excerpts justifying these labels. They then compare two approaches on this benchmark: a text-only LLM baseline that directly infers outcomes from raw contract text and an ontology-driven pipeline that answers the same scenarios by querying the instantiated KG.
AI.result: The benchmark enables a head-to-head comparison between a text-only LLM approach and an ontology-based KG approach for determining coverage gaps and overlaps. The experiments show that the explicit, ontology-driven modeling of contract content leads to more consistent gap/overlap determinations and makes it easier to diagnose why a particular decision was made, compared to relying solely on LLM inference over text. The resource is executable (via SPARQL queries), auditable (backed by clause-level evidence), and structured to support systematic evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Explicit ontology-based knowledge graph modeling provides clear advantages for gap and overlap analysis of policy-like documents, yielding more consistent and diagnosable results than text-only LLM baselines. The presented benchmark—comprising contracts, ontology, populated KG, scenarios, queries, and evidence—serves not only to demonstrate this point but also as a reusable template for broader KG quality evaluation and related tasks such as ontology learning, KG population, and evidence-grounded QA.

title: Synthius-Mem: Brain-Inspired Hallucination-Resistant Persona Memory Achieving 94.4% Memory Accuracy and 99.6% Adversarial Robustness on LoCoMo
summary: Providing AI agents with reliable long-term memory that does not hallucinate remains an open problem. Current approaches to memory for LLM agents -- sliding windows, summarization, embedding-based RAG, and flat fact extraction -- each reduce token cost but introduce catastrophic information loss, semantic drift, or uncontrolled hallucination about the user. The structural reason is architectural: every published memory system on the LoCoMo benchmark treats conversation as a retrieval problem over raw or lightly summarized dialogue segments, and none reports adversarial robustness, the ability to refuse questions about facts the user never disclosed. We present Synthius-Mem, a brain-inspired structured persona memory system that takes a fundamentally different approach. Instead of retrieving what was said, Synthius-Mem extracts what is known about the person: a full persona extraction pipeline decomposes conversations into six cognitive domains (biography, experiences, preferences, social circle, work, psychometrics), consolidates and deduplicates per domain, and retrieves structured facts via CategoryRAG at 21.79 ms latency. On the LoCoMo benchmark (ACL 2024, 10 conversations, 1,813 questions), Synthius-Mem achieves 94.37% accuracy, exceeding all published systems including MemMachine (91.69%, adversarial score is not reported) and human performance (87.9 F1). Core memory fact accuracy reaches 98.64%. Adversarial robustness, the hallucination resistance metric that no competing system reports, reaches 99.55%. Synthius-Mem reduces token consumption by ~5x compared to full-context replay while achieving higher accuracy. Synthius-Mem achieves state-of-the-art results on LoCoMo and is, to our knowledge, the only persona memory system that both exceeds human-level performance and reports adversarial robustness.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Synthius-Mem, a structured, brain-inspired persona memory system for LLM agents that achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and hallucination resistance on the LoCoMo benchmark while using far fewer tokens than full-context replay.
AI.motivation: Existing long-term memory methods for LLM agents—sliding windows, summarization, embedding-based RAG, and flat fact extraction—either lose important information, drift semantically over time, or hallucinate details the user never disclosed. Moreover, current systems treat memory purely as retrieval over dialogue snippets and do not report adversarial robustness (the ability to refuse answering about undisclosed facts). The authors aim to design a memory architecture that is accurate, efficient, and robust against hallucination, especially for modeling user personas over long conversations.
AI.method: Synthius-Mem uses a brain-inspired, structured memory architecture focused on what is known about the person rather than what was said verbatim. A persona extraction pipeline decomposes conversation into six cognitive domains (biography, experiences, preferences, social circle, work, psychometrics). Within each domain, the system consolidates and deduplicates facts, storing them as structured persona memory. At query time, a CategoryRAG mechanism retrieves relevant structured facts very efficiently (~21.79 ms latency). The model is evaluated on the LoCoMo benchmark (10 conversations, 1,813 questions) and is also assessed for adversarial robustness—its ability to avoid hallucinating facts not present in the user’s disclosures.
AI.result: On the LoCoMo benchmark, Synthius-Mem achieves 94.37% accuracy, outperforming all published baselines, including MemMachine (91.69%) and reported human performance (87.9 F1). Its accuracy on core memory facts reaches 98.64%. It attains 99.55% adversarial robustness, meaning it almost never hallucinates answers about undisclosed user facts. Additionally, Synthius-Mem reduces token usage by about fivefold compared with full-context replay, while still obtaining higher accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that Synthius-Mem provides an effective, structured, and robust long-term persona memory for LLM agents. By organizing memory into cognitive domains and focusing on structured facts rather than raw dialogue retrieval, it achieves state-of-the-art performance on LoCoMo, surpasses human-level results, and is, to their knowledge, the only system that both reports and excels in adversarial robustness. This suggests structured persona-centric memory architectures may be a promising direction for building reliable long-term memory in AI agents.

title: Beyond Statistical Co-occurrence: Unlocking Intrinsic Semantics for Tabular Data Clustering
summary: Deep Clustering (DC) has emerged as a powerful tool for tabular data analysis in real-world domains like finance and healthcare. However, most existing methods rely on data-level statistical co-occurrence to infer the latent metric space, often overlooking the intrinsic semantic knowledge encapsulated in feature names and values. As a result, semantically related concepts like `Flu' and `Cold' are often treated as symbolic tokens, causing conceptually related samples to be isolated. To bridge the gap between dataset-specific statistics and intrinsic semantic knowledge, this paper proposes Tabular-Augmented Contrastive Clustering (TagCC), a novel framework that anchors statistical tabular representations to open-world textual concepts. Specifically, TagCC utilizes Large Language Models (LLMs) to distill underlying data semantics into textual anchors via semantic-aware transformation. Through Contrastive Learning (CL), the framework enriches the statistical tabular representations with the open-world semantics encapsulated in these anchors. This CL framework is jointly optimized with a clustering objective, ensuring that the learned representations are both semantically coherent and clustering-friendly. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that TagCC significantly outperforms its counterparts.

AI.tldr: They introduce TagCC, a deep clustering framework for tabular data that injects semantic knowledge from language models via contrastive learning, improving clustering quality over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing deep clustering methods for tabular data depend mainly on statistical co-occurrence patterns and ignore semantic relationships embedded in feature names and values. This leads to conceptually similar items (e.g., flu and cold) being treated as unrelated, which hurts clustering quality in domains like finance and healthcare. There is a need to connect tabular representations with richer open-world semantic knowledge.
AI.method: They propose Tabular-Augmented Contrastive Clustering (TagCC). First, Large Language Models are used to transform tabular features and values into textual anchors that capture underlying semantics. Then, a contrastive learning framework is employed to align and enrich the tabular statistical representations with these semantic anchors. This contrastive objective is trained jointly with a clustering loss so that the resulting latent space is simultaneously semantically meaningful and suitable for clustering.
AI.result: On multiple benchmark tabular datasets, TagCC yields better clustering performance than state-of-the-art deep clustering baselines, showing that incorporating LLM-derived semantic anchors improves representation quality and cluster structure.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating open-world semantics from LLM-generated textual anchors into tabular deep clustering via joint contrastive and clustering objectives leads to more semantically coherent and effective clusters than relying purely on statistical co-occurrence. This demonstrates the value of bridging tabular statistics with external semantic knowledge for real-world applications.

title: Phonological distances for linguistic typology and the origin of Indo-European languages
summary: We show that short-range phoneme dependencies encode large-scale patterns of linguistic relatedness, with direct implications for quantitative typology and evolutionary linguistics. Specifically, using an information-theoretic framework, we argue that phoneme sequences modeled as second-order Markov chains essentially capture the statistical correlations of a phonological system. This finding enables us to quantify distances among 67 modern languages from a multilingual parallel corpus employing a distance metric that incorporates articulatory features of phonemes. The resulting phonological distance matrix recovers major language families and reveals signatures of contact-induced convergence. Remarkably, we obtain a clear correlation with geographic distance, allowing us to constrain a plausible homeland region for the Indo-European family, consistent with the Steppe hypothesis.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that simple phoneme-level statistical models can capture large-scale relationships among languages and correlate with geography.
AI.motivation: To understand whether low-level phonological statistics (short-range phoneme dependencies) are sufficient to reflect higher-level genealogical and areal relationships between languages, and to provide quantitative tools for typology and evolutionary linguistics.
AI.method: Model phoneme sequences in 67 modern languages as second-order Markov chains within an information-theoretic framework; define an articulatorily informed phonological distance metric between these models; construct a phonological distance matrix from a multilingual parallel corpus and analyze it for family structure, contact effects, and geographic correlations.
AI.result: The phonological distance matrix derived from the Markov-chain models recovers major language families, shows patterns consistent with contact-induced convergence, and exhibits a clear correlation between phonological and geographic distance, which helps narrow down a plausible homeland region for the Indo-European family consistent with the Steppe hypothesis.
AI.conclusion: Short-range phoneme dependencies are powerful enough to encode large-scale patterns of linguistic relatedness. Simple second-order Markov models over phoneme sequences, when combined with articulatory features, provide an effective, quantitative tool for typology and evolutionary linguistics and can even inform debates about language homelands.

title: A Quantitative Definition of Intelligence
summary: We propose an operational, quantitative definition of intelligence for arbitrary physical systems. The intelligence density of a system is the ratio of the logarithm of its independent outputs to its total description length. A system memorizes if its description length grows with its output count; it knows if its description length remains fixed while its output count diverges. The criterion for knowing is generalization: a system knows its domain if a single finite mechanism can produce correct outputs across an unbounded range of inputs, rather than storing each answer individually. We argue that meaning over a domain is a selection and ordering of functions that produces correct outputs, and that a system whose intelligence density diverges necessarily captures this structure. The definition (1) places intelligence on a substrate-independent continuum from logic gates to brains, (2) blocks Putnam's pancomputationalist triviality argument via an independence condition on outputs, and (3) resolves Searle's Chinese Room Argument by showing that any finite rulebook handling an infinite domain must generalize.

AI.tldr: Defines a quantitative measure of intelligence density for physical systems based on output complexity vs. description length, linking intelligence to generalization over infinite domains.
AI.motivation: Current discussions of intelligence and computation lack a precise, substrate-independent quantitative definition that distinguishes genuine understanding and generalization from mere memorization or lookup tables, and that can address classic philosophical objections like Putnam’s pancomputationalism and Searle’s Chinese Room.
AI.method: Formally define “intelligence density” as the ratio of the logarithm of a system’s independent outputs to its total description length. Analyze how description length scales with the number of correct outputs over a domain, distinguishing systems whose description length grows with outputs (memorization) from those whose description length is bounded while outputs diverge (knowing). Use this formalism to characterize meaning as the selection and ordering of functions that map inputs to correct outputs, and to examine implications for philosophical arguments about computation and understanding.
AI.result: The paper shows that systems with bounded description length that nonetheless produce correct outputs across an unbounded input range must be performing genuine generalization rather than rote memorization. Such systems have diverging intelligence density and, by construction, embody the functional structure (meaning) of their domain. The framework yields a graded, substrate-independent measure that can be applied from minimal computational circuits up to biological brains.
AI.conclusion: Intelligence can be quantitatively captured as intelligence density: systems “know” a domain when a single finite mechanism yields correct outputs for infinitely many inputs, reflecting true generalization and encoded domain structure. This definition creates a continuum of intelligence across physical systems, blocks trivial pancomputationalist readings via an independence condition on outputs, and undercuts Searle’s Chinese Room by showing that any finite rulebook adequate for an infinite domain must constitute understanding in virtue of its generalization capacity.

title: MIXAR: Scaling Autoregressive Pixel-based Language Models to Multiple Languages and Scripts
summary: Pixel-based language models are gaining momentum as alternatives to traditional token-based approaches, promising to circumvent tokenization challenges. However, the inherent perceptual diversity across languages poses a significant hurdle for multilingual generalization in pixel space. This paper introduces MIXAR, the first generative pixel-based language model trained on eight different languages utilizing a range of different scripts. We empirically evaluate MIXAR against previous pixel-based models as well as comparable tokenizer-based models, demonstrating substantial performance improvement on discriminative and generative multilingual tasks. Additionally, we show how MIXAR is robust to languages never seen during the training. These results are further strengthened when scaling the model to 0.5B parameters which not only improves its capabilities in generative tasks like LAMBADA but also its robustness when challenged with input perturbations such as orthographic attacks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MIXAR, a multilingual pixel-based language model that outperforms prior pixel and token-based baselines and is robust to unseen languages and input perturbations.
AI.motivation: Tokenization creates complications for multilingual language modeling, especially across diverse scripts. Pixel-based language models avoid tokenization but struggle with perceptual diversity across languages and scripts in pixel space, limiting multilingual generalization. The authors aim to design a pixel-based model that can handle multiple languages and scripts effectively and robustly.
AI.method: They build MIXAR, a generative pixel-based language model trained directly on rendered text images from eight languages using various writing systems. They empirically compare MIXAR with earlier pixel-based models and with standard tokenizer-based models on both discriminative and generative multilingual tasks. They also explore scaling MIXAR up to 0.5B parameters and test robustness to unseen languages and to input perturbations such as orthographic attacks.
AI.result: MIXAR substantially outperforms previous pixel-based models and competitive tokenizer-based models on multilingual discriminative and generative benchmarks. The scaled 0.5B-parameter version further improves performance on generative tasks such as LAMBADA and shows enhanced robustness to adversarial perturbations of the input text. MIXAR also generalizes to languages that were not present during training.
AI.conclusion: A well-designed, scaled pixel-based language model can serve as a strong multilingual alternative to token-based approaches, effectively handling diverse scripts, generalizing to unseen languages, and exhibiting robustness to orthographic perturbations. Pixel-level modeling is therefore a viable pathway to overcoming tokenization-induced limitations in multilingual NLP.

title: ZoomR: Memory Efficient Reasoning through Multi-Granularity Key Value Retrieval
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown great performance on complex reasoning tasks but often require generating long intermediate thoughts before reaching a final answer. During generation, LLMs rely on a key-value (KV) cache for autoregressive decoding. However, the memory footprint of the KV cache grows with output length. Prior work on KV cache optimization mostly focus on compressing the long input context, while retaining the full KV cache for decoding. For tasks requiring long output generation, this leads to increased computational and memory costs. In this paper, we introduce ZoomR, a novel approach that enables LLMs to adaptively compress verbose reasoning thoughts into summaries and uses a dynamic KV cache selection policy that leverages these summaries while also strategically "zooming in" on fine-grained details. By using summary keys as a coarse-grained index during decoding, ZoomR uses the query to retrieve details for only the most important thoughts. This hierarchical strategy significantly reduces memory usage by avoiding full-cache attention at each step. Experiments across math and reasoning tasks show that our approach achieves competitive performance compared to baselines, while reducing inference memory requirements by more than $4\times$. These results demonstrate that a multi-granularity KV selection enables more memory efficient decoding, especially for long output generation.

AI.tldr: ZoomR compresses intermediate reasoning into summaries and uses a hierarchical KV-cache selection strategy to cut memory while preserving performance on long-form reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs need to store growing key-value (KV) caches during long autoregressive decoding, especially for chain-of-thought reasoning. Existing KV optimizations largely compress only the input context but leave the output-side KV cache intact, so memory and compute still scale poorly with long generated thoughts. There is a need for a decoding method that maintains reasoning quality while reducing the KV cache footprint for long outputs.
AI.method: ZoomR trains or configures LLMs to periodically summarize verbose intermediate reasoning into compact representations. It maintains a hierarchical KV cache with both fine-grained tokens and coarse-grained summary keys. During decoding, the model first attends over the summaries as a coarse index, and only if needed, "zooms in" to retrieve detailed token-level KV entries relevant to the current query. A dynamic KV selection policy decides which parts of the detailed cache to keep or fetch based on importance, thereby avoiding full-cache attention at every step.
AI.result: On math and general reasoning benchmarks, ZoomR matches or closely approaches the accuracy of strong baselines that use full KV caches, while reducing inference-time memory usage by more than 4×. The method maintains competitive task performance despite aggressive KV cache reduction, particularly on tasks requiring long chains of thought and long outputs.
AI.conclusion: A multi-granularity KV selection mechanism—combining compressed summaries with selective access to detailed token-level states—can significantly reduce KV cache memory usage during long-form decoding without substantially degrading reasoning performance. This demonstrates a practical direction for more memory-efficient deployment of LLMs on tasks with long intermediate reasoning and outputs.

title: Decomposing and Reducing Hidden Measurement Error in LLM Evaluation Pipelines
summary: LLM evaluations drive which models get deployed, which safety standards get adopted, and which research conclusions get published. Yet these scores carry hidden uncertainty: rephrasing the prompt, switching the judge model, or changing the temperature can shift results enough to flip rankings and reverse conclusions. Standard confidence intervals ignore this variance, producing under-coverage that worsens with more data. The unmeasured variance also creates an exploitable surface: model developers can optimize against measurement noise rather than genuine capability. This paper decomposes LLM pipeline uncertainty into its sources, distinguishes variance that shrinks with more data from sensitivity to researcher design choices, and projects the most efficient path to reducing total error. For benchmark builders, the same decomposition identifies which design choices contribute exploitable surface for gaming and prescribes designs that minimize it. Across ideology annotation, safety classification, MMLU benchmarking, and a human-validated propaganda audit, projection-optimized pipelines outperform 73\% of possible naive pipelines against a human baseline. On MMLU, optimized budget allocation halves estimation error compared to standard single-prompt evaluation at equivalent cost. A small-sample variance estimation exercise is sufficient to derive confidence intervals that approach nominal coverage when the model includes the relevant pipeline facets, and to generate recommendations for reducing measurement error and improving benchmark robustness.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes and quantifies hidden sources of uncertainty in LLM evaluation pipelines, shows that standard confidence intervals understate this uncertainty, and proposes a variance-decomposition framework that guides better benchmark design, budget allocation, and more reliable confidence intervals.
AI.motivation: LLM evaluation scores are central to decisions about deployment, safety standards, and research conclusions, yet they are unstable: small changes in prompts, judge models, or sampling parameters can flip model rankings. Existing practice largely ignores this instability, using confidence intervals that under-cover and leaving room for developers to overfit to measurement noise rather than true capability. There is a need for a principled understanding of where evaluation uncertainty comes from and how to reduce it efficiently.
AI.method: The authors decompose the total uncertainty in an LLM evaluation pipeline into multiple variance components associated with different design facets (e.g., prompt wording, judge choice, temperature). They distinguish components that decrease with more samples from those that are due to researcher design choices and thus do not vanish with data. They then formulate a projection-based or variance-decomposition framework to (1) estimate these components from relatively small samples, (2) optimize allocation of evaluation budget (e.g., over prompts, judges, items) to minimize total error, and (3) diagnose which benchmark design choices create exploitable surface for gaming. This framework is applied across several tasks, including ideology annotation, safety classification, MMLU, and a propaganda audit with human validation.
AI.result: Using their variance-decomposition and projection-optimized design, the authors construct evaluation pipelines that outperform 73% of naive pipelines when compared to a human baseline across multiple tasks. For MMLU, their optimized budget allocation achieves about a 50% reduction in estimation error relative to a standard single-prompt evaluation at the same total cost. They also show that with only a small-sample variance estimation exercise, one can build confidence intervals that are much closer to nominal coverage, provided the variance model correctly includes the key pipeline facets. The analysis reveals which components of pipeline design most contribute to exploitable gaming surface.
AI.conclusion: LLM evaluation pipelines contain substantial, structured uncertainty arising from both stochastic variation and researcher design choices, and standard evaluation practices substantially understate this uncertainty. By decomposing variance across pipeline facets and optimizing evaluation design, one can materially reduce estimation error, improve coverage of confidence intervals, and make benchmarks more robust to gaming. The proposed framework offers practical guidance to benchmark builders and practitioners on where to invest evaluation budget and how to design pipelines that better reflect true model capabilities.

title: CASK: Core-Aware Selective KV Compression for Reasoning Traces
comment: 25 pages, 8 figures, 3 main tables, appendices included
summary: In large language models performing long-form reasoning, the KV cache grows rapidly with decode length, creating bottlenecks in memory and inference stability. Existing reasoning-oriented KV compression has mostly followed an eviction-centered view: estimate token importance more accurately, then discard lower-ranked entries. Our analysis suggests that scorer refinement alone often fails to substantially reorganize the actual keep-set and may therefore not be the main lever for preserving reasoning behavior. We instead frame reasoning KV compression as a behavior-preserving structured consolidation problem. CASK partitions the decode-time reasoning trace into a protected core that anchors answer formation and intermediate state, and mergeable scratch with high redundancy. The core is preserved, while selective consolidation is applied only to the scratch. To address prompt-heavy regimes where the prefix can exhaust the budget before decode-stage compression becomes active, CASK further uses a two-stage design: prefix eviction followed by decode-stage consolidation. On the H100 reasoning gate, CASK shows higher full-KV continuation fidelity than TriAttention at matched budgets on both AIME24 and AIME25, with recurring cask@384 > triattention@512 crossings. In prompt-heavy replay, multi_news and vcsum act as decode-active witnesses, while qmsum and gov_report expose the prefix_budget_exhausted boundary. The overall evidence supports a simple conclusion: effective reasoning KV compression depends less on more elaborate scorer engineering than on combining core preservation with selective scratch consolidation to lower the usable budget frontier.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CASK, a new approach to KV-cache compression for long-form reasoning in LLMs that focuses on preserving core reasoning behavior via structured consolidation rather than just better token-importance scoring and eviction.
AI.motivation: Long-form reasoning with large language models requires storing a rapidly growing KV cache, which creates memory bottlenecks and can harm inference stability. Existing KV compression techniques for reasoning mostly try to improve how they score and evict tokens, but the authors find that simply refining the importance scorer often barely changes which tokens are kept and thus does not effectively protect reasoning behavior. There is a need for a KV compression method that more directly preserves the reasoning process while reducing memory usage, especially in prompt-heavy settings where the initial prefix alone can exceed the KV budget before decode-time compression even starts.
AI.method: The authors reconceptualize reasoning-oriented KV compression as a behavior-preserving structured consolidation problem. They propose CASK, which partitions the decode-time reasoning trace into two parts: (1) a protected core that anchors answer formation and maintains essential intermediate states, and (2) a mergeable scratch region that contains more redundant information. The core tokens are always preserved, while only the scratch tokens are subject to selective consolidation (merging). To handle prompt-heavy regimes where the long prefix may exhaust KV budget before decoding, CASK uses a two-stage scheme: first applying prefix eviction, then applying decode-stage consolidation during generation.
AI.result: On the H100 reasoning benchmark ("reasoning gate"), CASK achieves higher fidelity to full-KV continuations than TriAttention under the same KV budgets on AIME24 and AIME25 tasks. In particular, the authors observe multiple cases where CASK at KV budget 384 outperforms TriAttention at budget 512, indicating more efficient use of KV capacity. In prompt-heavy replay scenarios, multi_news and vcsum behave as tasks where decode-time consolidation is active, while qmsum and gov_report demonstrate conditions where the KV budget is already exhausted by the prefix, highlighting the regime boundary that CASK’s design targets.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that the key to effective KV compression for reasoning LLMs is not primarily more sophisticated token-importance scoring for eviction. Instead, performance gains come from explicitly preserving a core set of reasoning-critical tokens and applying selective consolidation only to redundant scratch regions, complemented by a two-stage prefix-then-decode strategy. This structured approach can push down the minimum usable KV budget while maintaining high-fidelity reasoning behavior compared to prior methods such as TriAttention.

title: A Triadic Suffix Tokenization Scheme for Numerical Reasoning
comment: 8 pages, 1 figure. This is a theoretical proposal of a novel numbers tokenization for LLMs. The code is available on GitHub. Previous version archived at Zenodo: DOI 10.5281/zenodo.18999577
summary: Standard subword tokenization methods fragment numbers inconsistently, causing large language models (LLMs) to lose positional and decimal structure - a primary driver of errors in arithmetic and scientific reasoning. We introduce Triadic Suffix Tokenization (TST), a deterministic scheme that partitions digits into three-digit triads and annotates each triad with an explicit magnitude marker. Critically, the scheme defines a fixed, one-to-one mapping between suffixes and orders of magnitude for the integer part (thousands, millions, billions, etc.) and a parallel system of replicated markers for fractional depth (tenths, thousandths, millionths, etc.). Unlike approaches that rely on positional inference, this method provides a consistent gradient signal, which should ensure stable convergence. Two implementation variants are proposed: (1) a vocabulary-based approach that adds at most 10,000 fixed tokens to an existing vocabulary, covering 33 orders of magnitude ($10^{-15}$ to $10^{18}$); and (2) a suffix-marker approach that uses a small set of special tokens to denote magnitude dynamically. Both variants preserve exact digits while making order-of-magnitude relationships transparent at the token level. The framework is inherently scalable, allowing for linear vocabulary expansion to accommodate arbitrary precision and range. TST is architecture-agnostic and can be integrated as a drop-in preprocessing step. Experimental validation is deferred to future work.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Triadic Suffix Tokenization (TST), a new number tokenization scheme for LLMs that preserves decimal structure and order-of-magnitude information via triads of digits with explicit magnitude markers, aiming to improve numerical and scientific reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing subword tokenizers break numbers into inconsistent pieces (e.g., mixing digits with punctuation and partial digit groups), so LLMs lose both the positional meaning of digits and clear decimal structure. This leads to frequent errors in arithmetic and scientific reasoning because the model cannot reliably infer order of magnitude or precise numeric relationships from its token inputs. The authors want a tokenization scheme that encodes numeric structure explicitly and consistently, while remaining scalable and compatible with current LLM architectures.
AI.method: They introduce Triadic Suffix Tokenization (TST), which deterministically splits the digits of a number into three-digit groups (triads) and attaches an explicit magnitude marker to each triad. For the integer part, each triad is labeled with a unique suffix corresponding to a specific order of magnitude (thousands, millions, billions, etc.); for the fractional part, a parallel marker system is used to encode tenths, thousandths, millionths, and deeper decimal places. TST guarantees a one-to-one mapping between suffixes and magnitudes, ensuring consistent representation across all numbers. The method is architecture-agnostic and can be implemented in two ways: (1) a vocabulary-based variant that predefines up to 10,000 extra tokens to cover numbers from 10^-15 to 10^18; and (2) a suffix-marker variant that uses a small fixed set of special tokens to indicate magnitude dynamically while keeping digit groups as standard tokens. Both variants maintain the exact digit sequence while enhancing transparency of magnitude relations at the token level.
AI.result: The paper is conceptual and does not report empirical results. It specifies the design of the TST scheme, demonstrates that it can cover at least 33 orders of magnitude with a bounded vocabulary expansion, and argues that the scheme yields a consistent gradient signal for learning numerical structure because token-to-magnitude mappings are fixed and unambiguous. It also analyzes scalability, claiming linear growth in vocabulary size with increased precision and numerical range.
AI.conclusion: Triadic Suffix Tokenization offers a principled way to encode numbers for LLMs by grouping digits into triads and annotating them with explicit magnitude markers for both integer and fractional parts. This preserves precise digits while making order-of-magnitude relationships directly observable to the model, which should improve learning stability and numerical reasoning. The method is modular, scalable, and independent of model architecture, enabling it to be used as a drop-in preprocessing step for existing and future LLMs. However, its practical benefits still need to be validated through experiments in subsequent work.

title: Self-Evolving LLM Memory Extraction Across Heterogeneous Tasks
summary: As LLM-based assistants become persistent and personalized, they must extract and retain useful information from past conversations as memory. However, the types of information worth remembering vary considerably across tasks. We formalize the \textit{heterogeneous memory extraction} task and introduce \textbf{BEHEMOTH}, a benchmark that repurposes 18 existing datasets spanning personalization, problem-solving, and agentic tasks, using a downstream utility-driven metric for systematic evaluation. Our empirical analysis confirms that no single static extraction prompt dominates across all task categories, and that existing self-evolving prompt optimization frameworks, originally designed for homogeneous distributions, degrade when training tasks are heterogeneous. To address this, we propose \textbf{CluE}, a cluster-based self-evolving strategy that groups training examples into clusters by extraction scenarios, analyzes each cluster independently, and synthesizes cross-cluster insights to update the extraction prompt. Experiments on BEHEMOTH show that CluE generalizes effectively across heterogeneous tasks ($+$9.04\% relative gain), consistently outperforming prior self-evolving frameworks.

AI.tldr: Benchmark and method for extracting useful heterogeneous memories from LLM conversations, showing a new cluster-based self-evolving strategy works better than prior methods.
AI.motivation: LLM assistants need to remember useful information from diverse past conversations, but what is useful varies by task type. Existing memory extraction prompts and self-evolving frameworks assume more homogeneous data and perform poorly when tasks are heterogeneous, creating a need for a benchmark and methods tailored to this setting.
AI.method: They formalize the heterogeneous memory extraction task and build BEHEMOTH, a benchmark combining 18 datasets across personalization, problem-solving, and agentic tasks, with a downstream utility-based evaluation metric. They empirically test static extraction prompts and existing self-evolving prompt optimization frameworks, then propose CluE, a cluster-based self-evolving method that clusters training examples by extraction scenarios, optimizes prompts per cluster, and then synthesizes cross-cluster insights into a unified extraction prompt.
AI.result: Experiments on BEHEMOTH show that no single static prompt performs best across all task types and that existing self-evolving approaches degrade under heterogeneous tasks. CluE achieves better generalization on heterogeneous tasks with a 9.04% relative performance gain over baselines and consistently outperforms prior self-evolving frameworks on the benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Heterogeneous memory extraction is a distinct, practically important challenge for LLM-based assistants. BEHEMOTH provides a systematic benchmark for this setting, and the proposed CluE framework, which explicitly models task heterogeneity via clustering and cross-cluster synthesis, yields significantly better memory extraction prompts than existing homogeneous self-evolving methods.

title: EvoNash-MARL: A Closed-Loop Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Framework for Medium-Horizon Equity Allocation
summary: Medium-to-long-horizon stock allocation presents significant challenges due toveak predictive structures, non-stadonary market regimes, and the degradationf signals following the application of transaction costs, capacity limits, and tail-isk constraints. Conventional approaches commonly rely on a single predictor orloosely coupled prediction-to-allocation pipeline, limiting robustness underThis work addresses a targeted design question: whetherlistribution shift. 1coupling reinforcement learning (RL), multi-agent policy populations, Policy-Space Response Oracle (PSRO)-style aggregation, league best-response trainingevolutionary replacement, and execution-aware checkpoint selection within ainified walk-forward loop improves allocator robustness at medium to longhorizons. The proposed framework, EvoNash-MARL, integrates these componentswithin an execution-aware allocation loop and further introduces a layeredpolicy architecture comprising a direction head and a risk head, nonlinear signalenhancement, feature-quality reweighting, and constraint-aware checkpointselection. Under a 120-window walk-forward protocol, the resolved v21configuration achieves mean excess Sharpe 0.7600 and robust score -0.0203,anking first among internal controls; on aligned daily out-of-sample returnsrom 2014-01-02 to 2024-01-05, it delivers 19.6% annualized return versus 11.7% for SPY, and in an extended walk-forward evaluation through 2026-02-10 it delivers 20.5% rersus 13.5%. The framework maintains positive performance under realistictress constraints and exhibits structured cross-market generalization; however,lobal strong significance under White's Reality Check (WRC) and SPA-lite testingestablished. Therefore, the results are presented as evidence supporting asnotnore stable medium-to long-horizon training and selection paradigm, ratherhan as prooffof universally superior market-timing performance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes EvoNash-MARL, a reinforcement-learning-based, multi-agent stock allocation framework that aims to improve robustness for medium-to-long investment horizons and shows better risk-adjusted performance than benchmarks, while stopping short of claiming universal market-timing superiority.
AI.motivation: Medium-to-long-horizon stock allocation is difficult because predictive signals are weak, market regimes shift over time, and transaction costs, capacity limits, and risk constraints severely degrade naive predictive models. Existing methods often use a single predictor or loosely connect prediction and allocation, making them fragile under distribution shifts. The authors want a more robust, execution-aware training and allocation paradigm.
AI.method: They design EvoNash-MARL, which combines reinforcement learning, multi-agent policy populations, Policy-Space Response Oracle (PSRO)-style policy aggregation, league-based best-response training, and evolutionary replacement, all within a unified walk-forward training loop. The framework is execution-aware (incorporating transaction and risk constraints), uses a layered policy with separate direction and risk heads, applies nonlinear enhancement of raw signals, reweights features based on quality, and selects checkpoints with awareness of constraints. Performance is evaluated using a 120-window walk-forward protocol and several robustness tests including White's Reality Check and SPA-lite.
AI.result: In the 120-window walk-forward setup, the chosen configuration (v21) attains a mean excess Sharpe ratio of 0.76 and a robust score of -0.0203, ranking first among the internal baselines. On daily out-of-sample data from 2014-01-02 to 2024-01-05, it achieves an annualized return of 19.6% compared to 11.7% for SPY. In an extended walk-forward period through 2026-02-10, it attains 20.5% annualized return versus 13.5% for SPY. The approach remains profitable under realistic stress constraints and shows some cross-market generalization, but fails to obtain strong global significance under WRC and SPA-lite tests.
AI.conclusion: EvoNash-MARL provides a more stable and robust medium-to-long-horizon training and selection paradigm for stock allocation compared with conventional single-predictor or loosely coupled approaches. Although it delivers promising performance and robustness metrics, statistical tests do not support a claim of universally superior market-timing ability, so the authors present it as an improved framework rather than definitive evidence of market-beating performance.

title: Utilizing and Calibrating Hindsight Process Rewards via Reinforcement with Mutual Information Self-Evaluation
summary: To overcome the sparse reward challenge in reinforcement learning (RL) for agents based on large language models (LLMs), we propose Mutual Information Self-Evaluation (MISE), an RL paradigm that utilizes hindsight generative self-evaluation as dense reward signals while simultaneously calibrating them against the environmental feedbacks. Empirically, MISE enables an agent to learn autonomously from dense internal rewards supplementing sparse extrinsic signals. Theoretically, our work provides the first formal foundation for the paradigm of generative self-rewarding. We prove that utilizing hindsight self-evaluation rewards is equivalent to minimizing an objective that combines mutual information with a KL divergence term between the policy and a proxy reward policy.
