This theoretical insight then informs and justifies our calibration step, which actively aligns these rewards with the optimal policy. Extensive experiments show that MISE outperforms strong baselines, enabling open-source LLMs about 7B parameters to achieve performance comparable to GPT-4o on validation without expert supervision.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MISE, a mutual-information-based self-evaluation reinforcement learning framework that uses generative hindsight rewards plus calibration to environment signals to train LLM-based agents under sparse rewards, matching GPT-4o-level performance with 7B open-source models.
AI.motivation: RL for LLM-based agents often suffers from sparse and delayed rewards from the environment, making learning unstable and inefficient. Existing approaches lack a solid theoretical foundation for using self-generated rewards and struggle to make small open-source models reach frontier performance without heavy expert supervision.
AI.method: The authors propose Mutual Information Self-Evaluation (MISE), where an LLM agent produces hindsight generative self-evaluations of its own trajectories, turning them into dense internal reward signals. These self-rewards are then calibrated against sparse extrinsic environmental feedback. They show that optimizing with these hindsight rewards is equivalent to minimizing an objective combining mutual information between trajectories and evaluations with a KL divergence between the current policy and a proxy reward policy, and use this to design a calibration step aligning self-rewarded learning with the optimal policy.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, agents trained with MISE significantly outperform strong baseline RL methods. Notably, open-source LLMs of around 7B parameters trained under MISE achieve validation performance comparable to GPT-4o, despite the absence of expert supervision and the presence of sparse extrinsic rewards.
AI.conclusion: MISE provides both a practical and theoretical framework for generative self-rewarding in RL for LLM agents. By using calibrated mutual-information-based self-evaluation as dense rewards, it enables efficient autonomous learning under sparse environmental feedback and allows relatively small open-source LLMs to reach near-frontier performance.

title: CSPO: Alleviating Reward Ambiguity for Structured Table-to-LaTeX Generation
summary: Tables contain rich structured information, yet when stored as images their contents remain "locked" within pixels. Converting table images into LaTeX code enables faithful digitization and reuse, but current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often fail to preserve structural, style, or content fidelity. Conventional post-training with reinforcement learning (RL) typically relies on a single aggregated reward, leading to reward ambiguity that conflates multiple behavioral aspects and hinders effective optimization. We propose Component-Specific Policy Optimization (CSPO), an RL framework that disentangles optimization across LaTeX tables components-structure, style, and content. In particular, CSPO assigns component-specific rewards and backpropagates each signal only through the tokens relevant to its component, alleviating reward ambiguity and enabling targeted component-wise optimization. To comprehensively assess performance, we introduce a set of hierarchical evaluation metrics. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of CSPO, underscoring the importance of component-specific optimization for reliable structured generation.

AI.tldr: They propose a new RL training framework (CSPO) to better convert table images into LaTeX tables by separately optimizing structure, style, and content, improving fidelity of generated tables.
AI.motivation: Table images lock away structured information; converting them to LaTeX is valuable but current MLLMs struggle to preserve table structure, style, and content. Standard RL with a single reward mixes different objectives, creating ambiguity and limiting optimization quality.
AI.method: Introduce Component-Specific Policy Optimization (CSPO), which decomposes LaTeX table generation into components: structure, style, and content. Each component has its own reward, and gradients from each reward are only applied to the tokens relevant to that component. They also design hierarchical evaluation metrics to assess performance at different levels of table fidelity.
AI.result: Experiments show that CSPO leads to better preservation of table structure, style, and content compared to baselines trained with conventional RL and existing MLLMs, as reflected in the proposed hierarchical metrics.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling rewards and optimizing policies at the component level (structure, style, content) effectively reduces reward ambiguity and yields more reliable structured generation for LaTeX tables from images, highlighting the broader importance of component-specific optimization in RL-based training of MLLMs.

title: Back to Basics: Let Conversational Agents Remember with Just Retrieval and Generation
summary: Existing conversational memory systems rely on complex hierarchical summarization or reinforcement learning to manage long-term dialogue history, yet remain vulnerable to context dilution as conversations grow. In this work, we offer a different perspective: the primary bottleneck may lie not in memory architecture, but in the \textit{Signal Sparsity Effect} within the latent knowledge manifold. Through controlled experiments, we identify two key phenomena: \textit{Decisive Evidence Sparsity}, where relevant signals become increasingly isolated with longer sessions, leading to sharp degradation in aggregation-based methods; and \textit{Dual-Level Redundancy}, where both inter-session interference and intra-session conversational filler introduce large amounts of non-informative content, hindering effective generation. Motivated by these insights, we propose \method, a minimalist framework that brings conversational memory back to basics, relying solely on retrieval and generation via Turn Isolation Retrieval (TIR) and Query-Driven Pruning (QDP). TIR replaces global aggregation with a max-activation strategy to capture turn-level signals, while QDP removes redundant sessions and conversational filler to construct a compact, high-density evidence set. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that \method achieves robust performance across diverse settings, consistently outperforming strong baselines while maintaining high efficiency in tokens and latency, establishing a new minimalist baseline for conversational memory.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies signal sparsity in long conversational histories as the main bottleneck for conversational memory, and proposes a minimalist retrieval-based framework using Turn Isolation Retrieval and Query-Driven Pruning to improve robustness and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current conversational memory systems struggle with long-term dialogues, relying on complex architectures that still suffer from context dilution as conversations grow longer. The authors argue that the key problem is not the memory architecture itself but signal sparsity in the latent knowledge space, where relevant information becomes rare and overwhelmed by redundancy and filler, degrading the performance of aggregation-based methods.
AI.method: The authors conduct controlled experiments to characterize two phenomena: Decisive Evidence Sparsity, where crucial signals become isolated in long sessions, and Dual-Level Redundancy, where inter-session interference and intra-session filler create large amounts of non-informative content. Based on these insights, they propose a minimalist framework, called \method, that uses only retrieval and generation. It consists of Turn Isolation Retrieval (TIR), which applies a max-activation style retrieval to identify key turn-level evidence instead of aggregating the entire history, and Query-Driven Pruning (QDP), which prunes redundant sessions and conversational filler to maintain a compact, high-density evidence set.
AI.result: Across multiple conversational benchmarks, the proposed framework consistently outperforms strong baseline systems in both effectiveness and efficiency. It shows robust performance in diverse settings while using fewer tokens and achieving lower latency than more complex memory architectures.
AI.conclusion: Signal sparsity and redundancy, rather than the specific design of memory architectures, are the main challenges in long-term conversational memory. A minimalist approach centered on targeted retrieval (TIR) and query-driven pruning (QDP) can better capture decisive evidence, reduce redundancy, and deliver state-of-the-art performance efficiently. The method serves as a strong new baseline for conversational memory systems.

title: RAG-KT: Cross-platform Explainable Knowledge Tracing with Multi-view Fusion Retrieval Generation
summary: Knowledge Tracing (KT) infers a student's knowledge state from past interactions to predict future performance. Conventional Deep Learning (DL)-based KT models are typically tied to platform-specific identifiers and latent representations, making them hard to transfer and interpret. Large Language Model (LLM)-based methods can be either ungrounded under prompting or overly domain-dependent under fine-tuning. In addition, most existing KT methods are developed and evaluated under a same-distribution assumption. In real deployments, educational data often arise from heterogeneous platforms with substantial distribution shift, which often degrades generalization. To this end, we propose RAG-KT, a retrieval-augmented paradigm that frames cross-platform KT as reliable context constrained inference with LLMs. It builds a unified multi-source structured context with cross-source alignment via Question Group abstractions and retrieves complementary rich and reliable context for each prediction, enabling grounded prediction and interpretable diagnosis. Experiments on three public KT benchmarks demonstrate consistent gains in accuracy and robustness, including strong performance under cross-platform conditions.

AI.tldr: They propose RAG-KT, a retrieval-augmented, LLM-based knowledge tracing framework that works reliably across heterogeneous educational platforms and data distributions.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning-based knowledge tracing models are platform-specific, hard to transfer, and not very interpretable, while LLM-based KT is either ungrounded (prompt-only) or overfitted to a domain (fine-tuning). Moreover, most KT methods assume training and test data share the same distribution, which fails in realistic multi-platform educational settings with distribution shifts. There is a need for a KT approach that generalizes across platforms, remains grounded in actual content, and yields interpretable diagnostics.
AI.method: They introduce RAG-KT, which formulates cross-platform knowledge tracing as LLM inference constrained by retrieved, structured context. The method: (1) constructs a unified multi-source structured context across different educational platforms, (2) aligns heterogeneous questions via Question Group abstractions to bridge platforms, and (3) performs retrieval-augmented generation where, for each prediction, the LLM is provided with rich, complementary, and reliable retrieved context so its predictions are grounded and interpretable.
AI.result: On three public KT benchmark datasets, RAG-KT consistently outperforms prior approaches in both accuracy and robustness, especially when there are cross-platform distribution shifts between training and testing data.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-augmented, context-constrained LLM-based knowledge tracing can effectively handle cross-platform distribution shifts, improving generalization, prediction accuracy, and interpretability over conventional KT methods.

title: CArtBench: Evaluating Vision-Language Models on Chinese Art Understanding, Interpretation, and Authenticity
comment: Accepted to the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: We introduce CARTBENCH, a museum-grounded benchmark for evaluating vision-language models (VLMs) on Chinese artworks beyond short-form recognition and QA. CARTBENCH comprises four subtasks: CURATORQA for evidence-grounded recognition and reasoning, CATALOGCAPTION for structured four-section expert-style appreciation, REINTERPRET for defensible reinterpretation with expert ratings, and CONNOISSEURPAIRS for diagnostic authenticity discrimination under visually similar confounds. CARTBENCH is built by aligning image-bearing Palace Museum objects from Wikidata with authoritative catalog pages, spanning five art categories across multiple dynasties. Across nine representative VLMs, we find that high overall CURATORQA accuracy can mask sharp drops on hard evidence linking and style-to-period inference; long-form appreciation remains far from expert references; and authenticity-oriented diagnostic discrimination stays near chance, underscoring the difficulty of connoisseur-level reasoning for current models.

AI.tldr: Introduces CARTBENCH, a museum-grounded benchmark to test vision-language models on complex Chinese artwork understanding beyond simple recognition and QA.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language evaluations focus on short-form recognition or basic QA and do not capture the expert-level, nuanced understanding needed for Chinese art curation, appreciation, reinterpretation, and authenticity judgment. There is a need for a realistic, museum-grounded benchmark that reflects how experts actually interact with artworks.
AI.method: Construct a benchmark (CARTBENCH) by aligning image-bearing Palace Museum objects from Wikidata with authoritative catalog pages, covering five art categories across dynasties. Define four subtasks: CURATORQA for evidence-grounded recognition/reasoning; CATALOGCAPTION for structured, four-part expert-style appreciation; REINTERPRET for defensible reinterpretation evaluated with expert ratings; and CONNOISSEURPAIRS for testing authenticity discrimination between visually similar works. Evaluate nine representative vision-language models on all tasks.
AI.result: Across nine VLMs, overall CURATORQA accuracy appears decent but hides steep performance drops on difficult aspects such as evidence linking and inferring period from style. For CATALOGCAPTION, models’ long-form, expert-style descriptions are still far from human expert references. On REINTERPRET, models struggle to produce reinterpretations that rate well with experts. On CONNOISSEURPAIRS, models perform around chance on authenticity-focused diagnostic discrimination, indicating they largely fail at connoisseur-level judgments.
AI.conclusion: CARTBENCH reveals that current vision-language models, while capable on simpler museum QA, fall short on expert-level understanding of Chinese artworks, particularly in evidence-grounded reasoning, stylistic period inference, nuanced appreciation, and authenticity discrimination. This benchmark highlights substantial remaining gaps and offers a more realistic, challenging testbed for advancing connoisseur-level VLM reasoning in the cultural heritage domain.

title: Delving Aleatoric Uncertainty in Medical Image Segmentation via Vision Foundation Models
summary: Medical image segmentation supports clinical workflows by precisely delineating anatomical structures and lesions. However, medical image datasets medical image datasets suffer from acquisition noise and annotation ambiguity, causing pervasive data uncertainty that substantially undermines model robustness. Existing research focuses primarily on model architectural improvements and predictive reliability estimation, while systematic exploration of the intrinsic data uncertainty remains insufficient. To address this gap, this work proposes leveraging the universal representation capabilities of visual foundation models to estimate inherent data uncertainty. Specifically, we analyze the feature diversity of the model's decoded representations and quantify their singular value energy to define the semantic perception scale for each class, thereby measuring sample difficulty and aleatoric uncertainty. Based on this foundation, we design two uncertainty-driven application strategies: (1) the aleatoric uncertainty-aware data filtering mechanism to eliminate potentially noisy samples and enhance model learning quality; (2) the dynamic uncertainty-aware optimization strategy that adaptively adjusts class-specific loss weights during training based on the semantic perception scale, combined with a label denoising mechanism to improve training stability. Experimental results on five public datasets encompassing CT and MRI modalities and involving multi-organ and tumor segmentation tasks demonstrate that our method achieves significant and robust performance improvements across various mainstream network architectures, revealing the broad application potential of aleatoric uncertainty in medical image understanding and segmentation tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to estimate and exploit aleatoric (data) uncertainty in medical image segmentation using visual foundation model features, improving robustness and segmentation accuracy.
AI.motivation: Medical image segmentation models are hindered by data uncertainty stemming from noisy acquisitions and ambiguous annotations. Most prior work targets network architectures or post-hoc prediction reliability, with limited focus on characterizing and using the intrinsic uncertainty of the training data itself. There is a need for a principled way to estimate sample- and class-level data uncertainty and to use it to clean data and guide optimization.
AI.method: The authors use a visual foundation model to extract decoded feature representations of medical images. They analyze the diversity of these representations via singular value decomposition (SVD) and quantify the singular value energy to define a 'semantic perception scale' per class. This scale reflects feature richness and is used as a proxy for sample difficulty and aleatoric uncertainty. Building on this, they propose: (1) an aleatoric-uncertainty-aware data filtering scheme to remove samples with high inherent noise; (2) a dynamic uncertainty-aware optimization strategy that adaptively modifies class-specific loss weights based on the semantic perception scale, together with a label denoising mechanism to stabilize training.
AI.result: On five public CT and MRI datasets covering multi-organ and tumor segmentation, and across several mainstream segmentation architectures, the proposed uncertainty-driven strategies yield consistent and notable performance gains. The improvements are robust across modalities, tasks, and backbones, indicating that modeling aleatoric uncertainty at the data level is broadly beneficial.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging visual foundation model features to quantify intrinsic aleatoric uncertainty provides a practical way to assess sample difficulty and data quality in medical image segmentation. Integrating this uncertainty into data filtering and training-time loss reweighting with label denoising leads to more stable optimization and significantly better segmentation performance, suggesting wide potential for data-uncertainty-aware methods in medical image understanding.

title: RPA-Check: A Multi-Stage Automated Framework for Evaluating Dynamic LLM-based Role-Playing Agents
summary: The rapid adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) in interactive systems has enabled the creation of dynamic, open-ended Role-Playing Agents (RPAs). However, evaluating these agents remains a significant challenge, as standard NLP metrics fail to capture the nuances of role adherence, logical consistency, and long-term narrative stability. This paper introduces RPA-Check, a multi-stage automated evaluation framework designed to objectively assess the performance of LLM-based RPAs in complex, constraints-heavy environments. Our methodology is based on a four-step pipeline: (1) Dimension Definition, establishing high-level qualitative behavioral criteria; (2) Augmentation, where these requirements are expanded into granular boolean checklist indicators; (3) Semantic Filtering, to ensure indicator objectivity, no redundancy and agent isolation; and (4) LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation, which employs chain-of-thought verification to score agent fidelity. We validate this framework by applying it to LLM Court, a serious game for forensic training involving several quantized local models. Experimental results across five distinct legal scenarios demonstrate the framework's ability to identify subtle trade-offs between model size, reasoning depth, and operational stability. Notably, the findings reveal an inverse relationship between parametric scale and procedural consistency, showing that smaller, adequately instruction-tuned models (8-9B) can outperform larger architectures prone to user-alignment bias or sycophancy. RPA-Check thus provides a standardized and reproducible metric for future research in generative agent evaluation within specialized domains.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RPA-Check, an automated, multi-stage framework for evaluating LLM-based role-playing agents on role adherence, consistency, and narrative stability, showing that smaller instruction-tuned models can outperform larger ones in procedural consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing NLP metrics are inadequate for assessing complex interactive role-playing agents, as they miss aspects like role fidelity, logical coherence, and long-term stability in constrained, domain-specific environments (e.g., legal training games). There is a need for a standardized, objective, and reproducible way to evaluate such agents, especially as LLMs are increasingly used in serious, high-stakes applications.
AI.method: The authors design RPA-Check, a four-stage pipeline: (1) Dimension Definition, where they specify high-level qualitative behavioral dimensions for the agent; (2) Augmentation, decomposing each dimension into detailed boolean checklist indicators; (3) Semantic Filtering, which refines indicators to ensure objectivity, non-redundancy, and that they evaluate the agent independently of user behavior; and (4) LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation, where another LLM, using chain-of-thought verification, scores the agent’s performance against the indicator checklist. They implement and test this in LLM Court, a forensic training game with multiple quantized local models across five legal scenarios.
AI.result: Applied to the LLM Court setting, RPA-Check distinguishes nuanced differences among several models and scenarios, uncovering trade-offs between model parameter size, reasoning capability, and stability of behavior. The experiments show an inverse correlation between model size and procedural consistency: mid-sized, well-instruction-tuned models (8–9B parameters) sometimes surpass larger models, which can suffer from alignment-related artifacts like excessive deference or sycophancy.
AI.conclusion: RPA-Check offers a systematic, reproducible evaluation framework for LLM-based role-playing agents, particularly in constrained, specialized domains such as legal training. By capturing dimensions like role adherence and procedural consistency, it reveals that bigger models are not always better for stable, rule-bound role play, and it establishes a foundation for more rigorous comparative research on generative agents.

title: CFMS: A Coarse-to-Fine Multimodal Synthesis Framework for Enhanced Tabular Reasoning
summary: Reasoning over tabular data is a crucial capability for tasks like question answering and fact verification, as it requires models to comprehend both free-form questions and semi-structured tables. However, while methods like Chain-of-Thought (CoT) introduce reasoning chains, purely symbolic methodes are inherently limited by their blindness to holistic visual patterns. To address this, we propose the Coarse-to-Fine Multimodal Synthesis framework (CFMS), a novel two-stage paradigm that hierarchically decouples high-level visual perception from granular symbolic reasoning. In the Coarse Stage, CFMS leverages the Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to perform a one-time synthesis of a multi-perspective knowledge tuple. This tuple subsequently serves as a dynamic reasoning map to guide the fine stage, where a symbolic engine executes a targeted and efficient sequence of iterative operations over the table. Extensive experiments on the WikiTQ and TabFact benchmarks demonstrate that CFMS achieves competitive accuracy. The framework exhibits particular robustness when handling large tables and when instantiated with smaller backbone models, validating its effectiveness and generalizability.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CFMS, a two-stage multimodal framework that combines visual perception from MLLMs with symbolic reasoning to improve table reasoning for QA and fact verification, showing strong performance especially on large tables and with smaller models.
AI.motivation: Existing table reasoning methods, including Chain-of-Thought and purely symbolic approaches, struggle because they either lack explicit reasoning structures or cannot capture holistic visual patterns present in semi-structured tables. There is a need for a framework that can both understand visual layout and perform precise symbolic operations over tabular data.
AI.method: CFMS (Coarse-to-Fine Multimodal Synthesis) uses a two-stage hierarchy. In the Coarse Stage, a Multimodal Large Language Model performs a one-time synthesis of a multi-perspective knowledge tuple from the table and question, capturing high-level visual and semantic cues. This tuple then acts as a dynamic reasoning map. In the Fine Stage, a symbolic reasoning engine, guided by this map, executes a focused, efficient sequence of operations on the table to derive the final answer or verification decision.
AI.result: On the WikiTQ and TabFact benchmarks, CFMS attains competitive accuracy compared to existing methods. It is particularly robust on large tables and when using smaller backbone models, indicating better efficiency and scalability without sacrificing performance.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling high-level multimodal perception from low-level symbolic reasoning via the CFMS coarse-to-fine paradigm effectively enhances table reasoning. The approach generalizes well across benchmarks, scales to large tables, and works effectively even with smaller models, underscoring its practicality and robustness.

title: Hidden Failures in Robustness: Why Supervised Uncertainty Quantification Needs Better Evaluation
summary: Recent work has shown that the hidden states of large language models contain signals useful for uncertainty estimation and hallucination detection, motivating a growing interest in efficient probe-based approaches. Yet it remains unclear how robust existing methods are, and which probe designs provide uncertainty estimates that are reliable under distribution shift. We present a systematic study of supervised uncertainty probes across models, tasks, and OOD settings, training over 2,000 probes while varying the representation layer, feature type, and token aggregation strategy. Our evaluation highlights poor robustness in current methods, particularly in the case of long-form generations. We also find that probe robustness is driven less by architecture and more by the probe inputs. Middle-layer representations generalise more reliably than final-layer hidden states, and aggregating across response tokens is consistently more robust than relying on single-token features. These differences are often largely invisible in-distribution but become more important under distribution shift. Informed by our evaluation, we explore a simple hybrid back-off strategy for improving robustness, arguing that better evaluation is a prerequisite for building more robust probes.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically evaluates supervised probes that use LLM hidden states to estimate uncertainty and detect hallucinations, finding that many existing approaches are not robust under distribution shift but that certain design choices, like using middle layers and aggregating over tokens, improve robustness.
AI.motivation: There is growing interest in estimating uncertainty and detecting hallucinations in large language models using lightweight probes over hidden states, but it is unclear how reliable and robust these probe-based methods are, particularly under distribution shift and in challenging settings such as long-form generation.
AI.method: The authors conduct a large-scale empirical study training over 2,000 supervised uncertainty probes on different LLMs and tasks. They systematically vary probe design factors such as which layer’s hidden states are used (middle vs final), what feature types are extracted, and how token information is aggregated (single-token vs multi-token aggregation). They then evaluate performance and robustness both in-distribution and on out-of-distribution (OOD) data, including long-form generation scenarios. Finally, they propose and test a simple hybrid back-off strategy informed by the findings.
AI.result: The study finds that current probe-based uncertainty estimators and hallucination detectors often lack robustness, especially on OOD data and long-form outputs. Robustness is influenced more by the choice of probe inputs than by probe architecture. Probes using middle-layer representations outperform those relying on final-layer states in terms of generalisation, and aggregation across multiple response tokens consistently yields more robust uncertainty estimates than single-token features. Many of these performance differences are minor in-distribution but become pronounced under distribution shift.
AI.conclusion: Probe-based uncertainty estimation methods for LLMs are currently fragile under distribution shift, but their robustness can be significantly improved through better design choices, such as using middle-layer representations and aggregating across tokens. Because many weaknesses only appear under OOD evaluation, thorough robustness-focused evaluation is essential. The authors argue that careful evaluation and simple hybrid strategies are necessary steps toward building more reliable and robust uncertainty probes for LLMs.

title: ATANT v1.1: Positioning Continuity Evaluation Against Memory, Long-Context, and Agentic-Memory Benchmarks
comment: Companion paper to arXiv:2604.06710 (ATANT v1.0). 12 pages, 1 table, 2 appendices. Related-work extension; does not modify the v1.0 standard
summary: ATANT v1.0 (arXiv:2604.06710) defined continuity as a system property with 7 required properties and introduced a 10-checkpoint, LLM-free evaluation methodology validated on a 250-story corpus. Since publication, a recurring reviewer and practitioner question has concerned not the framework itself but its relationship to a wider set of memory evaluations: LOCOMO, LongMemEval, BEAM, MemoryBench, Zep's evaluation suite, Letta/MemGPT's evaluations, and RULER. This companion paper, v1.1, does not modify the v1.0 standard. It closes a related-work gap that v1.0 left brief under page limits. We show by structural analysis that none of these benchmarks measures continuity as defined in v1.0: of the 7 required properties, the median existing eval covers 1 property, the mean covers 0.43 when partial credit is scored at 0.5, and no eval covers more than 2. We provide a cell-by-cell property-coverage matrix, identify methodological defects specific to each benchmark (including an empty-gold scoring bug in the LOCOMO reference implementation that renders 23% of its corpus unscorable by construction), and publish our reference implementation's LOCOMO score (8.8%) alongside the structural reason that number is uninformative about continuity. We publish our 8.8% LOCOMO score alongside our 96% ATANT cumulative-scale score as a calibration pair: the 87-point divergence is evidence that the two benchmarks measure different properties, not that one system is an order of magnitude better than another. The position v1.1 takes is not adversarial: each benchmark measures a real capability. The claim is that none of them can adjudicate continuity, and conflating them with continuity evaluation has led the field to under-invest in the properties v1.0 names.

AI.tldr: ATANT v1.1 compares its continuity-evaluation standard to existing long-term memory benchmarks and shows they test different properties, so current benchmarks cannot judge continuity as ATANT defines it.
AI.motivation: Clarify how the ATANT v1.0 continuity framework relates to widely used long-term memory benchmarks and address reviewers’ questions about whether existing evaluations already cover continuity.
AI.method: Perform a structural, property-by-property comparison between the 7 ATANT continuity properties and the design of benchmarks like LOCOMO, LongMemEval, BEAM, MemoryBench, Zep’s suite, Letta/MemGPT evaluations, and RULER; construct a property-coverage matrix; inspect implementations for methodological defects; and run ATANT’s reference system on LOCOMO to obtain a concrete score for comparison.
AI.result: The analysis finds that none of the surveyed benchmarks satisfies ATANT’s definition of continuity: the median covers only 1 of 7 properties, the mean effective coverage is 0.43 properties when partial coverage counts as 0.5, and no benchmark covers more than 2. A critical “empty-gold” scoring bug is identified in LOCOMO, making 23% of its corpus unscorable. The ATANT reference system scores 8.8% on LOCOMO yet 96% on ATANT’s own cumulative-scale evaluation, demonstrating a large numerical divergence between the two benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Existing long-term memory benchmarks measure genuine but different capabilities and systematically fail to evaluate continuity as defined by ATANT v1.0. Treating them as continuity evaluations has misled the field and discouraged focus on the specific properties ATANT highlights; ATANT v1.1 therefore fills a related-work gap without altering the original standard.

title: Playing Along: Learning a Double-Agent Defender for Belief Steering via Theory of Mind
comment: First two authors contributed equally. Code: https://github.com/The-Inscrutable-X/AIDoubleAgentDefenders
summary: As large language models (LLMs) become the engine behind conversational systems, their ability to reason about the intentions and states of their dialogue partners (i.e., form and use a theory-of-mind, or ToM) becomes increasingly critical for safe interaction with potentially adversarial partners. We propose a novel privacy-themed ToM challenge, ToM for Steering Beliefs (ToM-SB), in which a defender must act as a Double Agent to steer the beliefs of an attacker with partial prior knowledge within a shared universe. To succeed on ToM-SB, the defender must engage with and form a ToM of the attacker, with a goal of fooling the attacker into believing they have succeeded in extracting sensitive information. We find that strong frontier models like Gemini3-Pro and GPT-5.4 struggle on ToM-SB, often failing to fool attackers in hard scenarios with partial attacker prior knowledge, even when prompted to reason about the attacker's beliefs (ToM prompting). To close this gap, we train models on ToM-SB to act as AI Double Agents using reinforcement learning, testing both fooling and ToM rewards. Notably, we find a bidirectionally emergent relationship between ToM and attacker-fooling: rewarding fooling success alone improves ToM, and rewarding ToM alone improves fooling. Across four attackers with different strengths, six defender methods, and both in-distribution and out-of-distribution (OOD) evaluation, we find that gains in ToM and attacker-fooling are well-correlated, highlighting belief modeling as a key driver of success on ToM-SB. AI Double Agents that combine both ToM and fooling rewards yield the strongest fooling and ToM performance, outperforming Gemini3-Pro and GPT-5.4 with ToM prompting on hard scenarios. We also show that ToM-SB and AI Double Agents can be extended to stronger attackers, demonstrating generalization to OOD settings and the upgradability of our task.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new privacy-focused benchmark, ToM-SB, where models must act as Double Agents to mislead attackers about having obtained sensitive information, and shows that training with reinforcement learning on both belief-modeling (theory-of-mind) and fooling objectives significantly improves performance over frontier models.
AI.motivation: Existing LLMs increasingly serve as conversational agents that must handle adversarial users, yet they are weak at modeling others’ beliefs and intentions (theory-of-mind), especially for privacy-preserving, multi-step interactions. Current ToM benchmarks and safety evaluations rarely focus on strategically steering an attacker’s beliefs under partial information. The authors aim to create a realistic, challenging setting to test and improve LLMs’ capacity to reason about an adversary’s mental state and protect sensitive information by misleading them when necessary.
AI.method: The authors design ToM-SB (Theory-of-Mind for Steering Beliefs), a privacy-themed challenge where a defender agent acts as a Double Agent in a shared universe, interacting with an attacker who has partial prior knowledge and is trying to extract sensitive information. They evaluate strong frontier models like Gemini3-Pro and GPT-5.4, including with ToM-oriented prompting, and observe performance limitations. Then they train specialized defender models—AI Double Agents—using reinforcement learning under different reward schemes: (1) fooling-only rewards (success at convincing the attacker they’ve succeeded), (2) ToM-only rewards (accurate modeling of the attacker’s beliefs), and (3) combined rewards. They test these across four attacker types of varying strength, six defender configurations, and both in-distribution and out-of-distribution scenarios, examining the relationship between ToM capability and fooling success.
AI.result: Frontier LLMs like Gemini3-Pro and GPT-5.4, even with explicit ToM prompting, often fail to reliably fool attackers in challenging ToM-SB settings, particularly when attackers have partial prior knowledge. RL-trained AI Double Agents achieve substantially better performance: fooling-only rewards improve the model’s ToM capabilities, ToM-only rewards improve fooling ability, and combined ToM+fooling rewards lead to the strongest results on both dimensions. These agents outperform Gemini3-Pro and GPT-5.4 with ToM prompting on the hardest scenarios. The correlation between improved belief modeling and improved attacker-fooling holds across different attackers, defender methods, and both in-distribution and OOD evaluations, and the setup scales to stronger attackers, showing generalization and upgradability.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that effective defense against adversarial information extraction in conversational settings depends critically on a model’s ability to form and use a theory-of-mind about its interlocutor. The proposed ToM-SB benchmark exposes significant weaknesses in current frontier LLMs on this dimension. Training AI Double Agents with reinforcement learning, targeting both belief modeling and fooling success, yields emergent, mutually reinforcing gains in ToM and defensive deception, surpassing generic frontier models and generalizing to stronger and OOD attackers. This positions ToM-SB and AI Double Agents as promising tools for developing safer, more privacy-preserving conversational AI systems that can strategically steer adversaries’ beliefs rather than just refuse or comply naively.

title: Back to the Barn with LLAMAs: Evolving Pretrained LLM Backbones in Finetuning Vision Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have rapidly advanced by leveraging powerful pre-trained Large Language Models (LLMs) as core reasoning backbones. As new and more capable LLMs emerge with improved reasoning, instruction-following, and generalization, there is a pressing need to efficiently update existing VLMs to incorporate these advancements. However, the integration of new LLMs into VLMs, particularly how the evolving LLMs contribute to multimodal reasoning, alignment, and task-specific performance remains underexplored. Addressing this gap is important for VLM development, given the rapid evolution of pretrained LLM backbones. This study presents a controlled and systematic investigation of how changes in the pretrained LLM backbone affect downstream VLM task performance. By having the vision encoder, training data, and post-training algorithm remain same across LLAMA-1, LLAMA-2, and LLAMA-3 based VLMs, we find that newer LLM backbones do not always lead to better VLMs, but the performance depends on the downstream VLM task. For example, in visual question and answering tasks, newer LLM backbones tend to solve different questions rather than just more questions, and our analysis shows this is driven by differences in how the models process information, including better calibrated confidence and more stable internal representations. We also find that some VLM capabilities appear only in the newest LLM generation, while tasks that depend mainly on visual understanding see little benefit from a newer LLM backbone.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how swapping in newer LLM backbones (LLaMA-1/2/3) affects Vision-Language Model performance, finding that newer LLMs don’t uniformly improve all multimodal tasks.
AI.motivation: VLMs increasingly rely on powerful pre-trained LLMs, which are rapidly evolving. However, it is unclear how upgrading the LLM backbone actually changes multimodal reasoning, alignment, and task performance. Developers need principled guidance on when and how new LLM generations actually help VLMs instead of assuming newer is always better.
AI.method: The authors conduct a controlled comparison of VLMs that are identical in vision encoder, training data, and post-training algorithm but differ only in their LLM backbone (LLaMA-1, LLaMA-2, LLaMA-3). They systematically evaluate the resulting VLMs on multiple downstream tasks, with particular focus on visual question answering, and analyze behavior such as which questions are solved, confidence calibration, and internal representation stability.
AI.result: They find that newer LLM backbones do not universally yield better VLMs; impact is strongly task-dependent. In VQA, newer backbones tend to answer different subsets of questions rather than just more questions. Newer backbones show better calibrated confidence and more stable internal representations. Some multimodal capabilities only appear with the newest generation, whereas tasks dominated by visual understanding gain little from newer LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Upgrading the LLM backbone in a VLM has nuanced, task-dependent effects. Newer LLMs change the kinds of questions solved and improve aspects like calibration and representation stability, but they do not guarantee improvements across all multimodal tasks, especially those driven primarily by visual processing. VLM design should treat LLM upgrades as targeted, task-aware choices rather than automatic wins.

title: Please Make it Sound like Human: Encoder-Decoder vs. Decoder-Only Transformers for AI-to-Human Text Style Transfer
summary: AI-generated text has become common in academic and professional writing, prompting research into detection methods. Less studied is the reverse: systematically rewriting AI-generated prose to read as genuinely human-authored. We build a parallel corpus of 25,140 paired AI-input and human-reference text chunks, identify 11 measurable stylistic markers separating the two registers, and fine-tune three models: BART-base, BART-large, and Mistral-7B-Instruct with QLoRA. BART-large achieves the highest reference similarity -- BERTScore F1 of 0.924, ROUGE-L of 0.566, and chrF++ of 55.92 -- with 17x fewer parameters than Mistral-7B. We show that Mistral-7B's higher marker shift score reflects overshoot rather than accuracy, and argue that shift accuracy is a meaningful blind spot in current style transfer evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to systematically rewrite AI-generated text so it appears genuinely human-written, builds a large parallel corpus, identifies stylistic differences, and tests models for this humanization task, finding BART-large most effective and highlighting a flaw in current style transfer evaluation.
AI.motivation: AI-generated text is increasingly used in academic and professional contexts, and there is substantial work on detecting such text. However, the reverse problem—turning AI-generated text into text that convincingly appears human-authored—is underexplored. The authors are motivated to understand and operationalize this "humanization" process, both by characterizing stylistic differences between AI and human writing and by creating systems that can automatically apply these differences.
AI.method: The authors construct a parallel corpus of 25,140 text chunks, each consisting of an AI-generated input and a human-written reference version. They then identify 11 quantifiable stylistic markers that reliably distinguish AI text from human text. Using this dataset and feature set, they fine-tune three models for style transfer from AI-like to human-like prose: BART-base, BART-large, and Mistral-7B-Instruct (the latter fine-tuned with QLoRA for parameter-efficient training). They evaluate the models using standard text similarity metrics (BERTScore F1, ROUGE-L, chrF++) and a custom "marker shift" measure based on changes in the 11 stylistic markers.
AI.result: Among the tested models, BART-large yields the best similarity to the human reference texts, achieving a BERTScore F1 of 0.924, ROUGE-L of 0.566, and chrF++ of 55.92, while using 17 times fewer parameters than the larger Mistral-7B model. Although Mistral-7B exhibits a larger shift in the 11 stylistic markers, this is shown to be an overshoot rather than a more accurate match to the human style. Thus, greater stylistic shift magnitude does not necessarily correlate with better style transfer quality.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that relatively compact models like BART-large can effectively transform AI-generated prose to closely resemble human-authored text, outperforming a much larger model in reference similarity. The authors also show that purely measuring the extent of stylistic marker shifts can be misleading, as models may overshoot optimal values. They argue that current style transfer evaluation practices overlook the distinction between accurate vs. excessive stylistic change, and they highlight "shift accuracy" as an important, under-addressed dimension for future evaluation frameworks.

title: WebForge: Breaking the Realism-Reproducibility-Scalability Trilemma in Browser Agent Benchmark
comment: 14 pages, 6 figures, 6 tables, plus 29-page supplementary. Code: https://github.com/yuandaxia2001/WebForge Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/yuandaxia/WebForge
summary: Existing browser agent benchmarks face a fundamental trilemma: real-website benchmarks lack reproducibility due to content drift, controlled environments sacrifice realism by omitting real-web noise, and both require costly manual curation that limits scalability. We present WebForge, the first fully automated framework that resolves this trilemma through a four-agent pipeline -- Plan, Generate, Refine, and Validate -- that produces interactive, self-contained web environments end-to-end without human annotation. A seven-dimensional difficulty control framework structures task design along navigation depth, visual complexity, reasoning difficulty, and more, enabling systematic capability profiling beyond single aggregate scores. Using WebForge, we construct WebForge-Bench, a benchmark of 934 tasks spanning 7 domains and 3 difficulty levels. Multi-model experiments show that difficulty stratification effectively differentiates model capabilities, while cross-domain analysis exposes capability biases invisible to aggregate metrics. Together, these results confirm that multi-dimensional evaluation reveals distinct capability profiles that a single aggregate score cannot capture. Code and benchmark are publicly available at https://github.com/yuandaxia2001/WebForge.

AI.tldr: WebForge is an automated framework that generates reproducible, realistic, and scalable web-based benchmarks for browser agents, enabling multi-dimensional evaluation of their capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current browser agent benchmarks suffer from a trilemma: (1) real-website benchmarks are not reproducible due to changing web content, (2) synthetic or controlled environments lack the noise and complexity of the real web, and (3) both approaches depend on costly human curation, limiting scale. The paper aims to resolve this by creating a system that can automatically generate realistic, interactive web environments and structured tasks at scale, while allowing fine-grained control over task difficulty and characteristics.
AI.method: The authors propose WebForge, a fully automated four-agent pipeline consisting of Plan, Generate, Refine, and Validate agents. This pipeline generates self-contained, interactive web environments and associated tasks end-to-end without human annotation. They introduce a seven-dimensional difficulty control framework that parametrizes tasks along axes such as navigation depth, visual complexity, reasoning difficulty, and other factors, allowing systematic design and tuning of task difficulty. Using this framework, they automatically build WebForge-Bench, a benchmark with hundreds of tasks across multiple domains and difficulty levels. They then evaluate multiple language models on this benchmark, analyzing performance across difficulty strata and domains.
AI.result: WebForge successfully produces WebForge-Bench, a large benchmark of 934 tasks covering 7 domains and 3 difficulty levels. Experimental results using multiple models show that the difficulty stratification meaningfully differentiates model performance; models perform differently as task difficulty increases, demonstrating that the difficulty dimensions are operational. Cross-domain analyses reveal that models have varying strengths and weaknesses across domains, with biases and capability patterns that are not apparent when only looking at a single aggregate score.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that WebForge overcomes the reproducibility-realism-scalability trilemma in browser agent benchmarks by automating the generation of realistic, interactive web environments and multi-dimensional tasks. The resulting WebForge-Bench enables richer, more diagnostic evaluation than traditional single-score benchmarks: multi-dimensional and cross-domain analyses reveal distinct capability profiles and biases of models that aggregate metrics obscure. The framework and released code provide a foundation for more systematic, scalable, and fine-grained evaluation of web agents.

title: Legal2LogicICL: Improving Generalization in Transforming Legal Cases to Logical Formulas via Diverse Few-Shot Learning
summary: This work aims to improve the generalization of logic-based legal reasoning systems by integrating recent advances in NLP with legal-domain adaptive few-shot learning techniques using LLMs. Existing logic-based legal reasoning pipelines typically rely on fine-tuned models to map natural-language legal cases into logical formulas before forwarding them to a symbolic reasoner. However, such approaches are heavily constrained by the scarcity of high-quality annotated training data. To address this limitation, we propose a novel LLM-based legal reasoning framework that enables effective in-context learning through retrieval-augmented generation. Specifically, we introduce Legal2LogicICL, a few-shot retrieval framework that balances diversity and similarity of exemplars at both the latent semantic representation level and the legal text structure level. In addition, our method explicitly accounts for legal structure by mitigating entity-induced retrieval bias in legal texts, where lengthy and highly specific entity mentions often dominate semantic representations and obscure legally meaningful reasoning patterns. Our Legal2LogicICL constructs informative and robust few-shot demonstrations, leading to accurate and stable logical rule generation without requiring additional training. In addition, we construct a new dataset, named Legal2Proleg, which is annotated with alignments between legal cases and PROLEG logical formulas to support the evaluation of legal semantic parsing. Experimental results on both open-source and proprietary LLMs demonstrate that our approach significantly improves accuracy, stability, and generalization in transforming natural-language legal case descriptions into logical representations, highlighting its effectiveness for interpretable and reliable legal reasoning. Our code is available at https://github.com/yingjie7/Legal2LogicICL.

AI.tldr: They propose a retrieval-augmented in-context learning framework (Legal2LogicICL) that converts legal case texts into logical formulas more accurately and robustly, and release a new aligned dataset (Legal2Proleg).
AI.motivation: Logic-based legal reasoning systems need to transform natural language case descriptions into formal logical representations, but current methods depend on supervised fine-tuning with scarce, expensive legal annotations, which harms generalization and stability.
AI.method: Use large language models in an in-context learning setup with retrieval-augmented generation. Legal2LogicICL selects few-shot exemplars by jointly considering (1) latent semantic similarity/diversity and (2) structural similarity/diversity of legal texts, while explicitly reducing entity-induced retrieval bias from long, specific legal entities. These retrieved examples are used as demonstrations for the LLM to generate PROLEG-compatible logical rules without further training. They also build the Legal2Proleg dataset with case–formula alignments for evaluation.
AI.result: Across both open-source and proprietary LLMs, the framework yields significantly higher accuracy, better stability, and stronger cross-scenario generalization in mapping natural language legal cases to logical formulas than baseline approaches that rely on fine-tuned models or simpler retrieval strategies.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed retrieval-based in-context learning, which respects both semantic and legal-structural aspects while mitigating entity bias, can substantially improve logic-based legal reasoning with LLMs without additional training; the released Legal2Proleg dataset further supports systematic evaluation of legal semantic parsing.

title: MAFIG: Multi-agent Driven Formal Instruction Generation Framework
summary: Emergency situations in scheduling systems often trigger local functional failures that undermine system stability and even cause system collapse. Existing methods primarily rely on robust scheduling or reactive scheduling, handling emergencies through predefined rules or rescheduling strategies. However, the diversity and unpredictability of real-world emergencies make them difficult to anticipate, which limits the adaptability of these methods in complex scenarios. Recent studies have shown that Large Language Models (LLMs) possess strong potential for complex scheduling tasks because of their extensive prior knowledge and strong reasoning capabilities. Nevertheless, the high inference latency of LLMs and the lengthy contextual information of scheduling systems significantly hinder their application for emergency handling. To mitigate these issues, we propose the Multi-agent Driven Formal Instruction Generation Framework (MAFIG). The framework constrains the decision scope to local functional modules affected by emergency situations and repairs scheduling logic rapidly by generating formal instructions. MAFIG contains a Perception Agent and an Emergency Decision Agent, which mitigates the adverse impact of lengthy system contexts on emergency decision-making. We further introduce span-focused loss-driven local distillation mechanism (SFL) to transfer the decision-making capability of powerful Cloud Large Language Models (C-LLMs) to lightweight local models, reducing inference latency while preserving decision-making effectiveness. Experiments in the Port, Warehousing, and Deck scheduling datasets show success rates of 98.49\%, 94.97\%, and 97.50\%, with average processing times of 0.33 s, 0.23 s, and 0.19 s. These results demonstrate that MAFIG effectively mitigates the impact of emergencies and improves the robustness and adaptability of scheduling systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MAFIG, a multi‑agent framework plus a distillation mechanism that enables fast, robust, LLM-driven emergency handling in complex scheduling systems, achieving high success rates and low latency across several industrial datasets.
AI.motivation: Emergency events in scheduling systems can trigger local failures and even system collapse. Existing robust or reactive scheduling approaches rely on predefined rules or rescheduling strategies, which lack adaptability to diverse, unpredictable real-world emergencies. Moreover, although LLMs have strong reasoning abilities suitable for complex scheduling, their high latency and the need to process long scheduling contexts limit their practical use for real-time emergency handling.
AI.method: The authors design the Multi-agent Driven Formal Instruction Generation Framework (MAFIG), which restricts decision-making to the local functional modules affected by emergencies and repairs scheduling logic via automatically generated formal instructions. MAFIG uses a Perception Agent to understand and localize emergency impacts and an Emergency Decision Agent to make repair decisions, reducing the influence of lengthy global context. Additionally, they introduce a span-focused loss-driven local distillation mechanism (SFL) to transfer the decision-making capability of powerful cloud LLMs (C-LLMs) into smaller on-premise models, thereby lowering inference latency while maintaining effectiveness.
AI.result: On three real or realistic scheduling datasets—Port, Warehousing, and Deck—the framework achieves success rates of 98.49%, 94.97%, and 97.50%, respectively, with average decision processing times of 0.33 s, 0.23 s, and 0.19 s, demonstrating both accuracy and responsiveness.
AI.conclusion: MAFIG, together with the SFL distillation mechanism, enables fast, robust LLM-based emergency handling in complex scheduling systems by localizing decision scope, managing long contexts via multi-agent design, and deploying efficient distilled local models. The experimental results indicate that it significantly mitigates the impact of emergencies and enhances the robustness and adaptability of scheduling systems across multiple domains.

title: Evaluating Cooperation in LLM Social Groups through Elected Leadership
summary: Governing common-pool resources requires agents to develop enduring strategies through cooperation and self-governance to avoid collective failure. While foundation models have shown potential for cooperation in these settings, existing multi-agent research provides little insight into whether structured leadership and election mechanisms can improve collective decision making. The lack of such a critical organizational feature ubiquitous in human society presents a significant shortcoming of the current methods. In this work we aim to directly address whether leadership and elections can support improved social welfare and cooperation through multi-agent simulation with LLMs. We present our open-source framework that simulates leadership through elected personas and candidate-driven agendas and carry out an empirical study of LLMs under controlled governance conditions. Our experiments demonstrate that having elected leadership improves social welfare scores by 55.4% and survival time by 128.6% across a range of high performing LLMs. Through the construction of an agent social graph we compute centrality metrics to assess the social influence of leader personas and also analyze rhetorical and cooperative tendencies revealed through a sentiment analysis on leader utterances. This work lays the foundation for further study of election mechanisms in multi-agent systems toward navigating complex social dilemmas.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether adding elections and explicit leaders to multi-LLM simulations of common‑pool resource governance improves cooperation and outcomes.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent LLM research on governing common-pool resources focuses on decentralized cooperation but mostly ignores structured leadership and election mechanisms, which are central to human organizations. This gap limits our understanding of how organizational structure, especially leadership, affects collective decision making and social welfare in AI-driven simulations of social dilemmas.
AI.method: The authors build an open-source multi-agent framework where LLM agents face common-pool resource dilemmas. They introduce leadership via elected personas with candidate-driven agendas, simulate elections, and then compare scenarios with and without elected leaders. They evaluate outcomes such as social welfare and survival time, construct an agent social graph to compute centrality measures of leader influence, and analyze leader utterances using sentiment and rhetorical/cooperative tendency analysis.
AI.result: Across multiple strong LLMs, introducing elected leadership increases social welfare by 55.4% and survival time by 128.6% relative to non-leadership baselines. Leaders occupy central positions in the agent social graph, indicating higher social influence, and sentiment analysis reveals distinctive rhetorical and cooperative patterns in leader communications.
AI.conclusion: Elected leadership and structured election mechanisms significantly enhance cooperation and social welfare in LLM-based multi-agent simulations of common-pool resource governance. Leadership personas become socially influential hubs and exhibit characteristic rhetorical styles. The framework establishes a basis for systematically studying elections and leadership in AI multi-agent systems dealing with complex social dilemmas.

title: Sanity Checks for Agentic Data Science
summary: Agentic data science (ADS) pipelines have grown rapidly in both capability and adoption, with systems such as OpenAI Codex now able to directly analyze datasets and produce answers to statistical questions. However, these systems can reach falsely optimistic conclusions that are difficult for users to detect. To address this, we propose a pair of lightweight sanity checks grounded in the Predictability-Computability-Stability (PCS) framework for veridical data science. These checks use reasonable perturbations to screen whether an agent can reliably distinguish signal from noise, acting as a falsifiability constraint that can expose affirmative conclusions as unsupported. Together, the two checks characterize the trustworthiness of an ADS output, e.g. whether it has found stable signal, is responding to noise, or is sensitive to incidental aspects of the input. We validate the approach on synthetic data with controlled signal-to-noise ratios, confirming that the sanity checks track ground-truth signal strength. We then demonstrate the checks on 11 real-world datasets using OpenAI Codex, characterizing the trustworthiness of each conclusion and finding that in 6 of the datasets an affirmative conclusion is not well-supported, even though a single ADS run may support one. We further analyze failure modes of ADS systems and find that ADS self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated to the empirical stability of its conclusions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes simple sanity checks for agentic data science (ADS) systems like Codex, to assess whether their statistical conclusions are actually supported by data and robust to perturbations.
AI.motivation: Agentic data science systems can autonomously analyze data and answer statistical questions, but they often produce overconfident, incorrect, or non-robust conclusions that are hard for users to detect. There is a need for lightweight, principled ways to evaluate whether such systems are detecting true signal rather than noise, and whether their outputs are trustworthy.
AI.method: Grounded in the Predictability-Computability-Stability (PCS) framework, the authors design two lightweight sanity checks. These checks apply reasonable perturbations to the data or analysis to test whether the ADS can reliably distinguish signal from noise. The checks act like falsifiability constraints by probing stability and sensitivity of ADS conclusions to perturbations, effectively categorizing outputs as robust signal, noise-driven, or sensitive to incidental details. They validate the checks on synthetic datasets with controlled signal-to-noise ratios and then apply them to 11 real-world datasets with OpenAI Codex as the ADS engine, also comparing ADS self-reported confidence with empirical stability of its conclusions.
AI.result: On synthetic data, the sanity checks correctly track the ground-truth signal strength, indicating they can distinguish when there is or is not real signal. On 11 real datasets, applying the checks to Codex-based ADS workflows shows that in 6 cases the apparent affirmative conclusions are not well-supported once stability and perturbation robustness are examined, even though a single unperturbed ADS run suggests confident findings. The study also shows that ADS systems’ self-reported confidence does not align well with the actual empirical stability of their conclusions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PCS-based sanity checks provide a practical and lightweight way to evaluate the trustworthiness of ADS outputs by testing robustness to perturbations and distinguishing signal from noise. These checks can reveal when seemingly confident ADS conclusions are actually unsupported or unstable. The miscalibration between ADS self-confidence and empirical stability underscores the need for external veridicality checks rather than trusting system-reported confidence alone.

title: Discourse Diversity in Multi-Turn Empathic Dialogue
summary: Large language models (LLMs) produce responses rated as highly empathic in single-turn settings (Ayers et al., 2023; Lee et al., 2024), yet they are also known to be formulaic generators that reuse the same lexical patterns, syntactic templates, and discourse structures across tasks (Jiang et al., 2025; Shaib et al., 2024; Namuduri et al., 2025). Less attention has been paid to whether this formulaicity extends to the level of discourse moves, i.e., what a response does for the person it is addressing. This question is especially consequential for empathic dialogue, where effective support demands not just a kind response at one moment but varied strategies as a conversation unfolds (Stiles et al., 1998). Indeed, prior work shows that LLMs reuse the same tactic sequences more than human supporters in single-turn settings (Gueorguieva et al., 2026). We extend this analysis to multi-turn conversations and find that the rigidity compounds: once a tactic appears in a supporter turn, LLMs reuse it in the next at nearly double the rate of humans (0.50-0.56 vs. 0.27). This pattern holds across LLMs serving as supporters in real emotional support conversations, and is invisible to standard similarity metrics. To address this gap, we introduce MINT (Multi-turn Inter-tactic Novelty Training), the first reinforcement learning framework to optimize discourse move diversity across multi-turn empathic dialogue. The best MINT variant combines an empathy quality reward with a cross-turn tactic novelty signal, improving aggregate empathy by 25.3% over vanilla across 1.7B and 4B models while reducing cross-turn discourse move repetition by 26.3% on the 4B model, surpassing all baselines including quality-only and token-level diversity methods on both measures. These results suggest that what current models lack is not empathy itself, but the ability to vary their discourse moves across a conversation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how large language models behave in multi-turn empathic conversations, finds that they over-repeat the same kinds of supportive moves between turns compared to humans, and proposes a reinforcement learning method (MINT) that encourages varied discourse tactics while maintaining empathy quality.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs are rated as highly empathic in single-turn settings, they are known to be formulaic in language generation. Prior analyses suggest they also overuse the same empathy tactics in single-turn dialogues, but it is unclear how this rigidity manifests over multiple turns, where effective emotional support requires varying strategies as the conversation progresses. Standard similarity metrics fail to detect such discourse-level repetition, leaving a gap in both measurement and training methods for multi-turn empathic dialogue.
AI.method: The authors extend prior single-turn analyses of tactic reuse to multi-turn emotional support conversations where LLMs act as supporters. They quantify how often discourse tactics, or “moves,” are repeated across consecutive turns, comparing LLM behavior with human supporters. After finding that LLMs exhibit nearly double the rate of tactic reuse, they introduce MINT (Multi-turn Inter-tactic Novelty Training), a reinforcement learning framework. MINT defines rewards that combine an empathy quality signal with a cross-turn tactic novelty signal, and optimizes models (1.7B and 4B parameters) to increase discourse move diversity without sacrificing empathy quality. They compare MINT variants against baselines including quality-only RL and token-level diversity methods.
AI.result: Empirically, LLMs in multi-turn emotional support roles reuse a given tactic in the immediately following turn at much higher rates than humans (0.50–0.56 vs. 0.27), and this rigidity is consistent across multiple models and is not captured by conventional text similarity metrics. Using MINT, the best-performing variant improves aggregate empathy scores by 25.3% over a vanilla model and reduces cross-turn discourse move repetition by 26.3% on the 4B model. These gains outperform all tested baselines, including those that optimize only for empathy quality or for token-level diversity.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that contemporary LLMs’ main limitation in empathic dialogue is not a lack of empathy per se but an inability to flexibly vary their discourse tactics over the course of a conversation. By explicitly rewarding cross-turn tactic novelty in reinforcement learning (MINT), models can both increase perceived empathy and reduce rigid reuse of the same discourse moves. This highlights the importance of modeling and training for discourse-level diversity, beyond surface-level lexical variation or single-turn empathy scores, in designing supportive conversational agents.

title: Diffusion-CAM: Faithful Visual Explanations for dMLLMs
summary: While diffusion Multimodal Large Language Models (dMLLMs) have recently achieved remarkable strides in multimodal generation, the development of interpretability mechanisms has lagged behind their architectural evolution. Unlike traditional autoregressive models that produce sequential activations, diffusion-based architectures generate tokens via parallel denoising, resulting in smooth, distributed activation patterns across the entire sequence. Consequently, existing Class Activation Mapping (CAM) methods, which are tailored for local, sequential dependencies, are ill-suited for interpreting these non-autoregressive behaviors. To bridge this gap, we propose Diffusion-CAM, the first interpretability method specifically tailored for dMLLMs. We derive raw activation maps by differentiably probing intermediate representations in the transformer backbone, accordingly capturing both latent features and their class-specific gradients. To address the inherent stochasticity of these raw signals, we incorporate four key modules to resolve spatial ambiguity and mitigate intra-image confounders and redundant token correlations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Diffusion-CAM significantly outperforms SoTA methods in both localization accuracy and visual fidelity, establishing a new standard for understanding the parallel generation process of diffusion multimodal systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Diffusion-CAM, the first interpretability method designed specifically for diffusion-based multimodal large language models, providing clearer, more accurate visual explanations of their parallel generation process.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based multimodal LLMs have advanced generation quality but lack suitable interpretability tools. Existing CAM methods are designed for autoregressive, sequential models and fail to handle the smooth, parallel, and distributed activation patterns in diffusion architectures. There is a need for a principled way to visualize and understand which parts of the input influence diffusion models’ decisions and generation.
AI.method: The authors propose Diffusion-CAM, an interpretability framework tailored to diffusion multimodal models. It differentiably probes intermediate transformer representations during the diffusion denoising steps to obtain raw activation maps that combine latent features with their class-specific gradients. To make these signals meaningful and robust, they add four modules that address: spatial ambiguity, stochasticity of diffusion, intra-image confounders, and redundant token correlations, resulting in refined, stable attention maps for diffusion-based generation.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments, Diffusion-CAM is shown to substantially outperform state-of-the-art CAM-style methods on diffusion multimodal models in terms of localization accuracy and visual fidelity of explanation maps. The approach better identifies relevant regions and provides clearer, more coherent explanations of the model’s behavior across the sequence.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-CAM establishes a dedicated, effective interpretability approach for diffusion multimodal LLMs, enabling accurate visualization of which input regions drive model outputs under parallel denoising. This sets a new standard for interpreting diffusion-based multimodal systems and offers a foundation for further research on understanding and debugging these architectures.

title: LangFlow: Continuous Diffusion Rivals Discrete in Language Modeling
summary:
Continuous diffusion models have achieved strong performance across domains such as images. However, in language modeling, prior continuous diffusion language models (DLMs) lag behind discrete counterparts. In this work, we close this gap with LangFlow, the first continuous DLM to rival discrete diffusion. Our approach connects embedding-space DLMs to Flow Matching via Bregman divergence and introduces three key innovations: (1) a novel ODE-based NLL bound for principled evaluation of continuous flow-based language models; (2) an information-uniform principle for noise scheduling, motivating a learnable scheduler based on a Gumbel distribution; and (3) an improved training protocol incorporating self-conditioning, which enhances both likelihood and sample quality.LangFlow achieves strong performance across benchmarks, reaching a perplexity (PPL) of 30.0 on LM1B and 24.6 on OpenWebText. It matches top discrete DLMs at comparable scale and surpasses autoregressive baselines in zero-shot transfer across multiple benchmarks. LangFlow provides clear evidence that continuous diffusion is a competitive and promising paradigm for language modeling.
  https://github.com/nealchen2003/LangFlow

AI.tldr: LangFlow is a continuous diffusion-based language model that, for the first time, matches the performance of top discrete diffusion language models and surpasses standard autoregressive models on zero-shot transfer tasks.
AI.motivation: Continuous diffusion models work very well in domains like images, but when applied to language they have historically underperformed discrete diffusion and traditional autoregressive language models. The paper is motivated by this performance gap and aims to understand how to design continuous diffusion language models that are both theoretically grounded and empirically competitive, ultimately showing that continuous diffusion can be a viable paradigm for language modeling rather than a niche alternative.
AI.method: The authors formulate language modeling in a continuous embedding space and connect these continuous diffusion language models to Flow Matching through Bregman divergence. Building on this, they introduce three main techniques: (1) a new ODE-based variational bound on the negative log-likelihood, enabling principled likelihood evaluation of continuous flow-based language models; (2) an information-uniform principle for choosing the noise schedule, which leads to a learnable noise scheduler parameterized via a Gumbel distribution; and (3) an enhanced training protocol using self-conditioning—feeding model predictions from previous steps back into the model—to improve both likelihood and generation quality.
AI.result: With these methods, the proposed LangFlow model achieves strong perplexity scores, obtaining PPL 30.0 on the LM1B dataset and 24.6 on OpenWebText. At similar model scales, LangFlow matches state-of-the-art discrete diffusion language models and outperforms standard autoregressive baselines in zero-shot transfer performance over several benchmarks, indicating better generalization without task-specific finetuning.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that with appropriate theoretical grounding (via Flow Matching and Bregman divergence), principled likelihood evaluation, optimized noise scheduling, and self-conditioning, continuous diffusion language models can close the gap with discrete diffusion and autoregressive models. LangFlow demonstrates that continuous diffusion is not only viable but competitive for large-scale language modeling, suggesting it as a promising direction for future research and applications in text generation.

title: Min-$k$ Sampling: Decoupling Truncation from Temperature Scaling via Relative Logit Dynamics
summary: The quality of text generated by large language models depends critically on the decoding sampling strategy. While mainstream methods such as Top-$k$, Top-$p$, and Min-$p$ achieve a balance between diversity and accuracy through probability-space truncation, they share an inherent limitation: extreme sensitivity to the temperature parameter. Recent logit-space approaches like Top-$nσ$ achieve temperature invariance but rely on global statistics that are susceptible to long-tail noise, failing to capture fine-grained confidence structures among top candidates. We propose \textbf{Min-$k$ Sampling}, a novel dynamic truncation strategy that analyzes the local shape of the sorted logit distribution to identify "semantic cliffs": sharp transitions from high-confidence core tokens to uncertain long-tail tokens. By computing a position-weighted relative decay rate, Min-$k$ dynamically determines truncation boundaries at each generation step.
