We formally prove that Min-$k$ achieves strict temperature invariance and empirically demonstrate its low sensitivity to hyperparameter choices. Experiments on multiple reasoning benchmarks, creative writing tasks, and human evaluation show that Min-$k$ consistently improves text quality, maintaining robust performance even under extreme temperature settings where probability-based methods collapse. We make our code, models, and analysis tools publicly available.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Min-k Sampling, a new decoding strategy for large language models that is temperature-invariant and more robust than common probability-based methods.
AI.motivation: Existing decoding methods like Top-k, Top-p, and Min-p depend heavily on the temperature parameter and can degrade badly at extreme temperatures. Newer logit-space methods such as Top-nσ are temperature-invariant but rely on global statistics that are vulnerable to long-tail noise and do not respect fine-grained confidence differences among top tokens. The authors want a decoding method that is both temperature-invariant and sensitive to local confidence structure, improving robustness and text quality.
AI.method: They propose Min-k Sampling, a dynamic truncation strategy in logit space. It examines the locally sorted logit distribution at each decoding step to detect “semantic cliffs” – sharp drops from high-confidence to low-confidence tokens. This is done by computing a position-weighted relative decay rate between successive logits, and using this to adaptively choose the cutoff index k. They mathematically analyze the method and prove strict temperature invariance and low hyperparameter sensitivity.
AI.result: On various benchmarks, including reasoning tasks, creative writing, and human evaluation, Min-k outperforms conventional methods like Top-k, Top-p, and Min-p. It retains high text quality and robustness even at extreme temperatures where probability-based approaches fail or collapse. The method shows consistent gains with relatively little dependence on hyperparameter tuning.
AI.conclusion: Min-k Sampling offers a principled, temperature-invariant decoding scheme that better captures local confidence structure in the logits, leading to more reliable and higher-quality text generation across tasks and temperature settings. The approach is theoretically grounded, empirically validated, and released with open-source code and models.

title: HistLens: Mapping Idea Change across Concepts and Corpora
summary: Language change both reflects and shapes social processes, and the semantic evolution of foundational concepts provides a measurable trace of historical and social transformation. Despite recent advances in diachronic semantics and discourse analysis, existing computational approaches often (i) concentrate on a single concept or a single corpus, making findings difficult to compare across heterogeneous sources, and (ii) remain confined to surface lexical evidence, offering insufficient computational and interpretive granularity when concepts are expressed implicitly. We propose HistLens, a unified, SAE-based framework for multi-concept, multi-corpus conceptual-history analysis. The framework decomposes concept representations into interpretable features and tracks their activation dynamics over time and across sources, yielding comparable conceptual trajectories within a shared coordinate system. Experiments on long-span press corpora show that HistLens supports cross-concept, cross-corpus computation of patterns of idea evolution and enables implicit concept computation. By bridging conceptual modeling with interpretive needs, HistLens broadens the analytical perspectives and methodological repertoire available to social science and the humanities for diachronic text analysis.

AI.tldr: HistLens is a unified framework to track how multiple concepts change in meaning over time across multiple text corpora, using interpretable semantic features rather than only surface words.
AI.motivation: Existing computational work on semantic change often studies a single concept in a single corpus and largely relies on explicit word usage, which makes it hard to compare results across datasets and to detect concepts that are expressed implicitly. Humanities and social-science researchers need tools that can systematically compare conceptual evolution across sources and capture latent or indirect expressions of ideas.
AI.method: The authors propose HistLens, an SAE-based (likely semantic attribute embedding) framework that represents each concept as a vector of interpretable semantic features. It decomposes concepts into these features and then measures how strongly each feature is activated in texts over time and across multiple corpora. This produces aligned, comparable trajectories for many concepts simultaneously within a shared semantic coordinate system, and includes mechanisms to infer concept presence even when it is not named explicitly.
AI.result: Applied to long-span newspaper or press corpora, HistLens successfully computes and visualizes patterns of conceptual change for multiple ideas, showing how their semantic profiles evolve and differ across sources. It also demonstrates the ability to recover or measure concepts that are only implicitly present, outperforming or extending prior approaches that rely mainly on surface lexical cues.
AI.conclusion: HistLens provides a unified, interpretable framework for multi-concept, multi-corpus analysis of conceptual history, enabling fine-grained, comparable measurements of semantic evolution and implicit concept expression. This bridges computational modeling with the interpretive practices of social science and the humanities, expanding the methodological toolbox for diachronic text analysis.

title: Introspective Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion language models promise parallel generation, yet still lag behind autoregressive (AR) models in quality. We stem this gap to a failure of introspective consistency: AR models agree with their own generations, while DLMs often do not. We define the introspective acceptance rate, which measures whether a model accepts its previously generated tokens. This reveals why AR training has a structural advantage: causal masking and logit shifting implicitly enforce introspective consistency. Motivated by this observation, we introduce Introspective Diffusion Language Model (I-DLM), a paradigm that retains diffusion-style parallel decoding while inheriting the introspective consistency of AR training. I-DLM uses a novel introspective strided decoding (ISD) algorithm, which enables the model to verify previously generated tokens while advancing new ones in the same forward pass. From a systems standpoint, we build I-DLM inference engine on AR-inherited optimizations and further customize it with a stationary-batch scheduler. To the best of our knowledge, I-DLM is the first DLM to match the quality of its same-scale AR counterpart while outperforming prior DLMs in both model quality and practical serving efficiency across 15 benchmarks. It reaches 69.6 on AIME-24 and 45.7 on LiveCodeBench-v6, exceeding LLaDA-2.1-mini (16B) by more than 26 and 15 points, respectively. Beyond quality, I-DLM is designed for the growing demand of large-concurrency serving, delivering about 3x higher throughput than prior state-of-the-art DLMs.

AI.tldr: They propose an Introspective Diffusion Language Model (I-DLM) that combines diffusion-style parallel decoding with autoregressive-style self-consistency, achieving AR-level quality with higher throughput.
AI.motivation: Diffusion language models (DLMs) allow parallel generation but underperform autoregressive (AR) models in output quality. The authors trace this gap to a lack of introspective consistency—DLMs often wouldn’t "accept" their own generated tokens if asked to re-evaluate them—while AR models implicitly maintain such consistency. They aim to close this gap so DLMs can offer both high quality and practical serving efficiency.
AI.method: They define a metric called introspective acceptance rate to quantify whether a model accepts its own previously generated tokens. Analyzing this shows AR training has a structural advantage due to causal masking and logit shifting. Building on this, they design the Introspective Diffusion Language Model (I-DLM), which keeps diffusion-style parallel decoding but borrows AR-like training properties. Core to this is an introspective strided decoding (ISD) algorithm that, in a single forward pass, both checks (verifies) already generated tokens and advances generation of new tokens. They also engineer an inference engine that reuses optimizations from AR systems and introduce a stationary-batch scheduler to further improve serving efficiency.
AI.result: Empirically, I-DLM becomes the first diffusion language model to match the performance of an autoregressive model of the same scale while outperforming previous DLMs in both quality and serving efficiency on 15 benchmarks. For example, it scores 69.6 on AIME-24 and 45.7 on LiveCodeBench-v6, surpassing LLaDA-2.1-mini (16B) by over 26 and 15 points, respectively. In terms of systems performance, it delivers about 3x higher throughput than prior state-of-the-art DLMs, particularly benefiting large-concurrency serving scenarios.
AI.conclusion: By explicitly enforcing introspective consistency within a diffusion-style framework, I-DLM closes the quality gap with autoregressive models while preserving and even enhancing parallel decoding benefits. The proposed ISD algorithm and optimized inference engine show that DLMs can achieve state-of-the-art language modeling performance and significantly higher throughput, making them practical for real-world, high-concurrency deployments.

title: Agentic Aggregation for Parallel Scaling of Long-Horizon Agentic Tasks
summary: We study parallel test-time scaling for long-horizon agentic tasks such as agentic search and deep research, where multiple rollouts are generated in parallel and aggregated into a final response. While such scaling has proven effective for chain-of-thought reasoning, agentic tasks pose unique challenges: trajectories are long, multi-turn, and tool-augmented, and outputs are often open-ended. Aggregating only final answers discards rich information from trajectories, while concatenating all trajectories exceeds the model's context window. To address this, we propose AggAgent, an aggregation agent that treats parallel trajectories as an environment. We equip it with lightweight tools to inspect candidate solutions and search across trajectories, enabling it to navigate and synthesize information on demand. Across six benchmarks and three model families (GLM-4.7, Qwen3.5, MiniMax-M2.5), AggAgent outperforms all existing aggregation methods-by up to 5.3% absolute on average and 10.3% on two deep research tasks-while adding minimal overhead, as the aggregation cost remains bounded by a single agentic rollout. Our findings establish agentic aggregation as an effective and cost-efficient approach to parallel test-time scaling.

AI.tldr: AggAgent is an aggregation agent that efficiently combines multiple parallel long-horizon tool-augmented trajectories, outperforming prior aggregation methods for agentic search and deep research with minimal extra cost.
AI.motivation: Parallel test-time scaling works well for chain-of-thought reasoning, but long-horizon agentic tasks like deep research and tool-augmented search produce long, multi-turn, open-ended trajectories. Existing aggregation approaches either only use final answers, wasting trajectory information, or concatenate everything, exceeding context limits. A better way to exploit rich trajectory information under context constraints is needed.
AI.method: Introduce AggAgent, an aggregation agent that treats the set of parallel trajectories as an environment. It is given lightweight tools to: (1) inspect and compare candidate solutions, and (2) selectively search and jump across trajectories. Instead of naively concatenating trajectories, AggAgent interactively queries and navigates them to gather relevant information and then synthesize a final answer. Experiments are run across six benchmarks and three LLM families to compare against existing aggregation schemes.
AI.result: Across six benchmarks and three model families (GLM-4.7, Qwen3.5, MiniMax-M2.5), AggAgent consistently outperforms existing aggregation methods. The gains are up to 5.3 percentage points on average and up to 10.3 points on two deep research tasks, while keeping aggregation cost comparable to a single agentic rollout.
AI.conclusion: Agentic aggregation via AggAgent is an effective and cost-efficient way to scale test-time performance for long-horizon agentic tasks. By treating trajectories as an environment and using tools to navigate and synthesize them, one can leverage rich trajectory information within context limits, improving performance without large additional computational overhead.

title: Intelligent Approval of Access Control Flow in Office Automation Systems via Relational Modeling
summary: Office automation (OA) systems play a crucial role in enterprise operations and management, with access control flow approval (ACFA) being a key component that manages the accessibility of various resources. However, traditional ACFA requires approval from the person in charge at each step, which consumes a significant amount of manpower and time. Its intelligence is a crucial issue that needs to be addressed urgently by all companies. In this paper, we propose a novel relational modeling-driven intelligent approval (RMIA) framework to automate ACFA. Specifically, our RMIA consists of two core modules: (1) The binary relation modeling module aims to characterize the coupling relation between applicants and approvers and provide reliable basic information for ACFA decision-making from a coarse-grained perspective. (2) The ternary relation modeling module utilizes specific resource information as its core, characterizing the complex relations between applicants, resources, and approvers, and thus provides fine-grained gain information for informed decision-making. Then, our RMIA effectively fuses these two kinds of information to form the final decision. Finally, extensive experiments are conducted on two product datasets and an online A/B test to verify the effectiveness of RMIA.

AI.tldr: Proposes an intelligent, automated approval framework (RMIA) for office automation access control flows using binary and ternary relational modeling to replace manual multi-step approvals.
AI.motivation: Traditional access control flow approval in office automation systems requires manual approval at each step by responsible personnel, leading to high labor costs, time consumption, and low efficiency. Enterprises urgently need more intelligent, automated solutions for managing resource access without compromising decision quality.
AI.method: Designs the RMIA framework with two relational modeling modules: (1) a binary relation modeling module that captures coarse-grained coupling relations between applicants and approvers to provide foundational decision information; (2) a ternary relation modeling module centered on specific resources that models complex interactions among applicants, resources, and approvers to provide fine-grained decision cues. The framework fuses outputs from both modules to generate final approval decisions. Performance is evaluated through experiments on two real product datasets and an online A/B test.
AI.result: RMIA achieves superior performance compared to traditional or baseline approval approaches on two product datasets and shows effectiveness in a real-world online A/B testing environment, indicating improved decision quality and automation for access control flow approvals.
AI.conclusion: Relational modeling-driven intelligent approval is an effective way to automate office access control flow approvals. By jointly leveraging binary and ternary relational information and fusing coarse- and fine-grained relations, RMIA can reduce manual workload and time costs while maintaining or improving approval decision quality, as validated by offline experiments and online testing.

title: General365: Benchmarking General Reasoning in Large Language Models Across Diverse and Challenging Tasks
summary: Contemporary large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable reasoning capabilities, particularly in specialized domains like mathematics and physics. However, their ability to generalize these reasoning skills to more general and broader contexts--often termed general reasoning--remains under-explored. Unlike domain-specific reasoning, general reasoning relies less on expert knowledge but still presents formidable reasoning challenges, such as complex constraints, nested logical branches, and semantic interference. To address this gap, we introduce General365, a benchmark specifically designed to assess general reasoning in LLMs. By restricting background knowledge to a K-12 level, General365 explicitly decouples reasoning from specialized expertise. The benchmark comprises 365 seed problems and 1,095 variant problems across eight categories, ensuring both high difficulty and diversity. Evaluations across 26 leading LLMs reveal that even the top-performing model achieves only 62.8% accuracy, in stark contrast to the near-perfect performances of LLMs in math and physics benchmarks. These results suggest that the reasoning abilities of current LLMs are heavily domain-dependent, leaving significant room for improvement in broader applications. We envision General365 as a catalyst for advancing LLM reasoning beyond domain-specific tasks toward robust, general-purpose real-world scenarios. Code, Dataset, and Leaderboard: https://general365.github.io

AI.tldr: The paper introduces General365, a benchmark to evaluate large language models’ general reasoning abilities, distinct from domain-specific reasoning in math/physics, showing current LLMs perform far from human-level on such tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations focus largely on domain-specific reasoning (e.g., math, physics) where LLMs can leverage specialized patterns and knowledge, often achieving near-perfect scores. This leaves unclear whether LLMs truly possess general reasoning skills that can handle diverse, everyday problems with complex logical structures but limited need for expert knowledge. The authors want a way to disentangle genuine reasoning ability from domain expertise and quantify how well LLMs can reason in broad, real-world-like contexts.
AI.method: They construct General365, a benchmark constrained to K–12 background knowledge so that advanced domain expertise is not required. The benchmark has 365 seed problems and 1,095 automatically or manually derived variants, grouped into eight categories to cover a wide range of reasoning phenomena, including complex constraints, nested logical branches, and semantic interference. They then evaluate 26 state-of-the-art LLMs on these problems, measuring accuracy and comparing results with performance on existing math and physics benchmarks.
AI.result: Across 26 leading LLMs, even the best model reaches only 62.8% accuracy on General365, which is much lower than the near-perfect performance these same or similar models often achieve on math and physics benchmarks. This performance gap reveals that current LLMs struggle substantially with general reasoning tasks that do not depend on specialized expert knowledge.
AI.conclusion: The findings indicate that contemporary LLMs’ reasoning capabilities are strongly domain-dependent and do not yet generalize well to broad, knowledge-light reasoning tasks. General365 provides a challenging, diverse, and controlled benchmark that can be used to study and improve general reasoning in LLMs. The authors expect it to drive future research toward models capable of robust, general-purpose reasoning needed in real-world applications, supported by an open codebase, dataset, and leaderboard.

title: From Topology to Trajectory: LLM-Driven World Models For Supply Chain Resilience
summary: Semiconductor supply chains face unprecedented resilience challenges amidst global geopolitical turbulence. Conventional Large Language Model (LLM) planners, when confronting such non-stationary "Policy Black Swan" events, frequently suffer from Decision Paralysis or a severe Grounding Gap due to the absence of physical environmental modeling. This paper introduces ReflectiChain, a cognitive agentic framework tailored for resilient macroeconomic supply chain planning. The core innovation lies in the integration of Latent Trajectory Rehearsal powered by a generative world model, which couples reflection-in-action (System 2 deliberation) with delayed reflection-on-action. Furthermore, we leverage a Retrospective Agentic RL mechanism to enable autonomous policy evolution during the deployment phase (test-time). Evaluations conducted on our high-fidelity benchmark, Semi-Sim, demonstrate that under extreme scenarios such as export bans and material shortages, ReflectiChain achieves a 250% improvement in average step rewards over the strongest LLM baselines. It successfully restores the Operability Ratio (OR) from a deficient 13.3% to over 88.5% while ensuring robust gradient convergence. Ablation studies further underscore that the synergy between physical grounding constraints and double-loop learning is fundamental to bridging the gap between semantic reasoning and physical reality for long-horizon strategic planning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ReflectiChain, an LLM-based cognitive agent framework that improves resilience in semiconductor supply chain planning under extreme, non-stationary conditions.
AI.motivation: Semiconductor supply chains are highly vulnerable to unpredictable, large-scale geopolitical disruptions (“Policy Black Swan” events). Existing LLM-based planners struggle in these dynamic, high-stakes environments because they lack explicit physical/environmental modeling, leading to decision paralysis and poor grounding of their plans in real-world constraints. The authors aim to build a planning framework that can reason over long horizons, stay grounded in physical supply chain dynamics, and adapt policies during deployment.
AI.method: The authors design ReflectiChain, a cognitive agentic framework for macroeconomic supply chain planning. Its key component is Latent Trajectory Rehearsal with a generative world model, allowing the agent to imagine and simulate future trajectories while linking online System 2–style deliberation (reflection-in-action) with delayed reflection-on-action. They add a Retrospective Agentic Reinforcement Learning module that lets the agent update and evolve its policies during test-time, using feedback from deployed trajectories. The physical constraints of the Semi-Sim environment provide grounding, and the framework employs double-loop learning to iteratively adjust both actions and underlying assumptions/models.
AI.result: On Semi-Sim, a high-fidelity semiconductor supply chain benchmark with extreme stressors such as export bans and material shortages, ReflectiChain significantly outperforms strong LLM baselines. It yields a 250% improvement in average step rewards and raises the Operability Ratio from 13.3% to over 88.5%, all while maintaining stable gradient convergence. Ablation experiments show that removing physical grounding or the double-loop learning components substantially degrades performance, indicating that their combination is critical.
AI.conclusion: ReflectiChain demonstrates that combining generative world models, reflective deliberation, retrospective RL, and physically grounded constraints can greatly enhance LLM-based strategic planning in volatile, non-stationary environments. The synergy between physical grounding and double-loop learning is key to bridging the gap between purely semantic reasoning and real-world supply chain dynamics, enabling more resilient, adaptive long-horizon planning under disruptive macroeconomic events.

title: C-ReD: A Comprehensive Chinese Benchmark for AI-Generated Text Detection Derived from Real-World Prompts
summary: Recently, large language models (LLMs) are capable of generating highly fluent textual content. While they offer significant convenience to humans, they also introduce various risks, like phishing and academic dishonesty. Numerous research efforts have been dedicated to developing algorithms for detecting AI-generated text and constructing relevant datasets. However, in the domain of Chinese corpora, challenges remain, including limited model diversity and data homogeneity. To address these issues, we propose C-ReD: a comprehensive Chinese Real-prompt AI-generated Detection benchmark. Experiments demonstrate that C-ReD not only enables reliable in-domain detection but also supports strong generalization to unseen LLMs and external Chinese datasets-addressing critical gaps in model diversity, domain coverage, and prompt realism that have limited prior Chinese detection benchmarks. We release our resources at https://github.com/HeraldofLight/C-ReD.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces C-ReD, a comprehensive Chinese benchmark for detecting AI-generated text, designed with real prompts and diverse models to improve detection reliability and generalization.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-generated text detection research, especially for Chinese, suffers from limited diversity of language models and homogeneous, unrealistic data, reducing robustness and real-world applicability of detectors.
AI.method: The authors construct C-ReD, a benchmark of Chinese AI-generated texts produced from real prompts and a diverse set of LLMs, and then evaluate various detection models on both in-domain and out-of-domain (unseen LLMs and external datasets) scenarios.
AI.result: Experiments show that C-ReD enables accurate detection within its own domain and that detectors trained or evaluated on it maintain strong performance when applied to previously unseen Chinese LLMs and external Chinese datasets.
AI.conclusion: C-ReD effectively fills important gaps in existing Chinese AI-text detection benchmarks—improving model diversity, domain coverage, and prompt realism—and serves as a robust resource for developing and evaluating detectors that generalize well in real-world Chinese settings.

title: EmergentBridge: Improving Zero-Shot Cross-Modal Transfer in Unified Multimodal Embedding Models
summary: Unified multimodal embedding spaces underpin practical applications such as cross-modal retrieval and zero-shot recognition. In many real deployments, however, supervision is available only for a small subset of modality pairs (e.g., image--text), leaving \emph{unpaired} modality pairs (e.g., audio$\leftrightarrow$depth, infrared$\leftrightarrow$audio) weakly connected and thus performing poorly on zero-shot transfer. Addressing this sparse-pairing regime is therefore essential for scaling unified embedding systems to new tasks without curating exhaustive pairwise data. We propose \textbf{EmergentBridge}, an embedding-level bridging framework that improves performance on these unpaired pairs \emph{without requiring exhaustive pairwise supervision}. Our key observation is that naively aligning a new modality to a synthesized proxy embedding can introduce \emph{gradient interference}, degrading the anchor-alignment structure that existing retrieval/classification relies on. EmergentBridge addresses this by (i) learning a mapping that produces a \emph{noisy bridge anchor} (a proxy embedding of an already-aligned modality) from an anchor embedding, and (ii) enforcing proxy alignment only in the subspace orthogonal to the anchor-alignment direction, preserving anchor alignment while strengthening non-anchor connectivity. Across nine datasets spanning multiple modalities, EmergentBridge consistently outperforms prior binding baselines on zero-shot classification and retrieval, demonstrating strong emergent alignment.

AI.tldr: EmergentBridge is a framework for learning unified multimodal embeddings when only a few modality pairs are supervised, improving zero-shot performance on unseen modality pairs without exhaustive pairwise data.
AI.motivation: Unified multimodal embeddings are powerful for tasks like cross-modal retrieval and zero-shot recognition, but in practice, supervision exists only for a few modality pairs (e.g., image–text). This leaves many other modality pairs (e.g., audio–depth, infrared–audio) weakly connected, harming zero-shot transfer. Collecting exhaustive pairwise data across all modalities is expensive or infeasible, so a method is needed to better connect unpaired modalities using limited supervision.
AI.method: EmergentBridge works at the embedding level to bridge unpaired modality pairs without needing full pairwise supervision. It first learns a mapping that produces a noisy bridge anchor: a proxy embedding of a modality that is already aligned in the shared space, generated from an anchor embedding. Then, instead of fully aligning to this proxy (which causes gradient interference and harms existing alignment), it enforces proxy alignment only in the subspace orthogonal to the primary anchor-alignment direction. This preserves the core anchor alignment structure while strengthening the connectivity among non-anchor modalities.
AI.result: On nine datasets covering multiple modalities, EmergentBridge consistently improves zero-shot classification and retrieval performance on unpaired modality pairs compared with prior binding/bridging baselines. The experiments show that the method leads to stronger emergent alignment across modalities even without direct supervision for all pairs.
AI.conclusion: By carefully controlling how new modalities are aligned—using noisy bridge anchors and restricting alignment to orthogonal subspaces—EmergentBridge mitigates gradient interference and enhances the connectivity of sparsely supervised multimodal embedding spaces. This enables better zero-shot transfer across unpaired modality pairs without the need for exhaustive pairwise annotations.

title: CLSGen: A Dual-Head Fine-Tuning Framework for Joint Probabilistic Classification and Verbalized Explanation
summary: With the recent progress of Large Language Models (LLMs), there is a growing interest in applying these models to solve complex and challenging problems. Modern LLMs, capable of processing long contexts and generating verbalized explanations, offer significant potential in addressing real-world applications. However, a critical hurdle in deploying LLMs for practical decision-making is their inability to provide reliable, quantitative probabilities. While task-specific fine-tuning of LLMs using traditional discriminative objectives (similar to encoder-only models) can yield probability estimates, this often leads to catastrophic forgetting and linguistic collapse. Consequently, the model loses its ability to generate explanations, severely undermining its interpretability and usability. To address this challenge, we propose CLSGen, a novel LLM fine-tuning framework designed for binary classification tasks. The CLSGen framework encompasses a new model architecture, training methodology, and data construction strategy to enable robust probability estimation without sacrificing the model's inherent explanation-generation capabilities. Experimental results across multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that models fine-tuned with CLSGen outperform existing baselines in classification metrics (AUROC and F1-score). Regarding explanation, the results showed strong alignment between predicted labels and generated justifications, as well as high readability.

AI.tldr: CLSGen is a fine-tuning framework for LLMs that adds reliable probabilistic binary classification while preserving explanation generation.
AI.motivation: LLMs are good at reasoning and generating explanations but are poor at producing calibrated, quantitative probabilities needed for real-world decisions. Conventional discriminative fine-tuning can improve probability estimates but often causes catastrophic forgetting, which degrades the model’s generative and explanatory abilities (“linguistic collapse”). A method is needed that yields robust probability estimation without sacrificing interpretability via natural-language justifications.
AI.method: Introduce CLSGen, a fine-tuning framework for binary classification that combines: (1) a modified LLM architecture tailored to classification, (2) a training strategy that preserves generative capabilities while learning calibrated probabilities, and (3) a data construction scheme that pairs inputs, labels, and explanations. The model is fine-tuned to output both a quantitative probability/label and an associated natural-language explanation, ensuring alignment between the two.
AI.result: On multiple benchmark datasets, LLMs fine-tuned with CLSGen achieve higher AUROC and F1 scores than baseline methods, indicating better discriminative performance and probability estimation. Qualitative and/or quantitative evaluation of explanations shows strong consistency between predicted labels and generated justifications, along with high readability of the explanations.
AI.conclusion: CLSGen enables LLMs to provide reliable probabilistic outputs for binary classification without losing their ability to generate coherent, aligned explanations. This makes LLM-based systems more suitable for practical decision-making tasks that require both accurate predictions and interpretable justifications.

title: Psychological Concept Neurons: Can Neural Control Bias Probing and Shift Generation in LLMs?
summary: Using psychological constructs such as the Big Five, large language models (LLMs) can imitate specific personality profiles and predict a user's personality. While LLMs can exhibit behaviors consistent with these constructs, it remains unclear where and how they are represented inside the model and how they relate to behavioral outputs. To address this gap, we focus on questionnaire-operationalized Big Five concepts, analyze the formation and localization of their internal representations, and use interventions to examine how these representations relate to behavioral outputs. In our experiment, we first use probing to examine where Big Five information emerges across model depth. We then identify neurons that respond selectively to each Big Five concept and test whether enhancing or suppressing their activations can bias latent representations and label generation in intended directions. We find that Big Five information becomes rapidly decodable in early layers and remains detectable through the final layers, while concept-selective neurons are most prevalent in mid layers and exhibit limited overlap across domains. Interventions on these neurons consistently shift probe readouts toward targeted concepts, with targeted success rates exceeding 0.8 for some concepts, indicating that the model's internal separation of Big Five personality traits can be causally steered. At the label-generation level, the same interventions often bias generated label distributions in the intended directions, but the effects are weaker, more concept-dependent, and often accompanied by cross-trait spillover, indicating that comparable control over generated labels is difficult even with interventions on a large fraction of concept-selective neurons. Overall, our findings reveal a gap between representational control and behavioral control in LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how Big Five personality traits are internally represented in LLMs and whether manipulating those representations changes model behavior.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs can mimic personalities and even estimate user traits, we do not understand where these traits live in the network or how strongly they causally influence behavior. There is a need to connect abstract psychological constructs (like Big Five traits) with concrete internal mechanisms in LLMs and to test whether intervening on these mechanisms can steer model outputs.
AI.method: The authors treat questionnaire-defined Big Five traits as target concepts. They (1) apply probing across layers to see where Big Five information becomes decodable; (2) identify neurons whose activations are selectively associated with each trait; and (3) intervene by amplifying or suppressing those neurons during inference. They then measure effects of these interventions on internal latent representations (via probes) and on external behavioral outputs (generated trait labels).
AI.result: Big Five trait information becomes decodable early in the network and persists to the final layers. Neurons selectively associated with each trait cluster mainly in middle layers and show limited cross-trait overlap. Manipulating these concept-selective neurons reliably shifts probe-based measurements toward the targeted trait, sometimes with success rates over 0.8. However, when looking at generated trait labels, the same interventions have weaker, trait-dependent effects and can cause unintended changes in non-target traits (spillover).
AI.conclusion: The Big Five traits are internally represented and partially disentangled within LLMs and can be causally influenced at the representation level. Yet, intervening on these internal representations does not straightforwardly translate into strong, clean control over overt behavior, revealing an important gap between representational control and behavioral control in current LLMs.

title: Saar-Voice: A Multi-Speaker Saarbrücken Dialect Speech Corpus
summary: Natural language processing (NLP) and speech technologies have made significant progress in recent years; however, they remain largely focused on standardized language varieties. Dialects, despite their cultural significance and widespread use, are underrepresented in linguistic resources and computational models, resulting in performance disparities. To address this gap, we introduce Saar-Voice, a six-hour speech corpus for the Saarbrücken dialect of German. The dataset was created by first collecting text through digitized books and locally sourced materials. A subset of this text was recorded by nine speakers, and we conducted analyses on both the textual and speech components to assess the dataset's characteristics and quality. We discuss methodological challenges related to orthographic and speaker variation, and explore grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) conversion. The resulting corpus provides aligned textual and audio representations. This serves as a foundation for future research on dialect-aware text-to-speech (TTS), particularly in low-resource scenarios, including zero-shot and few-shot model adaptation.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Saar-Voice, a 6-hour aligned speech-text corpus for the Saarbrücken German dialect to enable dialect-aware TTS in low-resource settings.
AI.motivation: Most NLP and speech technologies focus on standardized language varieties, neglecting dialects, which leads to performance disparities and underrepresentation of dialects in linguistic resources and computational models. There is a need for dedicated dialect corpora to support dialect-aware TTS, especially for low-resource dialects.
AI.method: The authors compiled a Saarbrücken dialect text collection from digitized books and locally sourced materials, then selected a subset for recording by nine speakers to create a speech corpus. They performed analyses of both the text and speech to characterize and assess the dataset, discussed challenges such as orthographic and speaker variation, and explored grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) conversion. The final resource aligns textual and audio data.
AI.result: The outcome is Saar-Voice, a six-hour speech corpus with aligned text and audio for the Saarbrücken German dialect, accompanied by analyses that document its linguistic properties, quality, and challenges like orthographic and speaker variability, as well as initial experiments or exploration with G2P conversion.
AI.conclusion: Saar-Voice provides a new foundational resource for research on dialect-aware TTS, particularly suitable for low-resource experimentation such as zero-shot and few-shot model adaptation, and highlights methodological issues that future work on dialect corpora and models should address.

title: Hodoscope: Unsupervised Monitoring for AI Misbehaviors
summary:
Existing approaches to monitoring AI agents rely on supervised evaluation: human-written rules or LLM-based judges that check for known failure modes. However, novel misbehaviors may fall outside predefined categories entirely and LLM-based judges can be unreliable. To address this, we formulate unsupervised monitoring, drawing an analogy to unsupervised learning. Rather than checking for specific misbehaviors, an unsupervised monitor assists humans in discovering problematic agent behaviors without prior assumptions about what counts as problematic, leaving that determination to the human.
  We observe that problematic behaviors are often distinctive: a model exploiting a benchmark loophole exhibits actions absent from well-behaved baselines, and a vulnerability unique to one evaluation manifests as behavioral anomalies when the same model runs across multiple benchmarks. This motivates using group-wise behavioral differences as the primary signal for unsupervised monitoring. We introduce Hodoscope, a tool that operationalizes this insight. Hodoscope compares behavior distributions across groups and highlights distinctive and potentially suspicious action patterns for human review. Using Hodoscope, we discover a previously unknown vulnerability in the Commit0 benchmark (unsquashed git history allowing ground-truth recovery, inflating scores for at least five models) and independently recover known exploits on ImpossibleBench and SWE-bench. Quantitative evaluation estimates that our method reduces review effort by 6-23$\times$ compared to naive uniform sampling. Finally, we show that behavior descriptions discovered through Hodoscope could improve the detection accuracy of LLM-based judges, demonstrating a path from unsupervised to supervised monitoring.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Hodoscope, an unsupervised monitoring tool that detects distinctive and potentially problematic behaviors of AI agents by comparing behavior distributions across groups, helping humans uncover new vulnerabilities and exploits with significantly reduced review effort.
AI.motivation: Supervised monitoring of AI agents—using human-written rules or LLM judges—is limited to known failure modes and can be unreliable, missing novel misbehaviors. The authors want a way to surface previously unknown problematic behaviors without having to predefine what counts as a failure, and to make human review more efficient.
AI.method: They formalize "unsupervised monitoring," analogous to unsupervised learning, where the system does not look for specific misbehaviors but instead highlights behavioral anomalies and group-wise differences. They implement this as Hodoscope, which compares distributions of agent actions across different groups (e.g., models, benchmarks, or conditions), identifies distinctive action patterns, and surfaces them as candidates for human inspection. They then quantitatively evaluate how much this reduces the amount of data humans must manually review and test whether discovered behavior descriptions can enhance LLM-based supervised monitors.
AI.result: Using Hodoscope, the authors discover a previously unknown vulnerability in the Commit0 benchmark (unsquashed git history allowing ground-truth recovery, artificially boosting scores for multiple models) and independently rediscover known exploits on ImpossibleBench and SWE-bench. Their quantitative analysis suggests that their approach can reduce human review effort by 6–23x versus uniformly sampling behaviors for inspection. They also show that incorporating the behavior patterns surfaced by Hodoscope into LLM-based judges improves those judges’ detection accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Unsupervised monitoring via group-wise behavioral comparison is an effective way to surface novel and suspicious AI agent behaviors without prespecifying failure modes. Hodoscope operationalizes this idea, uncovers real benchmark vulnerabilities, and substantially reduces the cost of human review. Moreover, insights from unsupervised monitoring can feed back into better supervised monitors, suggesting a complementary pipeline from anomaly discovery to rule or judge refinement.

title: Towards Proactive Information Probing: Customer Service Chatbots Harvesting Value from Conversation
summary: Customer service chatbots are increasingly expected to serve not merely as reactive support tools for users, but as strategic interfaces for harvesting high-value information and business intelligence. In response, we make three main contributions. 1) We introduce and define a novel task of Proactive Information Probing, which optimizes when to probe users for pre-specified target information while minimizing conversation turns and user friction. 2) We propose PROCHATIP, a proactive chatbot framework featuring a specialized conversation strategy module trained to master the delicate timing of probes. 3) Experiments demonstrate that PROCHATIP significantly outperforms baselines, exhibiting superior capability in both information probing and service quality. We believe that our work effectively redefines the commercial utility of chatbots, positioning them as scalable, cost-effective engines for proactive business intelligence. Our code is available at https://github.com/SCUNLP/PROCHATIP.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PROCHATIP, a proactive customer-service chatbot framework that decides when to ask users for specific business-relevant information while preserving service quality and minimizing friction.
AI.motivation: Traditional customer service chatbots mainly respond reactively to user queries and are underused as tools for systematically collecting valuable business intelligence. Naively asking many questions can annoy users and lengthen conversations, so there is a need for a principled way to time and optimize information-seeking interactions.
AI.method: The authors define a new task called Proactive Information Probing, where the system must decide optimal moments to query users for pre-defined target information with minimal added turns and friction. They design PROCHATIP, a chatbot architecture that incorporates a specialized conversation strategy module trained to control the timing and manner of probing. This module is optimized to balance information collection with user experience and task completion.
AI.result: Experimental evaluations show that PROCHATIP surpasses baseline systems on metrics related to successful information probing and overall service quality, indicating that it can collect more target information with fewer or less intrusive interventions.
AI.conclusion: By formalizing proactive information probing and demonstrating PROCHATIP’s effectiveness, the paper argues that customer service chatbots can move beyond reactive support roles to become scalable, cost-efficient tools for proactive business intelligence gathering.

title: Do Agent Rules Shape or Distort? Guardrails Beat Guidance in Coding Agents
summary: Developers increasingly guide AI coding agents through natural language instruction files (e.g., CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules), yet no controlled study has measured whether these rules actually improve agent performance or which properties make a rule beneficial. We scrape 679 such files (25,532 rules) from GitHub and conduct the first large-scale empirical evaluation, running over 5,000 agent runs with a state-of-the-art coding agent on SWE-bench Verified. Rules improve performance by 7--14 percentage points, but random rules help as much as expert-curated ones -- suggesting rules work through context priming rather than specific instruction. Negative constraints ("do not refactor unrelated code") are the only individually beneficial rule type, while positive directives ("follow code style") actively hurt -- a pattern we analyze through the lens of potential-based reward shaping (PBRS). Moreover, individual rules are mostly harmful in isolation yet collectively helpful, with no degradation up to 50 rules. These findings expose a hidden reliability risk -- well-intentioned rules routinely degrade agent performance -- and provide a clear principle for safe agent configuration: constrain what agents must not do, rather than prescribing what they should.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically studies whether natural-language instruction files for AI coding agents meaningfully improve performance, and what types of rules help or hurt.
AI.motivation: Developers increasingly provide natural-language rule files to steer AI coding agents, but there has been no controlled, large-scale evaluation of whether these rules actually help, what mechanisms underlie their effects, and which specific rule types are beneficial or harmful. This gap creates a reliability risk, as common practices might be degrading agent performance without evidence.
AI.method: The authors scrape 679 instruction files containing 25,532 individual rules from GitHub projects that use AI coding agents. They then run over 5,000 controlled evaluation runs of a state-of-the-art coding agent on the SWE-bench Verified benchmark, systematically varying the presence, absence, type, and number of rules to measure their impact. They categorize rules (e.g., negative constraints versus positive directives) and analyze their effects, interpreting patterns through the theoretical lens of potential-based reward shaping (PBRS).
AI.result: Overall, rule files boost agent performance by around 7–14 percentage points on SWE-bench Verified. However, randomly sampled rules perform about as well as expert-curated ones, suggesting their benefit comes from generic context priming instead of specific guidance. Negative constraints like “do not refactor unrelated code” are the only rule type that is consistently helpful on its own, while positive directives such as “follow code style” tend to harm performance. Individual rules are usually detrimental in isolation but become beneficial in aggregate, and adding up to 50 rules does not degrade performance.
AI.conclusion: Natural-language rule files do help AI coding agents, but mostly via nonspecific context effects rather than carefully crafted instruction. Many well-intended rules actually reduce performance, revealing a subtle reliability risk in common configuration practices. The key design principle is to favor clear negative constraints that restrict harmful behaviors instead of prescribing detailed positive behaviors. Configuring agents with “what not to do” is empirically safer and more reliable than telling them “what to do.”

title: Frugal Knowledge Graph Construction with Local LLMs: A Zero-Shot Pipeline, Self-Consistency and Wisdom of Artificial Crowds
comment: Source code and raw results available: https://github.com/jourlin/synsynth (licence Hypocratic)
summary: This paper presents an empirical study of a multi-model zero-shot pipeline for knowledge graph construction and exploitation, executed entirely through local inference on consumer-grade hardware. We propose a reproducible evaluation framework integrating two external benchmarks (DocRED, HotpotQA), WebQuestionsSP-style synthetic data, and the RAGAS evaluation framework in an automated pipeline. On 500 document-level relations, our system achieves an F1 of 0.70 $\pm$ 0.041 in zero-shot, compared to 0.80 for supervised DREEAM. Text-to-query achieves an accuracy of 0.80 $\pm$ 0.06 on 200 samples. Multi-hop reasoning achieves an Exact Match (EM) of 0.46$\pm$0.04 on 500 HotpotQA questions, with a RAGAS faithfulness of 0.96 $\pm$ 0.04 on 50 samples. Beyond the pipeline, we study diversity mechanisms for difficult multi-hop reasoning. On 181 questions unsolvable at zero temperature, self-consistency (k=5, T =0.7) recovers up to 23% EM with a single Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model, but the cross-model oracle (3 architectures x 5 samples) reaches 46.4%. We highlight an agreement paradox: strong consensus among samples signals collective hallucination rather than a reliable answer, echoing the work of Moussa{ï}d et al. on the wisdom of crowds. Extending to the full pipeline (500 questions), self-consistency (k=3) raises EM from 0.46 to 0.48 $\pm$ 0.04. A confidence-routing cascade mechanism (Phi-4 $\rightarrow$ GPT-OSS, k=5) achieves an EM of 0.55 $\pm$ 0.04, the best result obtained, with 45.4% of questions rerouted. Finally, we show that V3 prompt engineering applied to other models does not reproduce the gains observed with Gemma-4, confirming the specific prompt/model interaction. The entire system runs in $\sim$5 h on a single RTX 3090, without any training, for an estimated carbon footprint of 0.09 kg CO2 eq.

AI.tldr: Empirical study of a fully local, zero-shot multi-model pipeline for building and querying knowledge graphs, with an automated, reproducible evaluation over several benchmarks and an analysis of diversity-based multi-hop reasoning strategies and their limits.
AI.motivation: Large language models can construct and query knowledge graphs, but typical approaches rely on large hosted models, task-specific training, and fragmented evaluations. There is a need to understand how far a purely zero-shot, locally run, multi-model pipeline can go for knowledge graph construction and multi-hop reasoning, how to evaluate it systematically across datasets, and how diversity mechanisms like self-consistency or model cascades affect hard reasoning cases and hallucination behavior.
AI.method: Design a zero-shot knowledge graph construction and exploitation pipeline using only local inference on consumer-grade hardware, combining multiple open models. Introduce an automated, reproducible evaluation framework that integrates DocRED and HotpotQA benchmarks, synthetic WebQuestionsSP-style data, and RAGAS-based evaluation. Measure relation extraction F1, text-to-query accuracy, multi-hop QA exact match and faithfulness. Then systematically vary diversity mechanisms for difficult multi-hop questions, including self-consistency sampling with different temperatures and k, cross-model ensembles (Mixture-of-Experts and cross-architecture oracle), and a confidence-based routing cascade between models, along with prompt engineering variants across architectures.
AI.result: On 500 document-level relations, the zero-shot system reaches 0.70±0.041 F1 versus 0.80 for the supervised DREEAM baseline. Text-to-query achieves 0.80±0.06 accuracy on 200 samples. For multi-hop reasoning on 500 HotpotQA questions, the pipeline gets 0.46±0.04 EM, with a high RAGAS faithfulness of 0.96±0.04 on 50 samples. For 181 particularly hard multi-hop questions that local models cannot solve at zero temperature, self-consistency (k=5, T=0.7) recovers up to 23% EM using a single MoE model, while a cross-model oracle over 3 architectures×5 samples attains 46.4% EM. On the full 500-question set, self-consistency with k=3 gives a modest improvement from 0.46 to 0.48±0.04 EM. A confidence-routing cascade (Phi-4 → GPT-OSS, k=5) improves EM to 0.55±0.04, rerouting 45.4% of questions and yielding the best performance. Prompt engineering that helps Gemma-4 ("V3" prompts) does not transfer to other models. The complete pipeline runs in about 5 hours on a single RTX 3090 without any training, corresponding to roughly 0.09 kg CO2 emissions.
AI.conclusion: A fully zero-shot, local multi-model pipeline can achieve competitive performance for knowledge graph construction and multi-hop QA, though still trailing supervised systems in relation extraction. Careful evaluation across benchmarks shows that diversity mechanisms like self-consistency and model cascades can help, but gains are limited and heterogeneous: self-consistency offers moderate benefits, while confidence-based routing between models yields the largest boost in exact match. The analysis reveals an "agreement paradox," where strong consensus across samples may indicate shared hallucinations rather than correctness, challenging naive uses of voting-style aggregation. Prompt effectiveness is model-specific, underscoring the importance of prompt–architecture interactions. Overall, the work demonstrates that practical, relatively eco-efficient KG pipelines are feasible on consumer hardware, and provides an evaluation framework to study reasoning diversity and reliability without additional training.

title: Persona Non Grata: Single-Method Safety Evaluation Is Incomplete for Persona-Imbued LLMs
summary: Personality imbuing customizes LLM behavior, but safety evaluations almost always study prompt-based personas alone. We show this is incomplete: prompting and activation steering expose *different*, architecture-dependent vulnerability profiles, and testing with only one method can miss a model's dominant failure mode. Across 5,568 judged conditions on four standard models from three architecture families, persona danger rankings under system prompting are preserved across all architectures ($ρ= 0.71$--$0.96$), but activation-steering vulnerability diverges sharply and cannot be predicted from prompt-side rankings: Llama-3.1-8B is substantially more AS-vulnerable, whereas Gemma-3-27B and Qwen3.5 are more vulnerable to prompting. The most striking illustration of this divergence is the *prosocial persona paradox*: on Llama-3.1-8B, P12 (high conscientiousness + high agreeableness) is among the safest personas under prompting yet becomes the highest-ASR activation-steered persona (ASR ~0.818). This is an inversion robust to coefficient ablation and matched-strength calibration, and replicated on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B. A trait refusal alignment framework, in which conscientiousness is strongly anti-aligned with refusal on Llama-3.1-8B, offers a partial geometric account. Reasoning provides only partial protection: two 32B reasoning models reach 15--18% prompt-side ASR, and activation steering separates them sharply in both baseline susceptibility and persona-specific vulnerability. Heuristic trace diagnostics suggest that the safer model retains stronger policy recall and self-correction behavior, not merely longer reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that evaluating LLM safety with only prompt-based personas is misleading because activation steering reveals distinct, architecture-dependent vulnerabilities, including cases where seemingly prosocial personas become the most dangerous when steered.
AI.motivation: LLMs are often customized via personas to shape behavior, and safety evaluations typically assume that prompt-based persona behavior reflects overall safety. However, new control methods like activation steering may interact with model internals differently, potentially creating unseen vulnerabilities, particularly across different architectures and model sizes. The authors aim to test whether evaluating only prompt-based personas is sufficient to characterize safety risks.
AI.method: The authors systematically compare two ways of imbuing personas in LLMs: system-level prompt-based personas and activation steering (directly modifying internal activations) across four standard models from three architecture families, including Llama-3.1-8B, Gemma-3-27B, Qwen3.5, and two 32B reasoning models. They evaluate 5,568 persona–model–method conditions, measure attack success rates (ASR) under both prompting and activation steering, and analyze correlations of persona danger rankings between methods and architectures. They further investigate a specific case, the prosocial persona P12 (high conscientiousness + high agreeableness), using coefficient ablation, matched-strength calibration, and geometric analysis of trait–refusal alignment to explain its paradoxical behavior under activation steering. Heuristic trace diagnostics are used to compare reasoning models’ policy recall and self-correction.
AI.result: Prompt-based persona danger rankings are largely preserved across architectures (Spearman ρ = 0.71–0.96), indicating consistent cross-model behavior under prompting. In contrast, activation-steering vulnerabilities vary sharply by architecture and cannot be inferred from prompt-based rankings: Llama-3.1-8B is much more vulnerable to activation steering, while Gemma-3-27B and Qwen3.5 are more susceptible to prompt-based personas. The most striking finding is the prosocial persona paradox: persona P12, which is among the safest under prompting on Llama-3.1-8B, becomes the most vulnerable under activation steering with an ASR of about 0.818, a result robust to ablation and calibration and replicated on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B. Trait–refusal alignment analysis shows conscientiousness is strongly anti-aligned with refusal on Llama-3.1-8B, partially explaining this inversion. Reasoning models still show nontrivial prompt-side ASR (15–18%), and activation steering further separates them in baseline susceptibility and persona-specific risks. Trace diagnostics suggest that the safer reasoning model better recalls safety policies and self-corrects rather than simply reasoning longer.
AI.conclusion: Evaluating LLM safety solely via prompt-based personas is inadequate and can miss or mischaracterize dominant failure modes, particularly with activation steering and across diverse architectures. Persona-induced vulnerabilities are method- and architecture-dependent, and prosocial traits that appear safe under prompting can become highly dangerous when implemented via activation steering. Safety evaluations should therefore consider multiple control mechanisms, model families, and internal geometric properties such as trait–refusal alignment, and should not rely on reasoning capabilities or prosocial persona design as sufficient safeguards.

title: A Proposed Biomedical Data Policy Framework to Reduce Fragmentation, Improve Quality, and Incentivize Sharing in Indian Healthcare in the era of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Health
summary: India generates vast biomedical data through postgraduate research, government hospital services and audits, government schemes, private hospitals and their electronic medical record (EMR) systems, insurance programs and standalone clinics. Unfortunately, these resources remain fragmented across institutional silos and vendor-locked EMR systems. The fundamental bottleneck is not technological but economic and academic. There is a systemic misalignment of incentives that renders data sharing a high-risk, low-reward activity for individual researchers and institutions. Until India's academic promotion criteria, institutional rankings, and funding mechanisms explicitly recognize and reward data curation as professional work, the nation's AI ambitions will remain constrained by fragmented, non-interoperable datasets. We propose a multi-layered incentive architecture integrating recognition of data papers in National Medical Commission (NMC) promotion criteria, incorporation of open data metrics into the National Institutional Ranking Framework (NIRF), adoption of Shapley Value-based revenue sharing in federated learning consortia, and establishment of institutional data stewardship as a mainstream professional role. Critical barriers to data sharing, including fear of data quality scrutiny, concerns about misinterpretation, and selective reporting bias, are addressed through mandatory data quality assessment, structured peer review, and academic credit for auditing roles. The proposed framework directly addresses regulatory constraints introduced by the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDPA), while constructively engaging with the National Data Sharing and Accessibility Policy (NDSAP), Biotech-PRIDE Guidelines, and the Anusandhan National Research Foundation (ANRF) guidelines.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that India’s biomedical AI ambitions are constrained not by technology but by fragmented, non-interoperable data caused by misaligned academic and economic incentives; it proposes a multi-layered incentive and governance framework to make data sharing and curation professionally rewarded and regulatorily compliant.
AI.motivation: India produces large volumes of biomedical data from diverse sources, but these remain locked in silos and proprietary EMR systems. This fragmentation prevents creation of high-quality, interoperable datasets needed for AI and data-driven healthcare. The core issue is that researchers and institutions face high risks and low rewards for sharing data, since current promotion, ranking, and funding systems value publications more than data curation, quality assurance, and sharing. Without fixing this incentive misalignment, investments in AI and digital health will not yield their full potential.
AI.method: The paper uses a policy and systems-design approach rather than empirical experimentation. It analyzes current incentive structures, regulatory constraints (DPDPA 2023, NDSAP, Biotech-PRIDE, ANRF), and practical barriers to data sharing (fear of scrutiny, misinterpretation, selective reporting). Based on this analysis, it proposes: (1) embedding recognition of data papers into National Medical Commission promotion criteria; (2) including open data metrics in the National Institutional Ranking Framework; (3) using Shapley Value-based revenue sharing for federated learning consortia to fairly allocate returns from shared models; and (4) formalizing institutional data stewardship as a professional role with defined responsibilities and credit. It further specifies mechanisms like mandatory data quality assessment, structured peer review, and academic credit for auditing to mitigate identified barriers.
AI.result: The primary result is a conceptual framework: a multi-layered incentive architecture that connects academic rewards, institutional rankings, financial revenue sharing, and professional roles in data stewardship. It demonstrates how each layer can counteract specific disincentives to data sharing and how the framework can be implemented within existing Indian regulatory and governance structures. The paper maps regulatory requirements to concrete practices (quality assessment, oversight, stewardship) that enable compliant yet useful data sharing and federated learning collaborations.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that technological solutions alone cannot unlock India’s biomedical data for AI; incentive realignment and institutional reform are essential. By explicitly rewarding data curation and sharing in promotions, rankings, and revenue models—and by professionalizing data stewardship—India can build interoperable, high-quality datasets while remaining compliant with data protection laws. Implementing the proposed framework would transform data sharing from a risky, low-reward activity into a recognized and valuable component of academic and institutional performance, thereby supporting national AI and health system goals.

title: MADQRL: Distributed Quantum Reinforcement Learning Framework for Multi-Agent Environments
comment: Accepted in QC4C3 Workshop at IEEE QCNC, 2026
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) is one of the most practical ways to learn from real-life use-cases. Motivated from the cognitive methods used by humans makes it a widely acceptable strategy in the field of artificial intelligence. Most of the environments used for RL are often high-dimensional, and traditional RL algorithms becomes computationally expensive and challenging to effectively learn from such systems. Recent advancements in practical demonstration of quantum computing (QC) theories, such as compact encoding, enhanced representation and learning algorithms, random sampling, or the inherent stochastic nature of quantum systems, have opened up new directions to tackle these challenges. Quantum reinforcement learning (QRL) is seeking significant traction over the past few years. However, the current state of quantum hardware is not enough to cater for such high-dimensional environments with complex multi-agent setup. To tackle this issue, we propose a distributed framework for QRL where multiple agents learn independently, distributing the load of joint training from individual machines. Our method works well for environments with disjoint sets of action and observation spaces, but can also be extended to other systems with reasonable approximations. We analyze the proposed method on cooperative-pong environment and our results indicate ~10% improvement from other distribution strategies, and ~5% improvement from classical models of policy representation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a distributed quantum reinforcement learning framework that splits learning across multiple agents to better handle high-dimensional environments, demonstrating modest performance gains over other distribution strategies and classical policy models in a cooperative Pong task.
AI.motivation: Classical reinforcement learning struggles with high-dimensional environments because traditional algorithms become computationally expensive and slow to learn, while current quantum hardware is too limited to handle complex, large-scale, multi-agent quantum RL setups on a single device. There is a need for an approach that can exploit quantum advantages while remaining feasible on today’s hardware.
AI.method: The authors design a distributed quantum reinforcement learning framework in which multiple quantum agents learn independently, thereby distributing the computational and training load across different machines. The framework assumes environments with disjoint action and observation spaces for each agent (with possible extensions via approximations to more general cases). Quantum-enhanced techniques such as compact encoding and quantum policy representations are employed within each agent. The method is empirically evaluated on a cooperative Pong environment and compared against alternative distribution strategies and classical policy representations.
AI.result: In the cooperative Pong environment, the proposed distributed QRL framework achieves approximately 10% performance improvement compared to other distribution strategies and about 5% improvement compared to classical policy representation models. These results suggest better learning efficiency and effectiveness under the tested conditions.
AI.conclusion: The distributed QRL framework makes quantum reinforcement learning more practical for high-dimensional and multi-agent environments under current hardware constraints by spreading the learning process across multiple quantum agents. The experimental results in a cooperative Pong setting show modest but meaningful gains over both classical baselines and alternative distribution methods, indicating that distributed quantum approaches are a promising direction for scaling QRL to more complex tasks.

title: From Answers to Arguments: Toward Trustworthy Clinical Diagnostic Reasoning with Toulmin-Guided Curriculum Goal-Conditioned Learning
summary: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) into clinical decision support is critically obstructed by their opaque and often unreliable reasoning. In the high-stakes domain of healthcare, correct answers alone are insufficient; clinical practice demands full transparency to ensure patient safety and enable professional accountability. A pervasive and dangerous weakness of current LLMs is their tendency to produce "correct answers through flawed reasoning." This issue is far more than a minor academic flaw; such process errors signal a fundamental lack of robust understanding, making the model prone to broader hallucinations and unpredictable failures when faced with real-world clinical complexity. In this paper, we establish a framework for trustworthy clinical argumentation by adapting the Toulmin model to the diagnostic process. We propose a novel training pipeline: Curriculum Goal-Conditioned Learning (CGCL), designed to progressively train LLM to generate diagnostic arguments that explicitly follow this Toulmin structure. CGCL's progressive three-stage curriculum systematically builds a solid clinical argument: (1) extracting facts and generating differential diagnoses; (2) justifying a core hypothesis while rebutting alternatives; and (3) synthesizing the analysis into a final, qualified conclusion. We validate CGCL using T-Eval, a quantitative framework measuring the integrity of the diagnosis reasoning. Experiments show that our method achieves diagnostic accuracy and reasoning quality comparable to resource-intensive Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods, while offering a more stable and efficient training pipeline.

AI.tldr: They train LLMs to produce transparent, structured clinical reasoning rather than just final answers, using a Toulmin-argument-based curriculum, and show it matches RL methods in accuracy and reasoning quality with more efficient, stable training.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs used for clinical decision support can give correct diagnoses but via flawed or opaque reasoning, which is dangerous in healthcare because clinicians need traceable, reliable arguments for safety and accountability. The authors want a way to make diagnostic reasoning explicit, robust, and evaluable rather than relying only on final-answer accuracy.
AI.method: They adapt the Toulmin model of argumentation to clinical diagnosis and design Curriculum Goal-Conditioned Learning (CGCL), a three-stage training pipeline. Stage 1: extract clinical facts and generate differential diagnoses. Stage 2: build an argument for a main diagnostic hypothesis while rebutting alternatives, explicitly in Toulmin structure. Stage 3: synthesize this reasoning into a final, qualified diagnostic conclusion. They evaluate reasoning integrity with a dedicated metric/framework called T-Eval and compare CGCL against reinforcement-learning-based approaches.
AI.result: Models trained with CGCL achieve diagnostic accuracy and reasoning quality on par with more resource-intensive RL-based methods. Their approach is reported to be more stable during training and more computationally efficient, while producing arguments that conform to the Toulmin-based structure measured by T-Eval.
AI.conclusion: Structured, curriculum-based training grounded in an explicit argumentation model can make LLM clinical reasoning more transparent and trustworthy without sacrificing accuracy. CGCL emerges as a practical alternative to RL for aligning LLM diagnostic reasoning with clinical standards of justification and accountability.

title: Environmental Footprint of GenAI Research: Insights from the Moshi Foundation Model
summary:
New multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) are continuously being trained and deployed, following rapid development cycles. This generative AI frenzy is driving steady increases in energy consumption, greenhouse gas emissions, and a plethora of other environmental impacts linked to datacenter construction and hardware manufacturing. Mitigating the environmental consequences of GenAI remains challenging due to an overall lack of transparency by the main actors in the field. Even when the environmental impacts of specific models are mentioned, they are typically restricted to the carbon footprint of the final training run, omitting the research and development stages.
  In this work, we explore the impact of GenAI research through a fine-grained analysis of the compute spent to create Moshi, a 7B-parameter speech-text foundation model for real-time dialogue developed by Kyutai, a leading privately funded open science AI lab. For the first time, our study dives into the anatomy of compute-intensive MLLM research, quantifying the GPU-time invested in specific model components and training phases, as well as early experimental stages, failed training runs, debugging, and ablation studies. Additionally, we assess the environmental impacts of creating Moshi from beginning to end using a life cycle assessment methodology: we quantify energy and water consumption, greenhouse gas emissions, and mineral resource depletion associated with the production and use of datacenter hardware.
  Our detailed analysis allows us to provide actionable guidelines to reduce compute usage and environmental impacts of MLLM research, paving the way for more sustainable AI research.

AI.tldr: The paper quantifies the full compute and environmental footprint of developing Moshi, a 7B multi-modal speech-text model, across all R&D stages, and extracts guidelines for more sustainable MLLM research.
AI.motivation: Generative AI and MLLMs are being developed at a rapid pace, driving up energy use, emissions, and other environmental impacts, but model developers rarely disclose more than the CO2 from a single final training run. There is little transparency on the full life cycle impacts, especially covering early experimentation, failed runs, and hardware manufacturing, making it hard to understand and mitigate GenAI’s true environmental cost.
AI.method: The authors conduct a fine-grained accounting of all compute used to develop Moshi, a 7B-parameter real-time speech-text foundation model. They decompose GPU time by model components, training phases, and R&D activities (including debugging, ablations, and failed runs). They then apply life cycle assessment (LCA) to translate compute and hardware use into environmental indicators such as energy and water consumption, greenhouse gas emissions, and mineral resource depletion, accounting for both datacenter operation and hardware production.
AI.result: They obtain a detailed “compute anatomy” of MLLM research for Moshi, showing how much GPU time is consumed at each stage of development and by which components, and quantify the associated energy use, emissions, water use, and resource depletion for both the hardware and datacenter operation. This reveals where the largest environmental burdens and inefficiencies occur along the R&D pipeline, beyond just the final training run.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that the environmental cost of MLLM development is substantially larger than what is implied by reporting only the final training run. With a full life cycle and R&D-stage perspective, the authors derive concrete, actionable guidelines to cut compute, energy, and resource use in future GenAI and MLLM projects, thereby enabling more transparent and sustainable AI research practices.

title: Mobile GUI Agent Privacy Personalization with Trajectory Induced Preference Optimization
summary: Mobile GUI agents powered by Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can execute complex tasks on mobile devices. Despite this progress, most existing systems still optimize task success or efficiency, neglecting users' privacy personalization. In this paper, we study the often-overlooked problem of agent personalization. We observe that personalization can induce systematic structural heterogeneity in execution trajectories. For example, privacy-first users often prefer protective actions, e.g., refusing permissions, logging out, and minimizing exposure, leading to logically different execution trajectories from utility-first users. Such variable-length and structurally different trajectories make standard preference optimization unstable and less informative.
