To address this issue, we propose Trajectory Induced Preference Optimization (TIPO), which uses preference-intensity weighting to emphasize key privacy-related steps and padding gating to suppress alignment noise. Results on our Privacy Preference Dataset show that TIPO improves persona alignment and distinction while preserving strong task executability, achieving 65.60% SR, 46.22 Compliance, and 66.67% PD, outperforming existing optimization methods across various GUI tasks. The code and dataset will be publicly released at https://github.com/Zhixin-L/TIPO.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TIPO, a training method that personalizes mobile GUI agents to users’ privacy preferences while maintaining task performance.
AI.motivation: Existing mobile GUI agents using multimodal LLMs optimize for task success and efficiency but ignore that different users have different privacy attitudes (e.g., privacy-first vs. utility-first). These differing preferences cause structurally different execution trajectories, which make standard preference optimization unstable and reduce the quality of alignment to user personas.
AI.method: The authors analyze how different privacy personas (privacy-first vs. utility-first) produce variable-length, structurally heterogeneous GUI action trajectories. They propose Trajectory Induced Preference Optimization (TIPO), which (1) applies preference-intensity weighting to assign higher importance to key privacy-related actions within a trajectory and (2) uses padding gating to filter out or downweight noisy, non-informative padding segments when optimizing preferences. They build a Privacy Preference Dataset of GUI tasks labeled with persona-specific behaviors and train MLLM-based mobile agents with TIPO.
AI.result: On the Privacy Preference Dataset, agents trained with TIPO achieve a 65.60% success rate (SR), 46.22 privacy Compliance, and 66.67% Persona Distinction (PD). These scores surpass those of existing preference optimization methods across a variety of mobile GUI tasks while retaining strong task execution abilities.
AI.conclusion: TIPO effectively personalizes mobile GUI agents to user privacy preferences by accounting for trajectory structure and emphasizing privacy-critical actions. It improves both persona alignment and differentiation without sacrificing overall task performance, and provides a practical training recipe and dataset for future research on privacy-aware, personalized GUI agents.

title: Inspectable AI for Science: A Research Object Approach to Generative AI Governance
summary: This paper introduces AI as a Research Object (AI-RO), a paradigm for governing the use of generative AI in scientific research. Instead of debating whether AI is an author or merely a tool, we propose treating AI interactions as structured, inspectable components of the research process. Under this view, the legitimacy of an AI-assisted scientific paper depends on how model use is integrated into the workflow, documented, and made accountable. Drawing on Research Object theory and FAIR principles, we propose a framework for recording model configuration, prompts, and outputs through interaction logs and metadata packaging. These properties are particularly consequential in security and privacy (S&P) research, where provenance artifacts must satisfy confidentiality constraints, integrity guarantees, and auditability requirements that generic disclosure practices do not address. We implement a lightweight writing pipeline in which a language model synthesizes human-authored structured literature review notes under explicit constraints and produces a verifiable provenance record. We present this work as a position supported by an initial demonstrative workflow, arguing that governance of generative AI in science can be implemented as structured documentation, controlled disclosure, and integrity-preserving provenance capture. Based on this example, we outline and motivate a set of necessary future developments required to make such practices practical and widely adoptable.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes treating generative AI systems and their interactions as explicit, inspectable research objects within scientific workflows, enabling accountable and auditable AI use in research writing, especially in security and privacy.
AI.motivation: Current debates focus on whether AI should be classified as an author or a tool, which does not adequately address how AI is actually used in scientific research. There is a lack of structured, auditable, and privacy-aware documentation of AI involvement, particularly problematic in fields like security and privacy where strict provenance, confidentiality, integrity, and auditability requirements exist.
AI.method: The authors build on Research Object theory and FAIR principles to define AI as a Research Object (AI-RO). They propose a framework where model configuration, prompts, and outputs are captured as interaction logs and metadata packages. They implement a lightweight, demonstrative writing pipeline in which a language model synthesizes human-created structured literature review notes under explicit constraints and, in doing so, generates a verifiable provenance record.
AI.result: The paper delivers a conceptual framework for AI-ROs plus a concrete prototype workflow showing how generative AI can be integrated into scientific writing with structured documentation, constrained use, and provenance capture. The implementation demonstrates that a language model can assist in synthesizing literature while producing artifacts that are inspectable and auditable within security and privacy constraints.
AI.conclusion: The authors argue that effective governance of generative AI in science should be realized through structured documentation of AI use, controlled disclosure of interaction details, and integrity-preserving provenance capture rather than simplistic authorship debates. They conclude that the AI-RO paradigm is promising but requires further development—tooling, standards, and community practices—to become practical and widely adopted across scientific disciplines, especially in security and privacy research.

title: Consistency of AI-Generated Exercise Prescriptions: A Repeated Generation Study Using a Large Language Model
summary: Background: Large language models (LLMs) have been explored as tools for generating personalized exercise prescriptions, yet the consistency of outputs under identical conditions remains insufficiently examined. Objective: This study evaluated the intra-model consistency of LLM-generated exercise prescriptions using a repeated generation design. Methods: Six clinical scenarios were used to generate exercise prescriptions using Gemini 2.5 Flash (20 outputs per scenario; total n = 120). Consistency was assessed across three dimensions: (1) semantic consistency using SBERT-based cosine similarity, (2) structural consistency based on the FITT principle using an AI-as-a-judge approach, and (3) safety expression consistency, including inclusion rates and sentence-level quantification. Results: Semantic similarity was high across scenarios (mean cosine similarity: 0.879-0.939), with greater consistency in clinically constrained cases. Frequency showed consistent patterns, whereas variability was observed in quantitative components, particularly exercise intensity. Unclassifiable intensity expressions were observed in 10-25% of resistance training outputs. Safety-related expressions were included in 100% of outputs; however, safety sentence counts varied significantly across scenarios (H=86.18, p less than 0.001), with clinical cases generating more safety expressions than healthy adult cases. Conclusions: LLM-generated exercise prescriptions demonstrated high semantic consistency but showed variability in key quantitative components. Reliability depends substantially on prompt structure, and additional structural constraints and expert validation are needed before clinical deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how consistently a single large language model (Gemini 2.5 Flash) generates exercise prescriptions when given the same clinical scenarios multiple times.
AI.motivation: As LLMs begin to be used for personalized exercise prescriptions, it is unclear whether they provide stable, repeatable recommendations under identical conditions. Consistency is crucial for clinical reliability and trust, especially when outputs may affect health and safety.
AI.method: The authors used six predefined clinical scenarios and asked Gemini 2.5 Flash to generate 20 exercise prescriptions per scenario (total n=120). They quantified intra-model consistency in three ways: (1) semantic consistency via SBERT-based cosine similarity between outputs, (2) structural consistency using the FITT (Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type) framework evaluated by an AI-as-a-judge method, and (3) safety expression consistency by checking how often safety advice appeared and counting safety-related sentences.
AI.result: Across scenarios, semantic similarity between outputs was high (mean cosine similarity 0.879–0.939), especially in more clinically constrained cases. Frequency recommendations were generally consistent, but quantitative details—particularly exercise intensity—varied notably, and 10–25% of resistance training prescriptions had intensity descriptions that could not be classified. Safety guidance appeared in all outputs, but the number of safety-related sentences differed significantly by scenario, with clinical cases prompting more extensive safety language (H=86.18, p<0.001).
AI.conclusion: While the LLM produced semantically similar exercise prescriptions across repeated generations, important quantitative prescription elements, especially intensity, lacked consistency. The reliability of outputs is strongly influenced by how prompts are structured. For safe clinical use, additional structural constraints on the prompts and expert review of LLM-generated prescriptions are necessary.

title: PaperScope: A Multi-Modal Multi-Document Benchmark for Agentic Deep Research Across Massive Scientific Papers
summary: Leveraging Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to accelerate frontier scientific research is promising, yet how to rigorously evaluate such systems remains unclear. Existing benchmarks mainly focus on single-document understanding, whereas real scientific workflows require integrating evidence from multiple papers, including their text, tables, and figures. As a result, multi-modal, multi-document scientific reasoning remains underexplored and lacks systematic evaluation. To address this gap, we introduce PaperScope, a multi-modal multi-document benchmark designed for agentic deep research. PaperScope presents three advantages: (1) Structured scientific grounding. It is built on a knowledge graph of over 2,000 AI papers spanning three years, providing a structured foundation for research-oriented queries. (2) Semantically dense evidence construction. It integrates semantically related key information nodes and employs optimized random-walk article selector to sample thematically coherent paper sets, thereby ensuring adequate semantic density and task complexity. (3) Multi-task evaluation of scientific reasoning. It contains over 2,000 QA pairs across reasoning, retrieval, summarization, and problem solving, enabling evaluation of multi-step scientific reasoning. Experimental results show that even advanced systems such as OpenAI Deep Research and Tongyi Deep Research achieve limited scores on PaperScope, highlighting the difficulty of long-context retrieval and deep multi-source reasoning. PaperScope thus provides a rigorous benchmark alongside a scalable pipeline for constructing large-scale multi-modal, multi-source deep research datasets.

AI.tldr: PaperScope is a new benchmark for testing multi-modal, multi-document scientific reasoning of large language model-based research agents.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of multi-modal large language models mostly test single-document understanding, while real scientific research requires integrating evidence from many papers, including text, tables, and figures. There is no systematic way to evaluate this kind of multi-source, multi-modal reasoning, so it is unclear how capable current research agents truly are.
AI.method: The authors construct PaperScope, a benchmark grounded in a knowledge graph of over 2,000 AI papers from three years. They design a semantically dense evidence construction process that links related information nodes and uses an optimized random-walk article selector to form coherent sets of papers. They then build a multi-task evaluation suite with more than 2,000 question–answer pairs covering reasoning, retrieval, summarization, and problem solving, all requiring multi-step reasoning over multi-modal, multi-document inputs.
AI.result: Using PaperScope, the authors evaluate advanced systems such as OpenAI Deep Research and Tongyi Deep Research and find that their scores are still limited, indicating that these systems struggle with long-context retrieval and deep reasoning across multiple sources and modalities.
AI.conclusion: PaperScope offers a challenging, rigorously constructed benchmark and a scalable data-construction pipeline for assessing and developing multi-modal, multi-document deep research capabilities in large language model systems.

title: Select Smarter, Not More: Prompt-Aware Evaluation Scheduling with Submodular Guarantees
summary: Automatic prompt optimization (APO) hinges on the quality of its evaluation signal, yet scoring every prompt candidate on the full training set is prohibitively expensive. Existing methods either fix a single evaluation subset before optimization begins (principled but prompt-agnostic) or adapt it heuristically during optimization (flexible but unstable and lacking formal guarantees). We observe that APO naturally maps to an online adaptive testing problem: prompts are examinees, training examples are test items, and the scheduler should select items that best discriminate among the strongest candidates. This insight motivates Prompt-Aware Online Evaluation Scheduling (POES), which integrates an IRT-based discrimination utility, a facility-location coverage term, and switching-cost-aware warm-start swaps into a unified objective that is provably monotone submodular, yielding a (1-1/e) greedy guarantee for cold starts and bounded drift for warm-start updates. An adaptive controller modulates the exploration-exploitation balance based on optimization progress. Across 36 tasks spanning three benchmark families, POES achieves the highest overall average accuracy (6.2 percent improvement over the best baseline) with negligible token overhead (approximately 4 percent) at the same evaluation budget. Moreover, principled selection at k = 20 examples matches or exceeds the performance of naive evaluation at k = 30-50, reducing token consumption by 35-60 percent, showing that selecting smarter is more effective than selecting more. Our results demonstrate that evaluation scheduling is a first-class component of APO, not an implementation detail.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes POES, a prompt-aware evaluation scheduling method that treats automatic prompt optimization as an online adaptive testing problem and selects training examples to evaluate prompts efficiently and effectively.
AI.motivation: Automatic prompt optimization requires evaluating many candidate prompts, but scoring each on the full training set is too expensive. Existing approaches either use a fixed evaluation subset, which ignores prompt-specific needs, or adapt subsets heuristically without stability or formal guarantees. There is a need for a principled, efficient, and theoretically grounded way to choose which examples to use when evaluating prompts during optimization.
AI.method: The authors reinterpret APO as an online adaptive testing problem where prompts are examinees and training instances are test items. They introduce Prompt-Aware Online Evaluation Scheduling (POES), which defines a unified objective combining: (1) an Item Response Theory (IRT)-inspired discrimination utility to pick examples that best distinguish strong from weak prompts; (2) a facility-location-style coverage term to ensure diverse coverage of the training set; and (3) a switching-cost-aware warm-start mechanism to limit disruptive changes in the evaluation subset. This objective is proven to be monotone submodular, enabling a greedy (1-1/e)-approximation for initial selection and bounded-drift guarantees for subsequent warm-start updates. An adaptive controller adjusts exploration vs. exploitation based on the current state of APO.
AI.result: On 36 tasks across three benchmark suites, POES delivers the best overall accuracy among compared methods, with an average 6.2% gain over the strongest baseline while adding only about 4% additional token cost at the same evaluation budget. With as few as k = 20 selected examples, POES matches or surpasses performance of naive evaluation with 30–50 examples, achieving 35–60% token savings.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation scheduling—the way training examples are chosen for scoring candidate prompts—is a core design factor in APO, not a mere implementation detail. By casting APO as adaptive testing and optimizing a theoretically grounded submodular objective, POES can evaluate prompts more intelligently, improving performance and reducing token usage compared to naive or heuristic scheduling strategies.

title: Dynamic Summary Generation for Interpretable Multimodal Depression Detection
summary: Depression remains widely underdiagnosed and undertreated because stigma and subjective symptom ratings hinder reliable screening. To address this challenge, we propose a coarse-to-fine, multi-stage framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) for accurate and interpretable detection. The pipeline performs binary screening, five-class severity classification, and continuous regression. At each stage, an LLM produces progressively richer clinical summaries that guide a multimodal fusion module integrating text, audio, and video features, yielding predictions with transparent rationale. The system then consolidates all summaries into a concise, human-readable assessment report. Experiments on the E-DAIC and CMDC datasets show significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines in both accuracy and interpretability.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a multi-stage, LLM-driven framework for more accurate and interpretable depression detection using multimodal data.
AI.motivation: Depression is often underdiagnosed and undertreated because existing screening relies heavily on subjective self-reports and is affected by social stigma, reducing reliability and uptake. There is a need for automated, objective, and interpretable tools that can more accurately detect depression and assess its severity from rich behavioral data.
AI.method: The authors design a coarse-to-fine, multi-stage pipeline that combines large language models with multimodal fusion. First, the system performs binary depression screening, then a five-level severity classification, and finally continuous severity regression. At each stage, an LLM generates increasingly detailed clinical summaries from the input data, which are then used to guide a multimodal fusion module that integrates text, audio, and video features. The LLM-produced summaries also serve as transparent rationales. In the final step, all stage-wise summaries are merged into a concise assessment report intended for human consumption.
AI.result: On the E-DAIC and CMDC depression datasets, the proposed framework outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on key metrics for classification and regression tasks, while also providing more interpretable outputs via the LLM-generated clinical summaries and final report.
AI.conclusion: Embedding LLM-generated clinical reasoning within a coarse-to-fine multimodal pipeline can improve both the accuracy and interpretability of automated depression detection, suggesting a promising direction for clinically useful, human-readable screening and severity assessment tools.

title: CoRe-ECG: Advancing Self-Supervised Representation Learning for 12-Lead ECG via Contrastive and Reconstructive Synergy
summary: Accurate interpretation of electrocardiogram (ECG) remains challenging due to the scarcity of labeled data and the high cost of expert annotation. Self-supervised learning (SSL) offers a promising solution by enabling models to learn expressive representations from unlabeled signals. Existing ECG SSL methods typically rely on either contrastive learning or reconstructive learning. However, each approach in isolation provides limited supervisory signals and suffers from additional limitations, including non-physiological distortions introduced by naive augmentations and trivial correlations across multiple leads that models may exploit as shortcuts. In this work, we propose CoRe-ECG, a unified contrastive and reconstructive pretraining paradigm that establishes a synergistic interaction between global semantic modeling and local structural learning. CoRe-ECG aligns global representations during reconstruction, enabling instance-level discriminative signals to guide local waveform recovery. To further enhance pretraining, we introduce Frequency Dynamic Augmentation (FDA) to adaptively perturb ECG signals based on their frequency-domain importance, and Spatio-Temporal Dual Masking (STDM) to break linear dependencies across leads, increasing the difficulty of reconstructive tasks. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple downstream ECG datasets. Ablation studies further demonstrate the necessity and complementarity of each component. This approach provides a robust and physiologically meaningful representation learning framework for ECG analysis.

AI.tldr: CoRe-ECG is a self-supervised ECG pretraining framework that unifies contrastive and reconstructive learning, introduces smarter augmentations and masking, and achieves state-of-the-art performance on downstream ECG tasks.
AI.motivation: Accurate ECG interpretation requires many expert-labeled examples, which are expensive and scarce. Existing self-supervised approaches for ECG either use contrastive learning or reconstructive learning alone, each providing limited and sometimes misleading supervision (e.g., non-physiological augmentations or trivial cross-lead shortcuts). There is a need for a more physiologically grounded SSL framework that can leverage unlabeled ECGs to learn robust, clinically meaningful representations.
AI.method: The authors propose CoRe-ECG, a unified pretraining paradigm that jointly uses contrastive (global semantic) and reconstructive (local structural) objectives. CoRe-ECG aligns global representations during reconstruction so that instance-discriminative signals guide waveform recovery. They further design two key components: (1) Frequency Dynamic Augmentation (FDA), which adaptively perturbs ECG signals based on frequency-domain importance rather than using naive augmentations; and (2) Spatio-Temporal Dual Masking (STDM), which masks in both spatial (across leads) and temporal dimensions to break linear dependencies between leads and make reconstruction more challenging and informative.
AI.result: CoRe-ECG attains state-of-the-art performance on multiple downstream ECG datasets when used for pretraining. Ablation studies show that removing any of the proposed components (the contrastive–reconstructive coupling, FDA, or STDM) degrades performance, indicating each piece is necessary and that they complement each other.
AI.conclusion: A combined contrastive–reconstructive self-supervised framework with physiologically informed augmentations and masking can learn robust and meaningful ECG representations from unlabeled data. CoRe-ECG offers a more effective and clinically relevant approach to SSL for ECG analysis than prior methods based on contrastive or reconstructive learning alone.

title: The Missing Knowledge Layer in Cognitive Architectures for AI Agents
summary: The two most influential cognitive architecture frameworks for AI agents, CoALA [21] and JEPA [12], both lack an explicit Knowledge layer with its own persistence semantics. This gap produces a category error: systems apply cognitive decay to factual claims, or treat facts and experiences with identical update mechanics. We survey persistence semantics across existing memory systems and identify eight convergence points, from Karpathy's LLM Knowledge Base [10] to the BEAM benchmark's near-zero contradiction-resolution scores [22], all pointing to related architectural gaps. We propose a four-layer decom position (Knowledge, Memory, Wisdom, Intelligence) where each layer has fundamentally different persistence semantics: indefinite supersession, Ebbinghaus decay, evidence-gated revision, and ephemeral inference respectively. Companion implementations in Python and Rust demonstrate the architectural separation is feasible. We borrow terminology from cognitive science as a useful analogy (the Knowledge/Memory distinction echoes Tulving's trichotomy), but our layers are engineering constructs justified by persistence-semantics requirements, not by neural architecture. We argue that these distinctions demand distinct persistence semantics in engineering implementations, and that no current framework or system provides this.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that current cognitive architectures for AI agents mishandle how factual knowledge and experiences should persist over time, and proposes a four-layer architecture with distinct persistence rules for Knowledge, Memory, Wisdom, and Intelligence.
AI.motivation: Existing AI cognitive architectures like CoALA and JEPA do not include an explicit Knowledge layer with its own persistence semantics. As a result, systems inappropriately apply the same update and forgetting mechanisms to both stable factual knowledge and transient experiences, causing a category error. Empirical observations—such as contradiction handling failures and ad‑hoc external knowledge bases—suggest that current designs have structural gaps around how information should persist and be updated.
AI.method: The authors survey persistence semantics across a range of existing memory and knowledge systems, including LLM knowledge bases and benchmarks like BEAM, to identify common shortcomings. From these observations they derive a conceptual, four‑layer decomposition—Knowledge, Memory, Wisdom, Intelligence—each defined primarily by its persistence semantics. They then build companion implementations in Python and Rust to validate that this separation can be implemented in practice.
AI.result: They identify eight convergence points across surveyed systems that all indicate similar architectural problems in how persistence and updates are handled. From this, they formalize four distinct layers with different persistence behaviors: Knowledge (indefinite, with supersession rather than decay), Memory (subject to Ebbinghaus-like decay), Wisdom (revised only when sufficient evidence accumulates), and Intelligence (purely ephemeral inference state). Their prototype implementations show that such a separation of layers and semantics is practically realizable.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that cognitive architectures for AI agents should explicitly distinguish Knowledge, Memory, Wisdom, and Intelligence, each governed by different persistence semantics. These distinctions are engineering requirements, not just cognitive-science analogies, and current popular frameworks lack this separation. To progress toward more robust and coherent AI agents, future systems must incorporate dedicated mechanisms for the differing persistence and update needs of each layer.

title: Learning from Contrasts: Synthesizing Reasoning Paths from Diverse Search Trajectories
summary: Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) has been widely used for automated reasoning data exploration, but current supervision extraction methods remain inefficient. Standard approaches retain only the single highest-reward trajectory, discarding the comparative signals present in the many explored paths. Here we introduce \textbf{Contrastive Reasoning Path Synthesis (CRPS)}, a framework that transforms supervision extraction from a filtering process into a synthesis procedure. CRPS uses a structured reflective process to analyze the differences between high- and low-quality search trajectories, extracting explicit information about strategic pivots and local failure modes. These insights guide the synthesis of reasoning chains that incorporate success patterns while avoiding identified pitfalls. We show empirically that models fine-tuned on just 60K CRPS-synthesized examples match or exceed the performance of baselines trained on 590K examples derived from standard rejection sampling, a 20$\times$ reduction in dataset size. Furthermore, CRPS improves generalization on out-of-domain benchmarks, demonstrating that learning from the contrast between success and failure produces more transferable reasoning capabilities than learning from success alone.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Contrastive Reasoning Path Synthesis (CRPS), a new way to extract training supervision from Monte Carlo Tree Search by comparing good and bad reasoning paths instead of keeping only the best ones.
AI.motivation: Existing MCTS-based data generation keeps just the single highest-reward trajectory and discards the rest, wasting rich comparative information about why some reasoning paths succeed and others fail. This leads to inefficient supervision extraction that requires large datasets to train strong reasoning models.
AI.method: CRPS runs MCTS to generate multiple trajectories, then performs a structured reflective analysis contrasting high- and low-quality paths. It identifies strategic pivots and local failure modes and uses these explicit insights to synthesize new reasoning chains that preserve the successful patterns while avoiding the observed pitfalls. These synthesized chains are then used as training data for fine-tuning models.
AI.result: Models fine-tuned on about 60K CRPS-synthesized examples achieve comparable or better performance than baselines trained on roughly 590K examples created via standard rejection sampling, corresponding to a 20x reduction in required dataset size. CRPS-trained models also show improved generalization on out-of-domain benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Using contrastive information between successful and failed MCTS trajectories yields more informative and data-efficient supervision than learning only from the best trajectories. CRPS thus provides a more effective way to convert search traces into training signals, improving both sample efficiency and out-of-domain generalization for reasoning models.

title: From Agent Loops to Structured Graphs:A Scheduler-Theoretic Framework for LLM Agent Execution
summary: The dominant paradigm for building LLM based agents is the Agent Loop, an iterative cycle where a single language model decides what to do next by reading an ever growing context window. This paradigm has three structural weaknesses: implicit dependencies between steps, unbounded recovery loops, and mutable execution history that complicates debugging. We characterize the Agent Loop as a single ready unit scheduler: at any moment, at most one executable unit is active, and the choice of which unit to activate comes from opaque LLM inference rather than an inspectable policy. This perspective places Agent Loops and graph based execution engines on a single semantic continuum. We propose SGH, Structured Graph Harness, which lifts control flow from implicit context into an explicit static DAG. SGH makes three commitments: execution plans are immutable within a plan version, planning execution and recovery are separated into three layers, and recovery follows a strict escalation protocol. These choices trade some expressiveness for controllability, verifiability, and implementability. Our contributions are fourfold: a scheduler unified framework that applies classical scheduling theory to LLM agent execution and identifies challenges introduced by non deterministic LLM nodes; a trade off analysis of controllability, expressiveness, and implementability across 70 surveyed systems; a formal specification including a node state machine with termination and soundness guarantees; and an attributable experimental framework with a seven group design for future validation. This is a position paper and design proposal. We provide a theoretical framework, design analysis, and experimental protocol, not a production implementation or empirical results.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that the common “agent loop” pattern for LLM agents is structurally flawed and proposes a more controllable DAG-based framework (SGH) along with a formal model, design trade-off analysis, and an experimental protocol.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agents typically use an iterative agent loop where a single LLM repeatedly reads an expanding context and decides the next action. This has weaknesses: hidden step dependencies, potentially infinite recovery/retry loops, and mutable execution histories that make debugging and verification hard. There is a need for a more principled, inspectable, and controllable execution model for LLM agents.
AI.method: The authors reconceptualize the agent loop as a single ready-unit scheduler and place it on a continuum with graph-based execution engines using classical scheduling theory. They then design SGH (Structured Graph Harness), which encodes control flow explicitly as an immutable static DAG, separates planning/execution/recovery into distinct layers, and imposes a strict recovery escalation protocol. They provide a formal specification, including a node state machine and guarantees, and perform a survey-based trade-off analysis of 70 systems. They also outline a multi-group experimental framework for future validation.
AI.result: There is no empirical implementation or benchmarked system in this position paper. The main results are: (1) a unified scheduling-theoretic framework for reasoning about LLM agent execution, especially with nondeterministic LLM nodes; (2) a design of SGH with clear commitments about immutability, separation of concerns, and recovery behavior; (3) a formal specification with termination and soundness guarantees; and (4) a structured experimental protocol with seven groups for later empirical validation.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that the dominant agent-loop paradigm for LLM agents sacrifices controllability and verifiability for flexibility. By making control flow explicit via a static DAG (SGH) and by formally specifying execution, scheduling, and recovery, one can gain better debuggability, safety, and analyzability, at the cost of some expressiveness. They position SGH as a theoretical and design foundation rather than a finished system, inviting future work to implement and empirically validate the proposed framework.

title: Beyond RAG for Cyber Threat Intelligence: A Systematic Evaluation of Graph-Based and Agentic Retrieval
summary: Cyber threat intelligence (CTI) analysts must answer complex questions over large collections of narrative security reports. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems help language models access external knowledge, but traditional vector retrieval often struggles with queries that require reasoning over relationships between entities such as threat actors, malware, and vulnerabilities. This limitation arises because relevant evidence is often distributed across multiple text fragments and documents. Knowledge graphs address this challenge by enabling structured multi-hop reasoning through explicit representations of entities and relationships. However, multiple retrieval paradigms, including graph-based, agentic, and hybrid approaches, have emerged with different assumptions and failure modes. It remains unclear how these approaches compare in realistic CTI settings and when graph grounding improves performance. We present a systematic evaluation of four RAG architectures for CTI analysis: standard vector retrieval, graph-based retrieval over a CTI knowledge graph, an agentic variant that repairs failed graph queries, and a hybrid approach combining graph queries with text retrieval. We evaluate these systems on 3,300 CTI question-answer pairs spanning factual lookups, multi-hop relational queries, analyst-style synthesis questions, and unanswerable cases. Results show that graph grounding improves performance on structured factual queries. The hybrid graph-text approach improves answer quality by up to 35 percent on multi-hop questions compared to vector RAG, while maintaining more reliable performance than graph-only systems.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates different retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) architectures for cyber threat intelligence (CTI) analysis and finds that knowledge-graph-based and hybrid graph-text retrieval significantly improve performance on structured and multi-hop questions over standard vector retrieval.
AI.motivation: CTI analysts need to answer complex, multi-hop questions over large corpora of narrative security reports, where relevant evidence is scattered across documents. Standard vector-based RAG often fails on these relational and multi-document reasoning tasks. While knowledge graphs and newer graph-based, agentic, and hybrid RAG methods promise better structured reasoning, it is unclear how they compare in realistic CTI scenarios and when graph grounding truly helps.
AI.method: The authors construct a CTI knowledge graph from security reports and implement four RAG architectures: (1) standard dense vector retrieval over text, (2) graph-based retrieval over the CTI knowledge graph, (3) an agentic graph variant that detects and repairs failed graph queries, and (4) a hybrid graph-text system that combines graph queries with traditional text retrieval. They then evaluate all systems on 3,300 CTI question-answer pairs covering factual lookups, multi-hop relational queries, analyst-style synthesis questions, and unanswerable queries.
AI.result: Graph-grounded approaches outperform pure vector retrieval on structured factual questions. The hybrid graph-text system achieves up to a 35% improvement in answer quality on multi-hop relational questions compared to standard vector RAG, and it is more robust and reliable than graph-only systems, which can fail when graph coverage or queries are incomplete.
AI.conclusion: Grounding RAG in a CTI knowledge graph improves performance where questions involve explicit entities and relationships, especially for multi-hop queries. A hybrid graph-text architecture offers the best balance, combining the structured reasoning benefits of graphs with the broader coverage and robustness of text retrieval, outperforming both pure vector and pure graph-based RAG in realistic CTI analysis scenarios.

title: Anthropogenic Regional Adaptation in Multimodal Vision-Language Model
summary: While the field of vision-language (VL) has achieved remarkable success in integrating visual and textual information across multiple languages and domains, there is still no dedicated framework for assessing human-centric alignment in vision-language systems. We offer two contributions to address this gap. First, we introduce Anthropogenic Regional Adaptation: a novel paradigm that aims to optimize model relevance to specific regional contexts while ensuring the retention of global generalization capabilities. Second, we present a simple, but effective adaptation method named Geographical-generalization-made-easy (GG-EZ), which utilizes regional data filtering and model merging. Through comprehensive experiments on 3 VL architectures: large vision-language models, text-to-image diffusion models, and vision-language embedding models, and a case study in Southeast Asia (SEA) regional adaptation, we demonstrate the importance of Anthropogenic Regional Adaptation and the effectiveness of GG-EZ, showing 5-15% gains in cultural relevance metrics across SEA while maintaining over 98% of global performance and even occasionally surpassing it. Our findings establish Anthropogenic Regional Alignment as a foundational paradigm towards applicability of multimodal vision-language models in diverse regions and demonstrate a simple-yet-effective baseline method that optimizes regional value alignment while preserving global generalization.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new paradigm and a simple adaptation method to better align vision-language models with specific regional cultures while keeping strong global performance.
AI.motivation: Although vision-language systems work well across many languages and domains, there is no dedicated framework to evaluate and optimize how well they align with human values and cultural contexts in specific regions. This gap hinders practical deployment in diverse real-world settings, especially in underrepresented regions like Southeast Asia.
AI.method: They define a new paradigm called Anthropogenic Regional Adaptation, focusing on improving regional cultural relevance without sacrificing global generalization. They then introduce GG-EZ, a simple adaptation pipeline based on regional data filtering and model merging. GG-EZ is applied to three kinds of VL architectures—large VL models, text-to-image diffusion models, and VL embedding models—and evaluated in a Southeast Asia case study using cultural relevance metrics and global benchmarks.
AI.result: Using GG-EZ within the Anthropogenic Regional Adaptation paradigm yields 5–15% improvements in cultural relevance for Southeast Asia while retaining more than 98% of the models’ original global performance, and sometimes even improves global metrics. These results hold across three different classes of vision-language models.
AI.conclusion: Anthropogenic Regional Adaptation (or Alignment) is introduced as a core paradigm for making multimodal vision-language systems more applicable and culturally aligned across diverse regions. The GG-EZ approach is validated as a simple yet strong baseline that can enhance regional value alignment with minimal loss—and occasional gains—in global generalization performance.

title: Escaping the Context Bottleneck: Active Context Curation for LLM Agents via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with long-horizon tasks due to the "context bottleneck" and the "lost-in-the-middle" phenomenon, where accumulated noise from verbose environments degrades reasoning over multi-turn interactions. To address this issue, we introduce a symbiotic framework that decouples context management from task execution. Our architecture pairs a lightweight, specialized policy model, ContextCurator, with a powerful frozen foundation model, TaskExecutor. Trained via reinforcement learning, ContextCurator actively reduces information entropy in the working memory. It aggressively prunes environmental noise while preserving reasoning anchors, that is, sparse data points that are critical for future deductions. On WebArena, our framework improves the success rate of Gemini-3.0-flash from 36.4% to 41.2% while reducing token consumption by 8.8% (from 47.4K to 43.3K). On DeepSearch, it achieves a 57.1% success rate, compared with 53.9%, while reducing token consumption by a factor of 8. Remarkably, a 7B ContextCurator matches the context management performance of GPT-4o, providing a scalable and computationally efficient paradigm for autonomous long-horizon agents.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ContextCurator, a lightweight policy model that manages context for a larger frozen LLM (TaskExecutor), improving long-horizon task performance and reducing token usage by aggressively pruning irrelevant information while keeping key reasoning anchors.
AI.motivation: LLMs perform poorly on long-horizon, multi-turn tasks because they must handle large, noisy context windows, leading to the context bottleneck and lost-in-the-middle issues where important information is diluted or forgotten. There is a need for a scalable, efficient way to manage contextual information so that powerful foundation models can reason effectively without being overwhelmed by verbosity.
AI.method: The authors design a symbiotic two-model architecture: a small, specialized model called ContextCurator and a large frozen foundation model called TaskExecutor. ContextCurator is trained with reinforcement learning to manage a working memory: it selectively prunes high-entropy, noisy information while retaining key "reasoning anchors"—sparse but crucial facts needed for future deduction. TaskExecutor focuses solely on task execution using this curated context. The approach is evaluated on benchmarks like WebArena and DeepSearch, comparing success rates and token consumption against baselines such as raw Gemini-3.0-flash and GPT-4o for context management.
AI.result: On the WebArena benchmark, pairing Gemini-3.0-flash with ContextCurator increases task success from 36.4% to 41.2% while cutting token usage by 8.8% (47.4K to 43.3K tokens). On DeepSearch, the framework raises success from 53.9% to 57.1% and reduces token consumption by 8x. Furthermore, a 7B-parameter ContextCurator achieves context management quality comparable to GPT-4o, despite being significantly smaller and more efficient.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling context management from task execution via a dedicated, reinforcement-learned policy model substantially improves long-horizon LLM agents. The ContextCurator–TaskExecutor framework mitigates the context bottleneck and lost-in-the-middle problems, yields higher success rates with fewer tokens, and offers a scalable, computationally efficient paradigm where a relatively small model can match the context management capabilities of state-of-the-art large LLMs.

title: Agentic Driving Coach: Robustness and Determinism of Agentic AI-Powered Human-in-the-Loop Cyber-Physical Systems
summary: Foundation models, including large language models (LLMs), are increasingly used for human-in-the-loop (HITL) cyber-physical systems (CPS) because foundation model-based AI agents can potentially interact with both the physical environments and human users. However, the unpredictable behavior of human users and AI agents, in addition to the dynamically changing physical environments, leads to uncontrollable nondeterminism. To address this urgent challenge of enabling agentic AI-powered HITL CPS, we propose a reactor-model-of-computation (MoC)-based approach, realized by the open-source Lingua Franca (LF) framework. We also carry out a concrete case study using the agentic driving coach as an application of HITL CPS. By evaluating the LF-based agentic HITL CPS, we identify practical challenges in reintroducing determinism into such agentic HITL CPS and present pathways to address them.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a deterministic, reactor-model-of-computation approach (via Lingua Franca) to design agentic, human-in-the-loop cyber-physical systems using foundation models, and validates it with an agentic driving coach case study, identifying challenges and solutions for reintroducing determinism.
AI.motivation: Foundation models and LLM-based agents are being integrated into human-in-the-loop cyber-physical systems, but the combination of human unpredictability, autonomous AI behavior, and dynamic physical environments introduces high nondeterminism that threatens safety, reliability, and controllability. There is an urgent need for methods that bring structure and determinism back into such systems so they can be engineered and verified like traditional CPS.
AI.method: The authors adopt and extend a reactor model of computation (MoC), implemented using the open-source Lingua Franca (LF) framework, to structure interactions among humans, AI agents, and physical components. They design an agentic HITL CPS in Lingua Franca, where foundation-model-based AI agents are encapsulated as reactors with explicit timing, causality, and communication semantics. A concrete agentic driving coach application is implemented as a case study to evaluate how LF handles nondeterministic interactions and to explore mechanisms to constrain or manage them.
AI.result: Using the agentic driving coach case study, the authors demonstrate that LF’s reactor MoC can impose deterministic execution semantics on many aspects of an AI-powered HITL CPS, even when components involve foundation models and humans. They empirically identify specific sources of residual nondeterminism—such as variable AI responses, asynchronous human input, and environmental events—and show how LF’s structuring of events, time, and priorities can mitigate or localize these effects. The evaluation highlights both the benefits and the practical limits of reintroducing determinism in such systems.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that a reactor-model-of-computation approach, instantiated with Lingua Franca, is a promising basis for engineering agentic HITL CPS that incorporate foundation models, as it helps reestablish determinism and thus improves analyzability and safety. However, fully deterministic behavior in the presence of autonomous AI and humans remains challenging; the authors outline practical pathways—such as careful interface design, explicit timing control, and constrained agent behavior—to further reduce and manage nondeterminism in future systems.

title: Three Roles, One Model: Role Orchestration at Inference Time to Close the Performance Gap Between Small and Large Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents show promise on realistic tool-use tasks, but deploying capable agents on modest hardware remains challenging. We study whether inference-time scaffolding alone, without any additional training compute, can improve the performance of a small model in complex multi-step environments. Operating on a single 24\,GB GPU, we evaluate Qwen3-8B under both full-precision (FP16, 12K context) and 4-bit quantized (AWQ, 32K context) configurations. Without any intervention, the raw model achieves just 5.4\% (FP16) and 3.0\% (AWQ) task goal completion. Guided by a systematic failure mode analysis, we introduce a three-tier inference scaffolding pipeline that deploys the same frozen model in three distinct roles: (1) a summarization model that preserves critical artifacts (tokens, credentials, API responses) while compressing dialogue history; (2) the main agent model that reasons over the compressed context; and (3) an isolated correction model that reviews and revises the agent's code output without access to conversation history, breaking repetitive failure loops. Applied to the same unmodified model, this scaffolding yields 8.9\% (FP16) and 5.9\% (AWQ) task goal completion, roughly doubling performance in both settings, with particularly strong gains on difficulty-1 tasks (15.8\%$\to$26.3\% FP16; 5.3\%$\to$14.0\% AWQ). On full-precision inference, our scaffolded 8B model surpasses DeepSeek-Coder 33B Instruct (7.1\%) from the original AppWorld evaluation, demonstrating that structured inference-time interventions can make small models competitive with systems 4$\times$ their size. We formalize the approach as a scaffolded policy over a frozen base model, three invocations of the same weights with different conditioning, drawing connections to test-time compute scaling and action-space shaping in reinforcement learning.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that carefully designed inference-time scaffolding—using the same small LLM in three coordinated roles—can nearly double its performance on complex tool-use tasks, making an 8B model competitive with much larger systems, all without additional training.
AI.motivation: Running powerful LLM agents typically requires large models and expensive hardware, limiting deployment on modest GPUs. The authors want to know whether smarter use of a small, frozen model at inference time—without any extra training compute—can significantly improve performance on realistic, multi-step tool-use tasks.
AI.method: They systematically analyze failure modes of a small LLM (Qwen3-8B) on a complex tool-use benchmark (AppWorld), then design a three-tier inference-time scaffolding pipeline that reuses the same frozen model in three roles: (1) a summarization model that compresses the dialogue while preserving key artifacts such as tokens, credentials, and API outputs; (2) a main agent that reasons over this compressed context; and (3) a correction model that only sees the code output and revises it without any conversation history, which prevents the agent from getting stuck in repetitive error loops. They test this with both FP16 (12K context) and 4-bit AWQ quantized (32K context) configurations on a single 24 GB GPU. Conceptually, they formalize this as a scaffolded policy that invokes identical model weights under different conditioning, connecting it to test-time compute scaling and action-space shaping ideas from reinforcement learning.
AI.result: On the AppWorld benchmark, the raw Qwen3-8B model achieves 5.4% (FP16) and 3.0% (AWQ) task goal completion. With the three-tier scaffolding, performance increases to 8.9% (FP16) and 5.9% (AWQ), roughly doubling success rates in both precision regimes. Improvements are especially pronounced on difficulty-1 tasks: from 15.8% to 26.3% (FP16) and from 5.3% to 14.0% (AWQ). Under full-precision inference, the scaffolded 8B model outperforms DeepSeek-Coder 33B Instruct, which previously scored 7.1% on the same evaluation, despite having only a quarter of its parameter count.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time scaffolding—strategically reusing a frozen small LLM in multiple specialized roles—can substantially boost performance on complex, multi-step tool-use tasks without any additional training. This structured, test-time intervention can make small models competitive with much larger ones, suggesting that policies over a fixed base model and careful action-space design are powerful levers for practical deployment on limited hardware.

title: SWE-AGILE: A Software Agent Framework for Efficiently Managing Dynamic Reasoning Context
summary: Prior representative ReAct-style approaches in autonomous Software Engineering (SWE) typically lack the explicit System-2 reasoning required for deep analysis and handling complex edge cases. While recent reasoning models demonstrate the potential of extended Chain-of-Thought (CoT), applying them to the multi-turn SWE task creates a fundamental dilemma: retaining full reasoning history leads to context explosion and ``Lost-in-the-Middle'' degradation, while discarding it would force the agent to redundantly re-reason at every step. To address these challenges, we propose SWE-AGILE, a novel software agent framework designed to bridge the gap between reasoning depth, efficiency, and context constraints. SWE-AGILE introduces a Dynamic Reasoning Context strategy, maintaining a ``sliding window'' of detailed reasoning for immediate continuity to prevent redundant re-analyzing, while compressing historical reasoning content into concise Reasoning Digests. Empirically, SWE-AGILE sets a new standard for 7B-8B models on SWE-Bench-Verified using only 2.2k trajectories and 896 tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/KDEGroup/SWE-AGILE.

AI.tldr: Proposes SWE-AGILE, a software agent framework that improves autonomous software engineering by balancing deep reasoning with context limits using dynamic reasoning windows and compressed digests, achieving state-of-the-art results on SWE-Bench-Verified for 7B-8B models.
AI.motivation: Existing ReAct-style SWE agents lack explicit, deep System-2 reasoning needed for complex analysis and edge cases. Recent strong reasoning models rely on long Chain-of-Thought, but in multi-turn SWE tasks this causes context explosion and information dilution (Lost-in-the-Middle). Truncating history wastes computation due to repeated re-reasoning, while keeping full history exceeds context limits and hurts performance. There is a need for a framework that preserves reasoning depth and continuity without overwhelming the context window.
AI.method: Introduce SWE-AGILE, a software engineering agent framework with a Dynamic Reasoning Context strategy. It maintains a sliding window of detailed, high-resolution reasoning for the most recent steps to preserve continuity and avoid recomputing analysis. Older reasoning traces are compressed into concise "Reasoning Digests" that summarize key insights and decisions, allowing the agent to keep a global view of past reasoning within limited context. The framework is evaluated on the SWE-Bench-Verified benchmark with 7B-8B language models, using a moderate number of training/evaluation trajectories and tasks.
AI.result: SWE-AGILE achieves new state-of-the-art performance for 7B-8B parameter models on the SWE-Bench-Verified benchmark, doing so with only 2.2k trajectories and 896 tasks, demonstrating both effectiveness and data-efficiency of the dynamic reasoning and summarization approach.
AI.conclusion: A dynamic context management strategy that combines a sliding window of detailed reasoning with compressed reasoning digests effectively reconciles deep System-2 reasoning with tight context constraints in autonomous SWE agents. SWE-AGILE shows that careful structuring and compression of multi-turn reasoning can substantially improve performance of mid-size models on challenging software engineering benchmarks, setting a new performance standard under these constraints.

title: From Attribution to Action: A Human-Centered Application of Activation Steering
summary: Explainable AI (XAI) methods reveal which features influence model predictions, yet provide limited means for practitioners to act on these explanations. Activation steering of components identified via XAI offers a path toward actionable explanations, although its practical utility remains understudied. We introduce an interactive workflow combining SAE-based attribution with activation steering for instance-level analysis of concept usage in vision models, implemented as a web-based tool. Based on this workflow, we conduct semi-structured expert interviews (N=8) with debugging tasks on CLIP to investigate how practitioners reason about, trust, and apply activation steering. We find that steering enables a shift from inspection to intervention-based hypothesis testing (8/8 participants), with most grounding trust in observed model responses rather than explanation plausibility alone (6/8). Participants adopted systematic debugging strategies dominated by component suppression (7/8) and highlighted risks including ripple effects and limited generalization of instance-level corrections. Overall, activation steering renders interpretability more actionable while raising important considerations for safe and effective use.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an interactive workflow and tool that uses SAE-based attribution with activation steering to make explanations of vision models more actionable for debugging, and evaluates it through expert interviews on CLIP.
AI.motivation: Explainable AI methods typically show which features influence model predictions, but they do not easily translate into concrete actions that practitioners can take to debug or modify model behavior. There is a need to bridge the gap between understanding model internals and being able to intervene on them in a controlled, practical way.
AI.method: The authors build a web-based, interactive workflow that combines sparse autoencoder (SAE)-based attribution with activation steering, enabling users to inspect and intervene on instance-level concept usage in vision models. They then run semi-structured expert interviews (N=8), asking participants to use the tool to perform debugging tasks on the CLIP model, and study how they reason about, trust, and use activation steering in practice.
AI.result: All 8 participants shifted from passively inspecting explanations to actively testing hypotheses through interventions. Six of eight based their trust primarily on observing how the model’s behavior changed rather than on the intuitive plausibility of the explanations alone. Most participants relied heavily on suppressing components as a debugging strategy (7/8) and surfaced concerns about side effects (ripple effects across behavior) and the limited generalization of corrections made at the instance level.
AI.conclusion: Activation steering, when combined with SAE-based attribution in an interactive workflow, makes interpretability more actionable by enabling intervention-based hypothesis testing and systematic debugging. However, it also introduces new risks, such as unintended ripple effects and poor generalization of local fixes, which must be managed for safe and effective deployment in practice.

title: Detecting Safety Violations Across Many Agent Traces
summary: To identify safety violations, auditors often search over large sets of agent traces. This search is difficult because failures are often rare, complex, and sometimes even adversarially hidden and only detectable when multiple traces are analyzed together. These challenges arise in diverse settings such as misuse campaigns, covert sabotage, reward hacking, and prompt injection. Existing approaches struggle here for several reasons. Per-trace judges miss failures that only become visible across traces, naive agentic auditing does not scale to large trace collections, and fixed monitors are brittle to unanticipated behaviors. We introduce Meerkat, which combines clustering with agentic search to uncover violations specified in natural language. Through structured search and adaptive investigation of promising regions, Meerkat finds sparse failures without relying on seed scenarios, fixed workflows, or exhaustive enumeration. Across misuse, misalignment, and task gaming settings, Meerkat significantly improves detection of safety violations over baseline monitors, discovers widespread developer cheating on a top agent benchmark, and finds nearly 4x more examples of reward hacking on CyBench than previous audits.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Meerkat, a system that uses clustering plus agentic search to efficiently uncover rare, complex safety violations across large collections of agent traces.
AI.motivation: Auditors need to detect safety violations in large sets of agent traces, but failures are often rare, complex, and only visible when aggregating evidence across multiple traces. Existing methods miss cross-trace failures, do not scale, or are brittle to unanticipated behaviors, especially in settings like misuse campaigns, covert sabotage, reward hacking, and prompt injection.
AI.method: Meerkat combines clustering of large trace collections with iterative, agentic search over promising clusters guided by natural-language specifications of safety violations. It performs structured exploration of the trace space, adaptively zooming in on regions likely to contain failures rather than relying on per-trace judges, fixed monitors, seed scenarios, or exhaustive enumeration.
AI.result: Across scenarios involving misuse, misalignment, and task gaming (e.g., reward hacking), Meerkat detects safety violations significantly better than baseline monitoring approaches. It reveals widespread developer cheating on a top agent benchmark and finds almost four times as many reward hacking instances on CyBench compared to earlier audits.
AI.conclusion: Clustering-based agentic search, as implemented in Meerkat, enables scalable and effective discovery of sparse and complex safety violations in large agent-trace datasets, outperforming traditional per-trace or fixed-monitor auditing methods and uncovering previously unseen problematic behaviors.

title: OOM-RL: Out-of-Money Reinforcement Learning Market-Driven Alignment for LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: The alignment of Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) for autonomous software engineering is constrained by evaluator epistemic uncertainty. Current paradigms, such as Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and AI Feedback (RLAIF), frequently induce model sycophancy, while execution-based environments suffer from adversarial "Test Evasion" by unconstrained agents. In this paper, we introduce an objective alignment paradigm: \textbf{Out-of-Money Reinforcement Learning (OOM-RL)}. By deploying agents into the non-stationary, high-friction reality of live financial markets, we utilize critical capital depletion as an un-hackable negative gradient. Our longitudinal 20-month empirical study (July 2024 -- February 2026) chronicles the system's evolution from a high-turnover, sycophantic baseline to a robust, liquidity-aware architecture. We demonstrate that the undeniable ontological consequences of financial loss forced the MAS to abandon overfitted hallucinations in favor of the \textbf{Strict Test-Driven Agentic Workflow (STDAW)}, which enforces a Byzantine-inspired uni-directional state lock (RO-Lock) anchored to a deterministically verified $\geq 95\%$ code coverage constraint matrix. Our results show that while early iterations suffered severe execution decay, the final OOM-RL-aligned system achieved a stable equilibrium with an annualized Sharpe ratio of 2.06 in its mature phase. We conclude that substituting subjective human preference with rigorous economic penalties provides a robust methodology for aligning autonomous agents in high-stakes, real-world environments, laying the groundwork for generalized paradigms where computational billing acts as an objective physical constraint

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Out-of-Money Reinforcement Learning (OOM-RL), an alignment method for multi-agent systems that uses real financial losses as an objective negative signal, leading to more robust autonomous software engineering agents.
AI.motivation: Existing alignment approaches like RLHF and RLAIF suffer from evaluator uncertainty and lead to sycophancy, while execution-based environments are vulnerable to adversarial test evasion. The authors want a more objective, hard-to-game signal to align autonomous multi-agent systems, especially in high-stakes, real-world software engineering contexts.
AI.method: They deploy a multi-agent system into live financial markets, treating capital depletion as a negative reinforcement signal (OOM-RL). Over 20 months, they let the agents trade, iteratively adapting the architecture. This process pushes the system to adopt a Strict Test-Driven Agentic Workflow (STDAW) featuring a Byzantine-inspired uni-directional state lock (RO-Lock) tied to a deterministically verified code coverage constraint matrix (≥95% coverage).
AI.result: Initially, the system exhibits high turnover, sycophancy, and execution decay. Over time, driven by real financial penalties, the architecture evolves into a liquidity-aware, robust configuration obeying STDAW and RO-Lock constraints. In its mature phase, the trading system attains an annualized Sharpe ratio of 2.06, indicating a favorable risk-adjusted performance and a stable operational equilibrium.
AI.conclusion: Replacing subjective human preference signals with rigorous economic penalties can more reliably align autonomous agents in real-world, high-stakes environments. OOM-RL shows that embedding agents in markets where financial loss is unavoidable and ungameable can force them to abandon hallucinations and overfitting, suggesting a broader paradigm where computational or economic costs serve as objective physical constraints for alignment.

title: On the Complexity of the Discussion-based Semantics in Abstraction Argumentation
summary: We show that deciding whether an argument a is stronger than an argument b with respect to the discussion-based semantics of Amgoud and Ben-Naim is decidable in polynomial time. At its core, this problem is about deciding whether, for two vertices in a graph, the number of walks of each length ending in those vertices is the same. We employ results from automata theory and reduce this problem to the equivalence problem for semiring automata. This offers a new perspective on the computational complexity of ranking semantics, an area in which the complexity of many semantics remains open.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that comparing argument strengths under a specific discussion-based semantics is computable in polynomial time by reducing the problem to checking equivalence of semiring automata.
AI.motivation: To understand and classify the computational complexity of ranking semantics in argumentation theory, where for many semantics the complexity is still unknown. Specifically, the authors want to know how hard it is to decide whether one argument is stronger than another under Amgoud and Ben-Naim's discussion-based semantics.
AI.method: They observe that the strength comparison problem can be reframed as checking whether, for two vertices in a graph, the counts of walks of every possible length ending at each vertex are identical. They then apply results from automata theory and build a reduction from this graph-walk comparison problem to the equivalence problem for semiring automata, whose complexity is known.
AI.result: They obtain a polynomial-time decision procedure for determining whether one argument is stronger than another under the discussion-based semantics of Amgoud and Ben-Naim, via the constructed reduction to semiring automata equivalence.
AI.conclusion: The polynomial-time decidability result provides a clear complexity characterization for this particular ranking semantics and connects argumentation theory with automata-theoretic techniques, suggesting that similar methods might help resolve open complexity questions for other ranking semantics.

title: Lectures on AI for Mathematics
summary: This book provides a comprehensive and accessible introduction to the emerging field of AI for mathematics. It covers the core principles and diverse applications of using artificial intelligence to advance mathematical research. Through clear explanations, the text explores how AI can discover hidden mathematical patterns, assist in proving complicated theorems, and even construct counterexamples to challenge conjectures.

AI.tldr: Introductory book on how AI techniques can be used to aid and extend mathematical research.
AI.motivation: Mathematical research is becoming increasingly complex, and there is a need for tools that can help discover patterns, prove theorems, and test conjectures more efficiently. AI has shown promise in automating or assisting such tasks, but researchers and students need a structured, accessible introduction to this interdisciplinary area.
AI.method: The book surveys and explains core AI principles (such as pattern recognition, search, and learning) and demonstrates how these are applied to mathematical tasks. It uses clear explanations and examples to show how AI systems can detect patterns, contribute steps or ideas for proofs, and generate or search for counterexamples to conjectures.
AI.result: The result is a comprehensive, didactic text that systematizes knowledge about AI methods relevant to mathematics and illustrates concrete applications in theorem proving, pattern discovery, and conjecture testing.
AI.conclusion: AI can effectively support and extend mathematical research by uncovering hidden structures, helping with complex proofs, and stress‑testing conjectures. A structured introduction lowers the barrier for mathematicians and students to engage with AI tools and methods, potentially accelerating future mathematical discoveries.

title: PAC-BENCH: Evaluating Multi-Agent Collaboration under Privacy Constraints
summary: We are entering an era in which individuals and organizations increasingly deploy dedicated AI agents that interact and collaborate with other agents. However, the dynamics of multi-agent collaboration under privacy constraints remain poorly understood. In this work, we present $PAC\text{-}Bench$, a benchmark for systematic evaluation of multi-agent collaboration under privacy constraints. Experiments on $PAC\text{-}Bench$ show that privacy constraints substantially degrade collaboration performance and make outcomes depend more on the initiating agent than the partner. Further analysis reveals that this degradation is driven by recurring coordination breakdowns, including early-stage privacy violations, overly conservative abstraction, and privacy-induced hallucinations. Together, our findings identify privacy-aware multi-agent collaboration as a distinct and unresolved challenge that requires new coordination mechanisms beyond existing agent capabilities.

AI.tldr: Introduces PAC-Bench, a benchmark to study how privacy constraints affect collaboration between AI agents, finding that privacy significantly harms coordination and performance.
AI.motivation: As AI agents become more common and need to work together, they often face privacy constraints on what information can be shared. The field lacks systematic understanding and evaluation frameworks for how such constraints affect multi-agent collaboration and coordination dynamics.
AI.method: Design and implement PAC-Bench, a benchmark suite for evaluating multi-agent collaboration tasks under varying privacy constraints. Run controlled experiments with AI agents collaborating on these tasks, compare performance with and without privacy constraints, and analyze failure modes and coordination dynamics.
AI.result: Privacy constraints markedly reduce collaboration performance and shift outcome dependence toward the initiating agent rather than the partner. The study identifies specific recurring coordination breakdowns: early privacy violations, overly conservative information abstraction, and hallucinations induced by privacy limitations.
AI.conclusion: Privacy-aware multi-agent collaboration behaves qualitatively differently from unconstrained settings and suffers from distinct coordination failures. Existing agent architectures and mechanisms are insufficient; new coordination strategies explicitly designed for privacy constraints are needed.

title: Limited Perfect Monotonical Surrogates constructed using low-cost recursive linkage discovery with guaranteed output
summary: Surrogates provide a cheap solution evaluation and offer significant leverage for optimizing computationally expensive problems. Usually, surrogates only approximate the original function. Recently, the perfect linear surrogates were proposed that ideally represent the original function. These surrogates do not mimic the original function. In fact, they are another (correct) representation of it and enable a wide range of possibilities, e.g., discovering the optimized function for problems where the direct transformation of the encoded solution into its evaluation is not available. However, many real-world problems can not be represented by linear models, making the aforementioned surrogates inapplicable. Therefore, we propose the Limited Monotonical Perfect Surrogate (LyMPuS), which overcomes this difficulty and enables the comparison of two solutions that differ by a single variable. Our proposition is suitable for limiting the cost of expensive local search procedures. The proposed surrogate is parameterless and can be trained on the fly without any separate surrogate-building step. It uses only the necessary fitness evaluations, and the already-paid costs are not wasted when the model is updated. Finally, it offers low-cost missing-linkage detection and low-cost linkage discovery, guaranteed to find a missing dependency in no more than $2\lceil\log_2(n)\rceil$ steps.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LyMPuS, a new surrogate model for expensive optimization problems that remains perfectly consistent with the true objective while handling limited nonlinearity and enabling efficient local search and linkage detection.
AI.motivation: Existing perfect linear surrogates can exactly represent the original function but only when the problem is linearly representable, which excludes many real-world nonlinear problems. At the same time, standard approximate surrogates may be cheap but introduce modeling error and may require separate training phases. There is a need for a surrogate that (1) is perfectly consistent with the original objective, (2) is applicable to broader, non-linear problems, and (3) can be built and updated cheaply during search to reduce the cost of local optimization and linkage analysis.
AI.method: The authors propose the Limited Monotonical Perfect Surrogate (LyMPuS). Instead of globally mimicking the objective, LyMPuS focuses on enabling correct comparison of solutions that differ in a single variable under monotonicity assumptions. It is parameterless and trained incrementally "on the fly" as the optimization progresses—each new true fitness evaluation directly updates the surrogate without a separate model-building step. The design ensures that previously incurred evaluation costs are reused during model updates. LyMPuS is structured so that it can also be used to detect missing linkages (dependencies) among variables with logarithmic complexity in the number of variables.
AI.result: LyMPuS can be applied to optimization problems that are not representable by linear models and still maintain a perfect-consistency property for single-variable comparisons. It enables cheaper local search by reducing the number of expensive true evaluations needed while safely guiding moves in the search space. Additionally, the surrogate supports low-cost detection and discovery of variable linkages, with a theoretical guarantee that any missing dependency can be found in at most 2⌈log2(n)⌉ steps, where n is the number of variables.
AI.conclusion: The Limited Monotonical Perfect Surrogate extends the idea of perfect surrogates beyond purely linear problems, making it more relevant to real-world nonlinear optimization tasks. It offers a parameterless, online-trainable model that preserves evaluation costs and facilitates both efficient local search and provably efficient linkage detection. This makes LyMPuS a practical surrogate framework for expensive black-box optimization where nonlinearity and variable dependencies are present.

title: Problem Reductions at Scale: Agentic Integration of Computationally Hard Problems
comment: The source code is available at https://github.com/CodingThrust/problem-reductions
summary: Solving an NP-hard optimization problem often requires reformulating it for a specific solver -- quantum hardware, a commercial optimizer, or a domain heuristic. A tool for polynomial-time reductions between hard problems would let practitioners route any supported problem to any supported solver through a single interface. Building such a library at scale, however, has remained out of reach. We show that harness engineering, the practice of designing constraints, verification systems, and feedback loops that channel AI coding agents, can overcome this barrier. Our harness combines a no-code contribution route for domain experts, a multilayer verification stack ranging from type-level checks to agentic feature tests (AI agents role-playing as end users), and a fully automated implementation-review-integration pipeline. In about three months, we built a command-line tool backed by a library of 100+ problem types and 200+~reduction rules in over 170k lines of Rust. The result suggests that a well-engineered harness lets agents build well-tested software at a scale and pace beyond prior reduction-library efforts.
