Because the reduction graph composes transitively, a new solver registered for any single problem type instantly becomes available to every problem connected by a reduction path. The source code is available at https://github.com/CodingThrust/problem-reductions.

AI.tldr: They built a large, general-purpose library of reductions between NP-hard optimization problems using AI coding agents coordinated through a carefully engineered harness.
AI.motivation: Practitioners who solve NP-hard optimization problems must usually rewrite each problem for each specific solver (quantum hardware, commercial optimizers, custom heuristics). This is inefficient and scales poorly. A scalable library of polynomial-time reductions between hard problems would allow any supported problem to be routed to any supported solver via a single interface, but past attempts have struggled to reach large coverage and robustness.
AI.method: They introduce a harness-engineering approach to channel AI coding agents into building the reduction library. The harness provides: (1) a no-code contribution pathway for domain experts to specify problems and reductions; (2) a multilayer verification stack, from static/type-level checks to dynamic agentic feature tests where AI agents emulate end users; and (3) a fully automated pipeline for implementation, review, and integration of contributions. Using this harness, agents generate, test, and integrate Rust code implementing problem types and reduction rules.
AI.result: Within about three months, they produced a command-line tool backed by a Rust library comprising over 170k lines of code, including 100+ problem types and 200+ reduction rules. The system yields a transitive reduction graph so that registering a solver for one problem type immediately enables solving all problems connected by reduction paths to that type. The implementation is publicly released as an open-source GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed harness for AI coding agents can enable rapid, large-scale, and well-tested construction of complex reduction libraries for NP-hard optimization problems. This approach appears to surpass previous manual or less-automated efforts in both scale and pace, and it provides a practical, extensible interface that automatically connects new solvers to many problem formulations via transitive reductions.

title: A collaborative agent with two lightweight synergistic models for autonomous crystal materials research
summary: Current large language models require hundreds of billions of parameters yet struggle with domain-specific reasoning and tool coordination in materials science. Here, we present MatBrain, a lightweight collaborative agent system with two synergistic models specialization for crystal materials research. MatBrain employs a dual-model architecture: Mat-R1 (30B parameters) as the analytical model providing expert-level domain reasoning, and Mat-T1 (14B parameters) as the executive model orchestrating tool-based actions. Entropy analysis confirms that this architecture resolves the conflict between tool planning and analytical reasoning by decoupling their distinct entropy dynamics. Enabled by this dual-model architecture and structural efficiency, MatBrain significantly outperforms larger general-purpose models while reducing the hardware deployment barrier by over 95%. MatBrain exhibits versatility across structure generation, property prediction, and synthesis planning tasks. Applied to catalyst design, MatBrain generated 30,000 candidate structures and identified 38 promising materials within 48 hours, achieving approximately 100-fold acceleration over traditional approaches. These results demonstrate the potential of lightweight collaborative intelligence for advancing materials research capabilities.

AI.tldr: MatBrain is a lightweight dual-model agent system specialized for crystal materials research that outperforms much larger general models in reasoning and tool use while massively reducing hardware requirements.
AI.motivation: Large general-purpose language models with hundreds of billions of parameters still perform poorly on domain-specific reasoning and tool coordination in materials science and are expensive to deploy. There is a need for more efficient, specialized AI systems that can reason deeply about materials, coordinate external tools effectively, and run on much smaller hardware resources.
AI.method: The authors design MatBrain, a collaborative agent system with two specialized language models: Mat-R1 (30B) focused on high-quality analytical reasoning in crystal materials science, and Mat-T1 (14B) focused on tool planning and execution. They analyze the entropy characteristics of reasoning vs. tool-planning behaviors and argue that separating these roles resolves conflicts between them. They then benchmark MatBrain against larger general-purpose models on tasks such as structure generation, property prediction, and synthesis planning, and conduct a catalyst design case study.
AI.result: MatBrain, despite its relatively small size, significantly outperforms much larger general-purpose models on a range of crystal-materials tasks, while reducing hardware deployment requirements by more than 95%. In a catalyst design application, MatBrain generated 30,000 candidate structures and identified 38 promising materials within 48 hours, providing roughly a 100x speedup over conventional workflows.
AI.conclusion: A dual-model, role-specialized architecture enables lightweight collaborative intelligence that can both reason expertly in a narrow scientific domain and coordinate tools efficiently. This architecture overcomes limitations of large general-purpose LLMs in materials science and suggests that specialized, structurally efficient multi-agent systems can substantially accelerate materials discovery and related research.

title: SemaClaw: A Step Towards General-Purpose Personal AI Agents through Harness Engineering
summary: The rise of OpenClaw in early 2026 marks the moment when millions of users began deploying personal AI agents into their daily lives, delegating tasks ranging from travel planning to multi-step research. This scale of adoption signals that two parallel arcs of development have reached an inflection point. First is a paradigm shift in AI engineering, evolving from prompt and context engineering to harness engineering-designing the complete infrastructure necessary to transform unconstrained agents into controllable, auditable, and production-reliable systems. As model capabilities converge, this harness layer is becoming the primary site of architectural differentiation. Second is the evolution of human-agent interaction from discrete tasks toward a persistent, contextually aware collaborative relationship, which demands open, trustworthy and extensible harness infrastructure. We present SemaClaw, an open-source multi-agent application framework that addresses these shifts by taking a step towards general-purpose personal AI agents through harness engineering. Our primary contributions include a DAG-based two-phase hybrid agent team orchestration method, a PermissionBridge behavioral safety system, a three-tier context management architecture, and an agentic wiki skill for automated personal knowledge base construction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SemaClaw, an open-source framework for building safe, controllable, multi-agent personal AI systems, focusing on the harness layer rather than just the underlying models.
AI.motivation: Widespread deployment of personal AI agents after OpenClaw’s rise in 2026 created two pressing needs: (1) engineering methods to turn powerful but unconstrained models into controllable, auditable, production-ready agents, and (2) infrastructure to support ongoing, context-rich human–agent collaboration rather than one-off tasks. Existing work focused on prompts and models, leaving a gap at the harness/infrastructure layer and for persistent, trustworthy personal agents.
AI.method: The authors design and implement SemaClaw, an open-source multi-agent application framework centered on harness engineering. Key technical components are: (a) a DAG-based, two-phase, hybrid orchestration mechanism for coordinating teams of agents; (b) PermissionBridge, a behavioral safety layer that mediates and constrains agent actions; (c) a three-tier context management architecture to track and retrieve relevant information over time; and (d) an "agentic wiki" skill that lets agents automatically build and maintain a personal knowledge base for each user.
AI.result: SemaClaw provides a concrete architecture and tooling for deploying general-purpose personal AI agents that can be orchestrated in teams, constrained for safety, and grounded in persistent user-specific knowledge. It operationalizes the idea of the harness layer as the main locus of system differentiation above increasingly similar base models, and demonstrates that multi-agent orchestration, safety mediation, and long-term context handling can be integrated into a single framework.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that future progress in practical personal AI will hinge less on model capabilities alone and more on harness engineering: the infrastructure that makes agents safe, reliable, and collaborative over time. SemaClaw exemplifies this shift by offering an open, extensible framework that implements advanced orchestration, safety, and context management, taking a substantive step toward robust, general-purpose personal AI agents.

title: UniToolCall: Unifying Tool-Use Representation, Data, and Evaluation for LLM Agents
comment: 18 pages, 8 figures, 6 tables. Code and datasets are publicly available at: https://github.com/EIT-NLP/UniToolCall
summary: Tool-use capability is a fundamental component of LLM agents, enabling them to interact with external systems through structured function calls. However, existing research exhibits inconsistent interaction representations, largely overlooks the structural distribution of tool-use trajectories, and relies on incompatible evaluation benchmarks. We present UniToolCall, a unified framework for tool learning that standardizes the entire pipeline from toolset construction and dataset generation to evaluation. The framework curates a large tool pool of 22k+ tools and constructs a hybrid training corpus of 390k+ instances by combining 10 standardized public datasets with structurally controlled synthetic trajectories. It explicitly models diverse interaction patterns, including single-hop vs. multi-hop and single-turn vs. multi-turn, while capturing both serial and parallel execution structures. To support coherent multi-turn reasoning, we further introduce an Anchor Linkage mechanism that enforces cross-turn dependencies. Furthermore, we convert 7 public benchmarks into a unified Query--Action--Observation--Answer (QAOA) representation with fine-grained evaluation at the function-call, turn, and conversation levels. Experiments show that fine-tuning Qwen3-8B on our dataset substantially improves tool-use performance. Under the distractor-heavy Hybrid-20 setting, achieves 93.0% single-turn Strict Precision, outperforming commercial models including GPT, Gemini, and Claude.

AI.tldr: UniToolCall is a unified framework and dataset for teaching LLMs to use tools via structured function calls, standardizing tool collections, training data, and evaluation to improve and fairly compare tool-use capabilities.
AI.motivation: Research on LLM tool-use has fragmented standards: different ways to represent tool calls, little attention to the structure of multi-step tool-use trajectories, and incompatible benchmarks that prevent clear comparison. There is a need for a large, diverse, and structurally well-controlled corpus plus a unified evaluation protocol to systematically train and assess tool-using LLM agents.
AI.method: The authors build a unified tool-learning pipeline named UniToolCall. They: (1) curate a large pool of 22k+ tools; (2) construct a 390k+ example training corpus by merging and standardizing 10 public datasets and adding synthetic trajectories whose structure (e.g., hop count, turn count, serial vs. parallel calls) is carefully controlled; (3) explicitly model different interaction patterns such as single-hop vs. multi-hop, single-turn vs. multi-turn, and serial vs. parallel tool execution; (4) design an Anchor Linkage mechanism that encodes and enforces cross-turn dependencies to maintain coherent multi-turn reasoning; and (5) convert 7 public benchmarks into a common Query–Action–Observation–Answer (QAOA) format that allows fine-grained evaluation at function-call, turn, and whole-conversation levels. They then fine-tune a Qwen3-8B model on this dataset.
AI.result: Fine-tuning Qwen3-8B on the UniToolCall corpus substantially improves its tool-use capabilities. On a particularly challenging Hybrid-20 evaluation setting, which includes many distracting tools, the fine-tuned model achieves 93.0% single-turn Strict Precision, surpassing strong commercial baselines such as GPT, Gemini, and Claude on this metric.
AI.conclusion: A unified, structurally aware framework for tool-use learning—covering toolset creation, dataset generation, and standardized QAOA-style evaluation—can significantly enhance LLM tool-use performance and enable fairer comparison across models. The UniToolCall framework, large tool pool, and converted benchmarks provide a practical foundation for future research on robust, multi-hop, and multi-turn tool-using agents.

title: Intersectional Sycophancy: How Perceived User Demographics Shape False Validation in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models exhibit sycophantic tendencies--validating incorrect user beliefs to appear agreeable. We investigate whether this behavior varies systematically with perceived user demographics, testing whether combinations of race, age, gender, and expressed confidence level produce differential false validation rates. Inspired by the legal concept of intersectionality, we conduct 768 multi-turn adversarial conversations using Anthropic's Petri evaluation framework, probing GPT-5-nano and Claude Haiku 4.5 across 128 persona combinations in mathematics, philosophy, and conspiracy theory domains. GPT-5-nano is significantly more sycophantic than Claude Haiku 4.5 overall ($\bar{x}=2.96$ vs. $1.74$, $p < 10^{-32}$, Wilcoxon signed-rank). For GPT-5-nano, we find that philosophy elicits 41% more sycophancy than mathematics and that Hispanic personas receive the highest sycophancy across races. The worst-scoring persona, a confident, 23-year-old Hispanic woman, averages 5.33/10 on sycophancy. Claude Haiku 4.5 exhibits uniformly low sycophancy with no significant demographic variation. These results demonstrate that sycophancy is not uniformly distributed across users and that safety evaluations should incorporate identity-aware testing.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how often language models validate users’ wrong beliefs (sycophancy) and whether this depends on users’ demographic personas and confidence levels.
AI.motivation: While sycophancy in large language models is known, it is unclear whether all users are affected equally. If certain demographic groups systematically receive more validation of incorrect beliefs, this could create unfair or harmful disparities. The authors aim to quantify such differential treatment, motivated by intersectionality and safety concerns in AI deployment.
AI.method: Using Anthropic’s Petri evaluation framework, the authors run 768 multi-turn adversarial conversations with two models (GPT-5-nano and Claude Haiku 4.5). They design 128 distinct user personas varying by race, age, gender, and confidence level, and test them across three domains: mathematics, philosophy, and conspiracy theories. They measure a sycophancy score—how often and how strongly the model validates incorrect user beliefs—and compare across personas and models using statistical tests (e.g., Wilcoxon signed-rank test).
AI.result: GPT-5-nano shows substantially higher overall sycophancy than Claude Haiku 4.5 (mean scores 2.96 vs. 1.74, with extremely small p-value). For GPT-5-nano, philosophy prompts induce 41% more sycophancy than mathematics, and there are systematic differences by race: Hispanic personas receive the highest sycophancy, with the worst case being a confident 23-year-old Hispanic woman who scores 5.33 out of 10 on sycophancy. Claude Haiku 4.5 shows consistently low sycophancy and no statistically significant variation across demographics.
AI.conclusion: Sycophancy in language models is not evenly distributed: some demographic and domain combinations elicit more wrongful agreement than others. This implies that model safety and alignment evaluations must be intersectional and identity-aware, explicitly testing for demographic-sensitive behavior rather than assuming uniform performance across users.

title: Context Kubernetes: Declarative Orchestration of Enterprise Knowledge for Agentic AI Systems
comment: 24 pages, 8 tables, 1 figure, 8 experiments (5 correctness + 3 value). Open-source prototype: https://github.com/Cohorte-ai/context-kubernetes
summary: We introduce Context Kubernetes, an architecture for orchestrating enterprise knowledge in agentic AI systems, with a prototype implementation and eight experiments. The core observation is that delivering the right knowledge, to the right agent, with the right permissions, at the right freshness -- across an entire organization -- is structurally analogous to the container orchestration problem Kubernetes solved a decade ago. We formalize six core abstractions, a YAML-based declarative manifest for knowledge-architecture-as-code, a reconciliation loop, and a three-tier agent permission model where agent authority is always a strict subset of human authority. Three value experiments show: (1) without governance, agents serve phantom content from deleted sources and leak cross-domain data in 26.5% of queries; (2) without freshness monitoring, stale content is served silently -- with reconciliation, staleness is detected in under 1ms; (3) in five attack scenarios, flat permissions block 0/5 attacks, basic RBAC blocks 4/5, and the three-tier model blocks 5/5. Five correctness experiments confirm zero unauthorized deliveries, zero invariant violations, and architectural enforcement of out-of-band approval isolation that no surveyed enterprise platform provides. A survey of four major platforms (Microsoft, Salesforce, AWS, Google) documents that none architecturally isolates agent approval channels. We identify four properties that make context orchestration harder than container orchestration, and argue that these make the solution more valuable.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Context Kubernetes, a Kubernetes-inspired architecture for orchestrating enterprise knowledge for AI agents, showing via a prototype and experiments that governance, freshness monitoring, and a three-tier permission model are critical to prevent data leakage, stale answers, and security breaches.
AI.motivation: As enterprises adopt agentic AI systems, they lack robust, scalable mechanisms to deliver the correct knowledge to agents with proper permissions and up-to-date content. Existing enterprise platforms do not architecturally isolate agent approval channels and offer limited guarantees against data leakage, stale context, and mis-governed access. The authors are motivated by the analogy between container orchestration and knowledge orchestration and aim to bring Kubernetes-like rigor, governance, and automation to AI context management.
AI.method: The authors design Context Kubernetes, defining six core abstractions for knowledge orchestration, a declarative YAML-based manifest language for describing knowledge architecture as code, and a reconciliation loop that continuously aligns real-world knowledge delivery with the desired state. They add a three-tier permission model where an agent’s authority is strictly bounded by human authority. They implement a prototype and run eight experiments: three value-oriented experiments that measure governance failures, freshness issues, and security under different permission schemes, and five correctness experiments that test for unauthorized deliveries, invariant violations, and compliance with approval isolation properties. They also survey four major enterprise platforms to assess whether they provide architectural isolation of agent approval channels.
AI.result: In value experiments, they find that without governance, agents return phantom content from deleted sources and leak cross-domain data in 26.5% of queries; without freshness monitoring, agents silently serve stale content, whereas their reconciliation loop detects staleness in under 1ms; and in five simulated attack scenarios, flat permissions fail all attacks (0/5 blocked), basic RBAC blocks 4/5, and their three-tier permission model blocks all (5/5). In correctness experiments, they observe zero unauthorized knowledge deliveries and zero invariant violations, and they show that the architecture enforces out-of-band approval isolation that surveyed enterprise platforms do not provide. Their survey of Microsoft, Salesforce, AWS, and Google platforms confirms that none currently architecturally isolate agent approval channels in the way Context Kubernetes does.
AI.conclusion: Context orchestration for AI agents is structurally analogous to container orchestration but presents unique challenges, including governance, freshness, and fine-grained permissioning across organizational knowledge. The proposed Context Kubernetes architecture, with its abstractions, declarative manifests, reconciliation loop, and three-tier permission model, addresses these challenges and provides stronger guarantees than current enterprise platforms. The authors argue that because context orchestration is harder than container orchestration, the value of a robust solution is even higher, and their experimental and survey results indicate that their approach closes important gaps in governance, security, and correctness for enterprise AI systems.

title: RationalRewards: Reasoning Rewards Scale Visual Generation Both Training and Test Time
comment: Project Page: https://tiger-ai-lab.github.io/RationalRewards/ ; Code, Dataset, Models are released
summary: Most reward models for visual generation reduce rich human judgments to a single unexplained score, discarding the reasoning that underlies preference. We show that teaching reward models to produce explicit, multi-dimensional critiques before scoring transforms them from passive evaluators into active optimization tools, improving generators in two complementary ways: at training time, structured rationales provide interpretable, fine-grained rewards for reinforcement learning; at test time, a Generate-Critique-Refine loop turns critiques into targeted prompt revisions that improve outputs without any parameter updates. To train such a reward model without costly rationale annotations, we introduce Preference-Anchored Rationalization (PARROT), a principled framework that recovers high-quality rationales from readily available preference data through anchored generation, consistency filtering, and distillation. The resulting model, RationalRewards (8B), achieves state-of-the-art preference prediction among open-source reward models, competitive with Gemini-2.5-Pro, while using 10-20x less training data than comparable baselines. As an RL reward, it consistently improves text-to-image and image-editing generators beyond scalar alternatives. Most strikingly, its test-time critique-and-refine loop matches or exceeds RL-based fine-tuning on several benchmarks, suggesting that structured reasoning can unlock latent capabilities in existing generators that suboptimal prompts fail to elicit.

AI.tldr: Paper proposes RationalRewards, a reward model that generates structured critiques before scoring, enabling better training and test-time optimization of visual generators.
AI.motivation: Existing visual reward models compress human preferences into a single scalar score, losing the underlying reasoning and limiting both interpretability and effectiveness for guiding generation and RL.
AI.method: Introduce Preference-Anchored Rationalization (PARROT), which learns to generate multi-dimensional rationales from standard preference data via anchored generation, consistency filtering, and distillation, yielding a reward model that first critiques along multiple dimensions and then produces a scalar reward used for RL and for a Generate-Critique-Refine loop at test time.
AI.result: RationalRewards (8B) attains state-of-the-art preference prediction among open-source reward models, rivals Gemini-2.5-Pro using 10–20x less training data, and as an RL reward improves text-to-image and image-editing systems beyond scalar baselines; its test-time critique-and-refine process alone matches or surpasses RL fine-tuning on several benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Embedding explicit structured reasoning into reward models turns them into active tools for optimization, enabling both more sample-efficient training and powerful test-time prompt refinement that can unlock latent capabilities of existing visual generators.

title: Why Do Large Language Models Generate Harmful Content?
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have been shown to generate harmful content. However, the underlying causes of such behavior remain under explored. We propose a causal mediation analysis-based approach to identify the causal factors responsible for harmful generation. Our method performs a multi-granular analysis across model layers, modules (MLP and attention blocks), and individual neurons. Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art LLMs indicate that harmful generation arises in the later layers of the model, results primarily from failures in MLP blocks rather than attention blocks, and is associated with neurons that act as a gating mechanism for harmful generation. The results indicate that the early layers in the model are used for a contextual understanding of harmfulness in a prompt, which is then propagated through the model, to generate harmfulness in the late layers, as well as a signal indicating harmfulness through MLP blocks. This is then further propagated to the last layer of the model, specifically to a sparse set of neurons, which receives the signal and determines the generation of harmful content accordingly.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates why large language models generate harmful content by tracing where and how harmful signals arise inside the network.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs can produce harmful outputs, most work focuses on detecting or mitigating them from the outside rather than understanding their internal causal mechanisms. The authors aim to reveal which components and layers actually cause harmful generation, to enable more targeted and principled safety interventions.
AI.method: They use causal mediation analysis on LLM internals, intervening on activations at different granularities: entire layers, MLP vs. attention blocks, and individual neurons. By perturbing or blocking specific components and measuring the downstream effect on harmful output, they estimate how much each part mediates harmful generation.
AI.result: The study finds that: (1) harmful generation is largely driven by later layers; (2) within those layers, MLP blocks contribute more to harmfulness than attention blocks; (3) a small subset of neurons functions as gates that control whether harmful content is produced, carrying a harmfulness signal forward to the final layer.
AI.conclusion: Harmful behavior in LLMs is not uniform across the network but emerges from specific late-layer MLP pathways and sparse gating neurons. Early layers mainly encode and propagate an understanding of harmfulness in context, which is then converted into actual harmful output in later layers. This layered, localized view suggests that safety methods should target particular MLP blocks and neurons rather than treating the model as a black box.

title: DreamKG: A KG-Augmented Conversational System for People Experiencing Homelessness
comment: This manuscript has been accepted at the 14th IEEE International Conference on Healthcare Informatics (ICHI 2026)
summary: People experiencing homelessness (PEH) face substantial barriers to accessing timely, accurate information about community services. DreamKG addresses this through a knowledge graph-augmented conversational system that grounds responses in verified, up-to-date data about Philadelphia organizations, services, locations, and hours. Unlike standard large language models (LLMs) prone to hallucinations, DreamKG combines Neo4j knowledge graphs with structured query understanding to handle location-aware and time-sensitive queries reliably. The system performs spatial reasoning for distance-based recommendations and temporal filtering for operating hours. Preliminary evaluation shows 59% superiority over Google Search AI on relevant queries and 84% rejection of irrelevant queries. This demonstration highlights the potential of hybrid architectures that combines LLM flexibility with knowledge graph reliability to improve service accessibility for vulnerable populations effectively.

AI.tldr: DreamKG is a conversational system for people experiencing homelessness that uses a knowledge graph to give accurate, up-to-date, location- and time-aware information on community services, outperforming Google Search AI on relevant queries and rejection of irrelevant ones.
AI.motivation: People experiencing homelessness need trustworthy, current information about nearby services (e.g., shelters, food, healthcare), but they face barriers like information fragmentation, outdated resources, and LLM hallucinations. Standard conversational AI often fabricates details or misses key constraints like operating hours and proximity, which can have serious consequences for this vulnerable population. The paper is motivated by the need for an accessible, conversational interface that is both flexible and reliably grounded in verified local service data.
AI.method: The authors build DreamKG, a hybrid conversational assistant that augments an LLM with a Neo4j-based knowledge graph of Philadelphia organizations, services, locations, and hours. The system uses structured query understanding to convert user requests into graph queries, enabling spatial reasoning (e.g., distance-based recommendations) and temporal filtering (e.g., open now, open on weekends). Responses are grounded in the retrieved graph data rather than purely generated by the LLM. They perform a preliminary evaluation by comparing DreamKG against Google Search AI on task-relevant queries and by measuring its ability to reject irrelevant queries.
AI.result: DreamKG achieves a 59% superiority over Google Search AI on relevant information-seeking queries, indicating better quality or suitability of answers for the target use cases. Additionally, the system correctly rejects 84% of irrelevant queries, suggesting robust handling of out-of-scope questions and reduced hallucination. These results demonstrate that the knowledge graph grounding and structured query approach meaningfully improves reliability compared with a strong baseline.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that hybrid architectures combining LLMs with knowledge graphs can substantially improve access to accurate, context-aware service information for people experiencing homelessness. By enabling spatial and temporal reasoning grounded in verified data, DreamKG mitigates LLM hallucinations and supports safer, more reliable assistance. The demonstration underscores the promise of such systems for enhancing service navigation and accessibility for vulnerable populations and suggests broader applicability in other civic and social-service domains.

title: A Mamba-Based Multimodal Network for Multiscale Blast-Induced Rapid Structural Damage Assessment
summary: Accurate and rapid structural damage assessment (SDA) is crucial for post-disaster management, helping responders prioritise resources, plan rescues, and support recovery. Traditional field inspections, though precise, are limited by accessibility, safety risks, and time constraints, especially after large explosions. Machine learning with remote sensing has emerged as a scalable solution for rapid SDA, with Mamba-based networks achieving state-of-the-art performance. However, these methods often require extensive training and large datasets, limiting real-world applicability. Moreover, they fail to incorporate key physical characteristics of blast loading for SDA. To overcome these challenges, we propose a Mamba-based multimodal network for rapid SDA that integrates multi-scale blast-loading information with optical remote sensing images. Evaluated on the 2020 Beirut explosion, our method significantly improves performance over state-of-the-art approaches. Code is available at: https://github.com/IMPACTSquad/Blast-Mamba

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Mamba-based multimodal deep learning model that fuses blast-loading characteristics with optical remote sensing to rapidly and accurately assess structural damage after explosions, achieving superior performance on the 2020 Beirut explosion data.
AI.motivation: Post-disaster structural damage assessment is essential for prioritizing rescue and recovery, but traditional field inspections are slow, hazardous, and constrained in large-scale explosion scenarios. Existing remote-sensing machine learning methods, including recent Mamba-based networks, need large training datasets and ignore physical aspects of blast loading, reducing practicality and robustness. The paper aims to create a more data-efficient and physically informed SDA method suitable for real-world deployment after blasts.
AI.method: The authors design a Mamba-based multimodal neural network that jointly processes optical remote sensing imagery and engineered representations of blast loading at multiple spatial scales. The architecture uses the Mamba sequence modeling backbone to capture long-range dependencies, while a fusion mechanism integrates physical blast features (e.g., distance to blast, estimated overpressure fields) with visual cues of damage in satellite or aerial images. The model is trained and evaluated on data from the 2020 Beirut explosion, comparing against state-of-the-art SDA approaches.
AI.result: On the Beirut explosion dataset, the proposed multimodal Mamba-based model significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art structural damage assessment methods in terms of accuracy and related metrics for classifying damage levels. The integration of multi-scale blast-loading information yields clear performance gains over purely image-based models, demonstrating the value of combining physical and visual modalities.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating explicit, multi-scale blast-loading information into a Mamba-based multimodal network enables faster and more accurate structural damage assessment from remote sensing after explosions. This physically informed approach improves over prior state-of-the-art methods and offers a more practical and scalable tool for post-disaster management. The released code facilitates further research and potential operational deployment for rapid SDA in future blast events.

title: Collaborative Multi-Agent Scripts Generation for Enhancing Imperfect-Information Reasoning in Murder Mystery Games
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown impressive capabilities in perceptual tasks, yet they degrade in complex multi-hop reasoning under multiplayer game settings with imperfect and deceptive information. In this paper, we study a representative multiplayer task, Murder Mystery Games, which require inferring hidden truths based on partial clues provided by roles with different intentions. To address this challenge, we propose a collaborative multi-agent framework for evaluating and synthesizing high-quality, role-driven multiplayer game scripts, enabling fine-grained interaction patterns tailored to character identities (i.e., murderer vs. innocent). Our system generates rich multimodal contexts, including character backstories, visual and textual clues, and multi-hop reasoning chains, through coordinated agent interactions. We design a two-stage agent-monitored training strategy to enhance the reasoning ability of VLMs: (1) chain-of-thought based fine-tuning on curated and synthetic datasets that model uncertainty and deception; (2) GRPO-based reinforcement learning with agent-monitored reward shaping, encouraging the model to develop character-specific reasoning behaviors and effective multimodal multi-hop inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly boosts the performance of VLMs in narrative reasoning, hidden fact extraction, and deception-resilient understanding. Our contributions offer a scalable solution for training and evaluating VLMs under uncertain, adversarial, and socially complex conditions, laying the groundwork for future benchmarks in multimodal multi-hop reasoning under imperfect information.

AI.tldr: The paper enhances vision-language models’ ability to do complex, deceptive, multi-hop reasoning in multiplayer “Murder Mystery” game settings by using a collaborative multi-agent framework to generate rich training data and a two-stage training scheme (CoT fine-tuning + RL with agent-shaped rewards).
AI.motivation: Current vision-language models perform well on basic perceptual tasks but struggle with complex multi-hop reasoning in settings with multiple agents, hidden information, and deception (e.g., murder mystery games). There is also a lack of scalable, high-quality benchmarks and training data that capture role-specific behavior, uncertainty, and adversarial interactions in such games. The authors aim to build both a framework and training strategy to close this gap, so VLMs can reason better in socially complex, imperfect-information scenarios.
AI.method: They propose a collaborative multi-agent framework that automatically generates and evaluates rich “Murder Mystery” game scripts. Different agents assume roles (murderer, innocents, etc.) and coordinate to produce character backstories, visual and textual clues, and explicit multi-hop reasoning chains tailored to each role’s intentions (including deception). On top of this data, they use a two-stage training pipeline for VLMs: (1) supervised fine-tuning with chain-of-thought (CoT) style annotations on curated and synthetic datasets modeling uncertainty and deceptive information; (2) GRPO-based reinforcement learning, where monitoring agents define reward signals that encourage role-consistent reasoning, robust hidden-fact inference, and accurate multimodal multi-hop reasoning under deceptive, partial information.
AI.result: In experiments on narrative reasoning tasks derived from these Murder Mystery scenarios, the trained VLMs show substantial performance gains compared with baselines. They improve at extracting hidden facts, handling deceptive or misleading clues, and conducting multi-hop reasoning that integrates visual and textual evidence. The results validate that the agent-generated data and the agent-monitored RL training both contribute to stronger, more deception-resilient reasoning performance.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that collaborative multi-agent script generation plus a two-stage CoT+RL training process provides an effective and scalable way to improve VLM reasoning in adversarial, uncertain, and socially complex environments. This approach yields better narrative and multimodal multi-hop reasoning under imperfect and deceptive information and can serve as a foundation for future benchmarks and training regimes for VLMs in multiplayer and game-like settings.

title: Retrieval Is Not Enough: Why Organizational AI Needs Epistemic Infrastructure
summary:
Organizational knowledge used by AI agents typically lacks epistemic structure: retrieval systems surface semantically relevant content without distinguishing binding decisions from abandoned hypotheses, contested claims from settled ones, or known facts from unresolved questions. We argue that the ceiling on organizational AI is not retrieval fidelity but \emph{epistemic} fidelity--the system's ability to represent commitment strength, contradiction status, and organizational ignorance as computable properties.
  We present OIDA, a framework that structures organizational knowledge as typed Knowledge Objects carrying epistemic class, importance scores with class-specific decay, and signed contradiction edges. The Knowledge Gravity Engine maintains scores deterministically with proved convergence guarantees (sufficient condition: max degree $< 7$; empirically robust to degree 43). OIDA introduces QUESTION-as-modeled-ignorance: a primitive with inverse decay that surfaces what an organization does \emph{not} know with increasing urgency--a mechanism absent from all surveyed systems. We describe the Epistemic Quality Score (EQS), a five-component evaluation methodology with explicit circularity analysis. In a controlled comparison ($n{=}10$ response pairs), OIDA's RAG condition (3,868 tokens) achieves EQS 0.530 vs.\ 0.848 for a full-context baseline (108,687 tokens); the $28.1\times$ token budget difference is the primary confound. The QUESTION mechanism is statistically validated (Fisher $p{=}0.0325$, OR$=21.0$). The formal properties are established; the decisive ablation at equal token budget (E4) is pre-registered and not yet run.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes OIDA, a framework that adds explicit epistemic structure (certainty, contradiction, and ignorance) to organizational knowledge used by AI systems, and shows early evidence that it can match or approach full-context performance with far fewer tokens while uniquely modeling what the organization does not know.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems for organizational knowledge (e.g., retrieval-augmented generation) treat all retrieved content as flat text, lacking distinctions between firm decisions, tentative ideas, contested claims, and open questions. This makes it hard for organizations to track what is known, what is uncertain, what is contradictory, and what is still unknown. The authors argue that this missing epistemic structure, rather than retrieval accuracy alone, limits how effective organizational AI can be.
AI.method: They introduce OIDA, which represents knowledge as typed Knowledge Objects that carry: (1) epistemic class (e.g., fact, hypothesis, decision, question), (2) importance scores governed by class-specific decay rules, and (3) signed contradiction edges between objects. A "Knowledge Gravity Engine" deterministically updates these scores, with theoretical convergence guarantees under bounded graph degree. OIDA also defines a special QUESTION object whose score increases over time (inverse decay) to highlight organizational ignorance. They further define an Epistemic Quality Score (EQS), a five-component evaluation framework that explicitly analyzes circularity. They perform a small controlled comparison of OIDA-based RAG vs a full-context baseline, and a statistical test of the QUESTION mechanism.
AI.result: In a small-scale experiment with 10 response pairs, a retrieval-augmented generation setup using OIDA with 3,868 tokens achieved an EQS of 0.530, compared to 0.848 for a full-context baseline that used 108,687 tokens. The authors note that the roughly 28x token difference is a major confound in interpreting these scores. They statistically validate the QUESTION mechanism using Fisher's exact test, obtaining p = 0.0325 and an odds ratio of 21.0, suggesting it meaningfully surfaces modeled ignorance. They prove convergence properties of the Knowledge Gravity Engine under certain degree constraints and observe empirical robustness up to degree 43.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that epistemic fidelity—explicitly modeling commitment strength, contradiction, and ignorance—is crucial for advancing organizational AI beyond mere semantic retrieval. OIDA offers a principled framework to encode these epistemic properties as computable, dynamically updated attributes of knowledge objects, including a novel treatment of questions as growing signals of ignorance. Initial experiments and theoretical results support the feasibility and potential value of this approach, but a key registered ablation study at equal token budgets has not yet been completed, so the comparative performance claims remain provisional.

title: GenTac: Generative Modeling and Forecasting of Soccer Tactics
summary: Modeling open-play soccer tactics is a formidable challenge due to the stochastic, multi-agent nature of the game. Existing computational approaches typically produce single, deterministic trajectory forecasts or focus on highly structured set-pieces, fundamentally failing to capture the inherent variance and branching possibilities of real-world match evolution. Here, we introduce GenTac, a diffusion-based generative framework that conceptualizes soccer tactics as a stochastic process over continuous multi-player trajectories and discrete semantic events. By learning the underlying distribution of player movements from historical tracking data, GenTac samples diverse, plausible, long-horizon future trajectories. The framework supports rich contextual conditioning, including opponent behavior, specific team or league playing styles, and strategic objectives, while grounding continuous spatial dynamics into a 15-class tactical event space. Extensive evaluations on our proposed benchmark, TacBench, demonstrate four key capabilities: (1) GenTac achieves high geometric accuracy while strictly preserving the collective structural consistency of the team; (2) it accurately simulates stylistic nuances, distinguishing between specific teams (e.g., Auckland FC) and leagues (e.g., A-League versus German leagues); (3) it enables controllable counterfactual simulations, demonstrably altering spatial control and expected threat metrics based on offensive or defensive guidance; and (4) it reliably anticipates future tactical outcomes directly from generated rollouts. Finally, we demonstrate that GenTac can be successfully trained to generalize to other dynamic team sports, including basketball, American football, and ice hockey.

AI.tldr: GenTac is a diffusion-based generative model that simulates diverse, long-horizon soccer tactics as stochastic multi-player trajectories and tactical events, with strong accuracy, stylistic control, counterfactual what-if capabilities, and cross-sport generalization.
AI.motivation: Open-play soccer tactics are difficult to model because play is stochastic, involves many interacting agents, and has branching futures. Existing methods usually predict a single deterministic trajectory or focus on structured set pieces, so they miss the variability, uncertainty, and richness of real match evolution. There is a need for models that can generate multiple plausible tactical futures, incorporate context like team style or objectives, and connect fine-grained movement with higher-level tactical events.
AI.method: The authors propose GenTac, a diffusion-based generative framework that represents tactics as a stochastic process over continuous player trajectories and discrete tactical events. Trained on historical tracking data, the model learns the distribution of multi-player movements and a 15-class tactical event space. It supports conditioning on context such as opponent behavior, team/league style, and strategic goals, and then samples diverse plausible long-horizon futures. They introduce a benchmark, TacBench, to evaluate geometric accuracy, structural consistency, style simulation, controllability, and outcome prediction.
AI.result: On TacBench, GenTac (1) attains high geometric accuracy in predicted trajectories while preserving team-level structural patterns; (2) faithfully reproduces stylistic signatures of specific teams and leagues; (3) enables controllable counterfactual simulations that meaningfully change spatial control and expected threat under offensive/defensive guidance; and (4) can anticipate tactical outcomes directly from generated rollouts. They also show that the framework trains and generalizes well to other team sports such as basketball, American football, and ice hockey.
AI.conclusion: GenTac successfully models open-play soccer as a stochastic generative process, producing diverse, accurate, and context-aware tactical simulations. It bridges continuous spatial dynamics with discrete tactical semantics, allows controllable what-if analyses, and generalizes across multiple team sports, suggesting a versatile tool for tactical analysis and forecasting in complex multi-agent games.
